title: CART: Context-Aware Terrain Adaptation using Temporal Sequence Selection for Legged Robots
summary: Animals in nature combine multiple modalities, such as sight and feel, to perceive terrain and develop an understanding of how to walk on uneven terrain in a stable manner. Similarly, legged robots need to develop their ability to stably walk on complex terrains by developing an understanding of the relationship between vision and proprioception. Most current terrain adaptation methods are susceptible to failure on complex, off-road terrain as they rely on prior experience, particularly observations from a vision sensor. This experience-based learning often creates a Visual-Texture Paradox between what has been seen and how it actually feels. In this work, we introduce CART, a high-level controller built on a context-aware terrain adaptation approach that integrates proprioception and exteroception from onboard sensing to achieve a robust understanding of terrain. We evaluate our method on multiple terrains using an ANYmal-C robot on the IsaacSim simulator and a Boston Dynamics SPOT robot for our real-world experiments. To evaluate the learned contextual terrain properties, we adapt vibrational stability on the base of the robot as a metric. We compare CART with various state-of-the-art baselines equipped with multimodal sensing in both simulation and the real world. CART achieves an average success rate improvement of 5% over all baselines in simulation and improves the overall stability up to 45% and 24% in the real world without increasing the time taken by the robot to accomplish locomotion tasks.

AI.tldr: CART is a context-aware multimodal terrain adaptation controller that fuses proprioception and exteroception to robustly walk on uneven terrain, addressing the Visual-Texture Paradox and outperforming baselines without extra locomotion time.
AI.motivation: Vision-only terrain perception can misrepresent terrain feel, causing failures on complex off-road terrains; integrating proprioceptive feedback and exteroceptive sensing with context improves robustness.
AI.method: A high-level controller CART that integrates onboard proprioception and exteroception for context-aware terrain adaptation; evaluated on ANYmal-C in IsaacSim and SPOT in real world; uses vibrational stability as a metric; compares against multimodal baselines.
AI.result: In simulation, CART yields an average 5% improvement in success rate over baselines; in real-world tests, stability improves up to 45% and 24% across terrains without increasing locomotion time; outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: CART demonstrates robust terrain understanding through multimodal sensing, improving stability and success without incurring additional task duration, with demonstrated sim-to-real transfer.

title: RoSLAC: Robust Simultaneous Localization and Calibration of Multiple Magnetometers
summary: Localization of autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) in enclosed or semi-enclosed environments such as offices, hotels, hospitals, indoor parking facilities, and underground spaces where GPS signals are weak or unavailable remains a major obstacle to the deployment of fully autonomous systems. Infrastructure-based localization approaches, such as QR codes and RFID, are constrained by high installation and maintenance costs as well as limited flexibility, while onboard sensor-based methods, including LiDAR- and vision-based solutions, are affected by ambiguous geometric features and frequent occlusions caused by dynamic obstacles such as pedestrians. Ambient magnetic field (AMF)-based localization has therefore attracted growing interest in recent years because it does not rely on external infrastructure or geometric features, making it well-suited for AMR applications such as service robots and security robots. However, magnetometer measurements are often corrupted by distortions caused by ferromagnetic materials present on the sensor platform, which bias the AMF and degrade localization reliability. As a result, accurate magnetometer calibration to estimate distortion parameters becomes essential. Conventional calibration methods that rely on rotating the magnetometer are impractical for large and heavy platforms. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a robust simultaneous localization and calibration (RoSLAC) approach based on alternating optimization, which iteratively and efficiently estimates both the platform pose and magnetometer calibration parameters. Extensive evaluations conducted in high-fidelity simulation and real-world environments demonstrate that the proposed RoSLAC method achieves high localization accuracy while maintaining low computational cost compared with state-of-the-art magnetometer calibration techniques.

AI.tldr: RoSLAC jointly estimates the AMR pose and magnetometer distortion parameters via alternating optimization, enabling robust AMF-based indoor localization without magnetometer rotation. It achieves high localization accuracy with low computational cost compared to existing magnetometer calibration methods.
AI.motivation: Indoor/ enclosed-environment localization for autonomous mobile robots using ambient magnetic fields is appealing due to no external infrastructure. However, ferromagnetic distortions bias magnetometer readings, requiring calibration. Traditional rotation-based calibration is impractical for large, heavy platforms, necessitating a scalable alternative.
AI.method: A robust alternating-optimization framework (RoSLAC) that jointly and iteratively refines the platform pose and magnetometer calibration parameters from magnetometer (and possibly IMU) data, without relying on magnetometer rotation. The approach emphasizes robustness and computational efficiency compared with state-of-the-art calibration techniques.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation in high-fidelity simulation and real-world environments shows RoSLAC achieves high localization accuracy while maintaining low computational cost, outperforming state-of-the-art magnetometer calibration methods in the considered scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RoSLAC provides a practical, scalable solution for AMR localization in GPS-denied environments by integrating magnetometer calibration into the localization loop, enabling reliable ambient magnetic field-based positioning without mechanical rotation of the sensor.

title: SpaceMind: A Modular and Self-Evolving Embodied Vision-Language Agent Framework for Autonomous On-orbit Servicing
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures, 7 tables. Code available at https://github.com/wuaodi/SpaceMind
summary: Autonomous on-orbit servicing demands embodied agents that perceive through visual sensors, reason about 3D spatial situations, and execute multi-phase tasks over extended horizons. We present SpaceMind, a modular and self-evolving vision-language model (VLM) agent framework that decomposes knowledge, tools, and reasoning into three independently extensible dimensions: skill modules with dynamic routing, Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools with configurable profiles, and injectable reasoning-mode skills. An MCP-Redis interface layer enables the same codebase to operate across simulation and physical hardware without modification, and a Skill Self-Evolution mechanism distills operational experience into persistent skill files without model fine-tuning. We validate SpaceMind through 192 closed-loop runs across five satellites, three task types, and two environments, a UE5 simulation and a physical laboratory, deliberately including degraded conditions to stress-test robustness. Under nominal conditions all modes achieve 90--100% navigation success; under degradation, the Prospective mode uniquely succeeds in search-and-approach tasks where other modes fail. A self-evolution study shows that the agent recovers from failure in four of six groups from a single failed episode, including complete failure to 100% success and inspection scores improving from 12 to 59 out of 100. Real-world validation confirms zero-code-modification transfer to a physical robot with 100% rendezvous success. Code: https://github.com/wuaodi/SpaceMind

AI.tldr: SpaceMind is a modular, self-evolving vision-language model agent for autonomous on-orbit servicing, decoupling knowledge, tools, and reasoning into extensible modules. It uses a Model Context Protocol (MCP) with a Redis interface for sim-to-real portability and a Skill Self-Evolution mechanism to distill experience without fine-tuning. Validated in 192 closed-loop runs across simulated and real hardware, showing strong nominal performance and robustness under degradation, with real-world zero-code-transfer achieving 100% rendezvous success.
AI.motivation: Reliable, long-horizon autonomous operation in space requires robust perception, planning, and execution across sim-to-real deployments. A modular, extensible framework is needed to handle varied tasks, tooling, and degraded conditions without retraining.
AI.method: SpaceMind decomposes capabilities into three dimensions: (1) skill modules with dynamic routing for task-specific behavior, (2) MCP tools with configurable profiles enabling flexible tool use across environments, and (3) injectable reasoning-mode skills for adaptable reasoning strategies. An MCP-Redis interface unifies control across simulation and hardware; a Skill Self-Evolution mechanism distills operational experience into persistent skill files without model fine-tuning. Evaluation involved 192 closed-loop runs over five satellites, three task types, and two environments (UE5 simulation and a physical lab), including degraded scenarios to test robustness.
AI.result: Nominally, all modes deliver 90–100% navigation success. Under degradation, Prospective mode uniquely succeeds in search-and-approach tasks where others fail. Self-evolution demonstrates recovery in four of six groups from a single failed episode, with inspection scores rising from 12 to 59/100. Real-world validation confirms zero-code-modification transfer to a physical robot with 100% rendezvous success.
AI.conclusion: SpaceMind demonstrates strong sim-to-real transferability, robustness to degraded conditions, and effective self-improvement without heavy fine-tuning. Its modular design and MCP-based tooling offer a scalable path for autonomous on-orbit servicing and similar long-horizon robotic tasks.

title: BIEVR-LIO: Robust LiDAR-Inertial Odometry through Bump-Image-Enhanced Voxel Maps
summary: Reliable odometry is essential for mobile robots as they increasingly enter more challenging environments, which often contain little information to constrain point cloud registration, resulting in degraded LiDAR-Inertial Odometry (LIO) accuracy or even divergence. To address this, we present BIEVR-LIO, a novel approach designed specifically to exploit subtle variations in the available geometry for improved robustness. We propose a high-resolution map representation that stores surfaces as compact voxel-wise oriented height images. This representation can directly be used for registration without the calculation of intermediate geometric primitives while still supporting efficient updates. Since informative geometry is often sparsely distributed in the environment, we further propose a map-informed point sampling strategy to focus registration on geometrically informative regions, improving robustness in uninformative environments while reducing computational cost compared to global high-resolution sampling. Experiments across multiple sensors, platforms, and environments demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in well-constrained scenes and substantial improvements in challenging scenarios where baseline methods diverge. Additionally, we demonstrate that the fine-grained geometry captured by BIEVR-LIO can be used for downstream tasks such as elevation mapping for robot locomotion.

AI.tldr: Introduces BIEVR-LIO, a robust LIO method that uses a high-resolution voxel-wise oriented height-image map and a map-informed sampling strategy to improve registration in challenging, information-sparse environments, achieving state-of-the-art results and enabling downstream elevation mapping.
AI.motivation: LiDAR-Inertial Odometry performance degrades in environments with little geometric information; need a robust, efficient representation and sampling strategy that can leverage subtle geometry variations for reliable registration.
AI.method: Develop a high-resolution map representation that stores surfaces as compact voxel-wise oriented height images, enabling direct registration without intermediate geometric primitives and supporting efficient updates. Introduce a map-informed point sampling strategy to focus registration on geometrically informative regions, reducing computational cost while improving robustness in uninformative areas.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple sensors, platforms, and environments show state-of-the-art performance in well-constrained scenes and substantial improvements in challenging scenarios where baseline methods diverge, with the approach also enabling downstream tasks such as elevation mapping for robot locomotion.
AI.conclusion: BIEVR-LIO provides robust, efficient odometry in geometry-sparse settings by combining a rich, easily registrable surface representation with informed sampling, and offers tangible benefits for downstream spatial tasks.

title: The Devil Is in Gradient Entanglement: Energy-Aware Gradient Coordinator for Robust Generalized Category Discovery
summary: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) leverages labeled data to categorize unlabeled samples from known or unknown classes. Most previous methods jointly optimize supervised and unsupervised objectives and achieve promising results. However, inherent optimization interference still limits their ability to improve further. Through quantitative analysis, we identify a key issue, i.e., gradient entanglement, which 1) distorts supervised gradients and weakens discrimination among known classes, and 2) induces representation-subspace overlap between known and novel classes, reducing the separability of novel categories. To address this issue, we propose the Energy-Aware Gradient Coordinator (EAGC), a plug-and-play gradient-level module that explicitly regulates the optimization process. EAGC comprises two components: Anchor-based Gradient Alignment (AGA) and Energy-aware Elastic Projection (EEP). AGA introduces a reference model to anchor the gradient directions of labeled samples, preserving the discriminative structure of known classes against the interference of unlabeled gradients. EEP softly projects unlabeled gradients onto the complement of the known-class subspace and derives an energy-based coefficient to adaptively scale the projection for each unlabeled sample according to its degree of alignment with the known subspace, thereby reducing subspace overlap without suppressing unlabeled samples that likely belong to known classes. Experiments show that EAGC consistently boosts existing methods and establishes new state-of-the-art results. Code is available at https://haiyangzheng.github.io/EAGC.

AI.tldr: Identifies gradient entanglement as a core bottleneck in Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) that hinders discrimination and increases subspace overlap between known and novel classes; proposes a plug-and-play gradient module (EAGC) with Anchor-based Gradient Alignment (AGA) and Energy-aware Elastic Projection (EEP) to align supervised gradients and softly project unlabeled gradients away from the known-subspace. This yields consistent improvements and new state-of-the-art results; code released.
AI.motivation: GCD methods struggle due to optimization interference from jointly leveraging labeled (supervised) and unlabeled data. Gradient entanglement distorts discriminative structure for known classes and causes subspace overlap with novel classes, reducing separability and performance.
AI.method: Propose Energy-Aware Gradient Coordinator (EAGC), a plug-and-play gradient-level module consisting of two components: (1) Anchor-based Gradient Alignment (AGA) uses a reference model to anchor the gradient directions of labeled samples, preserving known-class discriminative structure; (2) Energy-aware Elastic Projection (EEP) softly projects unlabeled gradients onto the complement of the known-class subspace and uses an energy-based coefficient to adaptively scale the projection per sample based on its alignment with the known subspace.
AI.result: Empirical results show that EAGC consistently boosts existing GCD methods and achieves new state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks; code available at the authors’ project page.
AI.conclusion: Regulating gradient optimization with EAGC mitigates gradient entanglement, preserves discriminative structure for known classes, and reduces subspace overlap between known and novel classes, enabling more effective generalization in GCD.

title: QualiaNet: An Experience-Before-Inference Network
summary: Human 3D vision involves two distinct stages: an Experience Module, where stereo depth is extracted relative to fixation, and an Inference Module, where this experience is interpreted to estimate 3D scene properties. Paradoxically, although our experience of stereo vision does not provide us with distance information, it does affect our inferences about visual scale. We propose the Inference Module exploits a natural scene statistic: near scenes produce vivid disparity gradients, while far scenes appear comparatively flat. QualiaNet implements this two-stage architecture computationally: disparity maps simulating human stereo experience are passed to a CNN trained to estimate distance. The network can recover distance from disparity gradients alone, validating this approach.

AI.tldr: A two-stage model (Experience -> Inference) shows distance can be inferred from disparity gradients; QualiaNet demonstrates this by using stereo-derived disparity maps fed into a CNN trained to estimate distance.
AI.motivation: To explain how perceptual experience of stereo (which lacks explicit distance) can influence judgments of scene scale, by leveraging natural scene statistics linking disparity gradients to depth.
AI.method: Propose QualiaNet: generate disparity maps representing human stereo experience relative to fixation, then train a CNN to estimate distance from these gradients; evaluate whether distance can be recovered from gradients alone.
AI.result: QualiaNet succeeds: the CNN can recover distance using disparity gradient cues alone, supporting the proposed two-stage architecture and the role of scene statistics in 3D inference.
AI.conclusion: The Inference Module can harness statistical regularities in natural scenes to map qualitative stereo experience to quantitative 3D scale, validating a separation between experience and inference in human 3D vision.

title: Interactive Exploration of Large-scale Streamlines of Vector Fields via a Curve Segment Neighborhood Graph
summary: Streamlines have been widely used to represent and analyze various steady vector fields. To sufficiently represent important features in complex vector fields (like flow), a large number of streamlines are required. Due to the lack of a rigorous definition of features or patterns in streamlines, user interaction and exploration are required to achieve effective interpretation. Existing approaches based on clustering or pattern search, while valuable for specific analysis tasks, often face challenges in supporting interactive and level-of-detail exploration of large-scale curve-based data, particularly when real-time parameter adjustment and iterative refinement are needed. To address this, we design and implement an interactive web-based system. Our system utilizes a Curve Segment Neighborhood Graph (CSNG) to encode the neighboring relationships between curve segments. CSNG enables us to adapt a fast community detection algorithm to identify coherent flow structures and spatial groupings in the streamlines interactively. CSNG also supports a multi-level exploration through an enhanced force-directed layout. Furthermore, our system integrates an adjacency matrix representation to reveal detailed inter-relations among segments. To achieve real-time performance within a web browser, our system employs matrix compression for memory-efficient CSNG storage and parallel processing. We have applied our system to analyze and interpret complex patterns in several streamline datasets. Our experiments show that we achieve real-time performance on datasets with hundreds of thousands of segments.

AI.tldr: A web-based interactive system for large-scale streamline analysis using Curve Segment Neighborhood Graph (CSNG) to detect coherent flow structures and support multi-level exploration with adjacency matrices and matrix compression, achieving real-time performance in the browser on datasets with hundreds of thousands of segments.
AI.motivation: Streamlines are widely used but require many curves to capture complex vector fields; lack of rigorous feature definitions makes interpretation challenging; interactive, scalable, and level-of-detail exploration is needed for large-scale data.
AI.method: Build CSNG to encode neighbor relations among curve segments; apply a fast community detection algorithm to identify coherent structures; enhance with a multi-level force-directed layout for exploration; include an adjacency matrix to reveal inter-segment relations; compress CSNG for memory efficiency and use parallel processing for real-time browser performance.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time performance on datasets with hundreds of thousands of segments; applied to multiple streamline datasets; system supports interactive exploration and interpretation of complex patterns.
AI.conclusion: CSNG-based, interactive web framework enables scalable, detailed analysis of complex streamline fields, combining community detection, enhanced layouts, and compressed representations to achieve real-time performance in the browser.

title: NuHF Claw: A Risk Constrained Cognitive Agent Framework for Human Centered Procedure Support in Digital Nuclear Control Rooms
summary: The rapid digitization of nuclear power plant main control rooms has fundamentally reshaped operator interaction patterns, introducing complex soft-control behaviors and elevated cognitive risks that are not adequately addressed by existing human reliability analysis approaches. Although recent advances in large language models and autonomous agents offer new opportunities for intelligent decision support, their deployment in safety critical environments remains constrained by risks of hallucinated reasoning and weakened human authority. This study proposes NuHF Claw, a persistent cognitive-risk agent framework that enables risk governed human centered autonomy for digital nuclear operations. The core methodological innovation lies in the introduction of a risk constrained agent runtime, which tightly couples cognitive state inference with probabilistic safety assessment to regulate autonomous system behavior in real time. By integrating cognitively grounded workload and situational awareness estimation with dynamic human error probability prediction, the framework transforms conventional offline reliability analysis into a proactive intervention mechanism embedded directly within operational workflows. Experimental validation on a high-fidelity digital control room simulator demonstrates that NuHF Claw can anticipate interface induced cognitive degradation, dynamically constrain unsafe autonomous recommendations, and provide risk-aware navigational guidance while preserving human decision authority. The results highlight a fundamental shift from automation-driven operation toward cognition-aware autonomy, offering a principled pathway for the safe integration of intelligent agents into next-generation nuclear control environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes NuHF Claw, a risk-constrained cognitive-agent runtime for digital nuclear operations that couples cognitive state inference with probabilistic safety assessment to guide autonomous behavior while preserving human authority.
AI.motivation: Digitized nuclear control rooms introduce complex cognitive risks and empowerment of AI assistants; current HRA methods and LLM safety concerns (hallucinations, authority erosion) are inadequate for real-time, safety-critical operation.
AI.method: Introduce a risk-constrained agent runtime that tightly couples cognitive state inference with probabilistic safety assessment; integrate cognitively grounded workload and situational awareness estimation with dynamic human error probability to make autonomous actions risk-aware in real time; embed this mechanism within operational workflows.
AI.result: In a high-fidelity digital control room simulator, NuHF Claw anticipates interface-induced cognitive degradation, dynamically constrains unsafe autonomous recommendations, and provides risk-aware navigational guidance while preserving human decision authority.
AI.conclusion: Shifts from automation-driven operation to cognition-aware autonomy and offers a principled pathway for safe integration of intelligent agents in next-generation nuclear control environments.

title: CooperDrive: Enhancing Driving Decisions Through Cooperative Perception
summary: Autonomous vehicles equipped with robust onboard perception, localization, and planning still face limitations in occlusion and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) scenarios, where delayed reactions can increase collision risk. We propose CooperDrive, a cooperative perception framework that augments situational awareness and enables earlier, safer driving decisions. CooperDrive offers two key advantages: (i) each vehicle retains its native perception, localization, and planning stack, and (ii) a lightweight object-level sharing and fusion strategy bridges perception and planning. Specifically, CooperDrive reuses detector Bird's-Eye View (BEV) features to estimate accurate vehicle poses without additional heavy encoders, thereby reconstructing BEV representations and feeding the planner with low latency. On the planning side, CooperDrive leverages the expanded object set to anticipate potential conflicts earlier and adjust speed and trajectory proactively, thereby transforming reactive behaviors into predictive and safer driving decisions. Real-world closed-loop tests at occlusion-heavy NLOS intersections demonstrate that CooperDrive increases reaction lead time, minimum time-to-collision (TTC), and stopping margin, while requiring only 90 kbps bandwidth and maintaining an average end-to-end latency of 89 ms.

AI.tldr: CooperDrive introduces cooperative perception with light-weight object-level sharing and BEV feature reuse to estimate vehicle poses and inform planning, achieving low-latency, low-bandwidth operation and improved safety at occluded/NLOS intersections.
AI.motivation: Occlusion and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) scenarios limit onboard perception and can cause late decisions; need to extend perception to include cooperative, low-latency information to boost safety.
AI.method: Two-part approach: (i) reuse detector Bird's-Eye View (BEV) features to estimate accurate vehicle poses without new heavy encoders, reconstruct BEV representations, and feed planner with low-latency data; (ii) expand the object set via lightweight object-level sharing and fusion to assist planning, while preserving native perception/planning stacks.
AI.result: Real-world closed-loop tests at occlusion-heavy NLOS intersections show increased reaction lead time, higher minimum time-to-collision (TTC), and larger stopping margins; operates at 90 kbps bandwidth with average end-to-end latency of 89 ms.
AI.conclusion: CooperDrive effectively bridges perception and planning with lightweight cooperation, enabling predictive and safer driving in occlusion-heavy scenarios; demonstrates practical viability with low bandwidth and low latency.

title: MixAtlas: Uncertainty-aware Data Mixture Optimization for Multimodal LLM Midtraining
summary: Domain reweighting can improve sample efficiency and downstream generalization, but data-mixture optimization for multimodal midtraining remains largely unexplored. Current multimodal training recipes tune mixtures along a single dimension, typically data format or task type. We introduce MixAtlas, a method that produces benchmark-targeted data recipes that can be inspected, adapted, and transferred to new corpora. MixAtlas decomposes the training corpus along two axes: image concepts (10 visual-domain clusters discovered via CLIP embeddings) and task supervision (5 objective types including captioning, OCR, grounding, detection, and VQA). Using small proxy models (Qwen2-0.5B) paired with a Gaussian-process surrogate and GP-UCB acquisition, MixAtlas searches the resulting mixture space with the same proxy budget as regression-based baselines but finds better-performing mixtures. We evaluate on 10 benchmarks spanning visual understanding, document reasoning, and multimodal reasoning. On Qwen2-7B, optimized mixtures improve average performance by 8.5%-17.6% over the strongest baseline; on Qwen2.5-7B, gains are 1.0%-3.3%. Both settings reach baseline-equivalent training loss in up to 2 times fewer steps. Recipes discovered on 0.5B proxies transfer to 7B-scale training across Qwen model families.

AI.tldr: MixAtlas optimizes multimodal midtraining data mixtures along two axes (image concepts and task supervision) using a Gaussian-process surrogate with GP-UCB search, producing benchmark-targeted, transferable data recipes that improve performance and reduce training steps across model scales.
AI.motivation: Data-mixture optimization for multimodal midtraining is underexplored; existing methods tune along a single dimension and lack transferability. Benchmark-targeted recipes could improve sample efficiency and generalization across corpora and model sizes.
AI.method: Decompose the training corpus into two axes: image concepts (10 clusters from CLIP embeddings) and task supervision types (5 objectives: captioning, OCR, grounding, detection, VQA). Use small proxy models (Qwen2-0.5B) with a Gaussian-process surrogate and GP-UCB to search the mixture space under a fixed proxy budget. Evaluate on 10 benchmarks spanning visual understanding, document reasoning, and multimodal reasoning; test transfer to larger models.
AI.result: Optimized mixtures on Qwen2-7B yield 8.5%–17.6% absolute improvements over the strongest baseline; on Qwen2.5-7B gains are 1.0%–3.3%. Training loss baseline-equivalent achieved in up to 2x fewer steps. Recipes discovered on 0.5B proxies transfer effectively to 7B-scale training across Qwen families.
AI.conclusion: MixAtlas provides benchmark-targeted, inspectable data recipes that transfer across model scales, offering a more efficient and effective approach to multimodal midtraining via a principled Gaussian-process guided search over a two-axis data mixture space.

title: HY-World 2.0: A Multi-Modal World Model for Reconstructing, Generating, and Simulating 3D Worlds
summary: We introduce HY-World 2.0, a multi-modal world model framework that advances our prior project HY-World 1.0. HY-World 2.0 accommodates diverse input modalities, including text prompts, single-view images, multi-view images, and videos, and produces 3D world representations. With text or single-view image inputs, the model performs world generation, synthesizing high-fidelity, navigable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes. This is achieved through a four-stage method: a) Panorama Generation with HY-Pano 2.0, b) Trajectory Planning with WorldNav, c) World Expansion with WorldStereo 2.0, and d) World Composition with WorldMirror 2.0. Specifically, we introduce key innovations to enhance panorama fidelity, enable 3D scene understanding and planning, and upgrade WorldStereo, our keyframe-based view generation model with consistent memory. We also upgrade WorldMirror, a feed-forward model for universal 3D prediction, by refining model architecture and learning strategy, enabling world reconstruction from multi-view images or videos. Also, we introduce WorldLens, a high-performance 3DGS rendering platform featuring a flexible engine-agnostic architecture, automatic IBL lighting, efficient collision detection, and training-rendering co-design, enabling interactive exploration of 3D worlds with character support. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HY-World 2.0 achieves state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks among open-source approaches, delivering results comparable to the closed-source model Marble. We release all model weights, code, and technical details to facilitate reproducibility and support further research on 3D world models.

AI.tldr: HY-World 2.0 is a multi-modal 3D world-model pipeline that converts text, single-/multi-view images, and videos into high-fidelity, navigable 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes via a four-stage system (HY-Pano 2.0, WorldNav, WorldStereo 2.0, WorldMirror 2.0) and a new WorldLens rendering platform. It achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source methods and rivals Marble, with full open releases.
AI.motivation: To build versatile, end-to-end 3D world models capable of fusing diverse input modalities to generate reliable, navigable 3D representations, addressing limitations of HY-World 1.0 and enabling interactive exploration, memory-consistent rendering, and cross-modal understanding.
AI.method: Four-stage pipeline: (a) Panorama Generation with HY-Pano 2.0, (b) Trajectory Planning with WorldNav, (c) World Expansion with WorldStereo 2.0, (d) World Composition with WorldMirror 2.0. Innovations include panorama fidelity enhancements, 3D scene understanding and planning, and upgrades to WorldStereo (keyframe-based with consistent memory) and WorldMirror (architecture/learning strategy refined for multi-view input). Introduction of WorldLens, an engine-agnostic 3D Gaussian Splatting rendering platform with automatic IBL lighting, efficient collision detection, and training-rendering co-design, enabling interactive 3D exploration with character support.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows HY-World 2.0 achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source approaches across multiple benchmarks and delivers performance comparable to the closed-source Marble model. The authors release all model weights, code, and technical details to support reproducibility and further research.
AI.conclusion: HY-World 2.0 significantly advances multi-modal 3D world modeling by integrating diverse inputs into a unified, high-fidelity 3D synthesis pipeline with interactive rendering, strong empirical performance, and open resources to foster reproducibility and ongoing research.

title: On the Doubling Dimension and the Perimeter of Geodesically Convex Sets in Fat Polygons
summary: Many algorithmic problems can be solved (almost) as efficiently in metric spaces of bounded doubling dimension as in Euclidean space. Unfortunately, the metric space defined by points in a simple polygon equipped with the geodesic distance does not necessarily have bounded doubling dimension. We therefore study the doubling dimension of fat polygons, for two well-known fatness definitions. We prove that locally-fat simple polygons do not always have bounded doubling dimension, while any $(α,β)$-covered polygon does have bounded doubling dimension (even if it has holes). We also study the perimeter of geodesically convex sets in $(α,β)$-covered polygons (possibly with holes), and show that this perimeter is at most a constant times the Euclidean diameter of the set. Using these two results, we obtain new results for several problems on $(α,β)$-covered polygons, including an algorithm that computes the closest pair of a set of $m$ points in an $(α,β)$-covered polygon with $n$ vertices that runs in $O(n + m\log{n})$ expected time.

AI.tldr: Shows a clear dichotomy for doubling dimension in polygonal geodesic spaces: locally-fat simple polygons can have unbounded doubling dimension, while (α,β)-covered polygons have bounded doubling dimension (even with holes). Also proves a constant-factor perimeter bound for geodesically convex sets in (α,β)-covered polygons and uses these results to yield an O(n + m log n) expected-time algorithm for closest-pair among m points in an (α,β)-covered polygon with n vertices.
AI.motivation: To understand when geodesic metric spaces arising from polygons have bounded doubling dimension, enabling efficient metric-geometry algorithms, and to extend such techniques to polygonal domains with holes via fatness notions.
AI.method: Geometric analysis of two fatness notions (locally-fat and (α,β)-covered). Prove unbounded doubling dimension for locally-fat polygons, and bounded doubling dimension for (α,β)-covered polygons even with holes. Derive a constant-factor bound on the perimeter of geodesically convex sets in (α,β)-covered polygons. Combine these results to design and analyze an algorithm for closest-pair that runs in O(n + m log n) expected time.
AI.result: 1) Locally-fat simple polygons may have unbounded doubling dimension. 2) Any (α,β)-covered polygon has bounded doubling dimension, even with holes. 3) The perimeter of a geodesically convex set in an (α,β)-covered polygon is at most a constant multiple of its Euclidean diameter. 4) An expected-time algorithm computes the closest pair among m points in an (α,β)-covered polygon with n vertices in O(n + m log n) time.
AI.conclusion: The work identifies a positive class ((α,β)-covered polygons) for which metric-geometry techniques using bounded doubling dimension apply, enabling efficient closest-pair computations in such polygons with holes, and highlights a limitation for locally-fat polygons. The perimeter bound further supports algorithmic efficiency for geodesic convexity-based structures in these domains.

title: Simulating Human Cognition: Heartbeat-Driven Autonomous Thinking Activity Scheduling for LLM-based AI systems
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning and tool use, yet they often suffer from rigid, reactive control flows that limit their adaptability and efficiency. Most existing frameworks rely on fixed pipelines or failure-triggered reflection, causing agents to act impulsively or correct errors only after they occur. In this paper, we introduce Heartbeat-Driven Autonomous Thinking Activity Scheduling, a mechanism that enables proactive, adaptive, and continuous self-regulation. Mirroring the natural rhythm of human cognition, our system employs a periodic ``heartbeat'' mechanism to orchestrate a dynamic repertoire of cognitive modules (e.g., Planner, Critic, Recaller, Dreamer). Unlike traditional approaches that rely on hard-coded symbolic rules or immediate reactive triggers, our scheduler learns to determine when to engage specific thinking activities -- such as recalling memories, summarizing experiences, or strategic planning -- based on temporal patterns and historical context. This functional approach allows cognitive modules to be dynamically added or removed without structural reengineering. Meanwhile, we propose a meta-learning strategy for continual policy adaptation, where the scheduler optimizes its cognitive strategy over time using historical interaction logs. Evaluation results demonstrate that our approach effectively learns to schedule cognitive activities based on historical data and can autonomously integrate new thinking modules.

AI.tldr: Proposes Heartbeat-Driven Autonomous Thinking Activity Scheduling to enable proactive, adaptive self-regulation in LLM agents by periodically coordinating cognitive modules through a learnable heartbeat, with dynamic module management and meta-learning for continual adaptation.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent architectures rely on fixed pipelines and reactive triggers, leading to impulsive behavior, late error correction, and limited adaptability. There is a need for proactive, periodic self-regulation that can adapt over time and integrate new cognitive skills without reengineering.
AI.method: Introduce a periodic heartbeat mechanism that orchestrates a dynamic set of cognitive modules (Planner, Critic, Recaller, Dreamer). The scheduler learns when to engage specific thinking activities based on temporal patterns and historical context, enabling dynamic addition/removal of modules without structural changes. A meta-learning strategy optimizes the cognitive policy over time using historical interaction logs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows the approach learns scheduling policies from historical data and supports autonomous integration of new thinking modules, improving proactive planning and guidance over reactive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Heartbeat-driven scheduling provides a flexible, scalable framework for proactive cognitive management in LLM agents, enabling continual policy improvement and modular augmentation without rewriting architectures.

title: A Nonasymptotic Theory of Gain-Dependent Error Dynamics in Behavior Cloning
summary: Behavior cloning (BC) policies on position-controlled robots inherit the closed-loop response of the underlying PD controller, yet the effect of controller gains on BC failure lacks a nonasymptotic theory. We show that independent sub-Gaussian action errors propagate through the gain-dependent closed-loop dynamics to yield sub-Gaussian position errors whose proxy matrix $X_\infty(K)$ governs the failure tail. The probability of horizon-$T$ task failure factorizes into a gain-dependent amplification index $Γ_T(K)$ and the validation loss plus a generalization slack, so training loss alone cannot predict closed-loop performance. Under shape-preserving upper-bound structural assumptions the proxy admits the scalar bound $X_\infty(K)\preceqΨ(K)\bar X$ with $Ψ(K)$ decomposed into label difficulty, injection strength, and contraction, ranking the four canonical regimes with compliant-overdamped (CO) tightest, stiff-underdamped (SU) loosest, and the stiff-overdamped versus compliant-underdamped ordering system-dependent. For the canonical scalar second-order PD system the closed-form continuous-time stationary variance $X_\infty^{\mathrm{c}}(α,β)=σ^2α/(2β)$ is strictly monotone in stiffness and damping over the entire stable orthant, covering both underdamped and overdamped regimes, and the exact zero-order-hold (ZOH) discretization inherits this monotonicity. The analysis provides the first nonasymptotic explanation of the empirical finding that compliant, overdamped controllers improve BC success rates.

AI.tldr: Nonasymptotic link between PD controller gains and BC failure; a gain-dependent proxy X_infty(K) governs tail risk; training loss alone fails to predict closed-loop performance; compliant overdamped controllers improve BC success.
AI.motivation: To explain nonasymptotic, tail-focused failure of behavior cloning on position-controlled robots and reconcile theory with the empirical benefit of compliant, overdamped controllers.
AI.method: Model BC policies as feedback-control systems with sub-Gaussian action noise. Derive a gain-dependent closed-loop proxy X_infty(K) that bounds position errors. Factor horizon-T failure into a gain amplification Γ_T(K) and validation loss plus slack. Under shape-preserving bounds, obtain X_infty(K) ≼ Ψ(K) X_bar with Ψ(K) decomposed into label difficulty, injection strength, and contraction. Rank four regimes (CO, SU, stiff-overdamped, compliant-underdamped) and derive canonical scalar PD results with closed-form stationary variance X_infty^c(α,β)=σ^2 α/(2β); monotone in stiffness α and damping β, for both continuous-time and ZOH discretization.
AI.result: First nonasymptotic explanation for why compliant, overdamped controllers improve BC success. Establishes a gain-dependent tail proxy X_infty(K) that dominates error; reveals a regime-based ordering of stability/short-term autonomy and shows monotone relation between stiffness/damping and stationary variance in the canonical PD case; trains loss is not a reliable predictor of closed-loop performance due to Γ_T(K) amplification.
AI.conclusion: The analysis provides actionable insight: in BC for position-controlled robots, design ought to bias toward compliant, overdamped controllers to tighten tail performance, and accommodate gain-aware evaluation beyond training loss; the nonasymptotic framework clarifies tail risk and regime-dependent behavior.

title: Portfolio Optimization Proxies under Label Scarcity and Regime Shifts via Bayesian and Deterministic Students under Semi-Supervised Sandwich Training
comment: 18 pages of main text. 10 pages of appendices. 35 references. Around 13 figures
summary: This paper proposes a machine learning assisted portfolio optimization framework designed for low data environments and regime uncertainty. We construct a teacher student learning pipeline in which a Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) optimizer generates supervisory labels, and neural models (Bayesian and deterministic) are trained using both real and synthetically augmented data. The synthetic data is generated using a factor based model with t copula residuals, enabling training beyond the limited real sample of 104 labeled observations. We evaluate four student models under a structured experimental framework comprising (i) controlled synthetic experiments (3 x 5 seed grid), (ii) in-distribution real market evaluation (C2A) and (iii) cross-universe generalization (D2A). In real-market settings, models are deployed using a rolling evaluation protocol where a frozen pretrained model is periodically fine tuned on recent observations and reset to its base state, ensuring stability while allowing limited adaptation. Results show that student models can match or outperform the CVaR teacher in several settings, while achieving improved robustness under regime shifts and reduced turnover. These findings suggest that hybrid optimization learning approaches can enhance portfolio construction in data constrained environments

AI.tldr: A teacher-student framework for portfolio optimization in low-data regimes where a CVaR optimizer acts as teacher and neural networks (Bayesian and deterministic) are trained on real and synthetic data; synthetic data is generated with a factor-based model and t copula residuals to supplement limited samples; evaluated across synthetic, in-distribution, and cross-universe settings with rolling evaluation; students often match or beat the CVaR teacher and show improved robustness and lower turnover.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and regime uncertainty in portfolio optimization by transferring supervisory signal from a risk-based optimizer to adaptable learner models, enabling robust performance in limited-sample, non-stationary environments.
AI.method: A teacher-student learning pipeline: CVaR optimizer provides supervisory labels; student models (Bayesian and deterministic neural nets) are trained on real observations augmented with synthetic data generated from a factor-based model with t copula residuals; evaluation uses controlled synthetic experiments (3x5 seeds), in-distribution market data (C2A), and cross-universe generalization (D2A); rolling evaluation with periodically frozen base model and limited fine-tuning.
AI.result: Student models can match or surpass the CVaR teacher in several settings, with enhanced robustness to regime shifts and reduced turnover, indicating the effectiveness of hybrid optimization-learning approaches under data constraints.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid optimization-learning frameworks can improve portfolio construction when data are scarce and regimes shift, by leveraging supervisory risk-based guidance and synthetic data to train transferable, robust models.

title: Geometrically Consistent Multi-View Scene Generation from Freehand Sketches
summary:
We tackle a new problem: generating geometrically consistent multi-view scenes from a single freehand sketch. Freehand sketches are the most geometrically impoverished input one could offer a multi-view generator. They convey scene intent through abstract strokes while introducing spatial distortions that actively conflict with any consistent 3D interpretation. No prior method attempts this; existing multi-view approaches require photographs or text, while sketch-to-3D methods need multiple views or costly per-scene optimisation.
  We address three compounding challenges; absent training data, the need for geometric reasoning from distorted 2D input, and cross-view consistency, through three mutually reinforcing contributions: (i) a curated dataset of $\sim$9k sketch-to-multiview samples, constructed via an automated generation and filtering pipeline; (ii) Parallel Camera-Aware Attention Adapters (CA3) that inject geometric inductive biases into the video transformer; and (iii) a Sparse Correspondence Supervision Loss (CSL) derived from Structure-from-Motion reconstructions.
  Our framework synthesizes all views in a single denoising process without requiring reference images, iterative refinement, or per-scene optimization. Our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art two-stage baselines, improving realism (FID) by over 60% and geometric consistency (Corr-Acc) by 23%, while providing up to a 3.7$\times$ inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Single-pass sketch-to-multi-view synthesis with geometry-aware transformers, using a 9k-sample dataset, CA3 adapters, and CSL loss to ensure cross-view consistency without per-scene optimization; achieves higher realism and geometry and faster inference.
AI.motivation: Overcome lack of training data, distorted 2D input, and cross-view consistency in sketch-to-multiview generation; existing methods rely on photos/text or require multi-view data and expensive per-scene optimization.
AI.method: Three contributions: (1) ~9k sketch-to-multiview dataset via automatic generation/ filtering; (2) Parallel Camera-Aware Attention Adapters (CA3) inserted into video transformer to inject geometry inductive biases; (3) Sparse Correspondence Supervision Loss (CSL) derived from Structure-from-Motion reconstructions; single denoising process to synthesize all views without reference images or per-scene optimization.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art two-stage baselines; realism (FID) improved by >60%; geometric consistency (Corr-Acc) improved by 23%; up to 3.7x faster inference.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective, efficient, geometry-aware sketch-to-multiview generation without per-scene optimization and with strong cross-view consistency, enabling practical applications.

title: Online Algorithms for Geometric Independent Set
summary:
In the classical online model, the maximum independent set problem admits an $Ω(n)$ lower bound on the competitive ratio even for interval graphs, motivating the study of the problem under additional assumptions. We first study the problem on graphs with a bounded independent kissing number $ζ$, defined as the size of the largest induced star in the graph minus one. We show that a simple greedy algorithm, requiring no geometric representation, achieves a competitive ratio of $ζ$. Moreover, this bound is optimal for deterministic online algorithms and asymptotically optimal for randomized ones. This extends previous results from specific geometric graph families to more general graph classes.
  Since this bound rules out further improvements through randomization alone, we investigate the power of randomization with access to geometric representation. When the geometric representation of the objects is known, we present randomized online algorithms with improved guarantees. For unit ball graphs in $\mathbb{R}^3$, we present an algorithm whose expected competitive ratio is strictly smaller than the deterministic lower bound implied by the independent kissing number. For $α$-fat objects and for axis-aligned hyper-rectangles in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with bounded diameters, we obtain algorithms with expected competitive ratios that depend polylogarithmically on the ratio between the maximum and minimum object diameters. In both cases, the randomized lower bound implied by the independent kissing number grows polynomially with the ratio between the maximum and minimum object diameters, implying substantial performance guarantees for our algorithms.

AI.tldr: Presents two-stage online MIS results: (1) with a bounded independent kissing number ζ, a simple greedy is ζ-competitive; deterministic optimal, randomized asymptotically optimal; (2) with geometric information, randomized algorithms beat deterministic lower bounds: for unit-ball graphs in R^3, α-fat objects, and axis-aligned rectangles with bounded diameter ratio, achieving expected competitive ratios that depend polylogarithmically on the diameter ratio, with lower bounds growing polynomially in that ratio.
AI.motivation: Overcome the classic Ω(n) competitive bound for online maximum independent set by leveraging structural constraints—bounded induced star (independent kissing number) and, when available, geometric representations—to obtain nontrivial competitive guarantees.
AI.method: 1) Graphs with bounded independent kissing number ζ: analyze a simple, geometry-free greedy algorithm; prove its competitive ratio equals ζ; establish optimality for deterministic online algorithms and asymptotic optimality for randomized ones. 2) Incorporate geometric representation: develop randomized online algorithms using geometry; derive improved guarantees for unit ball graphs in R^3, α-fat objects, and axis-aligned hyper-rectangles in Rd with bounded diameters; show the expected competitive ratios depend polylogarithmically on the ratio between max and min object diameters; contrast with a lower bound that scales polynomially with this diameter ratio.
AI.result: Deterministic MIS on graphs with bounded independent kissing number ζ admits a ζ-competitive greedy algorithm; this bound is optimal for deterministic online algorithms and asymptotically optimal for randomized ones. When geometry is available, randomized online algorithms achieve better expected competitive ratios than the deterministic ζ-bound: for unit ball graphs in R^3, the ratio is strictly smaller than the deterministic bound implied by ζ; for α-fat objects and axis-aligned rectangles in Rd with bounded diameters, ratios depend polylogarithmically on the diameter ratio; the diameter-ratio lower bound for independent kissing numbers grows polynomially, ensuring meaningful improvements.
AI.conclusion: Randomization paired with geometric information can meaningfully surpass purely combinatorial online bounds governed by the independent kissing number. The results generalize beyond specific geometric families, highlighting a substantial performance gap closed by geometry-and-randomization and revealing diameter-ratio-driven behavior in achievable competitiveness.

title: Fun-TSG: A Function-Driven Multivariate Time Series Generator with Variable-Level Anomaly Labeling
summary: Reliable evaluation of anomaly detection methods in multivariate time series remains an open challenge, largely due to the limitations of existing benchmark datasets. Current resources often lack fine-grained anomaly annotations, do not provide explicit intervariable and temporal dependencies, and offer little insight into the underlying generative mechanisms. These shortcomings hinder the development and rigorous comparison of detection models, especially those targeting interpretable and variable-specific outputs. To address this gap, we introduce Fun-TSG, a fully customizable time series generator designed to support high-quality evaluation of anomaly detection systems. Our tool enables both fully automated generation, based on randomly sampled dependency structures and anomaly types, and manual generation through user-defined equations and anomaly configurations. In both cases, it provides full transparency over the data generation process, including access to ground-truth anomaly labels at the variable and timestamp levels. Fun-TSG supports the creation of diverse, interpretable, and reproducible benchmarking scenarios, enabling fine-grained performance analysis for both classical and modern anomaly detection models.

AI.tldr: A customizable time-series generator, Fun-TSG, for robust, interpretable, and reproducible anomaly-detection benchmarking with ground-truth labels at variable and timestamp levels.
AI.motivation: Current benchmark datasets for multivariate time-series anomaly detection lack fine-grained annotations, explicit inter-variable/temporal dependencies, and transparent generative mechanisms, hindering fair evaluation and interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce Fun-TSG, a fully customizable generator supporting automated generation via random dependency structures and anomaly types, and manual generation through user-defined equations and anomaly configurations; provides full transparency of data generation and ground-truth labels; supports diverse, interpretable benchmarking scenarios for both classical and modern detectors.
AI.result: Framework proposal with design details; enables high-quality evaluation and fine-grained analysis, but no empirical results reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Fun-TSG addresses core dataset limitations, enabling reproducible, interpretable benchmarking and rigorous comparison of anomaly detection models.

title: CT-VIR: Continuous-Time Visual-Inertial-Ranging Fusion for Indoor Localization with Sparse Anchors
summary: Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) is widely used for mobile robot localization, but its long-term accuracy degrades without global constraints. Incorporating ranging sensors such as ultra-wideband (UWB) can mitigate drift; however, high-accuracy ranging usually requires well-deployed anchors, which is difficult to ensure in narrow or low-power environments. Moreover, most existing visual-inertial-ranging (VIR) fusion methods rely on discrete time-based filtering or optimization, making it difficult to balance positioning accuracy, trajectory consistency, and fusion efficiency under asynchronous multi-sensor sampling. To address these issues, we propose a spline-based continuous-time state estimation method for VIR fusion localization. In the preprocessing stage, VIO motion priors and UWB ranging measurements are used to construct virtual anchors and reject outliers, thereby alleviating geometric degeneration and improving range reliability. In the estimation stage, the pose trajectory is parameterized in continuous time using a B-spline, while inertial, visual, and ranging constraints are formulated as factors in a sliding-window graph. The spline control points, together with a small set of auxiliary parameters, are then jointly optimized to obtain a continuous-time trajectory estimate. Evaluations on public datasets and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and practical potential of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: A spline-based continuous-time VIR fusion framework that uses VIO priors to build virtual anchors from UWB ranging, with a B-spline trajectory and sliding-window optimization of inertial, visual, and ranging factors; demonstrates improved accuracy and robustness in asynchronous sensing on public datasets and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Long-term VIO drift due to lack of global constraints; UWB ranging can help but requires dense anchor deployment, which is impractical in constrained environments; asynchronous multi-sensor sampling complicates fusion with discrete-time methods.
AI.method: Preprocessing constructs virtual anchors from VIO motion priors and UWB measurements with outlier rejection to improve geometry and range reliability. Trajectory is represented as a continuous-time B-spline; inertial, visual, and ranging constraints are encoded as factors in a sliding-window graph. Jointly optimize spline control points and a small number of auxiliary parameters to obtain a continuous-time trajectory.
AI.result: Evaluation on public datasets and real-world experiments shows the approach is effective and practically viable, improving localization accuracy and robustness under asynchronous sensing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed continuous-time VIR fusion with virtual anchors offers a practical solution to mitigate drift, handle asynchrony, and maintain trajectory consistency without heavy reliance on dense anchor deployment.

title: Towards Verified and Targeted Explanations through Formal Methods
comment: Paper has been accepted at JAIR
summary: As deep neural networks are deployed in safety-critical domains such as autonomous driving and medical diagnosis, stakeholders need explanations that are interpretable but also trustworthy with formal guarantees. Existing XAI methods fall short: heuristic attribution techniques (e.g., LIME, Integrated Gradients) highlight influential features but offer no mathematical guarantees about decision boundaries, while formal methods verify robustness yet remain untargeted, analyzing the nearest boundary regardless of whether it represents a critical risk. In safety-critical systems, not all misclassifications carry equal consequences; confusing a "Stop" sign for a "60 kph" sign is far more dangerous than confusing it with a "No Passing" sign. We introduce ViTaX (Verified and Targeted Explanations), a formal XAI framework that generates targeted semifactual explanations with mathematical guarantees. For a given input (class y) and a user-specified critical alternative (class t), ViTaX: (1) identifies the minimal feature subset most sensitive to the y->t transition, and (2) applies formal reachability analysis to guarantee that perturbing these features by epsilon cannot flip the classification to t. We formalize this through Targeted epsilon-Robustness, certifying whether a feature subset remains robust under perturbation toward a specific target class. ViTaX is the first method to provide formally guaranteed explanations of a model's resilience against user-identified alternatives. Evaluations on MNIST, GTSRB, EMNIST, and TaxiNet demonstrate over 30% fidelity improvement with minimal explanation cardinality.

AI.tldr: ViTaX is a formal XAI framework that provides targeted semifactual explanations with mathematical guarantees. It identifies the minimal feature subset affecting a user-specified risky misclassification (y→t) and certifies robustness against perturbations toward t using Targeted epsilon-Robustness.
AI.motivation: In safety-critical domains, explanations must be both interpretable and *guaranteed*. Heuristic attribution methods lack formal decision-boundary guarantees, while untargeted robustness analyses miss risk-sensitive, user-defined misclassifications. A targeted, formally verified explanation addresses critical risk scenarios.
AI.method: ViTaX first identifies the smallest subset of features most sensitive to the y→t transition. It then applies formal reachability analysis to prove that perturbing these features by epsilon cannot change the prediction to the targeted class t, formalized as Targeted epsilon-Robustness. This yields explanations that are interpretable and have a formal resilience guarantee against a user-specified risk.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on MNIST, GTSRB, EMNIST, and TaxiNet shows over 30% fidelity improvement with minimal explanation cardinality, indicating more concise explanations that preserve the model's resilience properties.
AI.conclusion: ViTaX is the first approach to deliver formally guaranteed, targeted explanations of a model’s resilience to user-identified misclassifications, bridging interpretability and formal guarantees for safety-critical applications.

title: Orthogonal Strip Partitioning of Polygons: Lattice-Theoretic Algorithms and Lower Bounds
summary:
We study a variant of a polygon partition problem, introduced by Chung, Iwama, Liao, and Ahn [ISAAC'25]. Given orthogonal unit vectors $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\in \mathbb{R}^2$ and a polygon $P$ with $n$ vertices, we partition $P$ into connected pieces by cuts parallel to $\mathbf{v}$ such that each resulting subpolygon has width at most one in direction $\mathbf{u}$. We consider the value version, which asks for the minimum number of strips, and the reporting version, which outputs a compact encoding of the cuts in an optimal strip partition.
  We give efficient algorithms and lower bounds for both versions on three classes of polygons of increasing generality: convex, simple, and self-overlapping. For convex polygons, we solve the value version in $O(\log n)$ time and the reporting version in $O\!\left(h \log\left(1 + \frac{n}{h}\right)\right)$ time, where $h$ is the width of $P$ in direction $\mathbf{u}$. We prove matching lower bounds in the decision-tree model, showing that the reporting algorithm is input-sensitive optimal with respect to $h$. For simple polygons, we present $O(n \log n)$-time, $O(n)$-space algorithms for both versions and prove an $Ω(n)$ lower bound. For self-overlapping polygons, we extend the approach for simple polygons to obtain $O(n \log n)$-time, $O(n)$-space algorithms for both versions, and we prove a matching $Ω(n \log n)$ lower bound in the algebraic computation-tree model via a reduction from the $δ$-closeness problem.
  Our approach relies on a lattice-theoretic formulation of the problem. We represent strip partitions as antichains of intervals in the Clarke--Cormack--Burkowski lattice, originally developed for minimal-interval semantics in information retrieval. Within this lattice framework, we design a dynamic programming algorithm that uses the lattice operations of meet and join.

AI.tldr: Efficient algorithms and matching lower bounds for partitioning polygons into width-1 strips; three polygon classes; lattice-based DP.
AI.motivation: Understand optimal strip partitions under width constraints with cuts parallel to a given direction; provide both minimal-strip counts and compact encodings; prove tight bounds.
AI.method: Lattice-theoretic formulation using Clarke–Cormack–Burkowski lattice; represent partitions as antichains of intervals; dynamic programming with meet and join; analyze convex, simple, and self-overlapping polygons; derive time/space bounds and lower bounds; employ reductions for delta-closeness in lower bounds.
AI.result: Convex: value in O(log n); reporting in O(h log(1+n/h)); simple: O(n log n) time/space with Ω(n) lower bound; self-overlapping: O(n log n) time/space with Ω(n log n) lower bound; lower bounds match in decision-tree and algebraic computation-tree models; input-sensitive optimality for convex reporting.
AI.conclusion: The lattice framework yields tight, efficient algorithms for width-constrained polygon partitioning across convex, simple, and self-overlapping classes; validates the use of Clarke–Cormack–Burkowski lattice and DP via meet/join and suggests applicability to related partitioning problems and extensions to higher dimensions.

title: Interpretable and Explainable Surrogate Modeling for Simulations: A State-of-the-Art Survey and Perspectives on Explainable AI for Decision-Making
comment: Accepted for publication in Archives of Computational Methods in Engineering, 2026, ID d9d36aab-3723-4a70-b2ce-166435179528
summary: The simulation of complex systems increasingly relies on sophisticated but fundamentally opaque computational black-box simulators. Surrogate models play a central role in reducing the computational cost of complex systems simulations across a wide range of scientific and engineering domains. Notwithstanding, they inevitably inherit and often exacerbate this black-box nature, obscuring how input variables drive physical responses. Conversely, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) offers powerful tools to unpack these models. Yet, XAI methods struggle with engineering-specific constraints, such as highly correlated inputs, dynamical systems, and rigorous reliability requirements. Consequently, surrogate modeling and XAI have largely evolved as distinct fields of research, despite their strong complementarity. To reconnect these approaches, this state-of-the-art survey provides a structured perspective that maps existing XAI techniques onto the various stages of surrogate modeling workflows for design and exploration. To ground this synthesis, we draw upon illustrative applications across both equation-based simulations and agent-based modeling. We survey a broad spectrum of techniques, highlighting their strengths for revealing interactions and supporting human comprehension. Finally, we identify pressing open challenges, including the explainability of dynamical systems and the handling of mixed-variable systems, and propose a research agenda to make explainability a core, embedded element of simulation-driven workflows from model construction through decision-making. By transforming opaque emulators into explainable tools, this agenda empowers practitioners to move beyond accelerating simulations to extracting actionable insights from complex system behaviors.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey aligning XAI methods with surrogate modeling workflows to improve explainability in simulation surrogates for complex systems, outlining challenges and a research agenda.
AI.motivation: Surrogates reduce computational cost but are opaque; XAI can reveal input–output relationships, yet engineering constraints (e.g., correlated inputs, dynamics, reliability) hinder current explainability; bridging XAI and surrogate modelling is needed.
AI.method: Structured literature survey mapping XAI techniques to stages of surrogate modeling workflows; includes illustrative applications in equation-based and agent-based models; evaluates strengths for revealing interactions and aiding human comprehension.
AI.result: Provides a taxonomy/mapping of XAI techniques to surrogate-modeling steps, identifies methods that best reveal interactions, and highlights open challenges and a defined research agenda for embedding explainability in simulation-driven workflows.
AI.conclusion: Explainability should be embedded throughout simulation workflows—from model construction to decision-making—transforming opaque emulators into explainable tools that yield actionable insights.

title: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning Exploits Passive Body Dynamics for High-Performance Biped Robot Locomotion
summary: Embodiment is a significant keyword in recent machine learning fields. This study focused on the passive nature of the body of a biped robot to generate walking and running locomotion using model-based deep reinforcement learning. We constructed two models in a simulator, one with passive elements (e.g., springs) and the other, which is similar to general humanoids, without passive elements. The training of the model with passive elements was highly affected by the attractor of the system. This lead that although the trajectories quickly converged to limit cycles, it took a long time to obtain large rewards. However, thanks to the attractor-driven learning, the acquired locomotion was robust and energy-efficient. The results revealed that robots with passive elements could efficiently acquire high-performance locomotion by utilizing stable limit cycles generated through dynamic interaction between the body and ground. This study demonstrates the importance of implementing passive properties in the body for future embodied AI.

AI.tldr: Passive-body elements enable robust, energy-efficient locomotion via attractor-driven limit cycles in model-based DRL, outperforming non-passive humanoid-like bodies in simulation.
AI.motivation: Investigate how passive body properties (springs, compliant elements) influence learning dynamics, robustness, and energy efficiency of locomotion in model-based deep reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Two simulated bipeds: one with passive elements (springs, compliance) and one without; train with model-based DRL; analyze convergence to limit cycles, reward learning dynamics, energy efficiency, and robustness; compare locomotion performance.
AI.result: The passive-element model’s learning is strongly influenced by the system’s attractor, with rapid convergence to limit cycles but slower achievement of high rewards; overall, locomotion is robust and energy-efficient due to stable limit cycles arising from body-ground interactions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the value of incorporating passive body properties for embodied AI, suggesting future robot design and learning strategies should consider passive dynamics to achieve robust, efficient locomotion.

title: Shapley Value-Guided Adaptive Ensemble Learning for Explainable Financial Fraud Detection with U.S. Regulatory Compliance Validation
comment: 28 pages. Submitted to Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence (Elsevier). IEEE-CIS dataset (590,540 transactions). Includes SGAE algorithm, SHAP stability evaluation, and OCC/SR 11-7 regulatory compliance mapping
summary: Financial crime costs U.S. institutions over $32 billion each year. Although AI tools for fraud detection have become more advanced, their use in real-world systems still faces a major obstacle: many of these models operate as black boxes that cannot provide the transparent, auditable explanations required by regulations such as OCC Bulletin 2011-12 and Federal Reserve SR 11-7. This study makes three main contributions. First, it offers a thorough evaluation of explanation quality across faithfulness (sufficiency and comprehensiveness at k=5, 10, and 15) and stability (Kendall's W across 30 bootstrap samples). XGBoost paired with TreeExplainer achieves near-perfect stability (W=0.9912), while LSTM with DeepExplainer shows weak results (W=0.4962). Second, the paper introduces the SHAP-Guided Adaptive Ensemble (SGAE), which dynamically adjusts per-transaction ensemble weights based on SHAP attribution agreement, achieving the highest AUC-ROC among all tested models (0.8837 held-out; 0.9245 cross-validation). Third, a complete three-architecture evaluation of LSTM, Transformer, and GNN-GraphSAGE on the full 590,540-transaction IEEE-CIS dataset is provided, with GNN-GraphSAGE achieving AUC-ROC 0.9248 and F1=0.6013. All results are mapped directly to OCC, SR 11-7, and BSA-AML regulatory compliance requirements.

AI.tldr: Evaluation of explanation quality and a SHAP-guided adaptive ensemble for fraud detection; XGBoost+TreeExplainer shows the strongest stability among explanations; SGAE yields top AUC; GraphSAGE-based GNN outperforms others on IEEE-CIS; results framed for OCC SR 11-7/BSA-AML compliance.
AI.motivation: Regulators require transparent, auditable fraud-detection models; many effective models are black boxes, hindering compliance.
AI.method: Assess explanation quality via faithfulness (sufficiency and comprehensiveness at k=5,10,15) and stability (Kendall's W across 30 bootstrap samples). Introduce SHAP-Guided Adaptive Ensemble (SGAE) that dynamically adjusts per-transaction ensemble weights based on SHAP attribution agreement. Conduct a full three-architecture evaluation (LSTM, Transformer, GraphSAGE) on the IEEE-CIS 590,540-transaction dataset; report AUC-ROC and F1; map outcomes to OCC SR 11-7 and BSA-AML regulatory requirements.
AI.result: XGBoost+TreeExplainer achieves near-perfect stability (W=0.9912); LSTM+DeepExplainer shows weaker stability (W=0.4962). SGAE attains the highest AUC-ROC (0.8837 on hold-out, 0.9245 in cross-validation). Full-architecture evaluation: LSTM, Transformer, GraphSAGE; GraphSAGE (GNN) yields AUC-ROC 0.9248 and F1=0.6013.
AI.conclusion: SHAP-based explanation quality assessment combined with an adaptive ensemble supports transparent, regulatory-friendly fraud detection. SGAE delivers strong predictive performance while leveraging SHAP attribution for per-transaction weighting; GraphSAGE demonstrates state-of-the-art performance among the three architectures on the IEEE-CIS dataset, supporting deployment within OCC SR 11-7/BSA-AML compliant pipelines.

title: Interpretable Human Activity Recognition for Subtle Robbery Detection in Surveillance Videos
summary: Non-violent street robberies (snatch-and-run) are difficult to detect automatically because they are brief, subtle, and often indistinguishable from benign human interactions in unconstrained surveillance footage. This paper presents a hybrid, pose-driven approach for detecting snatch-and-run events that combines real-time perception with an interpretable classification stage suitable for edge deployment. The system uses a YOLO-based pose estimator to extract body keypoints for each tracked person and computes kinematic and interaction features describing hand speed, arm extension, proximity, and relative motion between an aggressor-victim pair. A Random Forest classifier is trained on these descriptors, and a temporal hysteresis filter is applied to stabilize frame-level predictions and reduce spurious alarms. We evaluate the method on a staged dataset and on a disjoint test set collected from internet videos, demonstrating promising generalization across different scenes and camera viewpoints. Finally, we implement the complete pipeline on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano and report real-time performance, supporting the feasibility of proactive, on-device robbery detection.

AI.tldr: Hybrid pose-driven pipeline detects snatch-and-run robberies using YOLO-based human pose estimation, hand/arm kinematics, and inter-person features fed to a Random Forest with temporal hysteresis for on-device, edge deployment; validated on staged and internet-sourced video with real-time Jetson Nano performance.
AI.motivation: Non-violent street robberies (snatch-and-run) are brief, subtle, and often indistinguishable from normal interactions in unconstrained surveillance. There is a need for automatic, edge-friendly, interpretable detection to enable proactive security.
AI.method: Extract body keypoints per tracked person with a YOLO-based pose estimator; compute kinematic and interaction features (hand speed, arm extension, proximity, relative motion between aggressor and victim); train a Random Forest classifier on these descriptors; apply a temporal hysteresis filter to stabilize predictions; implement end-to-end pipeline on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano for real-time, on-device operation.
AI.result: Evaluation on a staged dataset and a disjoint test set from internet videos showed promising generalization across scenes and camera viewpoints. Demonstrated real-time performance on Jetson Nano, supporting feasibility of on-device proactive robbery detection.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields an interpretable, edge-deployable solution for snatch-and-run detection with robust cross-scene generalization. While effective in current datasets, broader validation and potential refinements (e.g., more diverse data, ablation studies) could strengthen the robustness and practical deployment.

title: Formalizing Kantian Ethics: Formula of the Universal Law Logic (FULL)
summary: The field of machine ethics aims to build Artificial Moral Agents (AMAs) to better understand morality and make AI agents safer. To do so, many approaches encode human moral intuition as a set of axioms on actions e.g., do not harm, you must help others. However, this introduces (at least) two limitations for future AMAs. First, it does not consider the agent's purposes in performing the action. Second, it assumes that we humans can enumerate our moral intuition. This paper explores formalizing a moral procedure that alleviates these two limitations.
