We specifically consider Kantian ethics and present a multi-sorted quantified modal logic we call the Formula of the Universal Law Logic (FULL). The FULL formalizes Kant's first formulation of the categorical imperative, the Formula of the Universal Law (FUL), and concepts such as causality and agency. We demonstrate on three cases from Kantian ethics that the FULL can reason to evaluate agents' actions for certain purposes without built-in moral intuition, given that it has sufficient (non-normative) background knowledge. Therefore, the FULL is a contribution towards more robust and autonomous AMAs, and a more formal understanding of Kantian ethics.

AI.tldr: A formal Kantian framework (FULL) uses multi-sorted quantified modal logic to implement Kant’s Formula of the Universal Law, enabling AMAs to assess actions for purposes beyond built-in moral intuitions by leveraging background knowledge; demonstrated on three Kantian scenarios.
AI.motivation: Address two limitations of axiomatic moral instruction: (1) disregard for agent purposes, and (2) reliance on enumerating human moral intuitions. The work seeks a formalist, autonomous method to evaluate actions under Kantian ethics without hard-coding intuitions.
AI.method: Introduce a multi-sorted quantified modal logic named FULL that encodes Kant’s FUL and related concepts like causality and agency. Apply FULL to three Kantian cases to show that, with sufficient non-normative background knowledge, it can evaluate actions for specified purposes without built-in moral rules.
AI.result: Demonstrates that FULL can reason about actions under Kantian ethics using background knowledge to determine permissibility for given purposes, suggesting a path toward more robust, autonomous AMAs and a formal understanding of Kantian ethics.
AI.conclusion: FULL contributes a formal tool to model Kantian ethics in AMAs, addressing limitations of intuition-based axioms by incorporating agent purposes and background knowledge; further work needed to expand cases, address computational complexity, and test across broader ethical theories.

title: Explainable Graph Neural Networks for Interbank Contagion Surveillance: A Regulatory-Aligned Framework for the U.S. Banking Sector
comment: 28 pages, submitted to Research in International Business and Finance (RIBAF)
summary: The Spatial-Temporal Graph Attention Network (ST-GAT) framework was created to serve as an explainable GNN-based solution for detecting bank distress early warning signs and for conducting macro-prudential surveillance of the interbank system in the United States. The ST-GAT framework models 8,103 FDIC insured institutions across 58 quarterly snapshots (2010Q1-2024Q2). Bilateral exposures were reconstructed from publicly available FDIC Call Reports using maximum entropy estimation to produce a dynamic directed weighted graph. The framework achieves the highest AUPRC among all GNN architectures (0.939 +/- 0.010), trailing only XGBoost (0.944). Ablation analysis confirms the BiLSTM temporal component contributes +0.020 AUPRC; temporal attention weights exhibit a monotonically decreasing pattern consistent with long-run structural vulnerability weighting. Permutation importance identifies ROA (0.309) and NPL Ratio (0.252) as dominant predictors, consistent with post-mortem analyses of the 2023 regional banking crisis. All data are publicly available FDIC Call Reports and FRED series; all code and results are released.

AI.tldr: ST-GAT is an explainable spatio-temporal GNN framework for early bank distress detection and macro-prudential surveillance, using dynamic directed graphs from FDIC data; achieves near state-of-the-art AUPRC among GNNs and releases data/code.
AI.motivation: Need for timely, interpretable detection of distress in the interbank network and for macroprudential monitoring; existing models lack explainability or fully capture dynamic bilateral exposures.
AI.method: Construct dynamic bilateral exposure graphs from FDIC Call Reports via maximum entropy; apply Spatial-Temporal Graph Attention Network with BiLSTM temporal component and temporal attention; perform ablation and permutation importance analyses; evaluate on 58 quarterly snapshots (2010Q1-2024Q2) across 8,103 institutions.
AI.result: AUPRC 0.939 ± 0.010, slightly below XGBoost 0.944; BiLSTM contributes +0.020 AUPRC; temporal attention weights decrease over time; ROA (0.309) and NPL Ratio (0.252) are top predictors; data and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: ST-GAT provides an effective, explainable framework for early warning and macro-prudential surveillance with interpretable temporal and feature importance signals; results align with post-mortem analyses of the 2023 regional banking crisis; open-data/code facilitates replication.

title: SatBLIP: Context Understanding and Feature Identification from Satellite Imagery with Vision-Language Learning
summary: Rural environmental risks are shaped by place-based conditions (e.g., housing quality, road access, land-surface patterns), yet standard vulnerability indices are coarse and provide limited insight into risk contexts. We propose SatBLIP, a satellite-specific vision-language framework for rural context understanding and feature identification that predicts county-level Social Vulnerability Index (SVI). SatBLIP addresses limitations of prior remote sensing pipelines-handcrafted features, manual virtual audits, and natural-image-trained VLMs-by coupling contrastive image-text alignment with bootstrapped captioning tailored to satellite semantics. We use GPT-4o to generate structured descriptions of satellite tiles (roof type/condition, house size, yard attributes, greenery, and road context), then fine-tune a satellite-adapted BLIP model to generate captions for unseen images. Captions are encoded with CLIP and fused with LLM-derived embeddings via attention for SVI estimation under spatial aggregation. Using SHAP, we identify salient attributes (e.g., roof form/condition, street width, vegetation, cars/open space) that consistently drive robust predictions, enabling interpretable mapping of rural risk environments.

AI.tldr: SatBLIP is a satellite-specific vision-language framework that maps rural context to county-level Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) by combining GPT-4o-generated structured satellite descriptions with a satellite-adapted BLIP, CLIP embeddings, and attention-based fusion, with SHAP-based interpretability.
AI.motivation: To overcome coarse, context-insensitive vulnerability indices and the limitations of handcrafted features, manual audits, and natural-image-trained VLMs in remote-sensing pipelines for rural risk assessment.
AI.method: 1) Use GPT-4o to produce structured tile descriptions (roof type/condition, house size, yard attributes, greenery, road context). 2) Fine-tune a satellite-adapted BLIP model to generate captions for unseen satellite images. 3) Encode captions with CLIP and fuse with LLM-derived embeddings via attention for SVI estimation with spatial aggregation. 4) Apply SHAP to identify salient features driving predictions (e.g., roof form/condition, street width, vegetation, cars/open space) for interpretable risk mapping.
AI.result: The approach yields robust county-level SVI predictions and highlights interpretable, salient features (roof form/condition, street width, vegetation, cars/open space) as key drivers, enabling mapping of rural risk environments with enhanced explainability.
AI.conclusion: SatBLIP advances remote-sensing-based vulnerability assessment by leveraging satellite-specific vision-language modeling, structured satellite descriptions, and SHAP-based interpretability to produce context-aware, interpretable rural risk maps at administrative scales.

title: GFT: From Imitation to Reward Fine-Tuning with Unbiased Group Advantages and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification
summary: Large language models are typically post-trained using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL), yet effectively unifying efficient knowledge injection with robust generalization remains challenging. In this work, we provide a training-dynamics analysis showing that SFT can be interpreted as a special case of policy gradient optimization with an extremely sparse implicit reward and unstable inverse-probability weighting, which together lead to single-path dependency, entropy collapse, and gradient explosion. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Group Fine-Tuning (GFT), a unified post-training framework that addresses these intrinsic limitations through two mechanisms: Group Advantage Learning, which constructs diverse response groups and derives normalized contrastive supervision to alleviate reward sparsity, and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification, which adaptively bounds inverse-probability weights to stabilize optimization while preserving efficient knowledge injection. Experiments demonstrate that GFT consistently surpasses SFT-based methods and yields policies that integrate more smoothly with subsequent RL training.

AI.tldr: Diagnoses SFT as policy gradient with sparse rewards and unstable inverse-probability weighting; introduces Group Fine-Tuning (GFT) with Group Advantage Learning and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification to enhance knowledge injection and RL compatibility.
AI.motivation: To unify efficient knowledge injection with robust generalization in large language model post-training, by addressing intrinsic limitations of SFT viewed as policy gradient—namely sparse implicit rewards, unstable importance weights, single-path dependency, entropy collapse, and gradient explosion.
AI.method: Propose Group Fine-Tuning (GFT), a unified post-training framework with two mechanisms: (1) Group Advantage Learning, which builds diverse response groups and applies normalized contrastive supervision to mitigate reward sparsity; (2) Dynamic Coefficient Rectification, which adaptively bounds inverse-probability weights to stabilize optimization while preserving efficient knowledge injection.
AI.result: Empirical experiments show GFT consistently outperforms SFT-based methods and yields policies that integrate more smoothly with subsequent RL training.
AI.conclusion: GFT offers a unified post-training framework that mitigates SFT limitations, enabling more robust knowledge injection and smoother integration with RL fine-tuning.

title: DigiForest: Digital Analytics and Robotics for Sustainable Forestry
summary: Covering one third of Earth's land surface, forests are vital to global biodiversity, climate regulation, and human well-being. In Europe, forests and woodlands reach approximately 40% of land area, and the forestry sector is central to achieving the EU's climate neutrality and biodiversity goals; these emphasize sustainable forest management, increased use of long-lived wood products, and resilient forest ecosystems. To meet these goals and properly address their inherent challenges, current practices require further innovation. This chapter introduces DigiForest, a novel, large-scale precision forestry approach leveraging digital technologies and autonomous robotics. DigiForest is structured around four main components: (1) autonomous, heterogeneous mobile robots (aerial, legged, and marsupial) for tree-level data collection; (2) automated extraction of tree traits to build forest inventories; (3) a Decision Support System (DSS) for forecasting forest growth and supporting decision-making; and (4) low-impact selective logging using purpose-built autonomous harvesters. These technologies have been extensively validated in real-world conditions in several locations, including forests in Finland, the UK, and Switzerland.

AI.tldr: DigiForest proposes an integrated, large-scale precision forestry platform using autonomous robots, automated tree-trait extraction, a decision-support system, and autonomous harvesters, validated in multiple European forests to advance sustainable management and low-impact harvesting.
AI.motivation: Forests cover large areas and are critical for biodiversity, climate regulation, and human well-being; EU climate neutrality and biodiversity goals demand innovation in forest management, accurate inventories, and sustainable harvesting.
AI.method: Four components: (1) autonomous heterogeneous robots (aerial, legged, marsupial) for tree-level data collection; (2) automated extraction of tree traits to build forest inventories; (3) a Decision Support System for forecasting forest growth and aiding decision-making; (4) low-impact selective logging using purpose-built autonomous harvesters; real-world validation in Finland, the UK, and Switzerland.
AI.result: Technologies were extensively validated in real-world conditions across several European forests, demonstrating feasibility and potential for improved inventories, growth forecasting, and low-impact harvesting.
AI.conclusion: DigiForest offers a comprehensive precision forestry solution that integrates sensing, analytics, and autonomous harvesting to support sustainable forest management aligned with EU goals; continued validation and deployment could enhance resilience and biodiversity in European forests.

title: Graph-Based Fraud Detection with Dual-Path Graph Filtering
summary: Fraud detection on graph data can be viewed as a demanding task that requires distinguishing between different types of nodes. Because graph neural networks (GNNs) are naturally suited for processing information encoded in graph form through their message-passing operations, methods based on GNN models have increasingly attracted attention in the fraud detection domain. However, fraud graphs inherently exhibit relation camouflage, high heterophily, and class imbalance, causing most GNNs to underperform in fraud detection tasks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Graph-Based Fraud Detection Model with Dual-Path Graph Filtering (DPF-GFD). DPF-GFD first applies a beta wavelet-based operator to the original graph to capture key structural patterns. It then constructs a similarity graph from distance-based node representations and applies an improved low-pass filter. The embeddings from the original and similarity graphs are fused through supervised representation learning to obtain node features, which are finally used by an ensemble tree model to assess the fraud risk of unlabeled nodes. Unlike existing single-graph smoothing approaches, DPF-GFD introduces a frequency-complementary dual-path filtering paradigm tailored for fraud detection, explicitly decoupling structural anomaly modeling and feature similarity modeling. This design enables more discriminative and stable node representations in highly heterophilous and imbalanced fraud graphs. Comprehensive experiments on four real-world financial fraud detection datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

AI.tldr: Proposes DPF-GFD, a dual-path graph filtering framework for fraud detection that leverages a beta wavelet-based operator on the original graph and a similarity graph with an improved low-pass filter, fused via supervised representation learning and an ensemble tree classifier to handle heterophily and class imbalance.
AI.motivation: Fraud graphs exhibit relation camouflage, high heterophily, and class imbalance, causing standard GNNs to underperform; there is a need for discriminative node representations that decouple structural anomalies from feature similarity.
AI.method: Apply a beta wavelet operator to the original graph to capture structural patterns; construct a similarity graph from distance-based node representations and apply an improved low-pass filter; fuse embeddings from the original and similarity graphs through supervised representation learning; use the fused features in an ensemble tree model to predict fraud risk; introduces a frequency-complementary dual-path filtering paradigm that decouples structure vs. feature similarity modeling.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on four real-world financial fraud datasets demonstrate the method's effectiveness, outperforming baselines (as claimed by the abstract).
AI.conclusion: The frequency-complementary dual-path filtering approach yields more discriminative and stable node representations in highly heterophilous and imbalanced fraud graphs, improving fraud detection.

title: FoodSense: A Multisensory Food Dataset and Benchmark for Predicting Taste, Smell, Texture, and Sound from Images
summary: Humans routinely infer taste, smell, texture, and even sound from food images a phenomenon well studied in cognitive science. However, prior vision language research on food has focused primarily on recognition tasks such as meal identification, ingredient detection, and nutrition estimation. Image-based prediction of multisensory experience remains largely unexplored. We introduce FoodSense, a human-annotated dataset for cross-sensory inference containing 66,842 participant-image pairs across 2,987 unique food images. Each pair includes numeric ratings (1-5) and free-text descriptors for four sensory dimensions: taste, smell, texture, and sound. To enable models to both predict and explain sensory expectations, we expand short human annotations into image-grounded reasoning traces. A large language model generates visual justifications conditioned on the image, ratings, and descriptors. Using these annotations, we train FoodSense-VL, a vision language benchmark model to produce both multisensory ratings and grounded explanations directly from food images. This work connects cognitive science findings on cross-sensory perception with modern instruction tuning for multimodal models and shows that many popular evaluation metrics are insufficient for visually sensory inference.

AI.tldr: Introduces FoodSense, a large human-annotated dataset for cross-sensory inference from food images, with four sensory dimensions (taste, smell, texture, sound) including ratings and free-text descriptors, plus image-grounded reasoning traces generated by an LLM; proposes FoodSense-VL, a vision-language model that predicts multisensory ratings and grounded explanations, highlighting evaluation metric challenges in visually sensory inference.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in vision-language research that has focused on recognition tasks, by enabling multisensory inference from food images and grounding explanations in visuals; integrate cognitive science insights on cross-sensory perception with modern instruction-tuned multimodal models.
AI.method: Collect 66,842 participant-image pairs over 2,987 food images with numeric ratings (1-5) and free-text descriptors for taste, smell, texture, and sound. Expand annotations into image-grounded reasoning traces by having a large language model generate visual justifications conditioned on the image, ratings, and descriptors. Train FoodSense-VL to predict multisensory ratings and produce grounded explanations directly from food images.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of predicting multisensory experiences and producing grounded explanations from visual input; shows that standard evaluation metrics may be insufficient for assessing visually grounded multisensory inference; provides a new benchmark and model for cross-sensory image understanding.
AI.conclusion: Links cognitive science findings on cross-sensory perception with instruction-tuned multimodal models, highlighting the need for better evaluation metrics and more comprehensive cross-sensory benchmarks in vision-language research.

title: Seeing Through Experts Eyes A Foundational Vision Language Model Trained on Radiologists Gaze and Reasoning
summary: Large scale vision language models have shown promise in automating chest Xray interpretation, yet their clinical utility remains limited by a gap between model outputs and radiologist reasoning. Most systems optimize for semantic information without emulating how experts visually examine medical images, often overlooking critical findings or diverging from established diagnostic workflows. Radiologists follow structured protocols (e.g., the ABCDEF approach) that ensure all clinically relevant regions are systematically examined, reducing missed findings and supporting reliable diagnostic reasoning. We introduce GazeX, a vision language model that leverages radiologists' eye tracking data as a behavioral prior to model expert diagnostic reasoning. By incorporating gaze trajectories and fixation patterns into pretraining, GazeX learns to follow the spatial and temporal structure of radiologist attention and integrates observations in a clinically meaningful sequence. Using a curated dataset of over 30,000 gaze key frames from five radiologists, we demonstrate that GazeX produces more accurate, interpretable, and expert consistent outputs across radiology report generation, disease grounding, and visual question answering, utilizing 231,835 radiographic studies, 780,014 question answer pairs, and 1,162 image sentence pairs with bounding boxes. Unlike autonomous reporting systems, GazeX produces verifiable evidence artifacts, including inspection trajectories and finding linked localized regions, enabling efficient human verification and safe human AI collaboration. Learning through expert eyes provides a practical route toward more trustworthy, explainable, and diagnostically robust AI systems for radiology and beyond.

AI.tldr: GazeX augments vision-language radiology models with radiologists' eye-tracking as a behavioral prior, improving accuracy, interpretability, and verifiability while producing expert-consistent outputs.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap between model outputs and radiologist reasoning by leveraging how experts visually inspect images. Traditional systems optimize semantic information without modeling the structured, protocol-driven inspection that minimizes missed findings in radiology workflows (e.g., ABCDEF).
AI.method: Pretrain a vision-language model with radiologists' gaze trajectories and fixation patterns as a behavioral prior to imprint spatial-temporal attention aligned with expert inspection. Train on a curated dataset (~30k gaze key frames from five radiologists) and large-scale radiographs (231,835 studies) with downstream tasks including report generation, disease grounding, and visual question answering; also produce verifiable evidence artifacts (inspection trajectories and localized findings).
AI.result: GazeX yields more accurate, interpretable, and expert-consistent outputs across tasks, along with verifiable evidence artifacts that support human verification and safe human-AI collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Learning from expert gaze provides a practical path to trustworthy, explainable, and diagnostically robust AI in radiology and potentially other domains.

title: World-Value-Action Model: Implicit Planning for Vision-Language-Action Systems
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for building embodied agents that ground perception and language into action. However, most existing approaches rely on direct action prediction, lacking the ability to reason over long-horizon trajectories and evaluate their consequences, which limits performance in complex decision-making tasks. In this work, we introduce World-Value-Action (WAV) model, a unified framework that enables implicit planning in VLA systems. Rather than performing explicit trajectory optimization, WAV model learn a structured latent representation of future trajectories conditioned on visual observations and language instructions. A learned world model predicts future states, while a trajectory value function evaluates their long-horizon utility. Action generation is then formulated as inference in this latent space, where the model progressively concentrates probability mass on high-value and dynamically feasible trajectories. We provide a theoretical perspective showing that planning directly in action space suffers from an exponential decay in the probability of feasible trajectories as the horizon increases. In contrast, latent-space inference reshapes the search distribution toward feasible regions, enabling efficient long-horizon decision making. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the WAV model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving significant improvements in task success rate, generalization ability, and robustness, especially in long-horizon and compositional scenarios.

AI.tldr: WAV proposes latent-world planning for Vision-Language-Action agents, using a learned world model and a trajectory value function to implicitly plan in latent space, improving long-horizon decision making and robustness.
AI.motivation: Direct action-space planning in VLA systems struggles with exponential decay in feasible trajectories as horizon grows and cannot easily evaluate long-horizon consequences; a framework enabling planning over latent future trajectories is needed.
AI.method: Introduce World-Value-Action (WAV) framework that learns a structured latent representation of future trajectories conditioned on visual observations and language. A learned world model predicts future states; a trajectory value function evaluates long-horizon utility. Action generation is performed by inference in latent space, concentrating probability mass on high-value, dynamically feasible trajectories. Theoretical analysis contrasts action-space planning (exponential decay in feasible trajectories) with latent-space planning, and empirical results validate the approach.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments show WAV consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with significant gains in task success rate, generalization, and robustness, particularly for long-horizon and compositional tasks.
AI.conclusion: Planning in latent space mitigates the exponential decay of feasible trajectories inherent to action-space methods, enabling efficient long-horizon decision making and providing a robust, generalizable framework for embodied VLA agents.

title: TOPCELL: Topology Optimization of Standard Cell via LLMs
comment: Accepted to the 63rd ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC 2026). 7 pages, 4 figures
summary: Transistor topology optimization is a critical step in standard cell design, directly dictating diffusion sharing efficiency and downstream routability. However, identifying optimal topologies remains a persistent bottleneck, as conventional exhaustive search methods become computationally intractable with increasing circuit complexity in advanced nodes. This paper introduces TOPCELL, a novel and scalable framework that reformulates high-dimensional topology exploration as a generative task using Large Language Models (LLMs). We employ Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to fine-tune the model, aligning its topology optimization strategy with logical (circuit) and spatial (layout) constraints. Experimental results within an industrial flow targeting an advanced 2nm technology node demonstrate that TOPCELL significantly outperforms foundation models in discovering routable, physically-aware topologies. When integrated into a state-of-the-art (SOTA) automation flow for a 7nm library generation task, TOPCELL exhibits robust zero-shot generalization and matches the layout quality of exhaustive solvers while achieving an 85.91x speedup.

AI.tldr: TOPCELL reframes transistor topology optimization as a generative design task using LLMs, fine-tuned with Group Relative Policy Optimization to satisfy circuit and layout constraints, achieving fast, routable, physically-aware topologies with strong zero-shot generalization and significant speedups in industrial flows.
AI.motivation: Exhaustive search for high-density transistor topologies scales poorly with circuit complexity and node technology; there is a need for scalable, constraint-aware approaches that can integrate with layout and automation flows.
AI.method: Formulate topology exploration as a generative task with an LLM; fine-tune using GRPO to align with both logical (circuit) and spatial (layout) constraints; evaluate in an industrial 2nm flow and a 7nm library generation task, highlighting zero-shot generalization and integration with state-of-the-art automation.
AI.result: TOPCELL outperforms foundation models in discovering routable, physically-aware topologies; matches the layout quality of exhaustive solvers; provides an 85.91× speedup when embedded in a SOTA automation flow for 7nm library generation.
AI.conclusion: TOPCELL demonstrates a scalable, constraint-aware LLM-based approach to transistor topology optimization that can complement or replace exhaustive search in modern standard-cell design, with strong cross-node generalization and practical integration benefits.

title: Zero-Ablation Overstates Register Content Dependence in DINO Vision Transformers
comment: 12 pages, 10 figures, to be published in CVPR 2026 HOW Vision Interpretability Workshop Proceedings
summary: Zero-ablation -- replacing token activations with zero vectors -- is widely used to probe token function in vision transformers. Register zeroing in DINOv2+registers and DINOv3 produces large drops (up to $-36.6$\,pp classification, $-30.9$\,pp segmentation), suggesting registers are functionally indispensable. However, three replacement controls -- mean-substitution, noise-substitution, and cross-image register-shuffling -- preserve performance across classification, correspondence, and segmentation, remaining within ${\sim}1$\,pp of the unmodified baseline. Per-patch cosine similarity shows these replacements genuinely perturb internal representations, while zeroing causes disproportionately large perturbations, consistent with why it alone degrades tasks. We conclude that zero-ablation overstates dependence on exact register content. In the frozen-feature evaluations we test, performance depends on plausible register-like activations rather than on exact image-specific values. Registers nevertheless buffer dense features from \texttt{[CLS]} dependence and are associated with compressed patch geometry. These findings, including the replacement-control results, replicate at ViT-B scale.

AI.tldr: Zero-ablation overstates the dependence on exact register content in ViT. Replacement controls (mean, noise, cross-image shuffle) preserve performance, indicating plausible register-like activations suffice; zeroing perturbs representations more, and frozen-feature tests show reliance on register-like statistics rather than exact image-specific values; results replicate at ViT-B scale.
AI.motivation: Reassess the functional role of token registers in vision transformers by comparing zero-ablation with alternative perturbations and frozen-feature evaluations.
AI.method: Apply zero-vector replacements to token activations (zero-ablation) in DINOv2+ registers and DINOv3; compare with mean-substitution, noise-substitution, and cross-image register-shuffling; analyze per-patch cosine similarity; perform frozen-feature evaluations; test at ViT-B scale.
AI.result: Replacement controls preserve performance within ~1 pp across classification, correspondence, segmentation despite genuine perturbations to representations; zero-ablation causes large task drops; frozen features reveal reliance on plausible register-like activations; effects reproduce at ViT-B scale.
AI.conclusion: Zero-ablation overstates dependence on exact register content; the model relies on plausible register-like activations and buffer mechanisms; registers contribute to robustness and encoded patch geometry rather than precise image-specific values.

title: Mistake gating leads to energy and memory efficient continual learning
summary: Synaptic plasticity is metabolically expensive, yet animals continuously update their internal models without exhausting energy reserves. However, when artificial neural networks are trained, the network parameters are typically updated on every sample that is presented, even if the sample was classified correctly. Inspired by the human negativity bias and error-related negativity, we propose 'memorized mistake-gated learning' -- a biologically plausible plasticity rule where synaptic updates are strictly gated by current and past classification errors. This reduces the number of updates the network needs to make by $50\%\sim80\%$. Mistake gating is particularly well suited in two cases: 1) For incremental learning where new knowledge is acquired on a background of pre-existing knowledge, 2) For online learning scenarios when data needs to be stored for later replay, as mistake-gating reduces storage buffer requirements. The algorithm can be implemented in a few lines of code, adds no hyper-parameters, and comes at negligible computational overhead. Learning on mistakes is an energy efficient and biologically relevant modification to commonly used learning rules that is well suited for continual learning.

AI.tldr: Biologically inspired learning rule that gates synaptic updates by current and past mistakes, reducing updates and memory needs for continual/online learning; claims simple, hyperparameter-free, energy-efficient, and easy to implement.
AI.motivation: Address the energy cost of synaptic plasticity and the inefficiency of updating on every sample in online/ continual learning; align with human negativity bias and error-related negativity to gate learning.
AI.method: Memorized mistake-gated learning: updates occur only when current or past classification errors are present; implements gating to reduce unnecessary updates; described as simple to implement with no hyperparameters and negligible overhead.
AI.result: Reduces the number of updates by 50-80%; reduces storage buffer requirements for replay; applicable to incremental and online learning; compatible with standard learning rules; low computational overhead; claims energy efficiency and biological plausibility.
AI.conclusion: Learning-on-mistakes is a lightweight, biologically grounded modification that supports continual learning with lower update frequency and storage needs, without adding hyperparameters.

title: Differentiable Object Pose Connectivity Metrics for Regrasp Sequence Optimization
summary: Regrasp planning is often required when one pick-and-place cannot transfer an object from an initial pose to a goal pose while maintaining grasp feasibility. The main challenge is to reason about shared-grasp connectivity across intermediate poses, where discrete search becomes brittle. We propose an implicit multi-step regrasp planning framework based on differentiable pose sequence connectivity metrics. We model grasp feasibility under an object pose using an Energy-Based Model (EBM) and leverage energy additivity to construct a continuous energy landscape that measures pose-pair connectivity, enabling gradient-based optimization of intermediate object poses. An adaptive iterative deepening strategy is introduced to determine the minimum number of intermediate steps automatically. Experiments show that the proposed cost formulation provides smooth and informative gradients, improving planning robustness over other alternatives. They also demonstrate generalization to unseen grasp poses and cross-end-effector transfer, where a model trained with suction constraints can guide parallel gripper grasp manipulation. The multi-step planning results further highlight the effectiveness of adaptive deepening and minimum-step search.

AI.tldr: Introduces a differentiable, multi-step regrasp planner using an energy-based model to quantify pose-pair connectivity; supports gradient-based optimization and adaptive step counting, with cross-end-effector transfer.
AI.motivation: Discrete search for shared-grasp connectivity across poses is brittle; continuous connectivity metrics are needed to enable robust, multi-step planning.
AI.method: Model grasp feasibility as an energy function under object pose; leverage energy additivity to create a continuous landscape; perform gradient-based optimization of intermediate poses; use adaptive iterative deepening to auto-determine the minimal number of steps; validate generalization to unseen grasps and cross-end-effector transfer.
AI.result: Energy-based landscape yields smooth, informative gradients and improved planning robustness compared with alternatives; demonstrates generalization to unseen grasps and suction-to-parallel-gripper transfer; adaptive deepening and minimum-step search improve efficiency and solution quality.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides robust, generalizable multi-step regrasp planning via differentiable pose connectivity, with additive energy modeling and adaptive step determination proven effective.

title: Optimistic Policy Learning under Pessimistic Adversaries with Regret and Violation Guarantees
summary:
Real-world decision-making systems operate in environments where state transitions depend not only on the agent's actions, but also on \textbf{exogenous factors outside its control}--competing agents, environmental disturbances, or strategic adversaries--formally, $s_{h+1} = f(s_h, a_h, \bar{a}_h)+ω_h$ where $\bar{a}_h$ is the adversary/external action, $a_h$ is the agent's action, and $ω_h$ is an additive noise. Ignoring such factors can yield policies that are optimal in isolation but \textbf{fail catastrophically in deployment}, particularly when safety constraints must be satisfied.
  Standard Constrained MDP formulations assume the agent is the sole driver of state evolution, an assumption that breaks down in safety-critical settings. Existing robust RL approaches address this via distributional robustness over transition kernels, but do not explicitly model the \textbf{strategic interaction} between agent and exogenous factor, and rely on strong assumptions about divergence from a known nominal model.
  We model the exogenous factor as an \textbf{adversarial policy} $\barπ$ that co-determines state transitions, and ask how an agent can remain both optimal and safe against such an adversary. \emph{To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study safety-constrained RL under explicit adversarial dynamics}. We propose \textbf{Robust Hallucinated Constrained Upper-Confidence RL} (\texttt{RHC-UCRL}), a model-based algorithm that maintains optimism over both agent and adversary policies, explicitly separating epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty. \texttt{RHC-UCRL} achieves sub-linear regret and constraint violation guarantees.

AI.tldr: Models exogenous adversarial factors in RL and proposes Robust Hallucinated Constrained UCRL (RHC-UCRL): a model-based, optimistic algorithm that handles safety constraints under explicit adversarial dynamics, with sub-linear regret and constraint violation guarantees.
AI.motivation: Real-world decision problems involve exogenous disturbances and strategic adversaries that affect state transitions. Ignoring these can yield unsafe policies. Existing robust RL treats uncertainty in transitions but does not model explicit adversarial dynamics or ensure safety.
AI.method: Model exogenous factor as an adversarial policy that co-determines transitions. Introduce Robust Hallucinated Constrained UCRL (RHC-UCRL), a model-based algorithm maintaining optimism over both agent and adversary policies and separating epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves sub-linear regret and constraint violation guarantees, providing safety-constrained performance under explicit adversarial dynamics.
AI.conclusion: This work is, to their knowledge, the first to study safety-constrained RL with explicit adversarial dynamics, offering a principled, optimistic, model-based framework (RHC-UCRL) to ensure safety and performance in adversarial environments.

title: Crowdsourcing of Real-world Image Annotation via Visual Properties
summary: Recent advances in data-centric artificial intelligence highlight inherent limitations in object recognition datasets. One of the primary issues stems from the semantic gap problem, which results in complex many-to-many mappings between visual data and linguistic descriptions. This bias adversely affects performance in computer vision tasks. This paper proposes an image annotation methodology that integrates knowledge representation, natural language processing, and computer vision techniques, aiming to reduce annotator subjectivity by applying visual property constraints. We introduce an interactive crowdsourcing framework that dynamically asks questions based on a predefined object category hierarchy and annotator feedback, guiding image annotation by visual properties. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of this methodology, and annotator feedback is discussed to optimize the crowdsourcing setup.

AI.tldr: Interactive crowdsourcing for image annotation using knowledge representation, NLP, and CV to reduce annotator subjectivity via visual-property constraints and a hierarchy-guided questioning strategy.
AI.motivation: Address semantic gap and bias in object recognition datasets that impede robust visual-linguistic mappings and downstream CV performance.
AI.method: A framework that integrates knowledge representation, natural language processing, and computer vision to constrain annotations by visual properties, plus an interactive crowdsourcing loop that asks questions guided by a predefined object-category hierarchy and annotator feedback.
AI.result: Experiments indicate the approach is effective in guiding annotation and reducing subjectivity; annotator feedback is analyzed to optimize the crowdsourcing setup.
AI.conclusion: An integrated, interactive annotation pipeline can mitigate semantic-gap-induced biases and improve data quality by enforcing visual-property constraints through hierarchy-guided questioning.

title: Credo: Declarative Control of LLM Pipelines via Beliefs and Policies
summary: Agentic AI systems are becoming commonplace in domains that require long-lived, stateful decision-making in continuously evolving conditions. As such, correctness depends not only on the output of individual model calls, but also on how to best adapt when incorporating new evidence or revising prior conclusions. However, existing frameworks rely on imperative control loops, ephemeral memory, and prompt-embedded logic, making agent behavior opaque, brittle, and difficult to verify. This paper introduces Credo, which represents semantic state as beliefs and regulates behavior using declarative policies defined over these beliefs. This design supports adaptive, auditable, and composable execution through a database-backed semantic control plane. We showcase these concepts in a decision-control scenario, where beliefs and policies declaratively guide critical execution choices (e.g., model selection, retrieval, corrective re-execution), enabling dynamic behavior without requiring any changes to the underlying pipeline code.

AI.tldr: Credo introduces semantic state (beliefs) and declarative policies over beliefs to achieve auditable, adaptive control for agentic AI, implemented via a database-backed semantic control plane; demonstrated in a decision-control scenario.
AI.motivation: Address opacity, brittleness, and verifiability gaps in agentic AI that rely on imperative control loops, ephemeral memory, and prompt-embedded logic.
AI.method: Replace imperative loops with a semantic state store (beliefs) and declarative policies that operate over those beliefs; implement a database-backed control plane to support persistence, auditability, and composability; demonstrate via a decision-control use case guiding model selection, retrieval, and re-execution.
AI.result: A conceptual framework with a working demonstration showing how beliefs and declarative policies guide critical execution choices without changing pipeline code, enabling adaptive and auditable behavior.
AI.conclusion: Framing state as beliefs and controlling behavior via declarative policies over that state enables auditable, composable, and adaptive agentic AI, potentially improving verifiability and maintainability of long-lived decision-making systems.

title: Keep It CALM: Toward Calibration-Free Kilometer-Level SLAM with Visual Geometry Foundation Models via an Assistant Eye
comment: 19 pages, 8 figures, submitted to IEEE TPAMI
summary: Visual Geometry Foundation Models (VGFMs) demonstrate remarkable zero-shot capabilities in local reconstruction. However, deploying them for kilometer-level Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) remains challenging. In such scenarios, current approaches mainly rely on linear transforms (e.g., Sim3 and SL4) for sub-map alignment, while we argue that a single linear transform is fundamentally insufficient to model the complex, non-linear geometric distortions inherent in VGFM outputs. Forcing such rigid alignment leads to the rapid accumulation of uncorrected residuals, eventually resulting in significant trajectory drift and map divergence. To address these limitations, we present CAL2M (Calibration-free Assistant-eye based Large-scale Localization and Mapping), a plug-and-play framework compatible with arbitrary VGFMs. Distinct from traditional systems, CAL2M introduces an "assistant eye" solely to leverage the prior of constant physical spacing, effectively eliminating scale ambiguity without any temporal or spatial pre-calibration. Furthermore, leveraging the assumption of accurate feature matching, we propose an epipolar-guided intrinsic and pose correction model. Supported by an online intrinsic search module, it can effectively rectify rotation and translation errors caused by inaccurate intrinsics through fundamental matrix decomposition. Finally, to ensure accurate mapping, we introduce a globally consistent mapping strategy based on anchor propagation. By constructing and fusing anchors across the trajectory, we establish a direct local-to-global mapping relationship. This enables the application of nonlinear transformations to elastically align sub-maps, effectively eliminating geometric misalignments and ensuring a globally consistent reconstruction. The source code of CAL2M will be publicly available at https://github.com/IRMVLab/CALM.

AI.tldr: CAL2M introduces a plug-and-play, nonlinear alignment framework for large-scale SLAM with VGFMs, using an 'assistant eye' to remove scale ambiguity and anchor-propagation for global consistency.
AI.motivation: Visual Geometry Foundation Models (VGFMs) offer strong zero-shot local reconstructions but struggle at kilometer-scale SLAM due to reliance on linear transforms (e.g., Sim3, SL4) that cannot capture complex non-linear distortions, leading to drift and map divergence; a scale-ambiguous, globally consistent, and calibration-free solution is needed.
AI.method: CAL2M adds an 'assistant eye' to preserve constant physical spacing and eliminate scale ambiguity without temporal/spatial pre-calibration; introduces an epipolar-guided intrinsic and pose correction model with an online intrinsic search that uses fundamental matrix decomposition; implements globally consistent mapping via anchor propagation by constructing/fusing anchors across the trajectory to enable nonlinear, elastic alignment of sub-maps; designed as a plug-and-play framework compatible with arbitrary VGFMs; source code to be released publicly.
AI.result: The abstract presents a conceptual framework and pipeline without reported quantitative experiments or metrics.
AI.conclusion: CAL2M enables scale-unambiguous, globally consistent large-scale SLAM with VGFMs by combining nonlinear elastic alignment and anchor-based global fusion; it is intended as a practical plug-and-play solution with open-source availability.

title: Awakening Dormant Experts:Counterfactual Routing to Mitigate MoE Hallucinations
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have achieved remarkable scalability, yet they remain vulnerable to hallucinations, particularly when processing long-tail knowledge. We identify that this fragility stems from static Top-$k$ routing: routers tend to favor high-frequency patterns over rare factual associations. Consequently, ``specialist experts'' possessing critical long-tail knowledge are often assigned low gating scores and remain ``dormant'' -- under-prioritized for specific tokens despite their proven causal importance on other inputs. To address this, we propose Counterfactual Routing (CoR), a training-free inference framework designed to awaken these dormant experts. CoR integrates layer-wise perturbation analysis with the Counterfactual Expert Impact (CEI) metric to dynamically shift computational resources from syntax-dominant to knowledge-intensive layers while maintaining a constant total activation count, effectively retrieving causally decisive experts via virtual ablation. Extensive experiments on TruthfulQA, FACTOR, and TriviaQA demonstrate that CoR improves factual accuracy by 3.1\% on average without increasing the inference budget, establishing a superior Pareto frontier compared to static scaling strategies.

AI.tldr: Counterfactual Routing (CoR) activates dormant experts in Sparse MoE models by layer-wise perturbation and Counterfactual Expert Impact (CEI), rebalancing computation from syntax-dominant to knowledge-intensive layers without increasing total activations, improving factual accuracy on long-tail knowledge without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Address the vulnerability of sparse MoE models to hallucinations, caused by static Top-k routing that underutilizes specialist experts with long-tail knowledge.
AI.method: Integrate layer-wise perturbation analysis with a Counterfactual Expert Impact (CEI) metric to dynamically reallocate activation budgets to causally decisive experts during inference, in a training-free manner, effectively simulating virtual ablation to awaken dormant experts.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on TruthfulQA, FACTOR, and TriviaQA showing an average factual accuracy improvement of 3.1 percentage points, with a better Pareto frontier than static scaling methods while keeping the activation budget constant.
AI.conclusion: CoR provides a training-free, inference-time mechanism to mitigate hallucinations in Sparse MoE by leveraging counterfactual reasoning to identify and utilize long-tail, causally impactful experts, enhancing factual correctness without increasing computation.

title: Co-distilled attention guided masked image modeling with noisy teacher for self-supervised learning on medical images
summary: Masked image modeling (MIM) is a highly effective self-supervised learning (SSL) approach to extract useful feature representations from unannotated data. Predominantly used random masking methods make SSL less effective for medical images due to the contextual similarity of neighboring patches, leading to information leakage and SSL simplification. Hierarchical shifted window (Swin) transformer, a highly effective approach for medical images cannot use advanced masking methods as it lacks a global [CLS] token. Hence, we introduced an attention guided masking mechanism for Swin within a co-distillation learning framework to selectively mask semantically co-occurring and discriminative patches, to reduce information leakage and increase the difficulty of SSL pretraining. However, attention guided masking inevitably reduces the diversity of attention heads, which negatively impacts downstream task performance. To address this, we for the first time, integrate a noisy teacher into the co-distillation framework (termed DAGMaN) that performs attentive masking while preserving high attention head diversity. We demonstrate the capability of DAGMaN on multiple tasks including full- and few-shot lung nodule classification, immunotherapy outcome prediction, tumor segmentation, and unsupervised organs clustering.

AI.tldr: DAGMaN presents attention-guided masking within a co-distillation framework for Swin-based masked image modeling, using a noisy teacher to preserve attention head diversity. It improves SSL for medical images and demonstrates cross-task efficacy in classification, prediction, segmentation, and clustering.
AI.motivation: Medical image SSL with random masking suffers from information leakage due to high patch similarity; Swin transformers lack a global CLS token hindering global masking strategies; need selective masking of semantically discriminative patches while maintaining attention head diversity to improve downstream performance.
AI.method: Integrates attention-guided masking into a Swin-based masked image modeling setup within a co-distillation framework. Introduces a noisy teacher to preserve high attention head diversity while performing attentive masking. Evaluates DAGMaN on multiple medical tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates capability on full- and few-shot lung nodule classification, immunotherapy outcome prediction, tumor segmentation, and unsupervised organs clustering, indicating robust cross-task SSL benefits.
AI.conclusion: DAGMaN mitigates information leakage and preserves attention head diversity through a noisy-teacher co-distillation, enabling effective Swin-based SSL for medical imaging and promising cross-task generalization.

title: Equifinality in Mixture of Experts: Routing Topology Does Not Determine Language Modeling Quality
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures employ increasingly sophisticated routing mechanisms -- learned routers, multi-hop trajectories, token-dependent gating. We ask: does routing topology actually determine language modeling quality? We build a geometric MoE (ST-MoE) using cosine-similarity routing against learned centroids in a low-dimensional space ($d_{space} = 64$), requiring 80% fewer routing parameters than standard linear routers. Through 62 controlled experiments on WikiText-103 at 76--84M parameters trained to convergence (50K steps, 1.64B tokens), we find that routing topology does not determine asymptotic perplexity (PPL): five cosine-routing variants are statistically equivalent within a 1-PPL margin (Two One-Sided Tests [TOST], $p < 0.05$ for all 10 pairwise comparisons; 15 runs across 3 seeds, observed range 33.93--34.72). The finding extends to hash, random-fixed, and top-1 routing (single-seed; graceful 1.1--2.2 PPL degradation) and replicates on OpenWebText (0.03 PPL gap, 6 runs, 3 seeds each). A standard linear router with 5.3$\times$ more routing parameters reaches PPL 32.76, but iso-parameter cosine routing closes 67% of this gap -- the true mechanism advantage is $\sim$1.2%. The mechanistic explanation is convergent redundancy: multi-hop updates are collinear ($\cos(Δh_0, Δh_1) = 0.805$), implementing magnitude amplification rather than compositional reasoning; a single learnable scalar replicates multi-hop performance. As a practical payoff, zero-shot relative-norm halting saves 25% of MoE FLOPs at +0.12% PPL. Expert-level specialization and causal controllability -- which coexist with topology-level equifinality -- are explored in a companion paper.

AI.tldr: Routing topology in sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has little impact on asymptotic language-model perplexity; cosine-routing with fewer routing parameters matches or nearly matches linear routers, due to convergent redundancy and magnitude amplification rather than compositional reasoning; a simple scalar can replicate multi-hop benefits; practical FLOP savings possible via halting with minimal PPL cost.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether routing topology governs language-model quality in sparse MoEs and whether geometry-based cosine routing can substitute learned routers while reducing routing parameters.
AI.method: Geometric MoE (ST-MoE) with cosine-similarity routing against learned centroids in 64-d space; ~80% fewer routing parameters than linear routers; 62 controlled experiments on WikiText-103 with 76–84M parameters, 50k steps, 1.64B tokens; replication on OpenWebText; comparisons include hash, random-fixed, top-1 routing, and a standard linear router (more routing parameters); evaluated using perplexity (PPL) and statistical equivalence via TOST (TOST test, p<0.05).
AI.result:
Routing topology did not determine asymptotic PPL; five cosine-routing variants were statistically equivalent within 1 PPL (15 runs, 3 seeds); open-world replication shows small gaps (OpenWebText ~0.03 PPL). Linear router achieved PPL 32.76 (with 5.3x more routing parameters), while iso-parameter cosine routing closed ~67% of that gap, implying a true mechanism advantage of ~1.2%. Mechanism attributed to convergent redundancy: multi-hop updates are highly collinear (cos(Δh0, Δh1)=0.805); magnitude amplification rather than compositional reasoning; a single learnable scalar can replicate multi-hop performance. Practical payoff includes zero-shot relative-norm halting reducing MoE FLOPs by ~25% with only +0.12% PPL.
Expert specialization and causal controllability are noted as coexisting with topology-level equifinality (to be discussed in a companion paper).
AI.conclusion: Topology-driven differences in routing do not drive language-model quality in this setting; the observed gains stem from redundancy and simple magnitude scaling rather than hierarchical composition. Equifinality across routing topologies implies that simpler cosine-based or fixed routing can match more parameter-heavy learned routers, with modest power savings possible through halting. The results motivate focusing on mechanism-level insights (e.g., redundancy) and controllability; further details on specialization/controllability are explored in a companion study.

title: Switch: Learning Agile Skills Switching for Humanoid Robots
summary: Recent advancements in whole-body control through deep reinforcement learning have enabled humanoid robots to achieve remarkable progress in real-world chal lenging locomotion skills. However, existing approaches often struggle with flexible transitions between distinct skills, cre ating safety concerns and practical limitations. To address this challenge, we introduce a hierarchical multi-skill system, Switch, enabling seamless skill transitions at any moment. Our approach comprises three key components: (1) a Skill Graph (SG) that establishes potential cross-skill transitions based on kinematic similarity within multi-skill motion data, (2) a whole-body tracking policy trained on this skill graph through deep reinforcement learning, and (3) an online skill scheduler to drive the tracking policy for robust skill execution and smooth transitions. For skill switching or significant tracking deviations, the scheduler performs online graph search to find the optimal feasible path, which ensures efficient, stable, and real-time execution of diverse locomotion skills. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that Switch empowers humanoid to execute agile skill transitions with high success rates while maintaining strong motion imitation performance.

AI.tldr: Switch is a hierarchical multi-skill control framework for humanoid locomotion that merges a Skill Graph for cross-skill transitions, a deep RL-based whole-body tracking policy trained on this graph, and an online skill scheduler that uses graph search to enable seamless, real-time skill transitions.
AI.motivation: Current deep reinforcement learning approaches for humanoid locomotion struggle with safe, seamless transitions between distinct skills, leading to safety concerns and limited real-world applicability. A mechanism enabling robust, real-time transitions across multiple skills is needed.
AI.method: The framework comprises three components: (1) a Skill Graph (SG) that encodes feasible cross-skill transitions based on kinematic similarity drawn from multi-skill motion data; (2) a whole-body tracking policy trained via deep reinforcement learning on the SG; and (3) an online skill scheduler that uses the tracking policy and performs online graph search to determine optimal feasible transition paths, ensuring robust execution and smooth transitions.
AI.result: Experiments show that Switch enables humanoid robots to perform agile skill transitions with high success rates while maintaining strong motion imitation performance, demonstrating robust, real-time execution across diverse locomotion skills.
AI.conclusion: Switch delivers a practical, scalable approach for seamless multi-skill humanoid locomotion by integrating a transition-aware Skill Graph, a DRL-based tracking policy, and an online scheduler capable of online path optimization, enabling real-time, robust skill execution.

title: Metric-Aware Principal Component Analysis (MAPCA):A Unified Framework for Scale-Invariant Representation Learning
summary:
We introduce Metric-Aware Principal Component Analysis (MAPCA), a unified framework for scale-invariant representation learning based on the generalised eigenproblem max Tr(W^T Sigma W) subject to W^T M W = I, where M is a symmetric positive definite metric matrix. The choice of M determines the representation geometry. The canonical beta-family M(beta) = Sigma^beta, beta in [0,1], provides continuous spectral bias control between standard PCA (beta=0) and output whitening (beta=1), with condition number kappa(beta) = (lambda_1/lambda_p)^(1-beta) decreasing monotonically to isotropy. The diagonal metric M = D = diag(Sigma) recovers Invariant PCA (IPCA), a method rooted in Frisch (1928) diagonal regression, as a distinct member of the broader framework. We prove that scale invariance holds if and only if the metric transforms as M_tilde = CMC under rescaling C, a condition satisfied exactly by IPCA but not by the general beta-family at intermediate values.
  Beyond its classical interpretation, MAPCA provides a geometric language that unifies several self-supervised learning objectives. Barlow Twins and ZCA whitening correspond to beta=1 (output whitening); VICReg's variance term corresponds to the diagonal metric. A key finding is that W-MSE, despite being described as a whitening-based method, corresponds to M = Sigma^{-1} (beta = -1), outside the spectral compression range entirely and in the opposite spectral direction to Barlow Twins. This distinction between input and output whitening is invisible at the level of loss functions and becomes precise only within the MAPCA framework.

AI.tldr: MAPCA recasts PCA as a generalized eigenproblem with a learnable metric M, yielding a family of scale-aware representations. The beta-family M(beta)=Sigma^beta interpolates between PCA (beta=0) and whitening (beta=1); IPCA is a scale-invariant special case (M=diag(Sigma)). The framework links several SSL objectives (Barlow Twins, ZCA, VICReg) and clarifies that W-MSE maps to beta=-1, outside the spectral compression range, highlighting input vs output whitening. Scale invariance holds iff M transforms as M_tilde = C M C under scaling, which IPCA satisfies but intermediate beta values do not.
AI.motivation: Provide a unified geometric framework to understand scale-invariant representations and connect classical PCA, whitening, and modern self-supervised learning losses through a single generalized eigenproblem with a tunable metric.
AI.method: Define optimization max Tr(W^T Sigma W) subject to W^T M W = I with M symmetric positive definite. Use M(beta)=Sigma^beta to parametrize the metric, analyze spectral bias via kappa(beta)=(lambda1/lambda_p)^(1-beta), and prove scale-invariance condition M_tilde = C M C. Map standard SSL losses to corresponding beta values (e.g., Barlow Twins and ZCA whitening at beta=1; VICReg relates to diagonal metric; W-MSE corresponds to beta=-1).
AI.result: Key results include: (i) kappa(beta) decreases monotonically with beta, interpolating between PCA and isotropy; (ii) IPCA (M=diag(Sigma)) is the unique scale-invariant member of the family; (iii) multiple SSL objectives admit a clean MAPCA interpretation (Barlow Twins/ZCA at beta=1; VICReg via diagonal metric); (iv) W-MSE lies at beta=-1, opposite the spectral direction of Barlow Twins, revealing an input vs output whitening distinction not evident from losses alone.
AI.conclusion: MAPCA provides a cohesive geometric lens to compare PCA, whitening, and SSL objectives. Scale invariance is a fragile property, preserved only for IPCA under rescaling. The beta-family offers a controlled means to tune spectral bias, and W-MSE’s placement at beta=-1 clarifies fundamental directional differences in the spectrum.

title: H2VLR: Heterogeneous Hypergraph Vision-Language Reasoning for Few-Shot Anomaly Detection
summary: As a classic vision task, anomaly detection has been widely applied in industrial inspection and medical imaging. In this task, data scarcity is often a frequently-faced issue. To solve it, the few-shot anomaly detection (FSAD) scheme is attracting increasing attention. In recent years, beyond traditional visual paradigm, Vision-Language Model (VLM) has been extensively explored to boost this field. However, in currently-existing VLM-based FSAD schemes, almost all perform anomaly inference only by pairwise feature matching, ignoring structural dependencies and global consistency. To further redound to FSAD via VLM, we propose a Heterogeneous Hypergraph Vision-Language Reasoning (H2VLR) framework. It reformulates the FSAD as a high-order inference problem of visual-semantic relations, by jointly modeling visual regions and semantic concepts in a unified hypergraph. Experimental comparisons verify the effectiveness and advantages of H2VLR. It could often achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on representative industrial and medical benchmarks. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A heterogenous hypergraph-based reasoning framework (H2VLR) for few-shot anomaly detection that jointly models visual regions and semantic concepts via high-order relations in a vision-language setting, achieving state-of-the-art results on industrial and medical benchmarks; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Current VLM-based FSAD methods rely largely on pairwise feature matching, which ignores structural dependencies and global consistency. Data scarcity in anomaly detection makes it essential to leverage high-order visual-semantic relations to improve generalization and robustness.
AI.method: Construct a heterogeneous hypergraph comprising visual-region nodes and semantic-concept nodes. Encode cross-modal features with a vision-language model, then perform high-order reasoning via hypergraph neural networks to propagate information across nodes and hyperedges, capturing complex relations among regions and concepts for anomaly inference.
AI.result: Experimental comparisons show the proposed H2VLR framework achieves competitive and often superior performance, attaining state-of-the-art results on representative industrial and medical anomaly benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: H2VLR demonstrates that modeling high-order visual-semantic relations in a unified hypergraph with VLMs enhances FSAD, providing improved accuracy and generalization; code will be released upon acceptance.

title: Demonstration of Pneuma-Seeker: Agentic System for Reifying and Fulfilling Information Needs on Tabular Data
summary: Data analysts working with relational data often start with vague or underspecified questions and refine them iteratively as they explore the data. To support this iterative process, we demonstrate Pneuma-Seeker, a system that reifies a user's information need as explicit, inspectable relational specifications, enabling iterative refinement of the information need, targeted data discovery, and provenance-aware execution. Through two real-world procurement use cases, we show how Pneuma-Seeker leverages LLMs as transparent, interactive analytical collaborators rather than opaque answer engines.

AI.tldr: Pneuma-Seeker turns vague data questions into explicit, inspectable relational specifications to support iterative refinement, targeted data discovery, and provenance-aware execution; demonstrates LLMs as transparent collaborators in two procurement use cases.
AI.motivation: Relational data analysts often start with underspecified questions; enabling explicit, inspectable specifications and provenance improves transparency, iterative refinement, and data discovery.
AI.method: Introduce Pneuma-Seeker system that reifies a user's information need as explicit relational specifications; integrates LLMs as interactive, transparent collaborators to refine specifications; supports provenance-aware execution and targeted data discovery; validated in two real-world procurement scenarios.
AI.result: Shows that LLMs can function as transparent, interactive analytical collaborators rather than opaque answer engines; demonstrates iterative refinement and provenance-aware execution in two procurement use cases.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, inspectable information needs enabling iterative refinement and provenance-aware data exploration with LLMs as collaborative partners is a promising direction for relational data analysis.

title: Graph Theoretical Outlier Rejection for 4D Radar Registration in Feature-Poor Environments
summary: Automotive 4D imaging radar is well suited for operation in dusty and low-visibility environments, but scan registration remains challenging due to scan sparsity and spurious detections caused by noise and multipath reflections. This difficulty is compounded in feature-poor open-pit mines, where the lack of distinctive landmarks reduces correspondence reliability. We integrate graph-based pairwise consistency maximization (PCM) as an outlier rejection step within the iterative closest points (ICP) loop. We propose a radar-adapted pairwise distance-invariant scoring function for graph-based (PCM) that incorporates anisotropic, per-detection uncertainty derived from a radar measurement model. The consistency maximization problem is approximated with a greedy heuristic that finds a large clique in the pairwise consistency graph. The refined correspondence set improves robustness when the initial association set is heavily contaminated. We evaluate a standard Euclidean distance residual and our uncertainty-aware residual on an open-pit mine dataset collected with a 4D imaging radar. Compared to the generalized ICP (GICP) baseline without PCM, our method reduces segment relative position error (RPE) by 29.6% on 1 m segments and by up to 55% on 100 m segments. The presented method is intended for integration into localization pipelines and is suitable for online use due to the greedy heuristic in graph-based (PCM).

AI.tldr: Radar-based PCM within ICP with radar uncertainty modeling yields robust, online scan registration in sparse, noisy data, achieving substantial RPE reduction in open-pit mine tests.
AI.motivation: 4D radar operates in dusty environments where scan sparsity and clutter hinder reliable correspondences; robust registration is essential for localization.
AI.method: Integrate graph-based pairwise consistency maximization as outlier rejection inside ICP. Develop radar-adapted distance-invariant scoring with anisotropic per-detection uncertainty from radar model. Approximate consistency with greedy clique in pairwise graph. Compare with standard Euclidean residual vs uncertainty-aware residual. Evaluate on an open-pit mine 4D radar dataset.
AI.result: Compared to GICP baseline without PCM, segment RPE reduced by 29.6% on 1 m segments and up to 55% on 100 m segments. Method suitable for online use due to greedy heuristic.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware PCM within ICP improves robustness of radar-based scan registration in feature-poor, noisy environments and is suitable for integration into real-time localization pipelines.

title: Calibrate-Then-Delegate: Safety Monitoring with Risk and Budget Guarantees via Model Cascades
summary: Monitoring LLM safety at scale requires balancing cost and accuracy: a cheap latent-space probe can screen every input, but hard cases should be escalated to a more expensive expert. Existing cascades delegate based on probe uncertainty, but uncertainty is a poor proxy for delegation benefit, as it ignores whether the expert would actually correct the error. To address this problem, we introduce Calibrate-Then-Delegate (CTD), a model-cascade approach that provides probabilistic guarantees on the computation cost while enabling instance-level (streaming) decisions. CTD builds on a novel delegation value (DV) probe, a lightweight model operating on the same internal representations as the safety probe that directly predicts the benefit of escalation. To enforce budget constraints, CTD calibrates a threshold on the DV signal using held-out data via multiple hypothesis testing, yielding finite-sample guarantees on the delegation rate. Evaluated on four safety datasets, CTD consistently outperforms uncertainty-based delegation at every budget level, avoids harmful over-delegation, and adapts budget allocation to input difficulty without requiring group labels.

AI.tldr: A model-cascade with a delegation-value (DV) probe enabling calibrated, cost-bounded escalation in LLM safety, outperforming uncertainty-based quorum at all budget levels.
AI.motivation: Balancing cheap screening and expensive expert review in LLM safety requires a delegation strategy that reflects true benefit of escalation, not just model uncertainty. Unreliable proxies can lead to under- or over-delegation and uncontrolled costs.
AI.method: Introduce Calibrate-Then-Delegate (CTD) with a lightweight DV probe operating on the same representations as the safety probe. CTD calibrates a threshold on the DV signal via held-out data using multiple hypothesis testing to guarantee finite-sample delegation-rate bounds, enabling per-instance streaming decisions and budget-aware control.
AI.result: CTD consistently beats uncertainty-based delegation across all budgets on four safety datasets, reduces harmful over-delegation, and adapts delegation to input difficulty without requiring group labels.
AI.conclusion: CTD provides probabilistic cost guarantees and improved delegation effectiveness, with a scalable, label-free method for streaming, budget-conscious LLM safety cascades.

title: Chain of Modality: From Static Fusion to Dynamic Orchestration in Omni-MLLMs
summary: Omni-modal Large Language Models (Omni-MLLMs) promise a unified integration of diverse sensory streams. However, recent evaluations reveal a critical performance paradox: unimodal baselines frequently outperform joint multimodal inference. We trace this perceptual fragility to the static fusion topologies universally employed by current models, identifying two structural pathologies: positional bias in sequential inputs and alignment traps in interleaved formats, which systematically distort attention regardless of task semantics. To resolve this functional rigidity, we propose Chain of Modality (CoM), an agentic framework that transitions multimodal fusion from passive concatenation to dynamic orchestration. CoM adaptively orchestrates input topologies, switching among parallel, sequential, and interleaved pathways to neutralize structural biases. Furthermore, CoM bifurcates cognitive execution into two task-aligned pathways: a streamlined ``Direct-Decide'' path for direct perception and a structured ``Reason-Decide'' path for analytical auditing. Operating in either a training-free or a data-efficient SFT setting, CoM achieves robust and consistent generalization across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Dynamic multimodal fusion framework CoM replaces static fusion with adaptive topology switching and dual cognitive paths, addressing biases that hamper joint multimodal inference; achieves robust generalization in training-free or data-efficient settings.
AI.motivation: Unimodal baselines often outperform joint multimodal inference due to static fusion topologies that introduce positional bias and alignment traps, leading to fragile cross-modal performance.
AI.method: Introduce Chain of Modality (CoM), an agentic framework that adaptively chooses input topology (parallel, sequential, interleaved) and uses two task-aligned pathways: Direct-Decide for direct perception and Reason-Decide for analytical auditing; supports training-free or data-efficient SFT.
AI.result: CoM demonstrates robust and consistent generalization across diverse benchmarks, mitigating structural biases inherent to static fusion topologies.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, agentic modality orchestration with dual execution paths mitigates structural biases in omni-modal LLMs, enabling more reliable cross-modal reasoning without heavy supervision.

title: Geometric Routing Enables Causal Expert Control in Mixture of Experts
summary:
Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale parameters while fixing active computation per token, but the specialization of individual experts remains opaque. In a companion paper we showed that routing topology is quality-neutral: five structurally different configurations converge to statistically equivalent language modeling quality. Here we show that expert identity is nonetheless causally meaningful: individual rank-1 experts are monosemantic by construction, and cosine-similarity routing in a low-dimensional metric space makes their specialization directly inspectable.
  We present four lines of evidence. First, projecting expert output vectors through the unembedding matrix yields a Semantic Dictionary: 15% of experts are monosemantic specialists spanning 10 categories (temporal, geographic, cardinal, discourse, emotional, financial, military, scientific). Second, routing exhibits a frequency-to-syntax gradient: early layers separate tokens by word frequency, deeper layers by syntactic class (Zipf-confound controls, all $p < 0.001$). Third, causal interventions confirm these labels: steering toward a temporal expert's centroid increases P(temporal) by +321% (median across 44 prompts); suppressing a geographic expert drops P(geographic) by -23%; rewriting an expert's output vector halves target-category probability, and effects compose additively across layers. Fourth, the interventions are not unique to cosine routing: linear routers support comparable steering, but only cosine routing provides geometric transparency -- expert specialization is readable directly from the centroid matrix.
  MoE expert-level specialization is a first-class interpretability primitive: architecturally monosemantic, causally validated, and controllable at inference with zero overhead.

AI.tldr: Expert identity in sparse Mixture-of-Experts is interpretable despite neutral routing topology; rank-1 experts are monosemantic, and cosine routing provides inspectable geometric specialization.
AI.motivation: To move beyond topology-neutral results and determine whether individual MoE experts carry meaningful, inspectable semantics that enable controllable inference.
AI.method: Four lines of evidence: (1) project expert outputs through the unembedding matrix to build a Semantic Dictionary, revealing monosemantic specialists across 10 categories; (2) routing shows a frequency-to-syntax gradient across layers, with Zipf controls ensuring significance; (3) causal interventions steer toward/away from specific experts, yielding large changes in category probabilities and composable, additive effects; (4) cosine routing offers geometric transparency (centroid-based readout) and comparable results with linear routers.
AI.result: About 15% of experts are monosemantic spanning 10 categories (temporal, geographic, cardinal, discourse, emotional, financial, military, scientific, etc.). Early layers separate tokens by word frequency; deeper layers separate by syntactic class.
