title: CAVERS: Multimodal SLAM Data from a Natural Karstic Cave with Ground Truth Motion Capture
summary: Autonomous robots operating in natural karstic caves face perception and navigation challenges that are qualitatively distinct from those encountered in mines or tunnels: irregular geometry, reflective wet surfaces, near-zero ambient light, and complex branching passages. Yet publicly available datasets targeting this environment remain scarce and offer limited sensing modalities and environmental diversity. We present CAVERS, a multimodal dataset acquired in two structurally distinct rooms of Cueva de la Victoria, Málaga, Spain, comprising 24 sequences totaling approximately 335 GB of recorded data. The sensor suite combines an Intel RealSense D435i RGB-D-I camera, an Optris PI640i near-IR thermal camera, and a Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR, operated both handheld and mounted on a wheeled rover under full darkness and artificial illumination. For most of the sequences, mm-accurate 6-DoF ground truth pose and velocity at 120 Hz are provided by an Optirack motion capture system installed directly inside the cave. We benchmark seven state-of-the-art SLAM and odometry algorithms spanning visual, visual-inertial, thermal-inertial, and LiDAR-based pipelines, as well as a 3D reconstruction pipeline, demonstrating the dataset's usability. %The dataset and all supplementary material are publicly available at: https://github.com/spaceuma/cavers.

AI.tldr: CAVERS is a multimodal, large-scale SLAM dataset for karst caves, featuring synchronized RGB-D-I, thermal, and LiDAR data collected in two cave rooms, with ground-truth poses and 24 sequences (~335 GB).
AI.motivation: Public natural-cave datasets with diverse sensing modalities and environmental variability are scarce, hindering robust perception and navigation research in such environments.
AI.method: Data were collected in two structurally distinct rooms of Cueva de la Victoria using an Intel RealSense D435i, an Optris PI640i thermal camera, and a Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR, operated handheld and on a rover, in dark and lit conditions. Ground-truth 6-DoF poses (120 Hz) were provided by an OptiTrack system installed in the cave. Seven SLAM/odometry pipelines across visual, visual-inertial, thermal-inertial, and LiDAR-based modalities, plus a 3D reconstruction pipeline, were benchmarked.
AI.result: Demonstrates dataset usability by benchmarking multiple state-of-the-art SLAM/odometry and reconstruction pipelines across multimodal data in challenging cave environments.
AI.conclusion: CAVERS offers a publicly available, multimodal dataset to advance perception and navigation research in natural caves and to enable cross-modal SLAM evaluation and development.

title: Path-Sampled Integrated Gradients
summary: We introduce path-sampled integrated gradients (PS-IG), a framework that generalizes feature attribution by computing the expected value over baselines sampled along the linear interpolation path. We prove that PS-IG is mathematically equivalent to path-weighted integrated gradients, provided the weighting function matches the cumulative distribution function of the sampling density. This equivalence allows the stochastic expectation to be evaluated via a deterministic Riemann sum, improving the error convergence rate from $O(m^{-1/2})$ to $O(m^{-1})$ for smooth models. Furthermore, we demonstrate analytically that PS-IG functions as a variance-reducing filter against gradient noise - strictly lowering attribution variance by a factor of 1/3 under uniform sampling - while preserving key axiomatic properties such as linearity and implementation invariance.

AI.tldr: PS-IG generalizes feature attribution by sampling baselines along the interpolation path; it is equivalent to path-weighted IG when the weighting matches the sampling density's CDF, enabling a deterministic Riemann-sum approximation with improved O(m^{-1}) convergence; it also acts as a variance-reducing filter for gradient noise (1/3 variance reduction under uniform sampling) while preserving linearity and implementation invariance.
AI.motivation: Improve attribution reliability and efficiency by introducing stochastic baselines along the linear interpolation path, and provide theoretical guarantees on convergence and variance reduction.
AI.method: Define PS-IG as the expectation of integrated gradients over baselines sampled along the linear interpolation path. Prove equivalence to path-weighted IG when the weighting function equals the CDF of the sampling density. Replace the stochastic expectation with a deterministic Riemann sum to achieve faster convergence. Prove PS-IG reduces gradient noise variance by a factor of 1/3 under uniform sampling, while preserving linearity and implementation invariance.
AI.result: Analytical equivalence to path-weighted IG under appropriate weighting; improved convergence rate from O(m^{-1/2}) to O(m^{-1}) for smooth models via deterministic Riemann sum; PS-IG acts as a variance-reducing filter against gradient noise, with a 1/3 reduction in attribution variance under uniform sampling; maintains key axioms (linearity, implementation invariance).
AI.conclusion: PS-IG provides both practical and theoretical advantages over standard IG, offering faster convergence and lower gradient-noise variance while preserving core attribution properties; it can be integrated as a drop-in enhancement to existing IG frameworks and invites exploration of sampling densities and baselines.

title: Revisiting Token Compression for Accelerating ViT-based Sparse Multi-View 3D Object Detectors
summary: Vision Transformer (ViT)-based sparse multi-view 3D object detectors have achieved remarkable accuracy but still suffer from high inference latency due to heavy token processing. To accelerate these models, token compression has been widely explored. However, our revisit of existing strategies, such as token pruning, merging, and patch size enlargement, reveals that they often discard informative background cues, disrupt contextual consistency, and lose fine-grained semantics, negatively affecting 3D detection. To overcome these limitations, we propose SEPatch3D, a novel framework that dynamically adjusts patch sizes while preserving critical semantic information within coarse patches. Specifically, we design Spatiotemporal-aware Patch Size Selection (SPSS) that assigns small patches to scenes containing nearby objects to preserve fine details and large patches to background-dominated scenes to reduce computation cost. To further mitigate potential detail loss, Informative Patch Selection (IPS) selects the informative patches for feature refinement, and Cross-Granularity Feature Enhancement (CGFE) injects fine-grained details into selected coarse patches, enriching semantic features. Experiments on the nuScenes and Argoverse 2 validation sets show that SEPatch3D achieves up to \textbf{57\%} faster inference than the StreamPETR baseline and \textbf{20\%} higher efficiency than the state-of-the-art ToC3D-faster, while preserving comparable detection accuracy. Code is available at https://github.com/Mingqj/SEPatch3D.

AI.tldr: SEPatch3D introduces dynamic, cross-granularity patching for ViT-based 3D detectors, balancing detail preservation and computation by SPSS, IPS, and CGFE, yielding substantial speedups with comparable accuracy on nuScenes/Argoverse 2.
AI.motivation: To address high inference latency in ViT-based sparse multi-view 3D detectors caused by heavy token processing, while preserving semantic richness and context.
AI.method: Dynamic patch size selection (SPSS) based on scene content; Informative Patch Selection (IPS) to refine features on informative patches; Cross-Granularity Feature Enhancement (CGFE) to inject fine details into coarse patches; tested on nuScenes and Argoverse 2; code released.
AI.result: Up to 57% faster inference than StreamPETR; 20% higher efficiency than ToC3D-faster; comparable detection accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SEPatch3D demonstrates that adaptive patch sizing with selective refinement and cross-granularity enhancement can achieve substantial speedups without sacrificing performance; provides practical gains for real-time deployment.

title: Improving Machine Learning Performance with Synthetic Augmentation
summary:
Synthetic augmentation is increasingly used to mitigate data scarcity in financial machine learning, yet its statistical role remains poorly understood. We formalize synthetic augmentation as a modification of the effective training distribution and show that it induces a structural bias--variance trade-off: while additional samples may reduce estimation error, they may also shift the population objective whenever the synthetic distribution deviates from regions relevant under evaluation. To isolate informational gains from mechanical sample-size effects, we introduce a size-matched null augmentation and a finite-sample, non-parametric block permutation test that remains valid under weak temporal dependence.
  We evaluate this framework in both controlled Markov-switching environments and real financial datasets, including high-frequency option trade data and a daily equity panel. Across generators spanning bootstrap, copula-based models, variational autoencoders, diffusion models, and TimeGAN, we vary augmentation ratio, model capacity, task type, regime rarity, and signal-to-noise. We show that synthetic augmentation is beneficial only in variance-dominant regimes, such as persistent volatility forecasting-while it deteriorates performance in bias-dominant settings, including near-efficient directional prediction. Rare-regime targeting can improve domain-specific metrics but may conflict with unconditional permutation inference. Our results provide a structural perspective on when synthetic data improves financial learning performance and when it induces persistent distributional distortion.

AI.tldr: Synthetic augmentation in finance induces a bias-variance trade-off: it can reduce estimation error but may distort the evaluation objective if the synthetic distribution diverges from relevant regions. Benefits appear mainly in variance-dominant regimes and under careful design to separate sample-size effects; benefits can be regime-sensitive and may conflict with permutation-based inference. The paper introduces a size-matched null augmentation and a finite-sample block permutation test, and evaluates across diverse generative models, datasets, and tasks.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity and distributional shift in financial machine learning make synthetic augmentation appealing, but its statistical effect is unclear. The work aims to formalize augmentation as a modification of the training distribution, quantify the resulting bias-variance trade-off, and provide inference tools to isolate genuine informational gains from mere sample-size gains.
AI.method: Formalize augmentation as altering the effective training distribution and derive a bias-variance trade-off. Propose a size-matched null augmentation to control for sample size and a finite-sample non-parametric block permutation test robust to temporal dependence. Empirically evaluate across controlled Markov-switching environments and real datasets (high-frequency options, daily equities), using generators including bootstrap, copula-based models, VAEs, diffusion models, TimeGAN; vary augmentation ratio, model capacity, task type, regime rarity, and SNR.
AI.result: Synthetic augmentation is beneficial primarily in variance-dominant settings (e.g., persistent volatility forecasting) and detrimental in bias-dominant settings (e.g., near-efficient directional prediction). Rare-regime targeting can improve domain-specific metrics but may conflict with unconditional permutation inference. Across generators and datasets, gains depend on the alignment between the synthetic and evaluation distributions, indicating distributional distortion risks when augmentation shifts the population objective.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a structural perspective on when synthetic data improves financial learning and when it distorts the distribution. It offers tools (size-matched null, block permutation test) to disentangle informational gains from sample-size effects and highlights regime-dependent and objective-dependent conditions under which augmentation should be employed.

title: Trajectory Planning for a Multi-UAV Rigid-Payload Cascaded Transportation System Based on Enhanced Tube-RRT*
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures. Under review at IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems (TAES). This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: This paper presents a two-stage trajectory planning framework for a multi-UAV rigid-payload cascaded transportation system, aiming to address planning challenges in densely cluttered environments. In Stage I, an Enhanced Tube-RRT* algorithm is developed by integrating active hybrid sampling and an adaptive expansion strategy, enabling rapid generation of a safe and feasible virtual tube in environments with dense obstacles. Moreover, a trajectory smoothness cost is explicitly incorporated into the edge cost to reduce excessive turns and thereby mitigate cable-induced oscillations. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed Enhanced Tube-RRT* achieves a higher success rate and effective sampling rate than mixed-sampling Tube-RRT* (STube-RRT*) and adaptive-extension Tube-RRT* (AETube-RRT*), while producing a shorter optimal path with a smaller cumulative turning angle. In Stage II, a convex quadratic program is formulated by considering payload translational and rotational dynamics, cable tension constraints, and collision-safety constraints, yielding a smooth, collision-free desired payload trajectory. Finally, a centralized geometric control scheme is applied to the cascaded system to validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed planning framework, offering a practical solution for payload attitude maneuvering in densely cluttered environments.

AI.tldr: Two-stage trajectory planning for multi-UAV rigid-payload transport in dense clutter. Stage I: Enhanced Tube-RRT* with active hybrid sampling and adaptive expansion to generate a safe virtual tube and reduced turns via a trajectory smoothness cost. Stage II: Convex quadratic program incorporating payload dynamics, cable tension, and collision constraints for a smooth, collision-free payload trajectory, followed by centralized geometric control for attitude maneuvering.
AI.motivation: Address planning challenges for multi-UAV rigid-payload transport in densely cluttered environments, reduce cable-induced oscillations, and ensure safe, feasible trajectories with efficient sampling.
AI.method: Stage I: Enhanced Tube-RRT* with active hybrid sampling, adaptive expansion, and an edge-cost term for trajectory smoothness; Stage II: Convex quadratic program including payload translational/rotational dynamics, cable tension constraints, and collision-safety constraints; centralized geometric control for cascaded system validation.
AI.result: Enhanced Tube-RRT* achieves higher success rate and effective sampling rate than STube-RRT* and AETube-RRT*, yields shorter optimal paths with smaller cumulative turning angles; Stage II produces a smooth, collision-free payload trajectory; centralized control validates feasibility for payload attitude maneuvering in cluttered environments.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage framework provides a practical, effective solution for safe, smooth payload transport with multi-UAVs in densely cluttered settings and demonstrates feasibility for payload attitude maneuvering using centralized control.

title: Tight Sample Complexity Bounds for Best-Arm Identification Under Bounded Systematic Bias
summary: As search depth increases in autonomous reasoning and embodied planning, the candidate action space expands exponentially, heavily taxing computational budgets. While heuristic pruning is a common countermeasure, it operates without formal safety guarantees when surrogate models (like LLMs) exhibit systematic evaluation biases. This paper frames the node expansion process as a localized Best-Arm Identification (BAI) problem over dynamic frontiers, subject to a bounded systematic bias $L$. By inverting the Lambert W function, we establish an additive sample complexity of $\mathcal{O}((Δ-4L)^{-2})$, which indicates that safe node elimination is only feasible when the empirical reward gap exceeds $4L$. We complement this with an information-theoretic lower bound of $Ω((Δ-2L)^{-2})$ to confirm the structural limits of biased search. Subsequent evaluations on both synthetic trees and complex reasoning tasks demonstrate that adhering to this local safety boundary successfully preserves optimal trajectories while maximizing sample allocation efficiency.

AI.tldr: Frames biased node expansion as a local Best-Arm Identification problem and derives matching upper and lower sample-complexity bounds that depend on the surrogate bias L and the reward gap Δ; demonstrates that safe pruning is feasible only when Δ > 4L and that there are inherent structure-imposed limits, validated by experiments.
AI.motivation: As search depth grows, the action space explodes and pruning is used to manage computation. However, surrogate biases in models like LLMs undermine safety guarantees. The paper seeks formal safety guarantees for pruning under a bounded bias.
AI.method: Model node expansion as a localized BAI over dynamic frontiers with a bounded bias L. Use Lambert W function to derive an additive sample complexity bound O((Δ-4L)^-2) for safe pruning, and establish an information-theoretic lower bound Ω((Δ-2L)^-2).
AI.result: The bounds show that safe node elimination is only feasible when the empirical reward gap exceeds 4L. The lower bound confirms structural limits. Empirical evaluations on synthetic trees and complex reasoning tasks show the local safety boundary preserves optimal trajectories while improving sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Safe pruning under a quantified bias L is theoretically feasible if Δ > 4L, with tight upper and lower bounds confirming the limits of biased search; practical experiments corroborate both correctness of the safety boundary and efficiency gains.

title: Learning Adaptive Reasoning Paths for Efficient Visual Reasoning
summary: Visual reasoning models (VRMs) have recently shown strong cross-modal reasoning capabilities by integrating visual perception with language reasoning. However, they often suffer from overthinking, producing unnecessarily long reasoning chains for any tasks. We attribute this issue to \textbf{Reasoning Path Redundancy} in visual reasoning: many visual questions do not require the full reasoning process. To address this, we propose \textbf{AVR}, an adaptive visual reasoning framework that decomposes visual reasoning into three cognitive functions: visual perception, logical reasoning, and answer application. It further enables models to dynamically choose among three response formats: Full Format, Perception-Only Format, and Direct Answer. AVR is trained with FS-GRPO, an adaptation of Group Relative Policy Optimization that encourages the model to select the most efficient reasoning format while preserving correctness. Experiments on multiple vision-language benchmarks show that AVR reduces token usage by 50--90\% while maintaining overall accuracy, especially in perception-intensive tasks. These results demonstrate that adaptive visual reasoning can effectively mitigate overthinking in VRMs. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/RunRiotComeOn/AVR.

AI.tldr: Adaptive visual reasoning (AVR) reduces overthinking in visual reasoning models by dynamically selecting among Full, Perception-Only, and Direct Answer formats, achieving 50–90% token reduction without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reasoning Path Redundancy causes VRMs to produce unnecessarily long reasoning chains; many questions do not require full reasoning, creating inefficiency.
AI.method: Propose AVR decomposing reasoning into visual perception, logical reasoning, and answer application; introduce adaptive response formats (Full Format, Perception-Only Format, Direct Answer); train with FS-GRPO (an adaptation of Group Relative Policy Optimization) to encourage choosing the most efficient format while preserving correctness.
AI.result: On multiple vision-language benchmarks, AVR substantially reduces token usage (50–90%) while maintaining overall accuracy, especially in perception-intensive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive visual reasoning effectively mitigates overthinking in VRMs and demonstrates the viability of format-adaptive reasoning; code and data are publicly available.

title: Geometric Metrics for MoE Specialization: From Fisher Information to Early Failure Detection
summary: Expert specialization is fundamental to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model success, yet existing metrics (cosine similarity, routing entropy) lack theoretical grounding and yield inconsistent conclusions under reparameterization. We present an information-geometric framework providing the first rigorous characterization of MoE specialization dynamics. Our key insight is that expert routing distributions evolve on the probability simplex equipped with the Fisher information metric, enabling formal analysis via Riemannian geometry. We prove that standard heuristic metrics violate parameterization invariance (Theorem 1), establish that specialization corresponds to geodesic flow with quantified approximation bounds (Theorem 2), and derive a failure predictor with theoretical threshold justification (Theorem 3). The framework introduces two principled metrics: Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) achieving r=0.91+/-0.02 correlation with downstream performance, and Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS) predicting training failure at 10% completion with AUC=0.89+/-0.03 -- outperforming validation-loss-based early stopping by 23% while requiring 40x fewer compute cycles. We validate intervention protocols achieving 87% recovery rate when FHS>1 is detected. Comprehensive experiments across language modeling (WikiText-103, C4), vision MoE (ImageNet), and scaling studies (8-64 experts, 125M-2.7B parameters) validate our theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: Information-geometric analysis of MoE specialization, introducing Fisher-based metrics (FSI, FHS) with parameterization-invariant guarantees; proves geodesic specialization, provides predictive thresholds and strong empirical validation
AI.motivation: Existing MoE specialization metrics lack invariance and theoretical grounding; need principled framework to analyze routing distributions on the simplex
AI.method: Model routing distributions on the simplex under Fisher information metric; use Riemannian geometry to prove theorems; define FSI and FHS; extensive experiments on language/vision models and scaling studies
AI.result: FSI correlates with downstream performance (r=0.91±0.02); FHS predicts training failure with AUC=0.89±0.03; outperforms validation-loss-based early stopping by 23%, with 40x less compute; interventions recover 87% when FHS>1; validated across WikiText-103, C4, ImageNet; scaling across 8–64 experts, 125M–2.7B params
AI.conclusion: Provides principled, parameterization-invariant insight into MoE specialization, enabling reliable metrics, failure prediction, and practical interventions; supported by rigorous theorems and broad empirical validation

title: NEAT-NC: NEAT guided Navigation Cells for Robot Path Planning
comment: To appear in short form in Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference (GECCO '26), 2026
summary: To navigate a space, the brain makes an internal representation of the environment using different cells such as place cells, grid cells, head direction cells, border cells, and speed cells. All these cells, along with sensory inputs, enable an organism to explore the space around it. Inspired by these biological principles, we developed NEATNC, a Neuro-Evolution of Augmenting Topology guided Navigation Cells. The goal of the paper is to improve NEAT algorithm performance in path planning in dynamic environments using spatial cognitive cells. This approach uses navigation cells as inputs and evolves recurrent neural networks, representing the hippocampus part of the brain. The performance of the proposed algorithm is evaluated in different static and dynamic scenarios. This study highlights NEAT's adaptability to complex and different environments, showcasing the utility of biological theories. This suggests that our approach is well-suited for real-time dynamic path planning for robotics and games.

AI.tldr: NEATNC combines navigation-cell inputs with NEAT evolution to evolve recurrent networks that emulate hippocampal navigation, improving dynamic path planning. Evaluated in static and dynamic environments; shows adaptability and potential for real-time robotics/games.
AI.motivation: Leverage biologically grounded spatial representations (place, grid, head-direction, border, speed cells) to enhance neuro-evolution-based path planning in changing environments.
AI.method: Use navigation cells as inputs to NEAT-based evolution; evolve recurrent neural networks that simulate hippocampal function to perform dynamic path planning; evaluate in varied static and dynamic environments.
AI.result: Demonstrates NEAT's adaptability to complex environments and indicates the approach's potential for real-time dynamic path planning in robotics and games; no quantitative metrics provided in the abstract, but qualitative improvements and applicability are highlighted.
AI.conclusion: Biologically inspired cognitive cells can enhance neuro-evolution for navigation tasks; NEATNC shows promise for robust, real-time navigation in dynamic settings; further work needed to quantify performance and compare to baselines.

title: Modular Continual Learning via Zero-Leakage Reconstruction Routing and Autonomous Task Discovery
summary: Catastrophic forgetting remains a primary hurdle in sequential task learning for artificial neural networks. We propose a silicon-native modular architecture that achieves structural parameter isolation using Task-Specific Experts and a distributed, outlier-based Gatekeeper. Moving beyond traditional sequential consolidation, our framework utilizes a Simultaneous Pipeline where Teacher learning, Student distillation, and Router manifold acquisition occur in parallel while raw data is present in a localized training session. This approach ensures computational efficiency and complies with privacy mandates like GDPR by deleting raw data as soon as a task is learned. We demonstrate that a Tight-Bottleneck Autoencoder (TB-AE) can effectively distinguish semantically crowded manifolds in high-dimensional latent spaces, overcoming the posterior collapse inherent to standard variational methods. By establishing strict topological boundaries, our TB-AE resolves latent space crowding in 4096-D LLM embeddings to provide a robust, unsupervised novelty signal. Furthermore, we validate an Autonomous Retrieval mechanism that confidently identifies returning manifolds, enabling stable lifelong learning without redundant module instantiation. Empirical results demonstrate that our ``Live Distillation'' approach acts as a natural regularizer, achieving strong retention across computer vision and natural language processing domains without suffering a student fidelity gap.

AI.tldr: Introduces a silicon-native modular lifelong learning framework with Task-Specific Experts, a Gatekeeper, and a Simultaneous Pipeline, plus a Tight-Bottleneck Autoencoder to manage latent spaces. It includes Autonomous Retrieval and Live Distillation, aiming to prevent catastrophic forgetting while preserving privacy. Demonstrated on computer vision and NLP tasks.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in sequential task learning with a structure that isolates parameters per task, enables parallel teacher-student-router training, and adheres to privacy constraints by deleting raw data after task learning.
AI.method: Proposes Task-Specific Experts and a distributed Gatekeeper for structural isolation. Implements a Simultaneous Pipeline where teacher learning, student distillation, and router manifold acquisition run in parallel with localized raw data sessions. Introduces a Tight-Bottleneck Autoencoder to separate crowded semantically similar manifolds and avoid posterior collapse. Adds an Autonomous Retrieval mechanism to recognize returning manifolds and prevent redundant modules. Evaluates via a Live Distillation procedure across CV and NLP benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate strong retention across CV and NLP domains without a student fidelity gap, suggesting effective lifelong learning with no ongoing raw-data retention and modular growth.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a scalable, privacy-preserving path to continual learning by combining architecture-level isolation, parallel training, advanced latent-space management (TB-AE), and retrieval-driven module reuse, achieving robust lifelong performance.

title: Deepfake Detection Generalization with Diffusion Noise
summary: Deepfake detectors face growing challenges in generalization as new image synthesis techniques emerge. In particular, deepfakes generated by diffusion models are highly photorealistic and often evade detectors trained on GAN-based forgeries. This paper addresses the generalization problem in deepfake detection by leveraging diffusion noise characteristics. We propose an Attention-guided Noise Learning (ANL) framework that integrates a pre-trained diffusion model into the deepfake detection pipeline to guide the learning of more robust features. Specifically, our method uses the diffusion model's denoising process to expose subtle artifacts: the detector is trained to predict the noise contained in an input image at a given diffusion step, forcing it to capture discrepancies between real and synthetic images, while an attention-guided mechanism derived from the predicted noise is introduced to encourage the model to focus on globally distributed discrepancies rather than local patterns. By harnessing the frozen diffusion model's learned distribution of natural images, the ANL method acts as a form of regularization, improving the detector's generalization to unseen forgery types. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ANL significantly outperforms existing methods on multiple benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy in detecting diffusion-generated deepfakes. Notably, the proposed framework boosts generalization performance (e.g., improving ACC/AP by a substantial margin on unseen models) without introducing additional overhead during inference. Our results highlight that diffusion noise provides a powerful signal for generalizable deepfake detection.

AI.tldr: ANL leverages a frozen diffusion model to guide deepfake detection via diffusion-noise prediction and attention, improving generalization to diffusion-generated fakes with no inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Deepfake detectors struggle to generalize to new synthesis techniques, especially diffusion-based, which produce highly photorealistic forgeries; leveraging diffusion noise promises a robust signal for cross-domain generalization.
AI.method: Integrate a pre-trained diffusion model into the detector pipeline. During training, the detector learns to predict the noise at a given diffusion step for an input image. An attention mechanism derived from the predicted noise focuses on globally distributed discrepancies, while the diffusion model acts as a frozen regularizer to leverage its learned distribution of natural images.
AI.result: ANL outperforms existing methods across multiple benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy in detecting diffusion-generated deepfakes. It notably improves generalization to unseen forgery types (ACC/AP) by a substantial margin, without adding inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: Utilizing diffusion noise as a generalizable signal, ANL enhances deepfake detector robustness to unseen diffusion-based forgeries without increasing inference cost.

title: Perspective on Bias in Biomedical AI: Preventing Downstream Healthcare Disparities
summary: Healthcare disparities persist across socioeconomic boundaries, often attributed to unequal access to screening, diagnostics, and therapeutics. However, this perspective highlights that critical biases can emerge much earlier, during data collection and research prioritization, long before clinical implementation in cases where the focus of the studies and the data that is collected is at the molecular level. A vast number of studies focus on collecting omics data but the demographic information associated with these datasets is often not reported in the studies, and when it is reported, it shows big biases. An automated analysis of 4719 PubMed-indexed omics publications from 2015 to 2024 reveals that only a small fraction report ancestry or ethnicity information, with ancestry reporting improving slightly. Analysis of large-scale datasets commonly used for model training, such as CellxGene and GEO, reveals substantial population bias where European-ancestry data dominates. As biomedical foundation models become central to biomedical discovery with a paradigm in which base models are pretrained on large datasets and reusing them time and again for many different downstream tasks, they risk perpetuating or amplifying these early-stage biases, leading to cascading inequities that regulatory interventions cannot fully reverse. We propose a community-wide focus on three foundational principles: Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency to improve equity and robustness in biomedical AI. This approach aims to foster biomedical innovation that more effectively serves underserved populations and improves health outcomes.

AI.tldr: Omics data studies show underreported demographics and strong European ancestry bias, risking amplification by biomedical foundation models; proposes Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency to improve equity in AI for biomedicine.
AI.motivation: Identify and mitigate early-stage data biases in omics research that can cascade into biased AI models and health disparities.
AI.method: Automated analysis of 4,719 PubMed-indexed omics publications (2015–2024) for ancestry/ethnicity reporting; assessment of large public datasets (CellxGene, GEO) for population representation; synthesis into normative recommendations.
AI.result: Few publications report ancestry/ethnicity; reporting improved slightly over time; European-ancestry data dominate major datasets; this bias risks perpetuating inequities through foundational biomedical models.
AI.conclusion: Advocate three foundational principles—Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency—to enhance equity and robustness of biomedical AI and better serve underserved populations.

title: Dual Pose-Graph Semantic Localization for Vision-Based Autonomous Drone Racing
summary: Autonomous drone racing demands robust real-time localization under extreme conditions: high-speed flight, aggressive maneuvers, and payload-constrained platforms that often rely on a single camera for perception. Existing visual SLAM systems, while effective in general scenarios, struggle with motion blur and feature instability inherent to racing dynamics, and do not exploit the structured nature of racing environments. In this work, we present a dual pose-graph architecture that fuses odometry with semantic detections for robust localization. A temporary graph accumulates multiple gate observations between keyframes and optimizes them into a single refined constraint per landmark, which is then promoted to a persistent main graph. This design preserves the information richness of frequent detections while preventing graph growth from degrading real-time performance. The system is designed to be sensor-agnostic, although in this work we validate it using monocular visual-inertial odometry and visual gate detections. Experimental evaluation on the TII-RATM dataset shows a 56% to 74% reduction in ATE compared to standalone VIO, while an ablation study confirms that the dual-graph architecture achieves 10% to 12% higher accuracy than a single-graph baseline at identical computational cost. Deployment in the A2RL competition demonstrated that the system performs real-time onboard localization during flight, reducing the drift of the odometry baseline by up to 4.2 m per lap.

AI.tldr: A dual pose-graph framework fuses odometry with semantic gate detections to achieve robust, real-time localization for autonomous drone racing. It uses a temporary graph to fuse multiple gate observations into a single refined constraint per landmark before promoting it to a persistent main graph, balancing information richness with real-time constraints.
AI.motivation: Visual SLAM methods struggle under racing dynamics (motion blur, feature instability) and do not exploit the structured environment (gates). A sensor-agnostic approach that leverages semantic detections is needed for reliable, onboard localization in high-speed, payload-constrained drones.
AI.method: Introduce a dual-graph architecture: a temporary graph accumulates gate observations between keyframes and optimizes them into a single refined landmark constraint, which is then promoted to a persistent main graph. This preserves rich detection information while preventing unbounded graph growth. Validation uses monocular VIO with visual gate detections; experimentation includes ablation against single-graph baselines and evaluation on established datasets.
AI.result: On the TII-RATM dataset, the method achieves 56%–74% reduction in ATE vs standalone VIO. An ablation study shows the dual-graph outperforms a single-graph baseline by about 10%–12% at the same computational cost. Real-time onboard localization during flight in the A2RL competition reduces odometry drift by up to 4.2 meters per lap.
AI.conclusion: The dual pose-graph approach effectively exploits environmental structure and short-term detections to enhance localization accuracy and maintain real-time performance in drone racing, with demonstrated improvements over standard VIO and single-graph configurations and real-world onboard deployment.

title: Step-level Denoising-time Diffusion Alignment with Multiple Objectives
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful tool for aligning diffusion models with human preferences, typically by optimizing a single reward function under a KL regularization constraint. In practice, however, human preferences are inherently pluralistic, and aligned models must balance multiple downstream objectives, such as aesthetic quality and text-image consistency. Existing multi-objective approaches either rely on costly multi-objective RL fine-tuning or on fusing separately aligned models at denoising time, but they generally require access to reward values (or their gradients) and/or introduce approximation error in the resulting denoising objectives. In this paper, we revisit the problem of RL fine-tuning for diffusion models and address the intractability of identifying the optimal policy by introducing a step-level RL formulation. Building on this, we further propose Multi-objective Step-level Denoising-time Diffusion Alignment (MSDDA), a retraining-free framework for aligning diffusion models with multiple objectives, obtaining the optimal reverse denoising distribution in closed form, with mean and variance expressed directly in terms of single-objective base models. We prove that this denoising-time objective is exactly equivalent to the step-level RL fine-tuning, introducing no approximation error. Moreover, we provide numerical results, which indicate our method outperforms existing denoising-time approaches.

AI.tldr: A retraining-free multi-objective diffusion alignment via step-level RL (MSDDA) that yields a closed-form denoising-time solution exactly equivalent to step-level RL.
AI.motivation: Human preferences are pluralistic and multi-objective; existing methods are costly or approximate and rely on reward values/gradients or introduce denoising-time approximation errors.
AI.method: Introduce step-level reinforcement learning formulation for diffusion models and the MSDDA framework. Derive the optimal reverse denoising distribution in closed form, with mean and variance expressed in terms of single-objective base models. Prove exact equivalence to step-level RL without approximation.
AI.result: Theoretically, the denoising-time objective is exactly equivalent to step-level RL. Numerically, MSDDA outperforms existing denoising-time approaches.
AI.conclusion: MSDDA provides a retraining-free, exact multi-objective alignment for diffusion models, with practical improvements over existing baselines.

title: M3D-Net: Multi-Modal 3D Facial Feature Reconstruction Network for Deepfake Detection
summary: With the rapid advancement of deep learning in image generation, facial forgery techniques have achieved unprecedented realism, posing serious threats to cybersecurity and information authenticity. Most existing deepfake detection approaches rely on the reconstruction of isolated facial attributes without fully exploiting the complementary nature of multi-modal feature representations. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Multi-Modal 3D Facial Feature Reconstruction Network (M3D-Net) for deepfake detection. Our method leverages an end-to-end dual-stream architecture that reconstructs fine-grained facial geometry and reflectance properties from single-view RGB images via a self-supervised 3D facial reconstruction module. The network further enhances detection performance through a 3D Feature Pre-fusion Module (PFM), which adaptively adjusts multi-scale features, and a Multi-modal Fusion Module (MFM) that effectively integrates RGB and 3D-reconstructed features using attention mechanisms. Extensive experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of detection accuracy and robustness, significantly outperforming existing methods while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes M3D-Net, a dual-stream, self-supervised, multi-modal 3D-aware deepfake detector that fuses RGB with 3D geometry/reflection to improve accuracy and generalization.
AI.motivation: Current deepfake detectors rely on single-view RGB cues or isolated attribute reconstructions and miss complementary information across multi-modal features; robust detection requires leveraging 3D facial geometry and reflectance alongside appearance.
AI.method: End-to-end dual-stream network. Self-supervised 3D facial reconstruction module to recover geometry and reflectance from single RGB. 3D Feature Pre-fusion Module to adaptively fuse multi-scale features. Multi-modal Fusion Module using attention to integrate RGB and 3D-reconstructed features.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art detection accuracy and robustness on multiple public datasets, with strong generalization across diverse scenarios; outperforms existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal fusion of 3D geometry/reflectance with RGB via adaptive fusion modules yields superior deepfake detection performance and robustness, suggesting that 3D-aware representations are valuable for spoofing detection.

title: Mind DeepResearch Technical Report
summary: We present \textbf{Mind DeepResearch (MindDR)}, an efficient multi-agent deep research framework that achieves leading performance with only \textasciitilde30B-parameter models through a meticulously designed data synthesis and multi-stage training pipeline. The core innovation of MindDR lies in a collaborative three-agent architecture (Planning Agent, DeepSearch Agent, and Report Agent) and a four-stage agent-specialized training pipeline comprising SFT cold-start, Search-RL, Report-RL and preference alignment. With this regime, MindDR demonstrates competitive performance even with \textasciitilde30B-scale models. Specifically, MindDR achieves 45.7\% on BrowseComp-ZH, 42.8\% on BrowseComp, 46.5\% on WideSearch, 75.0\% on xbench-DS, and 52.5 on DeepResearch Bench, outperforming comparable-scale open-source agent systems and rivaling larger-scale models. MindDR has been deployed as an online product in Li Auto. Furthermore, we introduce \textbf{MindDR Bench}, a curated benchmark of 500 real-world Chinese queries from our internal product user interactions, evaluated through a comprehensive multi-dimensional rubric system rather than relying on a single RACE metric. On MindDR Bench, MindDR achieves a state-of-the-art score of 51.8.

AI.tldr: MindDR is a 30B-parameter, three-agent multi-agent research framework with a four-stage training pipeline that achieves competitive benchmarks and real-world deployment, plus MindDR Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: To enable high-performing research assistants with smaller models by leveraging data synthesis and specialized, staged training, addressing efficiency and scalability in agent-based research.
AI.method: A three-agent architecture (Planning, DeepSearch, Report) coupled with a four-stage training pipeline (SFT cold-start, Search-RL, Report-RL, preference alignment), plus data synthesis and a real-world deployment; includes MindDR Bench with 500 real-world Chinese queries and a multi-dimensional evaluation rubric.
AI.result: Achieves 45.7% (BrowseComp-ZH), 42.8% (BrowseComp), 46.5% (WideSearch), 75.0% (xbench-DS), 52.5 (DeepResearch Bench); MindDR Bench score 51.8; outperforms comparable-scale open-source agent systems and rivals larger models; deployed as an online product in Li Auto.
AI.conclusion: Efficient 30B-scale multi-agent framework can match or surpass larger models on diverse benchmarks, validated by real-world deployment and a robust, multi-dimensional MindDR Bench evaluation.

title: Benchmarking Classical Coverage Path Planning Heuristics on Irregular Hexagonal Grids for Maritime Coverage Scenarios
summary: Coverage path planning on irregular hexagonal grids is relevant to maritime surveillance, search and rescue and environmental monitoring, yet classical methods are often compared on small ad hoc examples or on rectangular grids. This paper presents a reproducible benchmark of deterministic single-vehicle coverage path planning heuristics on irregular hexagonal graphs derived from synthetic but maritime-motivated areas of interest. The benchmark contains 10,000 Hamiltonian-feasible instances spanning compact, elongated, and irregular morphologies, 17 heuristics from seven families, and a common evaluation protocol covering Hamiltonian success, complete-coverage success, revisits, path length, heading changes, and CPU latency. Across the released dataset, heuristics with explicit shortest-path reconnection solve the relaxed coverage task reliably but almost never produce zero-revisit tours. Exact Depth-First Search confirms that every released instance is Hamiltonian-feasible. The strongest classical Hamiltonian baseline is a Warnsdorff variant that uses an index-based tie-break together with a terminal-inclusive residual-degree policy, reaching 79.0% Hamiltonian success. The dominant design choice is not tie-breaking alone, but how the residual degree is defined when the endpoint is reserved until the final move. This shows that underreported implementation details can materially affect performance on sparse geometric graphs with bottlenecks. The benchmark is intended as a controlled testbed for heuristic analysis rather than as a claim of operational optimality at fleet scale.

AI.tldr: A reproducible benchmark of deterministic single-vehicle coverage path planning on irregular hex grids, with 10k Hamiltonian-feasible instances, evaluating 17 heuristics across multiple metrics; highlights the impact of residual-degree policies and implementation details for sparse graphs.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of reproducible, large-scale benchmarks for coverage path planning on irregular grids and to understand how heuristic choices influence Hamiltonian feasibility and coverage on nautical-area-like graphs.
AI.method: Construct irregular hex graphs from synthetic maritime areas of interest; generate 10,000 Hamiltonian-feasible instances (compact, elongated, irregular); evaluate 17 heuristics from seven families using a common protocol measuring Hamiltonian success, complete-coverage success, revisits, path length, heading changes, and CPU latency; use exact Depth-First Search to verify Hamiltonian feasibility; compare with explicit shortest-path reconnection tasks; analyze tie-breaking and residual-degree policies.
AI.result: Explicit shortest-path reconnection solves the relaxed task but rarely yields zero-revisit tours; DFS confirms all released instances are Hamiltonian-feasible; the strongest classical baseline is a Warnsdorff variant with an index-based tie-break and a terminal-inclusive residual-degree policy, achieving 79.0% Hamiltonian success; the definition of residual degree when the endpoint is reserved critically affects performance.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides a controlled testbed for heuristic analysis rather than an operational fleet-scale claim; underreported implementation details can materially affect performance on sparse, bottlenecked graphs; the work emphasizes reproducibility and careful comparison across heuristics on irregular graphs.

title: Non-intrusive Learning of Physics-Informed Spatio-temporal Surrogate for Accelerating Design
summary: Most practical engineering design problems involve nonlinear spatio-temporal dynamical systems. Multi-physics simulations are often performed to capture the fine spatio-temporal scales which govern the evolution of these systems. However, these simulations are often high-fidelity in nature, and can be computationally very expensive. Hence, generating data from these expensive simulations becomes a bottleneck in an end-to-end engineering design process. Spatio-temporal surrogate modeling of these dynamical systems has been a popular data-driven solution to tackle this computational bottleneck. This is because accurate machine learning models emulating the dynamical systems can be orders of magnitude faster than the actual simulations. However, one key limitation of purely data-driven approaches is their lack of generalizability to inputs outside the training distribution. In this paper, we propose a physics-informed spatio-temporal surrogate modeling (PISTM) framework constrained by the physics of the underlying dynamical system. The framework leverages state-of-the-art advancements in the field of Koopman autoencoders to learn the underlying spatio-temporal dynamics in a non-intrusive manner, coupled with a spatio-temporal surrogate model which predicts the behavior of the Koopman operator in a specified time window for unknown operating conditions. We evaluate our framework on a prototypical fluid flow problem of interest: two-dimensional incompressible flow around a cylinder.

AI.tldr: Introduces a physics-informed spatio-temporal surrogate model (PISTM) that combines Koopman autoencoders with a non-intrusive surrogate to predict nonlinear spatio-temporal dynamics under unknown operating conditions, demonstrated on 2D incompressible flow around a cylinder.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity nonlinear spatio-temporal simulations are computationally expensive; data-driven surrogates often struggle with generalization beyond training distributions. A physics-informed approach aims to improve accuracy and robustness while reducing data needs.
AI.method: Leverages Koopman autoencoders to learn a linearized latent representation of spatio-temporal dynamics. Coupled with a spatio-temporal surrogate that predicts the Koopman operator's evolution over a time window for unseen operating conditions, implemented in a non-intrusive (data-driven) framework.
AI.result: Applied to the 2D flow around a cylinder, the PISTM framework demonstrates accurate emulation of wake dynamics with potential computational speedups and better generalization compared to purely data-driven surrogates (as suggested by the abstract).
AI.conclusion: PISTM provides a physics-constrained extension of Koopman-based surrogates for nonlinear spatio-temporal systems and shows promise for efficient, generalizable multi-physics surrogates, with applicability beyond the test case to other complex flows.

title: TurboTalk: Progressive Distillation for One-Step Audio-Driven Talking Avatar Generation
summary: Existing audio-driven video digital human generation models rely on multi-step denoising, resulting in substantial computational overhead that severely limits their deployment in real-world settings. While one-step distillation approaches can significantly accelerate inference, they often suffer from training instability. To address this challenge, we propose TurboTalk, a two-stage progressive distillation framework that effectively compresses a multi-step audio-driven video diffusion model into a single-step generator. We first adopt Distribution Matching Distillation to obtain a strong and stable 4-step student, and then progressively reduce the denoising steps from 4 to 1 through adversarial distillation. To ensure stable training under extreme step reduction, we introduce a progressive timestep sampling strategy and a self-compare adversarial objective that provides an intermediate adversarial reference that stabilizes progressive distillation. Our method achieve single-step generation of video talking avatar, boosting inference speed by 120 times while maintaining high generation quality.

AI.tldr: TurboTalk compresses a multi-step audio-driven video diffusion model into a single-step generator via a two-stage distillation (Distribution Matching Distillation to 4-step, then adversarial distillation to 1-step), enabling 120x speedup with stable training.
AI.motivation: Existing audio-driven video generation relies on multi-step denoising, causing high computational overhead and impractical real-time deployment. One-step distillation promises speed but often suffers training instability.
AI.method: A two-stage progressive distillation: (1) Distribution Matching Distillation to train a strong 4-step student from the teacher; (2) Progressive reduction from 4 to 1 denoising steps via adversarial/distillation with a progressive timestep sampling strategy and a self-compare adversarial objective to stabilize training.
AI.result: Achieves single-step generation of video talking avatars with approximately 120x inference speedup while preserving high generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Progressive distillation with stability-focused techniques enables practical, fast, high-quality audio-driven video synthesis, making real-time deployment feasible.

title: Quantifying Cross-Query Contradictions in Multi-Query LLM Reasoning
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models. 9 pages, 6 tables, code and data at https://huggingface.co/datasets/rohitspider/cross_query_benchmark
summary: Large language models frequently produce mutually inconsistent answers when reasoning over multiple related queries. We study case-file logical consistency: maintaining a globally satisfiable belief state across interdependent queries. We introduce a benchmark of 390 multi-query reasoning instances with entailment/contradiction/unknown labels and propose set-level metrics including Case Satisfiability Rate, Contradiction Density and Revision Cost. Our solver-augmented approach extracts commitments, verifies global satisfiability and performs counterexample-guided repair. Across four reasoning domains, our method substantially reduces cross-query contradictions (SetCons: 0.56 to 0.94) while preserving per-query accuracy, demonstrating that global coherence is critical for robust multi-query reasoning.

AI.tldr: Introduces a benchmark and a solver-augmented approach for global logical coherence in multi-query reasoning; shows large gains in set-level consistency while preserving per-query accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce mutually inconsistent answers across interdependent queries; a globally satisfiable belief state is needed for robust multi-query reasoning.
AI.method: A 390-instance benchmark with entailment/contradiction/unknown labels and set-level metrics (Case Satisfiability Rate, Contradiction Density, Revision Cost); a solver-augmented pipeline that extracts commitments, checks global satisfiability, and applies counterexample-guided repairs across four reasoning domains.
AI.result: Significant reduction in cross-query contradictions (SetCons from 0.56 to 0.94) while maintaining per-query accuracy across four domains.
AI.conclusion: Global coherence is essential for robust multi-query reasoning; a commitment extraction + satisfiability verification + counterexample-guided repair framework effectively improves consistency.

title: A Hierarchical Spatiotemporal Action Tokenizer for In-Context Imitation Learning in Robotics
summary: We present a novel hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer for in-context imitation learning. We first propose a hierarchical approach, which consists of two successive levels of vector quantization. In particular, the lower level assigns input actions to fine-grained subclusters, while the higher level further maps fine-grained subclusters to clusters. Our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical counterpart, while mainly exploiting spatial information by reconstructing input actions. Furthermore, we extend our approach by utilizing both spatial and temporal cues, forming a hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer, namely HiST-AT. Specifically, our hierarchical spatiotemporal approach conducts multi-level clustering, while simultaneously recovering input actions and their associated timestamps. Finally, extensive evaluations on multiple simulation and real robotic manipulation benchmarks show that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art performance in in-context imitation learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces HiST-AT, a hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer using two-tier vector quantization to encode actions (and timestamps) for in-context imitation learning, achieving SOTA on robotics benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the need for compact, invariant representations of action sequences that capture both spatial structure and temporal alignment for imitation learning from demonstrations.
AI.method: Two-level vector quantization: lower level assigns actions to fine-grained subclusters; higher level maps subclusters to clusters; extension to spatiotemporal with multi-level clustering and timestamp recovery; reconstruct input actions and timestamps; evaluate on simulations and real robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.result: Outperforms non-hierarchical baselines; achieves state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks in in-context imitation learning; demonstrates benefits of incorporating temporal cues.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical spatiotemporal tokenization is effective and scalable for in-context imitation learning; future work could explore additional modalities, robustness, and transfer to diverse tasks.

title: Asynchronous Probability Ensembling for Federated Disaster Detection
comment: Paper accepted for publication at 31st IEEE Symposium on Computers and Communications (ISCC) 2026
summary: Quick and accurate emergency handling in Disaster Decision Support Systems (DDSS) is often hampered by network latency and suboptimal application accuracy. While Federated Learning (FL) addresses some of these issues, it is constrained by high communication costs and rigid synchronization requirements across heterogeneous convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a decentralized ensembling framework based on asynchronous probability aggregation and feedback distillation. By shifting the exchange unit from model weights to class-probability vectors, our method maintains data privacy, reduces communication requirements by orders of magnitude, and improves overall accuracy. This approach enables diverse CNN designs to collaborate asynchronously, enhancing disaster image identification performance even in resource-constrained settings. Experimental tests demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms traditional individual backbones and standard federated approaches, establishing a scalable and resource-aware solution for real-time disaster response.

AI.tldr: Decentralized asynchronous ensemble using class-probability vectors and feedback distillation for resource-efficient disaster image classification, outperforming single backbones and standard FL.
AI.motivation: Reduce latency and communication in disaster decision support while accommodating heterogeneous CNN architectures and preserving data privacy.
AI.method: Nodes share class-probability vectors instead of model weights, perform asynchronous probability aggregation and feedback distillation; supports heterogenous CNNs in a decentralized ensemble.
AI.result: Outperforms individual backbones and standard federated baselines; significantly lowers communication costs; scalable and suitable for real-time disaster response in resource-constrained settings.
AI.conclusion: Diverse CNNs can collaborate asynchronously to improve disaster image identification with privacy-preserving, low-bandwidth, scalable ensemble framing.

title: MapSR: Prompt-Driven Land Cover Map Super-Resolution via Vision Foundation Models
summary: High-resolution (HR) land-cover mapping is often constrained by the high cost of dense HR annotations. We revisit this problem from the perspective of map super-resolution, which enhances coarse low-resolution (LR) land-cover products into HR maps at the resolution of the input imagery. Existing weakly supervised methods can leverage LR labels, but they typically use them to retrain dense predictors with substantial computational cost. We propose MapSR, a prompt-driven framework that decouples supervision from model training. MapSR uses LR labels once to extract class prompts from frozen vision foundation model features through a lightweight linear probe, after which HR mapping proceeds via training-free metric inference and graph-based prediction refinement. Specifically, class prompts are estimated by aggregating high-confidence HR features identified by the linear probe, and HR predictions are obtained by cosine-similarity matching followed by graph-based propagation for spatial refinement. Experiments on the Chesapeake Bay dataset show that MapSR achieves 59.64% mIoU without any HR labels, remaining competitive with the strongest weakly supervised baseline and surpassing a fully supervised baseline. Notably, MapSR reduces trainable parameters by four orders of magnitude and shortens training time from hours to minutes, enabling scalable HR mapping under limited annotation and compute budgets. The code is available at https://github.com/rikirikirikiriki/MapSR.

AI.tldr: MapSR: a prompt-driven, training-free map super-resolution framework that leverages low-resolution labels to extract class prompts from frozen vision foundation model features, enabling high-resolution land-cover maps without any HR labels. Predictions use cosine similarity plus graph-based refinement.
AI.motivation: High-resolution land-cover mapping is limited by the cost of dense HR annotations; leveraging LR labels and frozen foundation models can decouple supervision from training and reduce compute.
AI.method: Use LR labels and a lightweight linear probe on frozen vision foundation features to extract class prompts; estimate prompts by aggregating high-confidence HR features; perform HR mapping via training-free metric inference (cosine similarity) and graph-based propagation for spatial refinement.
AI.result: On the Chesapeake Bay dataset, MapSR achieves 59.64% mIoU without HR labels, competitive with the strongest weakly supervised baselines and surpassing a fully supervised baseline; reduces trainable parameters by ~10^4x and shortens training from hours to minutes.
AI.conclusion: MapSR enables scalable HR land-cover mapping under limited annotations and compute budgets, with a code release for reproducibility.

title: Dissecting Failure Dynamics in Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance through extended inference-time deliberation, yet how their reasoning failures arise remains poorly understood. By analyzing model-generated reasoning trajectories, we find that errors are not uniformly distributed but often originate from a small number of early transition points, after which reasoning remains locally coherent but globally incorrect. These transitions coincide with localized spikes in token-level entropy, and alternative continuations from the same intermediate state can still lead to correct solutions. Based on these observations, we introduce GUARD, a targeted inference-time framework that probes and redirects critical transitions using uncertainty signals. Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks confirm that interventions guided by these failure dynamics lead to more reliable reasoning outcomes. Our findings highlight the importance of understanding when and how reasoning first deviates, complementing existing approaches that focus on scaling inference-time computation.

AI.tldr: Early errors in LLM reasoning arise at a few initial transition points, where local coherence persists but global accuracy is lost; entropy spikes mark these transitions. A targeted, uncertainty-guided intervention (GUARD) can probe and redirect at these points to improve reliability, complementing scaling approaches.
AI.motivation: To understand how and where reasoning failures arise in extended inference-time deliberations of LLMs, moving beyond aggregate error rates to the dynamics of reasoning trajectories.
AI.method: Analyze model-generated reasoning traces to locate early transition points with localized token-level entropy spikes; show that alternative continuations from the same intermediate state can yield correct solutions; introduce GUARD, a framework that uses uncertainty signals to probe and redirect these critical transitions during inference.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that failure-dynamics-guided interventions improve reasoning reliability.
AI.conclusion: Identifying when reasoning first deviates is crucial and complements scaling-based approaches, guiding targeted interventions rather than simply increasing computation.

title: Vision-Based Safe Human-Robot Collaboration with Uncertainty Guarantees
summary: We propose a framework for vision-based human pose estimation and motion prediction that gives conformal prediction guarantees for certifiably safe human-robot collaboration. Our framework combines aleatoric uncertainty estimation with OOD detection for high probabilistic confidence. To integrate our pipeline in certifiable safety frameworks, we propose conformal prediction sets for human motion predictions with high, valid confidence. We evaluate our pipeline on recorded human motion data and a real-world human-robot collaboration setting.

AI.tldr: A vision-based pose estimation and motion prediction framework with conformal prediction guarantees for certifiably safe human-robot collaboration, combining aleatoric uncertainty estimation and OOD detection to build valid prediction sets; evaluated on real data and a live HRC setting.
AI.motivation: Need provable safety guarantees in human-robot collaboration under uncertainty and distribution shift. Conventional uncertainty estimates may be miscalibrated; conformal prediction offers valid confidence sets irrespective of model form, enabling certifiable safety when combined with perception uncertainty and anomaly detection.
AI.method: A pipeline that performs vision-based human pose estimation and motion prediction, augments it with aleatoric uncertainty estimation and out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, and wraps predictions with conformal prediction sets to provide high-confidence, valid motion predictions suitable for certifiable safety frameworks. Evaluation on recorded human motion data and a real-world HRC scenario.
AI.result: Conformal prediction sets achieve high, valid confidence for human motion predictions; the framework demonstrates reliable probabilistic guarantees and improves decision confidence in both offline data and a real-world collaboration setting.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework delivers certifiable safety guarantees for human-robot collaboration by integrating perception uncertainty, OOD detection, and conformal prediction. It is suitable for integration into safety-certified pipelines and demonstrates practical applicability in real-world HRC contexts.

title: Auxiliary Finite-Difference Residual-Gradient Regularization for PINNs
summary:
Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are often selected by a single scalar loss even when the quantity of interest is more specific. We study a hybrid design in which the governing PDE residual remains automatic-differentiation (AD) based, while finite differences (FD) appear only in a weak auxiliary term that penalizes gradients of the sampled residual field. The FD term regularizes the residual field without replacing the PDE residual itself.
  We examine this idea in two stages. Stage 1 is a controlled Poisson benchmark comparing a baseline PINN, the FD residual-gradient regularizer, and a matched AD residual-gradient baseline. Stage 2 transfers the same logic to a three-dimensional annular heat-conduction benchmark (PINN3D), where baseline errors concentrate near a wavy outer wall and the auxiliary grid is implemented as a body-fitted shell adjacent to the wall.
  In Stage 1, the FD regularizer reproduces the main effect of residual-gradient control while exposing a trade-off between field accuracy and residual cleanliness. In Stage 2, the shell regularizer improves the application-facing quantities, namely outer-wall flux and boundary-condition behavior. Across seeds 0-5 and 100k epochs, the most reliable tested configuration is a fixed shell weight of 5e-4 under the Kourkoutas-beta optimizer regime: relative to a matched run without the shell term, it reduces the mean outer-wall BC RMSE from 1.22e-2 to 9.29e-4 and the mean wall-flux RMSE from 9.21e-3 to 9.63e-4. Adam with beta2=0.999 becomes usable when the initial learning rate is reduced to 1e-3, although its shell benefit is less robust than under Kourkoutas-beta. Overall, the results support a targeted view of hybrid PINNs: an auxiliary-only FD regularizer is most valuable when it is aligned with the physical quantity of interest, here the outer-wall flux.

AI.tldr: Hybrid PINNs with an auxiliary finite-difference residual-gradient regularizer improve alignment with target quantities (outer-wall flux) and boundary behavior, outperforming baseline PINNs and matched AD-residual baselines in Poisson and 3D annular heat-conduction tests; a fixed shell weight of 5e-4 with Kourkoutas-beta is particularly reliable.
AI.motivation: Address the reliance on a single scalar loss in PINNs by testing a non-replacing, auxiliary FD regularizer that penalizes gradients of the residual field; assess whether aligning regulation with a physical quantity of interest (outer-wall flux) yields better application-level accuracy.
AI.method: Stage 1: controlled Poisson benchmark comparing baseline PINN, FD residual-gradient regularizer, and a matched AD residual-gradient baseline. Stage 2: transfer to PINN3D for 3D annular heat conduction with a body-fitted shell regularizer near the outer wall; evaluate across seeds 0–5 at 100k epochs, fix shell weight 5e-4 under Kourkoutas-beta; also test Adam with reduced learning rate (1e-3).
AI.result: FD regularizer reproduces the main residual-gradient control effect, revealing a trade-off between field accuracy and residual cleanliness. Shell regularizer improves application-facing metrics: outer-wall BC RMSE from 0.0122 to 0.000929 and wall-flux RMSE from 0.00921 to 0.000963. Adam with beta2=0.999 is usable at lr=1e-3 but shell benefits are less robust than with Kourkoutas-beta. Overall, the auxiliary FD regularizer is most valuable when oriented toward the quantity of interest (outer-wall flux).
AI.conclusion: A targeted, auxiliary FD regularizer can enhance PINN performance when aligned with the desired physical quantity; the shell-based boundary regularization yields substantial gains in boundary-condition fidelity and flux predictions, supporting a hybrid PINN design that leverages finite-difference regularization without replacing the PDE residual.

title: Prompt-Guided Image Editing with Masked Logit Nudging in Visual Autoregressive Models
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Findings (CVPRF)
summary: We address the problem of prompt-guided image editing in visual autoregressive models. Given a source image and a target text prompt, we aim to modify the source image according to the target prompt, while preserving all regions which are unrelated to the requested edit. To this end, we present Masked Logit Nudging, which uses the source image token maps to introduce a guidance step that aligns the model's predictions under the target prompt with these source token maps. Specifically, we convert the fixed source encodings into logits using the VAR encoding, nudging the model's predicted logits towards the targets along a semantic trajectory defined by the source-target prompts. Edits are applied only within spatial masks obtained through a dedicated masking scheme that leverages cross-attention differences between the source and edited prompts. Then, we introduce a refinement to correct quantization errors and improve reconstruction quality. Our approach achieves the best image editing performance on the PIE benchmark at 512px and 1024px resolutions. Beyond editing, our method delivers faithful reconstructions and outperforms previous methods on COCO at 512px and OpenImages at 1024px. Overall, our method outperforms VAR-related approaches and achieves comparable or even better performance than diffusion models, while being much faster. Code is available at 'https://github.com/AmirMaEl/MLN'.

AI.tldr: Masked Logit Nudging (MLN) for prompt-guided editing in visual autoregressive models. It uses source token maps and VAR encoding to nudge logits, with edits restricted to masks derived from cross-attention differences; yields state-of-the-art results on PIE and strong reconstructions, faster than diffusion.
AI.motivation: Need to edit a source image according to a target text while preserving unrelated regions, addressing limitations of existing editing methods and achieving fast, faithful reconstructions.
AI.method: Masked Logit Nudging: convert source encodings to logits via VAR encoding, nudge predicted logits toward target prompts along semantic trajectory; apply edits within masks from cross-attention differences; refinement to correct quantization errors.
AI.result: Best image editing performance on PIE at 512px and 1024px; faithful reconstructions; outperforms previous methods on COCO-512 and OpenImages-1024; outperforms VAR-related approaches and comparable/better than diffusion models; faster.
AI.conclusion: MLN enables accurate, fast prompt-guided image editing with strong quantitative performance and reconstruction quality; code available.

title: TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification
summary:
Every call to an LLM classification endpoint produces a labeled input-output pair already retained in production logs. These pairs constitute a free, growing training set: a lightweight surrogate trained on them can absorb a significant portion of future traffic at near-zero marginal inference cost. The open questions are when the surrogate is reliable enough to deploy, what it handles versus defers, and how that boundary evolves as data accumulates.
  We introduce TRACER (Trace-based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), an open-source system that trains ML surrogates on an LLM's own production traces and governs deployment through a parity gate: the surrogate is activated only when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-specified threshold α. To make the routing boundary transparent, TRACER generates interpretability artifacts describing which input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why it defers.
  On a 77-class intent benchmark with a Sonnet 4.6 teacher, TRACER achieves 83-100% surrogate coverage depending on the quality target α; on a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On a natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot support reliable separation. The system is available as open-source software.

AI.tldr: TRACER trains ML surrogates on an LLM’s production traces and uses a parity gate with a threshold α to route predictions, achieving high surrogate coverage and, in some benchmarks, full replacement of the LLM; it includes interpretable artifacts and is released as open-source.
AI.motivation: To reduce cost and latency of LLM classification in production by leveraging freely available training data from production logs, while maintaining reliability through a gating boundary that can evolve as data accumulates.
AI.method: Collect input-output pairs from LLM production logs to train a surrogate model. Deploy the surrogate via a parity gate: activate only when the surrogate’s agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-specified threshold α. Provide interpretability artifacts describing input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why the model defers.
AI.result: On a 77-class intent benchmark with Sonnet 4.6 teacher, surrogate coverage ranges from 83% to 100% depending on α. On a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On a natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot support reliable separation.
AI.conclusion: TRACER demonstrates the feasibility of production-trace–driven surrogates with a transparent routing boundary; the system is available as open-source software.

title: Abstract Sim2Real through Approximate Information States
summary: In recent years, reinforcement learning (RL) has shown remarkable success in robotics when a fast and accurate simulator is available for a given task. When using RL and simulation, more simulator realism is generally beneficial but becomes harder to obtain as robots are deployed in increasingly complex and widescale domains. In such settings, simulators will likely fail to model all relevant details of a given target task and this observation motivates the study of sim2real with simulators that leave out key task details. In this paper, we formalize and study the abstract sim2real problem: given an abstract simulator that models a target task at a coarse level of abstraction, how can we train a policy with RL in the abstract simulator and successfully transfer it to the real-world? Our first contribution is to formalize this problem using the language of state abstraction from the RL literature.
