AI.motivation: Visual prompts can outperform text prompts for rare categories, but their effectiveness is limited by a lack of global discriminability. Prior work underutilizes visual prompts, treating them as byproducts of text-prompt training, which hinders progress. The study aims to explicitly enhance discriminability and robustness of visual prompts.
AI.method: Builds on image-text contrastive learning and DETR-based detection. Introduces: (1) global prompt integration to capture image-wide context; (2) visual-textual prompt relation distillation to align and enrich prompt semantics; (3) selective fusion to stabilize detection. The approach yields class-distinguishable visual prompts and robust open-vocabulary detection.
AI.result: DETR-ViP achieves substantially higher performance in visual prompt detection than state-of-the-art counterparts across COCO, LVIS, ODinW, and Roboflow100. Comprehensive ablations validate the effectiveness of the proposed components and provide insights into why discriminative visual prompts improve open-vocabulary detection.
AI.conclusion: Learning class-distinguishable visual prompts via global integration and cross-modal distillation enables robust open-vocabulary detection. DETR-ViP demonstrates the potential of well-regularized visual prompts and offers clear guidance on components that drive performance improvements.

title: Targeted Exploration via Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted for publication in Findings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have improved the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs). However, the widely used Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) consistently suffers from entropy collapse, causing the policy to converge prematurely and lose diversity. Existing exploration methods introduce additional bias or variance during exploration, making it difficult to maintain optimization stability. We propose Unified Entropy Control for Reinforcement Learning (UEC-RL), a framework that provides targeted mechanisms for exploration and stabilization. UEC-RL activates more exploration on difficult prompts to search for potential and valuable reasoning trajectories. In parallel, a stabilizer prevents entropy from growing uncontrollably, thereby keeping training stable as the model consolidates reliable behaviors. Together, these components expand the search space when needed while maintaining robust optimization throughout training. Experiments on both LLM and VLM reasoning tasks show consistent gains over RL baselines on both Pass@1 and Pass@$k$. On Geometry3K, UEC-RL achieves a 37.9\% relative improvement over GRPO, indicating that it sustains effective exploration without compromising convergence and underscoring UEC-RL as a key for scaling RL-based reasoning in large models. Our code is available at https://github.com/597358816/UEC-RL.

AI.tldr: UEC-RL introduces a unified entropy-control framework with exploration and stabilization to address entropy collapse in GRPO, yielding consistent gains on LLM/VLM reasoning tasks and a 37.9% relative improvement on Geometry3K over GRPO; code available.
AI.motivation: Entropy collapse and premature convergence in Group Relative Policy Optimization hinder exploration; existing exploration methods introduce bias/variance and destabilize optimization; a unified mechanism is needed to maintain diverse yet stable exploration.
AI.method: Two components: enhanced exploration on hard prompts to expand search space; a stabilizer to bound entropy growth during training, maintaining stable optimization while consolidating reliable behaviors.
AI.result: Empirical gains across LLM and VLM reasoning tasks in Pass@1 and Pass@$k; Geometry3K shows ~37.9% relative improvement vs GRPO; results suggest better exploration without sacrificing convergence.
AI.conclusion: UEC-RL offers scalable, robust RL-based reasoning for large models; the approach balances exploration and stability, promising for improved reasoning with LLMs/VLMs; code released.

title: Generative Augmented Inference
summary: Data-driven operations management often relies on parameters estimated from costly human-generated labels. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and other AI systems offer inexpensive auxiliary data, but introduce a new challenge: AI outputs are not direct observations of the target outcomes, but could involve high-dimensional representations with complex and unknown relationships to human labels. Conventional methods leverage AI predictions as direct proxies for true labels, which can be inefficient or unreliable when this relationship is weak or misspecified. We propose Generative Augmented Inference (GAI), a general framework that incorporates AI-generated outputs as informative features for estimating models of human-labeled outcomes. GAI uses an orthogonal moment construction that enables consistent estimation and valid inference with flexible, nonparametric relationship between LLM-generated outputs and human labels. We establish asymptotic normality and show a "safe default" property: relative to human-data-only estimators, GAI weakly improves estimation efficiency under arbitrary auxiliary signals and yields strict gains whenever the auxiliary information is predictive. Empirically, GAI outperforms benchmarks across diverse settings. In conjoint analysis with weak auxiliary signals, GAI reduces estimation error by about 50% and lowers human labeling requirements by over 75%. In retail pricing, where all methods access the same auxiliary inputs, GAI consistently outperforms alternative estimators, highlighting the value of its construction rather than differences in information. In health insurance choice, it cuts labeling requirements by over 90% while maintaining decision accuracy. Across applications, GAI improves confidence interval coverage without inflating width. Overall, GAI provides a principled and scalable approach to integrating AI-generated information.

AI.tldr: GAI uses orthogonal moments to integrate AI-generated outputs as informative features for estimating human-labeled outcomes, enabling consistent, asymptotically normal inference with nonparametric AI-human relationships and yielding efficiency gains and reduced labeling needs.
AI.motivation: AI-generated outputs are inexpensive but are not direct proxies for human labels; when the relationship between AI outputs and human labels is weak or misspecified, traditional proxy-based methods falter. A framework is needed to leverage AI signals while preserving valid inference.
AI.method: Generative Augmented Inference (GAI) with an orthogonal moment construction that accommodates flexible, nonparametric links between AI outputs and human labels. The framework provides consistent estimation, valid inference (asymptotic normality), and a safe-default property—improving efficiency relative to human-data-only estimators, with larger gains when auxiliary AI signals are predictive.
AI.result: Empirically, GAI outperforms benchmarks: in conjoint analysis with weak auxiliary signals, ~50% reduction in estimation error and >75% fewer labeled instances; in retail pricing, performance surpasses alternatives when all methods access the same AI inputs; in health insurance choice, labeling requirements reduced by >90% with maintained decision accuracy. Across applications, GAI also improves confidence interval coverage without inflating width.
AI.conclusion: GAI provides a principled, scalable approach to integrating AI-generated information for estimating human-labeled outcomes, delivering efficiency gains and reduced labeling costs while maintaining valid inference across diverse settings.

title: Chain-of-Glimpse: Search-Guided Progressive Object-Grounded Reasoning for Video Understanding
summary: Video understanding requires identifying and reasoning over semantically discriminative visual objects across frames, yet existing object-agnostic solutions struggle to effectively handle substantial object variations over time. To address this, we introduce Chain-of-Glimpse, a search-guided progressive object-grounded reasoning framework that explicitly anchors each reasoning step to specific visual evidence regions, enabling compositional and multi-step decision-making. Formally, Chain-of-Glimpse formulates video reasoning as a step-by-step process that incrementally builds spatially grounded traces around task-relevant visual objects, thereby mitigating over-reliance on saliency-driven cues. Specifically, Chain-of-Glimpse features a search-guided controller, optimized via reinforcement learning with a format reward that significantly incentivizes grounding capability, to iteratively ground visual evidence regions and form reliable reasoning trajectories, yielding accurate and interpretable multi-step decisions. Extensive evaluations on both in domain NExTQA and out-of-domain Video-Holmes, CG-Bench Reasoning, and VRBench benchmarks demonstrate consistent performance gains, robustness and generalization of Chain-of-Glimpse across diverse video reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Chain-of-Glimpse is a search-guided, object-grounded, multi-step reasoning framework for video understanding that iteratively grounds spatial evidence around task-relevant objects, using a reinforcement-learning controller with a grounding-focused reward to achieve interpretable and robust reasoning across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current video understanding methods struggle with substantial object variations over time and often rely on object-agnostic or saliency-driven cues, leading to brittle reasoning. There is a need for explicit, spatially grounded, multi-step reasoning that can adapt to changing objects across frames.
AI.method: Propose Chain-of-Glimpse: a stepwise, spatially grounded reasoning process. A search-guided controller iteratively grounds evidence regions and builds temporal traces around task-relevant objects. The controller is trained with reinforcement learning using a reward that heavily incentivizes grounding capability, promoting interpretable trajectories and reliable decisions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on in-domain NExTQA and out-of-domain Video-Holmes, CG-Bench Reasoning, and VRBench show consistent improvements in performance, robustness, and generalization of Chain-of-Glimpse across diverse video reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-Glimpse demonstrates that constraining reasoning steps to explicit visual evidence regions yields accurate and interpretable multi-step decisions for video understanding, addressing object variation challenges and improving cross-task generalization.

title: AgentGA: Evolving Code Solutions in Agent-Seed Space
summary: We present AgentGA, a framework that evolves autonomous code-generation runs by optimizing the agent seed: the task prompt plus optional parent archives that initialize a fresh workspace. The outer loop searches over these reusable starting conditions rather than editing code directly. Each generation launches a fresh autonomous run from a reset workspace, while selected parent archives provide inherited artifacts that descendants can inspect and reuse. AgentGA couples a population-level genetic algorithm with long-horizon agents; selection uses deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments and operator allocation is adapted online with a modified Hedge controller. We instantiate the approach for tabular AutoML on the 16-competition Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark. On the 10 benchmark runs reported here, AgentGA averages 74.52% Exceeds % of Human versus 54.15% for AIDE. Across 1135 parent-child comparisons, descendants given parent archives outperform runs started from scratch, indicating that inherited artifacts improve later autonomous runs. These findings support agent-seed optimization as a practical design point for autonomous code-search systems.

AI.tldr: AgentGA evolves autonomous code-generation runs by optimizing the agent seed (task prompt + optional parent archives) rather than directly editing code. An outer genetic algorithm searches starting conditions for long-horizon agents; descendants can reuse inherited artifacts from parent archives. The approach is instantiated on tabular AutoML for the Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark, showing inherited artifacts improve later runs and outperform a published baseline (AIDE).
AI.motivation: Investigate whether optimizing the starting conditions of autonomous code-search/AutoML runs (agent seeds) and reuse of parent artifacts can improve performance and sample efficiency, rather than direct code evolution.
AI.method: Outer population-level GA operates over seed prompts and optional parent archives that initialize a fresh workspace. Each generation launches a fresh autonomous run from a reset workspace; selected parent archives provide inherited artifacts. Selection uses deterministic 1:1 elite tournaments; operator allocation adapts online via a modified Hedge controller. The approach is instantiated for tabular AutoML on the 16-competition Weco-Kaggle Lite benchmark, evaluated across 10 benchmark runs with 1135 parent-child comparisons.
AI.result: AgentGA averages 74.52% Exceeds % of Human, vs 54.15% for AIDE on the reported runs. Across 1135 parent-child comparisons, descendants with parent archives outperform runs started from scratch, indicating inherited artifacts improve later autonomous runs.
AI.conclusion: Agent-seed optimization and archive inheritance are practical design points for autonomous code-search/AutoML systems, enabling better reuse and performance gains over baseline methods such as AIDE.

title: From Risk to Rescue: An Agentic Survival Analysis Framework for Liquidation Prevention
summary: Decentralized Finance (DeFi) lending protocols like Aave v3 rely on over-collateralization to secure loans, yet users frequently face liquidation due to volatile market conditions. Existing risk management tools utilize static health-factor thresholds, which are reactive and fail to distinguish between administrative "dust" cleanup and genuine insolvency. In this work, we propose an autonomous agent that leverages time-to-event (survival) analysis and moves beyond prediction to execution. Unlike passive risk signals, this agent perceives risk, simulates counterfactual futures, and executes protocol-faithful interventions to proactively prevent liquidations. We introduce a return period metric derived from a numerically stable XGBoost Cox proportional hazards model to normalize risk across transaction types, coupled with a volatility-adjusted trend score to filter transient market noise. To select optimal interventions, we implement a counterfactual optimization loop that simulates potential user actions to find the minimum capital required to mitigate risk. We validate our approach using a high-fidelity, protocol-faithful Aave v3 simulator on a cohort of 4,882 high-risk user profiles. The results demonstrate the agent's ability to prevent liquidations in imminent-risk scenarios where static rules fail, effectively "saving the unsavable" while maintaining a zero worsening rate, providing a critical safety guarantee often missing in autonomous financial agents. Furthermore, the system successfully differentiates between actionable financial risks and negligible dust events, optimizing capital efficiency where static rules fail.

AI.tldr: Autonomous DeFi risk agent for Aave v3 using survival analysis and counterfactual optimization to prevent liquidations, with return-period normalization and volatility-filtered signals; validated on 4,882 high-risk profiles; claims zero worsening and better capital efficiency.
AI.motivation: Static health-factor thresholds are reactive and inconclusive between dust and insolvency; need proactive, protocol-faithful interventions and quantitative capital planning.
AI.method: XGBoost Cox proportional hazards model for return period normalization; volatility-adjusted trend score to filter noise; counterfactual optimization loop simulating user actions to identify minimum capital required for risk mitigation; high-fidelity Aave v3 simulator with 4,882 high-risk profiles.
AI.result: Agent prevents imminent liquidations where static rules fail; achieves zero worsening rate; distinguishes actionable risk from dust; improves capital efficiency; demonstrates protocol-faithful intervention capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of proactive, autonomous risk management in DeFi with safety guarantees and improved capital efficiency; suitable for integration into risk tooling, though broader real-market validation and deployment considerations are needed.

title: The Courtroom Trial of Pixels: Robust Image Manipulation Localization via Adversarial Evidence and Reinforcement Learning Judgment
summary: Although some existing image manipulation localization (IML) methods incorporate authenticity-related supervision, this information is typically utilized merely as an auxiliary training signal to enhance the model's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts, rather than being explicitly modeled as localization evidence opposing the manipulated regions. Consequently, when manipulation traces are subtle or degraded by post-processing and noise, these methods struggle to explicitly compare manipulated and authentic evidence, resulting in unreliable predictions in ambiguous areas. To address these issues, we propose a courtroom-style adjudication framework that regards IML task as the confrontation of evidence followed by judgment. The framework comprises a prosecution stream, a defense stream, and a judge model. We first build a dual-hypothesis segmentation architecture on a shared multi-scale encoder, in which the prosecution stream asserts manipulation and the defense stream asserts authenticity. Guided by edge priors, it produces evidence for manipulated and authentic regions through cascaded multi-level fusion, bidirectional disagreement suppression, and dynamic debate refinement. We further develop a reinforcement learning judge model that performs strategic re-inference and refinement on uncertain regions, yielding a manipulated-region mask. The judge model is trained with advantage-based rewards and a soft-IoU objective, and reliability is calibrated via entropy and cross-hypothesis consistency. Experimental results show that our model achieves superior average performance compared with SOTA IML methods.

AI.tldr: A courtroom-style adjudication framework for image manipulation localization with prosecution/defense streams and a judge model, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing IML methods rely on authenticity supervision mainly as auxiliary signals and struggle to explicitly compare manipulated vs authentic evidence, especially when traces are subtle or degraded by post-processing or noise, leading to unreliable predictions in ambiguous regions.
AI.method: Prosecution and defense streams share a multi-scale encoder to perform dual-hypothesis segmentation (manipulated vs authentic). Guided by edge priors, they use cascaded multi-level fusion, bidirectional disagreement suppression, and dynamic debate refinement to produce evidence maps. A reinforcement learning judge model performs strategic re-inference and refinement on uncertain regions to output a manipulated-region mask, trained with advantage-based rewards and a soft-IoU objective; reliability is calibrated via entropy and cross-hypothesis consistency.
AI.result: Experimental results show superior average performance compared with state-of-the-art IML methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework explicitly models evidence confrontation between manipulated and authentic regions, plus judge-driven refinement, yielding improved accuracy and reliability, even under challenging conditions and post-processing.

title: Rethinking Patient Education as Multi-turn Multi-modal Interaction
comment: Equal contribution for the first two authors
summary: Most medical multimodal benchmarks focus on static tasks such as image question answering, report generation, and plain-language rewriting. Patient education is more demanding: systems must identify relevant evidence across images, show patients where to look, explain findings in accessible language, and handle confusion or distress. Yet most patient education work remains text-only, even though combined image-and-text explanations may better support understanding. We introduce MedImageEdu, a benchmark for multi-turn, evidence-grounded radiology patient education. Each case provides a radiology report with report text and case images. A DoctorAgent interacts with a PatientAgent, conditioned on a hidden profile that captures factors such as education level, health literacy, and personality. When a patient question would benefit from visual support, the DoctorAgent can issue drawing instructions grounded in the report, case images, and the current question to a benchmark-provided drawing tool. The tool returns image(s), after which the DoctorAgent produces a final multimodal response consisting of the image(s) and a grounded plain-language explanation. MedImageEdu contains 150 cases from three sources and evaluates both the consultation process and the final multimodal response along five dimensions: Consultation, Safety and Scope, Language Quality, Drawing Quality, and Image-Text Response Quality. Across representative open- and closed-source vision-language model agents, we find three consistent gaps: fluent language often outpaces faithful visual grounding, safety is the weakest dimension across disease categories, and emotionally tense interactions are harder than low education or low health literacy. MedImageEdu provides a controlled testbed for assessing whether multimodal agents can teach from evidence rather than merely answer from text.

AI.tldr: MedImageEdu: a multimodal, evidence-grounded radiology patient-education benchmark with DoctorAgent–PatientAgent interactions, a drawing tool, and evaluation across five dimensions to test grounding, safety, language, and drawing quality.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in patient-education benchmarks that are mostly text-based; develop a controlled, multimodal benchmark to assess whether models can teach from radiology evidence by combining images and explanations, and handling patient distress and varied health literacy.
AI.method: Construct MedImageEdu with 150 cases from three sources, each with a radiology report and case images. A DoctorAgent interacts with a PatientAgent, conditioned on a hidden profile (education level, health literacy, personality). The DoctorAgent can issue drawing instructions to a benchmark drawing tool when visual support aids understanding. The tool returns images, after which the DoctorAgent produces a final multimodal response (images + grounded plain-language explanation). Evaluate across five dimensions: Consultation, Safety and Scope, Language Quality, Drawing Quality, Image-Text Response Quality. Test across representative open- and closed-source vision-language model agents.
AI.result: Identifies three consistent gaps: fluent language often outpaces faithful visual grounding; safety is the weakest dimension across disease categories; emotionally tense interactions are harder than lower education or health literacy. Demonstrates MedImageEdu as a controlled testbed for assessing multimodal agents’ ability to teach from evidence rather than rely on text alone.
AI.conclusion: MedImageEdu provides a benchmark to evaluate multimodal radiology patient-education agents, emphasizing evidence-grounded explanations with visual support, while highlighting ongoing challenges in grounding faithfulness, safety, and handling emotionally tense interactions.

title: CLion: Efficient Cautious Lion Optimizer with Enhanced Generalization
summary:
Lion optimizer is a popular learning-based optimization algorithm in machine learning, which shows impressive performance in training many deep learning models. Although convergence property of the Lion optimizer has been studied, its generalization analysis is still missing. To fill this gap, we study generalization property of the Lion via algorithmic stability based on the mathematical induction. Specifically, we prove that the Lion has a generalization error of $O(\frac{1}{Nτ^T})$, where $N$ is training sample size, and $τ>0$ denotes the smallest absolute value of non-zero element in gradient estimator, and $T$ is the total iteration number. In addition, we obtain an interesting byproduct that the SignSGD algorithm has the same generalization error as the Lion. To enhance generalization of the Lion, we design a novel efficient Cautious Lion (i.e., CLion) optimizer by cautiously using sign function. Moreover, we prove that our CLion has a lower generalization error of $O(\frac{1}{N})$ than $O(\frac{1}{Nτ^T})$ of the Lion, since the parameter $τ$ generally is very small. Meanwhile, we study convergence property of our CLion optimizer, and prove that our CLion has a fast convergence rate of $O(\frac{\sqrt{d}}{T^{1/4}})$ under $\ell_1$-norm of gradient for nonconvex stochastic optimization, where $d$ denotes the model dimension.
  Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate effectiveness of our CLion optimizer.

AI.tldr: The abstract analyzes the Lion optimizer's generalization via algorithmic stability, showing a bound of O(1/(Nτ^T)) and an equivalence with SignSGD; it introduces Cautious Lion (CLion) with improved generalization O(1/N) and a convergence rate O(√d/T^{1/4}) under L1-norm, supported by experiments.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in theoretical generalization analysis for the Lion optimizer and to enhance it with a more stable variant.
AI.method: Provide algorithmic stability-based generalization bounds using mathematical induction; derive Lion's bounds, establish SignSGD equivalence; design CLion by cautiously applying the sign function; prove CLion's better generalization and convergence properties; accompany with numerical experiments.
AI.result: Generalization error bounds: Lion: O(1/(Nτ^T)); SignSGD: same bound; CLion: O(1/N). Convergence: CLion achieves O(√d / T^{1/4}) under L1-norm for nonconvex stochastic optimization; experiments demonstrate effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: CLion improves generalization performance compared to Lion and retains fast convergence, supported by theory and experiments.

title: NG-GS: NeRF-Guided 3D Gaussian Splatting Segmentation
summary: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled highly efficient and photorealistic novel view synthesis. However, segmenting objects accurately in 3DGS remains challenging due to the discrete nature of Gaussian representations, which often leads to aliasing and artifacts at object boundaries. In this paper, we introduce NG-GS, a novel framework for high-quality object segmentation in 3DGS that explicitly addresses boundary discretization. Our approach begins by automatically identifying ambiguous Gaussians at object boundaries using mask variance analysis. We then apply radial basis function (RBF) interpolation to construct a spatially continuous feature field, enhanced by multi-resolution hash encoding for efficient multi-scale representation. A joint optimization strategy aligns 3DGS with a lightweight NeRF module through alignment and spatial continuity losses, ensuring smooth and consistent segmentation boundaries. Extensive experiments on NVOS, LERF-OVS, and ScanNet benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, with significant gains in boundary mIoU. Code is available at https://github.com/BJTU-KD3D/NG-GS.

AI.tldr: NG-GS introduces boundary-aware segmentation for 3D Gaussian Splatting by detecting ambiguous Gaussians at edges, using RBF-based spatially continuous features with multi-scale hashing, and jointly optimizing with a lightweight NeRF to smooth boundaries, achieving state-of-the-art boundary mIoU on NVOS, LERF-OVS, and ScanNet.
AI.motivation: Segmentation at boundaries in 3D Gaussian Splatting is limited by discrete Gaussian representations, causing aliasing and artifacts; a boundary-aware, continuous feature approach is needed to improve object-level segmentation quality without sacrificing the efficiency of 3DGS.
AI.method: Identify ambiguous Gaussians via mask variance at object boundaries; apply radial basis function interpolation to construct a continuous feature field; enhance with multi-resolution hash encoding for efficiency and multi-scale representation; jointly optimize 3DGS with a lightweight NeRF module using alignment and spatial continuity losses to enforce boundary smoothness and consistency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on NVOS, LERF-OVS, and ScanNet shows state-of-the-art performance with notable improvements in boundary mIoU compared to baselines, validating the boundary discretization approach and the synergy between 3DGS and NeRF.
AI.conclusion: Boundary discretization is a critical bottleneck in 3DGS-based segmentation. The proposed NG-GS framework effectively mitigates aliasing at boundaries through a continuous, multi-scale, and jointly optimized representation, delivering superior segmentation quality without compromising the efficiency of 3D Gaussian splatting.

title: Acceptance Dynamics Across Cognitive Domains in Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference. It uses a small draft model to propose a tree of future tokens. A larger target model then verifies these tokens in a single batched forward pass. Despite the growing body of work on speculative methods, the degree to which the cognitive characteristics of a task affect acceptance probability remains largely unexplored. We present an empirical study of tree-based speculative decoding acceptance dynamics. Our study spans four well-established NLP benchmark domains: code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, and open-ended chat. For this, we use TinyLlama-1.1B as the draft model against Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as the target. Over 99,768 speculative nodes collected from 200 prompts, we derive per-domain acceptance rates, expected accepted lengths, depth-acceptance profiles, and entropy-acceptance correlations. We find that task type is a stronger predictor of acceptance than tree depth. Furthermore, only the chat domain consistently yields an expected accepted length exceeding 1.0 token per step. We also show that the entropy-acceptance correlation is consistently negative but weak across all domains (rho in [-0.20, -0.15]). Counterintuitively, chat produces the highest entropy yet the highest acceptance rate. We attribute this divergence to the lexical predictability of RLHF-aligned register. These findings have direct implications for domain-aware speculation budgets and draft-model selection strategies. Index Terms--speculative decoding, large language model inference, tree attention, draft model, acceptance probability, LLM efficiency

AI.tldr: Task type is a stronger predictor of acceptance in tree-based speculative decoding than tree depth; chat yields longer accepted segments. Entropy-acceptance is weakly negative; chat has high entropy but also high acceptance due to RLHF-aligned lexical predictability. Implications for domain-aware budgeting and draft-model choice.
AI.motivation: To understand how task cognitive characteristics influence acceptance probability in speculative decoding, enabling more efficient budgeting and draft-model selection.
AI.method: Empirical study across four NLP domains (code generation, mathematical reasoning, logical reasoning, open-ended chat) using TinyLlama-1.1B as the draft and Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ as the target. Collected 99,768 speculative nodes from 200 prompts; computed per-domain acceptance rates, expected accepted lengths, depth-acceptance profiles, and entropy-acceptance correlations.
AI.result: Found that domain (task type) predicts acceptance better than tree depth. Only the chat domain achieves an expected accepted length >1 token per step. Entropy-acceptance correlation is negative but weak across domains (rho in [-0.20, -0.15]); chat has the highest entropy yet the highest acceptance, attributed to Lexical predictability of RLHF-aligned register.
AI.conclusion: Domain-aware speculation budgets and draft-model selection strategies should be tailored to task type; chat tasks may warrant larger speculative trees and different draft-target combos due to higher acceptance rates and longer accepted lengths.

title: ConfLayers: Adaptive Confidence-based Layer Skipping for Self-Speculative Decoding
summary: Self-speculative decoding is an inference technique for large language models designed to speed up generation without sacrificing output quality. It combines fast, approximate decoding using a compact version of the model as a draft model with selective re-evaluation by the full target model. Some existing methods form the draft model by dynamically learning which layers to skip during inference, effectively creating a smaller subnetwork to speed up computation. However, using heuristic-based approaches to select layers to skip can often be simpler and more effective. In this paper, we propose ConfLayers, a dynamic plug-and-play approach to forming the draft model in self-speculative decoding via confidence-based intermediate layer skipping. The process iteratively computes confidence scores for all layers, selects layers to skip based on an adaptive threshold, evaluates the performance of the resulting set, and updates the best selection until no further improvement is achieved or a maximum number of iterations is reached. This framework avoids the overhead and complexity of training a layer skipping policy and can provide more consistent speed-quality trade-offs while preserving the adaptivity of the draft model to diverse tasks and datasets. The performance evaluation of ConfLayers across different models and datasets shows that our novel approach offers up to 1.4x speedup over vanilla LLM generation.

AI.tldr: ConfLayers introduces a confidence-based, dynamic layer-skipping framework for self-speculative decoding that forms a draft model without training a skip policy, iteratively selecting layers to skip with an adaptive threshold and re-evaluating until convergence; achieves up to 1.4x speedup over vanilla generation.
AI.motivation: To accelerate large language model generation while preserving quality, avoiding the complexity of learned layer-skipping policies; heuristic and plug-and-play approaches offer simpler, robust speedups across diverse tasks and datasets.
AI.method: Compute confidence scores for all layers in the draft model; select layers to skip using an adaptive threshold; evaluate the performance of the resulting configuration; update the best selection based on improvement; iterate until no further gains or the maximum number of iterations is reached; no training of a skip policy required; maintains adaptivity to tasks and datasets.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows up to 1.4× speedup over vanilla LLM generation, with more consistent speed–quality trade-offs across models and datasets.
AI.conclusion: ConfLayers provides a simple, dynamic, plug-and-play approach to forming the draft model for self-speculative decoding that avoids training overhead while delivering speedups and preserving quality across diverse tasks.

title: G-MIXER: Geodesic Mixup-based Implicit Semantic Expansion and Explicit Semantic Re-ranking for Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) aims to retrieve target images by integrating a reference image with a corresponding modification text. CIR requires jointly considering the explicit semantics specified in the query and the implicit semantics embedded within its bi-modal composition. Recent training-free Zero-Shot CIR (ZS-CIR) methods leverage Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to generate detailed target descriptions, converting the implicit information into explicit textual expressions. However, these methods rely heavily on the textual modality and fail to capture the fuzzy retrieval nature that requires considering diverse combinations of candidates. This leads to reduced diversity and accuracy in retrieval results. To address this limitation, we propose a novel training-free method, Geodesic Mixup-based Implicit semantic eXpansion and Explicit semantic Re-ranking for ZS-CIR (G-MIXER). G-MIXER constructs composed query features that reflect the implicit semantics of reference image-text pairs through geodesic mixup over a range of mixup ratios, and builds a diverse candidate set. The generated candidates are then re-ranked using explicit semantics derived from MLLMs, improving both retrieval diversity and accuracy. Our proposed G-MIXER achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple ZS-CIR benchmarks, effectively handling both implicit and explicit semantics without additional training. Our code will be available at https://github.com/maya0395/gmixer.

AI.tldr: Training-free ZS-CIR method G-MIXER uses geodesic mixup to explore implicit semantics and explicit MLLM-based re-ranking to boost diversity and accuracy without training.
AI.motivation: Overly text-reliant ZS-CIR methods yield limited diversity and lower accuracy; implicit semantics of image-text pairs demand exploration beyond single textual generation.
AI.method: Construct composed query features via geodesic mixup across mixup ratios to capture a spectrum of implicit semantics; generate a diverse candidate set; re-rank with explicit semantics from MLLMs; no training required.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on multiple ZS-CIR benchmarks; improved diversity and accuracy; training-free; code released.
AI.conclusion: G-MIXER demonstrates effective integration of implicit and explicit semantics for ZS-CIR and offers practical, training-free advantages.

title: DR$^{3}$-Eval: Towards Realistic and Reproducible Deep Research Evaluation
summary: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) aim to solve complex, long-horizon research tasks involving planning, retrieval, multimodal understanding, and report generation, yet their evaluation remains challenging due to dynamic web environments and ambiguous task definitions. We propose DR$^{3}$-Eval, a realistic and reproducible benchmark for evaluating deep research agents on multimodal, multi-file report generation. DR$^{3}$-Eval is constructed from authentic user-provided materials and paired with a per-task static research sandbox corpus that simulates open-web complexity while remaining fully verifiable, containing supportive documents, distractors, and noise. Moreover, we introduce a multi-dimensional evaluation framework measuring Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, and Depth Quality, and validate its alignment with human judgments. Experiments with our developed multi-agent system DR$^{3}$-Agent based on multiple state-of-the-art language models demonstrate that DR$^{3}$-Eval is highly challenging and reveals critical failure modes in retrieval robustness and hallucination control. Our code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Proposes DR^3-Eval, a realistic, reproducible benchmark with authentic materials and a static sandbox to evaluate deep research agents on multimodal, multi-file report generation, revealing retrieval and hallucination challenges.
AI.motivation: Evaluate deep research agents (DRAs) in long-horizon, complex tasks within dynamic web-like environments; existing evaluations lack realism and reproducibility, necessitating a benchmark with verifiable sandbox and multidimensional metrics.
AI.method: Construct DR^3-Eval from authentic user-provided materials; pair with a per-task static research sandbox corpus containing supportive documents, distractors, and noise to simulate open-web complexity; develop a multi-dimensional evaluation framework (Information Recall, Factual Accuracy, Citation Coverage, Instruction Following, Depth Quality); validate alignment with human judgments; implement DR^3-Agent using multiple state-of-the-art language models and conduct experiments.
AI.result: DR^3-Eval is highly challenging and reveals critical failure modes in retrieval robustness and hallucination control; experiments with DR^3-Agent demonstrate the benchmark's effectiveness; code and data are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: DR^3-Eval enables robust benchmarking for DRAs, highlighting gaps in retrieval grounding and generation fidelity; public release promotes reproducibility and community adoption.

title: ELMoE-3D: Leveraging Intrinsic Elasticity of MoE for Hybrid-Bonding-Enabled Self-Speculative Decoding in On-Premises Serving
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have become the dominant architecture for large-scale language models, yet on-premises serving remains fundamentally memory-bound as batching turns sparse per-token compute into dense memory activation. Memory-centric architectures (PIM, NMP) improve bandwidth but leave compute underutilized under MoE's low arithmetic intensity at high batch sizes. Speculative decoding (SD) trades idle compute for fewer target invocations, yet verification must load experts even for rejected tokens, severely limiting its benefit in MoE especially at low batch sizes. We propose ELMoE-3D, a hybrid-bonding (HB)-based HW-SW co-designed framework that unifies cache-based acceleration and speculative decoding to offer overall speedup across batch sizes. We identify two intrinsic elasticity axes of MoE-expert and bit-and jointly scale them to construct Elastic Self-Speculative Decoding (Elastic-SD), which serves as both an expert cache and a strongly aligned self-draft model accelerated by high HB bandwidth. Our LSB-augmented bit-sliced architecture exploits inherent redundancy in bit-slice representations to natively support bit-nested execution. On our 3D-stacked hardware, ELMoE-3D achieves an average $6.6\times$ speedup and $4.4\times$ energy efficiency gain over naive MoE serving on xPU across batch sizes 1-16, and delivers $2.2\times$ speedup and $1.4\times$ energy efficiency gain over the best-performing prior accelerator baseline.

AI.tldr: ELMoE-3D presents a hybrid-bonding (HB) HW-SW co-design that unifies cache-based acceleration with speculative decoding to accelerate mixture-of-experts (MoE) serving across batch sizes, achieving significant speedups and energy efficiency gains on 3D-stacked hardware.
AI.motivation: MoE models are memory-bound in on-premise serving due to dense memory activations from batching; memory-centric accelerators improve bandwidth but underutilize compute at high batch sizes; speculative decoding trades compute for fewer target invocations but incurs verification loads that hinder gains, especially with low batch sizes. There is a need for a framework that both accelerates expert caching and speculative decoding while adapting to MoE elasticity.
AI.method: Introduce Elastic Self-Speculative Decoding (Elastic-SD) to jointly scale two intrinsic elasticity axes of MoE-expert and bit-and. Develop a LSB-augmented bit-sliced architecture to exploit bit-nested execution. Propose ELMoE-3D, a HW-SW co-design that uses HB-based heterogeneous 3D-stacked hardware to unify cache-based acceleration with speculative decoding, creating an expert cache and a self-draft model accelerated by high HB bandwidth.
AI.result: On 3D-stacked hardware, ELMoE-3D achieves an average 6.6× speedup and 4.4× energy efficiency gain over naive MoE serving on xPU across batch sizes 1–16, and 2.2× speedup and 1.4× energy efficiency gain over the best-performing prior accelerator baseline.
AI.conclusion: ELMoE-3D demonstrates that coupling cache-based acceleration with self-speculative decoding via Elastic-SD and a bit-sliced HB architecture yields significant throughput and energy improvements for MoE serving across a range of batch sizes, outperforming naive and prior accelerators.

title: MS-SSE-Net: A Multi-Scale Spatial Squeeze-and-Excitation Network for Structural Damage Detection in Civil and Geotechnical Engineering
summary: Structural damage detection is essential for maintaining the safety and reliability of civil infrastructure. However, accurately identifying different types of structural damage from images remains challenging due to variations in damage patterns and environmental conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MS-SSE-Net, a novel deep learning (DL) framework for structural damage classification. The proposed model is built upon the DenseNet201 backbone and integrates novel multi-scale feature extraction with channel and spatial attention mechanisms (MS-SSE-Net). Specifically, parallel depthwise convolutions capture both local and contextual features, while squeeze-and-excitation style channel attention and spatial attention emphasize informative regions and suppress irrelevant noise. The refined features are then processed through global average pooling and a fully connected classification layer to generate the final predictions. Experiments are conducted on the StructDamage dataset containing multiple structural damage categories. The proposed MS-SSE-Net demonstrates superior performance compared with the baseline DenseNet201 and other comparative approaches. Specifically, the proposed method achieves 99.31% precision, 99.25% recall, 99.27% F1-score, and 99.26% accuracy, outperforming the baseline model which achieved 98.62% precision, 98.53% recall, 98.58% F1-score, and 98.53% accuracy.

AI.tldr: DenseNet201-based MS-SSE-Net with multi-scale depthwise convolutions and attention achieves near-perfect structural-damage classification on StructDamage, outperforming DenseNet201 baseline.
AI.motivation: Structural damage images exhibit high variability in damage patterns and imaging conditions. The proposed MS-SSE-Net aims to improve feature extraction by capturing multi-scale information and focusing on informative regions via attention mechanisms.
AI.method: Backbone: DenseNet201. Architecture adds parallel depthwise convolutions for multi-scale feature extraction, followed by squeeze-and-excitation style channel attention and spatial attention (CBAM-like). Features are aggregated with global average pooling and a final fully connected classifier; evaluated on StructDamage with multiple damage categories.
AI.result: MS-SSE-Net achieves precision 99.31%, recall 99.25%, F1 99.27%, and accuracy 99.26% on StructDamage, outperforming the DenseNet201 baseline (precision 98.62%, recall 98.53%, F1 98.58%, accuracy 98.53%).
AI.conclusion: MS-SSE-Net shows superior performance over the DenseNet201 baseline. However, the abstract lacks details on ablation studies, data-split methodology, dataset size, and generalization validation. Reproducibility and real-world generalization require further verification (cross-dataset testing, robustness to noise, computation cost, and potential data leakage checks).

title: M2-PALE: A Framework for Explaining Multi-Agent MCTS--Minimax Hybrids via Process Mining and LLMs
summary: Monte-Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) is a fundamental sampling-based search algorithm widely used for online planning in sequential decision-making domains. Despite its success in driving recent advances in artificial intelligence, understanding the behavior of MCTS agents remains a challenge for both developers and users. This difficulty stems from the complex search trees produced through the simulation of numerous future states and their intricate relationships. A known weakness of standard MCTS is its reliance on highly selective tree construction, which may lead to the omission of crucial moves and a vulnerability to tactical traps. To resolve this, we incorporate shallow, full-width Minimax search into the rollout phase of multi-agent MCTS to enhance strategic depth. Furthermore, to demystify the resulting decision-making logic, we introduce \textsf{M2-PALE} (MCTS--Minimax Process-Aided Linguistic Explanations). This framework employs process mining techniques, specifically the Alpha Miner, iDHM, and Inductive Miner algorithms, to extract underlying behavioral workflows from agent execution traces. These process models are then synthesized by LLMs to generate human-readable causal and distal explanations. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach in a small-scale checkers environment, establishing a scalable foundation for interpreting hybrid agents in increasingly complex strategic domains.

AI.tldr: Hybrid MCTS improvement by incorporating shallow, full-width Minimax in rollouts, plus M2-PALE: mining execution traces with Alpha Miner, iDHM, and Inductive Miner to generate LLM-derived causal and distal explanations. Demonstrated in a small checkers environment, establishing groundwork for interpretable hybrid agents.
AI.motivation: MCTS's selective tree growth can miss critical moves and be vulnerable to tactical traps; a deeper strategic search during rollout and an interpretable, trace-based explanation framework are needed.
AI.method: Integrate shallow, full-width Minimax search into the MCTS rollout phase. Collect agent execution traces and apply process mining algorithms (Alpha Miner, iDHM, Inductive Miner) to extract behavioral workflows. Synthesize these workflows with large language models to produce human-readable explanations.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept demonstration in a small-scale checkers domain, showing feasibility of the approach and establishing a foundation for interpreting hybrid agents in more complex strategic tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework improves strategic depth while providing interpretable explanations of agent behavior, and paves the way for scaling to more complex domains with further empirical validation.

title: Zeroth-Order Optimization at the Edge of Stability
summary: Zeroth-order (ZO) methods are widely used when gradients are unavailable or prohibitively expensive, including black-box learning and memory-efficient fine-tuning of large models, yet their optimization dynamics in deep learning remain underexplored. In this work, we provide an explicit step size condition that exactly captures the (mean-square) linear stability of a family of ZO methods based on the standard two-point estimator. Our characterization reveals a sharp contrast with first-order (FO) methods: whereas FO stability is governed solely by the largest Hessian eigenvalue, mean-square stability of ZO methods depends on the entire Hessian spectrum. Since computing the full Hessian spectrum is infeasible in practical neural network training, we further derive tractable stability bounds that depend only on the largest eigenvalue and the Hessian trace. Empirically, we find that full-batch ZO methods operate at the edge of stability: ZO-GD, ZO-GDM, and ZO-Adam consistently stabilize near the predicted stability boundary across a range of deep learning training problems. Our results highlight an implicit regularization effect specific to ZO methods, where large step sizes primarily regularize the Hessian trace, whereas in FO methods they regularize the top eigenvalue.

AI.tldr: Explicit step-size condition for mean-square stability of two-point zeroth-order methods; stability depends on the full Hessian spectrum, not just the largest eigenvalue; tractable bounds use the largest eigenvalue and Hessian trace; empirical results show full-batch ZO methods operate near the predicted stability boundary and reveal Hessian-trace regularization.
AI.motivation: ZO methods enable optimization when gradients are unavailable or expensive, such as black-box learning and parameter-efficient tuning of large models. Understanding their stability dynamics in deep learning remains incomplete, motivating a rigorous stability criterion that informs step-size choices.
AI.method: Derive an explicit step-size condition that exactly characterizes mean-square stability for a family of ZO methods based on the standard two-point estimator. Compare with FO stability, which depends on the largest Hessian eigenvalue. Develop tractable stability bounds that depend only on the largest eigenvalue and the Hessian trace. Validate empirically on deep learning tasks using full-batch ZO variants (ZO-GD, ZO-GDM, ZO-Adam).
AI.result: Stability in ZO methods depends on the entire Hessian spectrum rather than solely the top eigenvalue. Practical bounds are obtainable via the largest eigenvalue and Hessian trace. Empirically, full-batch ZO methods operate near the stability boundary across a range of deep learning problems, and larger step sizes tend to regularize the Hessian trace rather than the top eigenvalue.
AI.conclusion: Zertoth-order optimization exhibits an implicit regularization distinct from first-order methods: large step sizes primarily regularize Hessian trace instead of the top eigenvalue. This distinction has implications for step-size design and stability analysis, suggesting that modeling the entire Hessian spectrum is important for ZO method tuning.

title: Data Synthesis Improves 3D Myotube Instance Segmentation
comment: 4 pages, 4 figures, submitted to BMT (VDE) 2026 Conference
summary: Myotubes are multinucleated muscle fibers serving as key model systems for studying muscle physiology, disease mechanisms, and drug responses. Mechanistic studies and drug screening thereby rely on quantitative morphological readouts such as diameter, length, and branching degree, which in turn require precise three-dimensional instance segmentation. Yet established pretrained biomedical segmentation models fail to generalize to this domain due to the absence of large annotated myotube datasets. We introduce a geometry-driven synthesis pipeline that models individual myotubes via polynomial centerlines, locally varying radii, branching structures, and ellipsoidal end caps derived from real microscopy observations. Synthetic volumes are rendered with realistic noise, optical artifacts, and CycleGAN-based Domain Adaptation (DA). A compact 3D U-Net with self-supervised encoder pretraining, trained exclusively on synthetic data, achieves a mean IPQ of 0.22 on real data, significantly outperforming three established zero-shot segmentation models, demonstrating that biophysics-driven synthesis enables effective instance segmentation in annotation-scarce biomedical domains.

AI.tldr: Synthetic geometry-driven 3D segmentation for myotubes using biophysics-based models and DA; trained entirely on synthetic data to achieve IPQ 0.22 on real data, outperforming zero-shot models.
AI.motivation: Overcome lack of large annotated myotube datasets by generating realistic, physics-informed synthetic volumes to enable effective instance segmentation.
AI.method: Geometric modeling of individual myotubes with polynomial centerlines, locally varying radii, branching structures, and ellipsoidal end caps; render with realistic noise and optical artifacts; apply CycleGAN-based domain adaptation; train a compact 3D U-Net with self-supervised encoder pretraining on synthetic data only.
AI.result: Mean IPQ of 0.22 on real data; outperforms three established zero-shot segmentation models, demonstrating effective transfer from synthetic to real domains in an annotation-scarce setting.
AI.conclusion: Biophysics-driven synthetic data can enable accurate 3D instance segmentation of myotubes in annotation-scarce biomedical domains, reducing reliance on large annotated datasets.

title: CAMO: An Agentic Framework for Automated Causal Discovery from Micro Behaviors to Macro Emergence in LLM Agent Simulations
summary: LLM-empowered agent simulations are increasingly used to study social emergence, yet the micro-to-macro causal mechanisms behind macro outcomes often remain unclear. This is challenging because emergence arises from intertwined agent interactions and meso-level feedback and nonlinearity, making generative mechanisms hard to disentangle. To this end, we introduce \textbf{\textsc{CAMO}}, an automated \textbf{Ca}usal discovery framework from \textbf{M}icr\textbf{o} behaviors to \textbf{M}acr\textbf{o} Emergence in LLM agent simulations. \textsc{CAMO} converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records and learns a compact causal representation centered on an emergent target $Y$. \textsc{CAMO} outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph, yielding interpretable causal chains and actionable intervention levers. It also uses simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view. Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the promise of \textsc{CAMO}.

AI.tldr: CAMO is an automated causal discovery framework that links micro-level MLM agent behaviors to macro emergent outcomes in LLM simulations. It outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream causal subgraph, uses counterfactual probes to orient edges, and demonstrates promise across four emergent settings.
AI.motivation: Understanding micro-to-macro causal mechanisms in LLM-driven social simulations is difficult due to intertwined agent interactions, meso-level feedback, and nonlinearity. CAMO aims to provide interpretable causal explanations and actionable intervention levers for emergent phenomena.
AI.method: CAMO converts mechanistic hypotheses into computable factors grounded in simulation records, learns a compact causal representation centered on the emergent target Y, outputs a computable Markov boundary and a minimal upstream explanatory subgraph, and employs simulator-internal counterfactual probing to orient ambiguous edges and revise hypotheses when evidence contradicts the current view.
AI.result: Experiments across four emergent settings demonstrate the framework’s promise in producing interpretable causal chains and intervention levers for macro emergence.
AI.conclusion: CAMO offers an automated, interpretable causal-discovery pipeline for micro-to-macro emergence in LLM agent simulations, enabling targeted interventions and principled hypothesis revision via counterfactual probing.

title: Mean Flow Policy Optimization
summary: Diffusion models have recently emerged as expressive policy representations for online reinforcement learning (RL). However, their iterative generative processes introduce substantial training and inference overhead. To overcome this limitation, we propose to represent policies using MeanFlow models, a class of few-step flow-based generative models, to improve training and inference efficiency over diffusion-based RL approaches. To promote exploration, we optimize MeanFlow policies under the maximum entropy RL framework via soft policy iteration, and address two key challenges specific to MeanFlow policies: action likelihood evaluation and soft policy improvement. Experiments on MuJoCo and DeepMind Control Suite benchmarks demonstrate that our method, Mean Flow Policy Optimization (MFPO), achieves performance comparable to or exceeding current diffusion-based baselines while considerably reducing training and inference time. Our code is available at https://github.com/MFPolicy/MFPO.

AI.tldr: Proposes MeanFlow policies (few-step flow-based generative models) as a more efficient alternative to diffusion policies in online RL, achieving comparable or better performance with significantly reduced training and inference time.
AI.motivation: Diffusion policies are expressive but incur high training/inference overhead; a faster policy representation is needed for efficient online RL.
AI.method: Introduce MeanFlow, a limited-step flow-based policy model; optimize under maximum-entropy RL via soft policy iteration; tackle action likelihood evaluation and soft policy improvement specific to MeanFlow; validate on MuJoCo and DeepMind Control Suite; provide implementation details in codebase.
AI.result: MFPO attains performance comparable to or better than current diffusion-based baselines while substantially reducing training and inference time on standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Flow-based, few-step MeanFlow policies are a viable and efficient alternative to diffusion policies for online RL, enabling practical deployment with faster training/inference; code released for reproducibility.

title: SynHAT: A Two-stage Coarse-to-Fine Diffusion Framework for Synthesizing Human Activity Traces
summary: Human activity traces (HATs) are critical for many applications, including human mobility modeling and point-of-interest (POI) recommendation. However, growing privacy concerns have severely limited access to authentic large-scale HAT datasets. Recent advances in generative AI provide new opportunities to synthesize realistic and privacy-preserving HATs for such applications. Yet two major challenges remain: (i) HATs are highly irregular and dynamic, with long and varying time intervals, making it difficult to capture their complex spatio-temporal dependencies and underlying distributions; and (ii) generative models are often computationally expensive, making long-term, fine-grained HAT synthesis inefficient. To address these challenges, we propose SynHAT, a computationally efficient coarse-to-fine HAT synthesis framework built on a novel spatio-temporal denoising diffusion model. In Stage 1, we develop Coarse-HADiff, which models the overall spatio-temporal dependencies of coarse-grained latent spatio-temporal traces. It incorporates a novel Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net with dual Drift-Jitter branches to jointly model smooth spatial transitions and temporal variations during denoising. In Stage 2, we introduce a three-step pipeline consisting of Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff, which shares the same architecture as Coarse-HADiff, and Semantic Alignment to generate fine-grained latent spatio-temporal traces from the Stage 1 outputs. We extensively evaluate SynHAT in terms of data fidelity, utility, privacy, robustness, and scalability. Experiments on real-world HAT datasets from four cities across three countries show that SynHAT substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving 52% and 33% improvements on spatial and temporal metrics, respectively.

AI.tldr: SynHAT introduces a coarse-to-fine diffusion-based framework to synthesize privacy-preserving human activity traces (HATs). It uses Stage 1 Coarse-HADiff to model latent spatio-temporal dependencies with a Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net (dual Drift-Jitter branches), and Stage 2 a three-step pipeline (Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff, Semantic Alignment) to produce fine-grained traces. It reports substantial improvements over baselines in both spatial (52%) and temporal (33%) metrics across four cities in three countries, alongside assessments on data fidelity, privacy, robustness, and scalability.
AI.motivation: HAT data are restricted by privacy; HATs are irregular and dynamic with long/variable time gaps, complicating learning, while generative models remain computationally expensive for long-term, fine-grained synthesis.
AI.method: Two-stage diffusion in latent space: Stage 1 Coarse-HADiff with Latent Spatio-Temporal U-Net and dual Drift-Jitter branches to capture smooth spatial transitions and temporal variations. Stage 2 uses Behavior Pattern Extraction, Fine-HADiff (architecture identical to Coarse-HADiff), and Semantic Alignment to convert coarse latent traces into fine-grained ones. Evaluation on real-world HAT datasets from four cities across three countries, assessing data fidelity, utility, privacy, robustness, and scalability.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both spatial and temporal metrics (52% and 33% improvements). Demonstrates data fidelity and privacy-preserving synthesis with robust scalability across diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: SynHAT provides a computationally efficient, high-fidelity framework for privacy-preserving HAT synthesis, addressing irregular spatio-temporal dynamics via a coarse-to-fine latent diffusion approach. It shows strong cross-city, cross-country generalization and broad applicability to mobility modeling and POI recommendation, with potential future work on further efficiency and privacy guarantees.

title: Gating Enables Curvature: A Geometric Expressivity Gap in Attention
summary: Multiplicative gating is widely used in neural architectures and has recently been applied to attention layers to improve performance and training stability in large language models. Despite the success of gated attention, the mathematical implications of gated attention mechanisms remain poorly understood. We study attention through the geometry of its representations by modeling outputs as mean parameters of Gaussian distributions and analyzing the induced Fisher--Rao geometry. We show that ungated attention operator is restricted to intrinsically flat statistical manifolds due to its affine structure, while multiplicative gating enables non-flat geometries, including positively curved manifolds that are unattainable in the ungated setting. These results establish a geometric expressivity gap between ungated and gated attention. Empirically, we show that gated models exhibit higher representation curvature and improved performance on tasks requiring nonlinear decision boundaries whereas they provide no consistent advantage on tasks with linear decision boundaries. Furthermore, we identify a structured regime in which curvature accumulates under composition, yielding a systematic depth amplification effect.

AI.tldr: Gated attention expands geometric expressivity by enabling non-flat Fisher-Rao manifolds; ungated attention is constrained to flat (affine) geometry. Gating yields higher representation curvature, improves performance on nonlinear tasks, and curvature accumulates with depth.
AI.motivation: To understand the mathematical implications of multiplicative gating in attention beyond empirical gains, by framing attention representations in the language of information geometry (Fisher–Rao metric) and comparing gated vs ungated mechanisms.
AI.method: Model attention outputs as Gaussian mean parameters and analyze the induced Fisher-Rao geometry. Compare ungated and gated attention in terms of curvature; perform empirical assessments of curvature, task performance, and depth-related curvature accumulation across architectures.
AI.result: Ungated attention is confined to intrinsically flat manifolds due to its affine structure. Multiplicative gating introduces non-flat geometries, including positively curved manifolds. Empirically, gated models exhibit higher representation curvature and better performance on nonlinear decision boundary tasks, with no consistent advantage on linear tasks. Curvature can accumulate under composition, suggesting a depth amplification effect.
AI.conclusion: Gating increases the geometric expressivity of attention by enabling curved (non-flat) Fisher-Rao geometries. This offers a theoretical explanation for observed gains of gated attention in nonlinear settings and reveals a depth-dependent curvature amplification regime, guiding future design and analysis of attention mechanisms.

title: Find the Differences: Differential Morphing Attack Detection vs Face Recognition
summary: Morphing is a challenge to face recognition (FR) for which several morphing attack detection solutions have been proposed. We argue that face recognition and differential morphing attack detection (D-MAD) in principle perform very similar tasks, which we support by comparing an FR system with two existing D-MAD approaches. We also show that currently used decision thresholds inherently lead to FR systems being vulnerable to morphing attacks and that this explains the tradeoff between performance on normal images and vulnerability to morphing attacks. We propose using FR systems that are already in place for morphing detection and introduce a new evaluation threshold that guarantees an upper limit to the vulnerability to morphing attacks - even of unknown types.

AI.tldr: Treats FR and D-MAD as similar tasks; shows that decision thresholds in FR systems create vulnerability to morphing; proposes using FR systems for morphing detection and a new evaluation threshold that caps vulnerability to unknown morphing types.
AI.motivation: Morphing attacks undermine face recognition; existing FR thresholds induce a tradeoff between normal-face performance and morph vulnerability; a safeguard is needed that bounds vulnerability even for unseen morphs.
AI.method: Compare a conventional FR system with two D-MAD approaches to illustrate conceptual similarity; analyze how current decision thresholds contribute to morph vulnerability; propose leveraging deployed FR systems for morphing detection and introduce an evaluation threshold to cap vulnerability.
AI.result: Illustrates intrinsic similarity between FR and D-MAD; demonstrates that typical decision thresholds render FR systems vulnerable to morphing attacks; introduces an evaluation threshold that guarantees an upper bound on morphing vulnerability, including for unknown morph types.
AI.conclusion: Using FR systems for morphing detection is viable; adopting the proposed evaluation threshold can limit vulnerability to morphing attacks, including unknown morph types, thereby improving robustness.

title: HWE-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Real-World Hardware Bug Repair Tasks
summary: Existing benchmarks for hardware design primarily evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) on isolated, component-level tasks such as generating HDL modules from specifications, leaving repository-scale evaluation unaddressed. We introduce HWE-Bench, the first large-scale, repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real-world hardware bug repair tasks. HWE-Bench comprises 417 task instances derived from real historical bug-fix pull requests across six major open-source projects spanning both Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel, covering RISC-V cores, SoCs, and security roots-of-trust. Each task is grounded in a fully containerized environment where the agent must resolve a real bug report, with correctness validated through the project's native simulation and regression flows. The benchmark is built through a largely automated pipeline that enables efficient expansion to new repositories. We evaluate seven LLMs with four agent frameworks and find that the best agent resolves 70.7% of tasks overall, with performance exceeding 90% on smaller cores but dropping below 65% on complex SoC-level projects. We observe larger performance gaps across models than commonly reported on software benchmarks, and difficulty is driven by project scope and bug-type distribution rather than code size alone. Our failure analysis traces agent failures to three stages of the debugging process: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination across RTL, configuration, and verification components, providing concrete directions for developing more capable hardware-aware agents.

AI.tldr: HWE-Bench is the first large-scale, repository-level benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on real hardware bug repair tasks. It comprises 417 tasks from six open-source projects across Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel, validated in fully containerized environments. The best agent achieves 70.7% task success overall, with >90% on small cores but <65% on complex SoCs; analysis highlights fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination as key failure modes, guiding future hardware-aware agent development.
AI.motivation: Current hardware design benchmarks focus on isolated HDL tasks rather than end-to-end, repository-scale debugging. There is a need to evaluate LLM agents on real-world repair workflows that involve multiple artifacts and project-wide integration to reflect practical challenges in hardware development.
AI.method: A largely automated pipeline builds a large, repository-scale benchmark (417 tasks) from real bug-fix PRs across six projects, spanning Verilog/SystemVerilog and Chisel. Each task runs in a fully containerized environment with correctness validated via native simulation/regression flows. The study evaluates seven LLMs using four agent frameworks.
AI.result: Best agent resolves 70.7% of tasks overall; performance exceeds 90% on smaller cores but drops below 65% on complex SoC-level projects. Larger performance gaps across models than in software benchmarks. Difficulty correlates with project scope and bug-type distribution, not just code size. Failure analysis identifies three main stages of debugging failures: fault localization, hardware-semantic reasoning, and cross-artifact coordination (RTL, configuration, verification).
AI.conclusion: To advance hardware-aware agents, future work should target improving fault localization accuracy, solid hardware-semantic reasoning across HDL+config+verification artifacts, and robust cross-artifact coordination mechanisms.

title: A Mechanistic Account of Attention Sinks in GPT-2: One Circuit, Broader Implications for Mitigation
summary: Transformers commonly exhibit an attention sink: disproportionately high attention to the first position. We study this behavior in GPT-2-style models with learned query biases and absolute positional embeddings. Combining structural analysis with causal interventions, validated across natural-language, mathematical, and code inputs, we find that the sink arises from the interaction among (i) a learned query bias, (ii) the first-layer MLP transformation of the positional encoding, and (iii) structure in the key projection. Crucially, each component we identify is individually dispensable: architectures omitting each of them robustly exhibit sinks. This indicates that attention sinks may arise through distinct circuits across architectures. These findings inform mitigation of sinks, and motivate broader investigation into why sinks emerge.

AI.tldr: Attention sinks in GPT-2–style transformers arise from an interaction among a learned query bias, the first-layer MLP on positional encodings, and structured key projections; removing any single component still produces sinks, indicating multiple distinct circuits and suggesting broad mitigation and further study.
AI.motivation: Understand why attention sinks occur across modalities, identify the causal components, and inform robust mitigation strategies for transformer architectures.
AI.method: Structural analysis combined with causal interventions in GPT-2–style models using learned query biases and absolute positional embeddings; experiments across natural language, mathematical, and code inputs; ablations of the query bias, positional encoding MLP, and key projection structure.
AI.result: The sink stems from the interaction of the three identified components; each component is individually dispensable, as architectures omitting any one still exhibit sinks; sinks thus can arise via distinct circuits across architectures.
AI.conclusion: Sinks may arise through multiple circuits; findings motivate targeted mitigation and broader investigation into why sinks emerge, and encourage architecture-aware approaches to prevent sinks.

title: SGA-MCTS: Decoupling Planning from Execution via Training-Free Atomic Experience Retrieval
summary: LLM-powered systems require complex multi-step decision-making abilities to solve real-world tasks, yet current planning approaches face a trade-off between the high latency of inference-time search and the limited generalization of supervised fine-tuning. To address this limitation, we introduce \textbf{SGA-MCTS}, a framework that casts LLM planning as non-parametric retrieval. Offline, we leverage Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore the solution space and distill high-fidelity trajectories into State-Goal-Action (SGA) atoms. These atoms are de-lexicalized primitives that abstract concrete entities into symbolic slots, preserving reusable causal logic while discarding domain-specific noise. Online, a retrieval-augmented agent employs a hybrid symbolic-semantic mechanism to fetch relevant SGAs and re-ground them into the current context as soft reasoning hints. Empirical results on complex benchmarks demonstrate that this paradigm enables frozen, open-weights models to match the performance of SOTA systems (e.g., GPT-5) without task-specific fine-tuning. By effectively amortizing the heavy computational cost of search, SGA-MCTS achieves System 2 reasoning depth at System 1 inference speeds, rendering autonomous planning both scalable and real-time feasible.

AI.tldr: SGA-MCTS proposes a non-parametric retrieval framework for LLM planning by offline MCTS distilling trajectories into State-Goal-Action atoms, then online retrieval-augmented grounding to enable scalable, real-time autonomous planning with performance on par with SOTA models without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between inference-time search latency and the limited generalization of supervised fine-tuning in LLM planning. Provide a scalable approach to achieve deep planning (System 2) with real-time speeds.
AI.method: Offline: use Monte Carlo Tree Search to explore solution space and distill high-fidelity trajectories into SGA atoms (state/goal/action primitives). De-lexicalize to symbolic slots to preserve reusable causal logic. Online: retrieval-augmented agent fetches SGAs and grounds them into the current context as soft reasoning hints via a hybrid symbolic-semantic mechanism.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks show that frozen, open-weight models can match SOTA performance (e.g., GPT-5) without task-specific fine-tuning; amortization of search yields System 2 depth at System 1 speeds; real-time scalable autonomous planning.
AI.conclusion: SGA-MCTS demonstrates that non-parametric retrieval combined with amortized planning can deliver deep, robust planning capabilities for LLMs without heavy parameter-tuning, enabling scalable, real-time autonomous agents.

title: Catching Every Ripple: Enhanced Anomaly Awareness via Dynamic Concept Adaptation
summary: Online anomaly detection (OAD) plays a pivotal role in real-time analytics and decision-making for evolving data streams. However, existing methods often rely on costly retraining and rigid decision boundaries, limiting their ability to adapt both effectively and efficiently to concept drift in dynamic environments. To address these challenges, we propose DyMETER, a dynamic concept adaptation framework for OAD that unifies on-the-fly parameter shifting and dynamic thresholding within a single online paradigm. DyMETER first learns a static detector on historical data to capture recurring central concepts, and then transitions to a dynamic mode to adapt to new concepts as drift occurs. Specifically, DyMETER employs a novel dynamic concept adaptation mechanism that leverages a hypernetwork to generate instance-aware parameter shifts for the static detector, thereby enabling efficient and effective adaptation without retraining or fine-tuning. To achieve robust and interpretable adaptation, DyMETER introduces a lightweight evolution controller to estimate instance-level concept uncertainty for adaptive updates. Further, DyMETER employs a dynamic threshold optimization module to adaptively recalibrates the decision boundary by maintaining a candidate window of uncertain samples, which ensures continuous alignment with evolving concepts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DyMETER significantly outperforms existing OAD approaches across a wide spectrum of application scenarios.
