AI.tldr: DyMETER is a dynamic concept adaptation framework for online anomaly detection that unifies on-the-fly parameter shifting and dynamic thresholding. It uses a hypernetwork to generate instance-aware parameter shifts from a static detector, a lightweight evolution controller to estimate concept uncertainty, and a dynamic threshold module to recalibrate decision boundaries via a candidate window of uncertain samples, enabling efficient, retraining-free adaptation to concept drift. It reportedly outperforms existing OAD methods across diverse scenarios.
AI.motivation: To address concept drift in online anomaly detection without costly retraining or rigid decision boundaries, enabling efficient and effective adaptation in dynamic data streams.
AI.method: 1) Train a static detector on historical data to capture recurring concepts. 2) Switch to dynamic mode where a hypernetwork generates instance-specific parameter shifts for the detector. 3) Use a lightweight evolution controller to estimate instance-level concept uncertainty for adaptive updates. 4) Apply dynamic threshold optimization by maintaining a candidate window of uncertain samples to recalibrate the decision boundary.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that DyMETER significantly outperforms existing OAD approaches across a wide spectrum of application scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DyMETER enables efficient, effective adaptation to evolving concepts in online anomaly detection by combining on-the-fly parameter shifts, instance-aware uncertainty, and dynamic threshold recalibration without retraining.

title: ASGNet: Adaptive Spectrum Guidance Network for Automatic Polyp Segmentation
summary: Early identification and removal of polyps can reduce the risk of developing colorectal cancer. However, the diverse morphologies, complex backgrounds and often concealed nature of polyps make polyp segmentation in colonoscopy images highly challenging. Despite the promising performance of existing deep learning-based polyp segmentation methods, their perceptual capabilities remain biased toward local regions, mainly because of the strong spatial correlations between neighboring pixels in the spatial domain. This limitation makes it difficult to capture the complete polyp structures, ultimately leading to sub-optimal segmentation results. In this paper, we propose a novel adaptive spectrum guidance network, called ASGNet, which addresses the limitations of spatial perception by integrating spectral features with global attributes. Specifically, we first design a spectrum-guided non-local perception module that jointly aggregates local and global information, therefore enhancing the discriminability of polyp structures, and refining their boundaries. Moreover, we introduce a multi-source semantic extractor that integrates rich high-level semantic information to assist in the preliminary localization of polyps. Furthermore, we construct a dense cross-layer interaction decoder that effectively integrates diverse information from different layers and strengthens it to generate high-quality representations for accurate polyp segmentation. Extensive quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate the superiority of our ASGNet approach over 21 state-of-the-art methods across five widely-used polyp segmentation benchmarks. The code will be publicly available at: https://github.com/CSYSI/ASGNet.

AI.tldr: ASGNet introduces spectrum-guided non-local perception, multi-source semantic extraction, and dense cross-layer decoding to improve polyp segmentation by overcoming local perception bias and leveraging spectral/global information; achieves state-of-the-art results on five benchmarks with 21 SOTA methods; code available.
AI.motivation: Polyp segmentation is hindered by diverse morphologies, complex backgrounds, and concealed polyps, with strong local pixel correlations causing bias toward local regions. A global-aware approach combining spectral features and high-level semantics is sought to accurately capture complete polyp structures and refine boundaries.
AI.method: Proposes spectrum-guided non-local perception module that fuses spectral features with global context to enhance discriminability and boundary refinement. Introduces a multi-source semantic extractor to inject rich high-level semantics for initial polyp localization. Develops a dense cross-layer interaction decoder to effectively integrate multi-layer information for robust, high-quality segmentation representations.
AI.result: Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate ASGNet's superiority over 21 state-of-the-art methods across five widely-used polyp segmentation benchmarks. Code will be publicly available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: ASGNet effectively addresses spatial perception bias by integrating spectral guidance, high-level semantics, and dense cross-layer fusion, yielding improved polyp segmentation performance and boundary accuracy.

title: Layered Mutability: Continuity and Governance in Persistent Self-Modifying Agents
comment: 17 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. self-modifying agents; AI governance; identity drift; persistent memory; runtime adaptation; model editing Primary: cs.AI Cross-list: cs.LG, cs.CY
summary: Persistent language-model agents increasingly combine tool use, tiered memory, reflective prompting, and runtime adaptation. In such systems, behavior is shaped not only by current prompts but by mutable internal conditions that influence future action. This paper introduces layered mutability, a framework for reasoning about that process across five layers: pretraining, post-training alignment, self-narrative, memory, and weight-level adaptation. The central claim is that governance difficulty rises when mutation is rapid, downstream coupling is strong, reversibility is weak, and observability is low, creating a systematic mismatch between the layers that most affect behavior and the layers humans can most easily inspect. I formalize this intuition with simple drift, governance-load, and hysteresis quantities, connect the framework to recent work on temporal identity in language-model agents, and report a preliminary ratchet experiment in which reverting an agent's visible self-description after memory accumulation fails to restore baseline behavior. In that experiment, the estimated identity hysteresis ratio is 0.68. The main implication is that the salient failure mode for persistent self-modifying agents is not abrupt misalignment but compositional drift: locally reasonable updates that accumulate into a behavioral trajectory that was never explicitly authorized.

AI.tldr: Proposes a five-layer 'layered mutability' framework for persistent language-model agents, linking rapid mutation, strong downstream coupling, weak reversibility, and low observability to higher governance difficulty. Introduces drift, governance-load, and hysteresis metrics; connects to temporal identity; reports a preliminary ratchet experiment with identity hysteresis ~0.68, arguing that compositional drift—not abrupt misalignment—is the key failure mode.
AI.motivation: Tackle governance and interpretability challenges of self-modifying agents whose internal states can influence future actions, across multiple mutable layers, and align long-term behavior with human intent.
AI.method: Formalizes a five-layer mutability model: pretraining, post-training alignment, self-narrative, memory, and weight-level adaptation. Defines drift, governance-load, and hysteresis metrics; situates framework relative to temporal identity work; conducts a preliminary ratchet experiment to test reversibility of self-description after memory accumulation.
AI.result: Provides a formal framework with quantitative metrics; empirical evidence from a ratchet experiment showing an identity hysteresis ratio of 0.68, suggesting partial irreversibility and a drift-based failure mode.
AI.conclusion: Persistent self-modifying agents are prone to compositional drift across layers; governance is harder when mutation is rapid, coupling is strong, reversibility is weak, and observability is low. Calls for cross-layer governance and design strategies to constrain drift.

title: Expressivity of Transformers: A Tropical Geometry Perspective
summary: To quantify the geometric expressivity of transformers, we introduce a tropical geometry framework to characterize their exact spatial partitioning capabilities. By modeling self-attention as a vector-valued tropical rational map, we prove it evaluates exactly to a Power Voronoi Diagram in the zero-temperature limit. Building on this equivalence, we establish a combinatorial rationale for Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA): via the Minkowski sum of Newton polytopes, multi-head aggregation expands the polyhedral complexity to $\mathcal{O}(N^H)$, overcoming the $\mathcal{O}(N)$ bottleneck of single heads. Extending this to deep architectures, we derive the first tight asymptotic bounds on the number of linear regions in transformers ($Θ(N^{d_{\text{model}}L})$), demonstrating a combinatorial explosion driven intrinsically by sequence length $N$, ambient embedding dimension $d_{\text{model}}$, and network depth $L$. Importantly, we guarantee that this idealized polyhedral skeleton is geometrically stable: finite-temperature soft attention preserves these topological partitions via exponentially tight differential approximation bounds.

AI.tldr: A tropical-geometry analysis of transformers: self-attention maps to Power Voronoi in the zero-temperature limit; MHSA increases polyhedral complexity via Minkowski sums; tight asymptotic bounds on linear regions are Theta(N^{d_model L}); finite-temperature soft attention preserves these partitions with exponential approximation bounds.
AI.motivation: Quantify and understand the geometric expressivity of transformer architectures, provide a principled combinatorial and polyhedral perspective on attention mechanisms, and establish fundamental bounds linking sequence length, embedding dimension, and depth.
AI.method: Model self-attention as a vector-valued tropical rational map; prove exact equivalence to a Power Voronoi diagram in the zero-temperature limit; analyze MHSA via Minkowski sums of Newton polytopes to derive polyhedral complexity; derive tight asymptotic bounds on the number of linear regions in Transformers as Theta(N^{d_model L}); study finite-temperature soft attention to establish stability of the polyhedral partitions with exponentially tight differential approximation bounds.
AI.result: Exact equivalence: self-attention evaluates to a Power Voronoi diagram in the zero-temperature limit. MHSA expands polyhedral complexity to O(N^H) through Minkowski sums. For deep transformers, number of linear regions is tightly bounded by Theta(N^{d_model L}). Finite-temperature soft attention preserves these partitions with exponentially tight approximation bounds, ensuring geometric stability of the skeleton.
AI.conclusion: The authors provide a rigorous polyhedral framework that characterizes the transformer's geometry, showing that expressivity grows combinatorially with sequence length, model dimension, and depth, and that the idealized polyhedral structure is robust under smooth attention approximations.

title: OmniGCD: Abstracting Generalized Category Discovery for Modality Agnosticism
summary: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) challenges methods to identify known and novel classes using partially labeled data, mirroring human category learning. Unlike prior GCD methods, which operate within a single modality and require dataset-specific fine-tuning, we propose a modality-agnostic GCD approach inspired by the human brain's abstract category formation. Our $\textbf{OmniGCD}$ leverages modality-specific encoders (e.g., vision, audio, text, remote sensing) to process inputs, followed by dimension reduction to construct a $\textbf{GCD latent space}$, which is transformed at test-time into a representation better suited for clustering using a novel synthetically trained Transformer-based model. To evaluate OmniGCD, we introduce a $\textbf{zero-shot GCD setting}$ where no dataset-specific fine-tuning is allowed, enabling modality-agnostic category discovery. $\textbf{Trained once on synthetic data}$, OmniGCD performs zero-shot GCD across 16 datasets spanning four modalities, improving classification accuracy for known and novel classes over baselines (average percentage point improvement of $\textbf{+6.2}$, $\textbf{+17.9}$, $\textbf{+1.5}$ and $\textbf{+12.7}$ for vision, text, audio and remote sensing). This highlights the importance of strong encoders while decoupling representation learning from category discovery. Improving modality-agnostic methods will propagate across modalities, enabling encoder development independent of GCD. Our work serves as a benchmark for future modality-agnostic GCD works, paving the way for scalable, human-inspired category discovery. All code is available $\href{https://github.com/Jordan-HS/OmniGCD}{here}$

AI.tldr: Modality-agnostic Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) via OmniGCD: modality-specific encoders feed into a shared reduced latent space, with a transformer-based mapper trained on synthetic data for zero-shot clustering across 16 datasets and 4 modalities; outperforms baselines and is released as open-source.
AI.motivation: GCD must identify known and novel classes across diverse modalities without dataset-specific fine-tuning. Prior single-modality methods limit applicability and require up-front tuning. Inspired by human abstract category formation, OmniGCD aims to decouple representation learning from category discovery and enable cross-modality generalization.
AI.method: Apply modality-specific encoders (vision, text, audio, remote sensing) to extract features, reduce dimensionality to a shared GCD latent space, then apply a test-time Transformer-based model trained on synthetic data to map representations to a clustering-friendly space for zero-shot GCD. Train once on synthetic data; evaluate zero-shot across 16 datasets spanning four modalities; provide benchmark and code.
AI.result: Across 16 datasets and four modalities, OmniGCD yields average improvements in known-novel class discrimination: vision +6.2 pp, text +17.9 pp, audio +1.5 pp, remote sensing +12.7 pp, indicating that strong encoders and decoupled representation learning aid cross-modal category discovery.
AI.conclusion: OmniGCD introduces a benchmark and methodology for modality-agnostic GCD, demonstrating that encoder quality and a synthetic-data trained transformer can support zero-shot discovery across diverse modalities. The work advocates modular encoders and a shared latent GCD space as a path toward scalable, human-inspired category discovery; code is released for further research.

title: The Agentification of Scientific Research: A Physicist's Perspective
summary: This article argues that the most important significance of the AI revolution, especially the rise of large language models, lies not simply in automation, but in a fundamental change in how complex information and human know-how are carried, replicated, and shared. From this perspective, AI for Science is especially important because it may transform not only the efficiency of research, but also the structure of scientific collaboration, discovery, publishing, and evaluation. The article outlines a gradual path from AI as a research tool to AI as a scientific collaborator, and discusses how AI is likely to fundamentally reshape scientific publication. It also argues that continuous learning and diversity of ideas are essential if AI is to play a meaningful role in original scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: AI, especially large language models, will transform how complex information and know-how are carried, replicated, and shared, reshaping scientific collaboration, publishing, and evaluation; the field may move from AI as a tool to a scientific collaborator, requiring continuous learning and diverse ideas.
AI.motivation: Reassess the significance of AI in science beyond automation and explore implications for collaboration, publication, and evaluation; outline a pathway for AI to become a scientific collaborator.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and scenario-based reasoning; proposes a phased transition from AI as a research tool to a scientific collaborator; discusses structural implications for publication and evaluation.
AI.result: Argues that AI for Science can alter research efficiency, collaboration structures, discovery processes, publishing formats, and evaluation; highlights the potential role of continuous learning and diversity in enabling original discovery.
AI.conclusion: To realize AI's transformative potential, ongoing learning and diverse, plural perspectives are essential; AI's integration into science is likely to reshape the entire scientific enterprise.

title: Assessing the Performance-Efficiency Trade-off of Foundation Models in Probabilistic Electricity Price Forecasting
comment: Submitted to the 7th International Workshop on Energy Data and Analytics (EDA), held in conjunction with ACM e-Energy 2026
summary: Large-scale renewable energy deployment introduces pronounced volatility into the electricity system, turning grid operation into a complex stochastic optimization problem. Accurate electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential not only to support operational decisions, such as optimal bidding strategies and balancing power preparation, but also to reduce economic risk and improve market efficiency. Probabilistic forecasts are particularly valuable because they quantify uncertainty stemming from renewable intermittency, market coupling, and regulatory changes, enabling market participants to make informed decisions that minimize losses and optimize expected revenues. However, it remains an open question which models to employ to produce accurate forecasts. Should these be task-specific machine learning (ML) models or Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs)? In this work, we compare four models for day-ahead probabilistic EPF (PEPF) in European bidding zones: a deterministic NHITS backbone with Quantile-Regression Averaging (NHITS+QRA) and a conditional Normalizing-Flow forecaster (NF) are compared with two TSFMs, namely Moirai and ChronosX. On the one hand, we find that TSFMs outperform task-specific deep learning models trained from scratch in terms of CRPS, Energy Score, and predictive interval calibration across market conditions. On the other hand, we find that well-configured task-specific models, particularly NHITS combined with QRA, achieve performance very close to TSFMs, and in some scenarios, such as when supplied with additional informative feature groups or adapted via few-shot learning from other European markets, they can even surpass TSFMs. Overall, our findings show that while TSFMs offer expressive modeling capabilities, conventional models remain highly competitive, emphasizing the need to weigh computational expense against marginal performance improvements in PEPF.

AI.tldr: TSFMs outperform task-specific deep-learning models for day-ahead probabilistic electricity price forecasting, but well-configured task-specific models like NHITS+QRA remain highly competitive and can surpass TSFMs in certain settings; computational cost versus marginal gain is a key consideration.
AI.motivation: Renewable energy integration increases price volatility and uncertainty. Accurate probabilistic forecasts are essential for bidding, balancing, risk management, and market efficiency. The study questions whether to use task-specific ML models or Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) for probabilistic EPF (PEPF).
AI.method: Empirical comparison of four models for day-ahead probabilistic EPF across European bidding zones: (1) NHITS backbone with Quantile-Regression Averaging (NHITS+QRA), (2) a conditional Normalizing-Flow forecaster (NF), and two TSFMs (Moirai and ChronosX).
AI.result: TSFMs outperform task-specific deep learning models trained from scratch in CRPS, Energy Score, and predictive interval calibration across market conditions. However, well-configured task-specific models, especially NHITS+QRA, achieve performance close to TSFMs and can surpass them in scenarios with extra informative features or few-shot transfer from other European markets.
AI.conclusion: Time Series Foundation Models offer strong expressive modeling but conventional task-specific models remain highly competitive. Practical deployments should weigh computational expense against marginal performance gains in probabilistic electricity price forecasting.

title: AIM: Asymmetric Information Masking for Visual Question Answering Continual Learning
comment: 18 pages, 9 figures. Submitted to ACM MM 2026
summary: In continual visual question answering (VQA), existing Continual Learning (CL) methods are mostly built for symmetric, unimodal architectures. However, modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) violate this assumption, as their trainable components are inherently asymmetric. This structural mismatch renders VLMs highly prone to catastrophic forgetting when learning from continuous data streams. Specifically, the asymmetry causes standard global regularization to favor the massive language decoder during optimization, leaving the smaller but critical visual projection layers highly vulnerable to interference. Consequently, this localized degradation leads to a severe loss of compositional reasoning capabilities. To address this, we propose Asymmetric Information Masking (AIM), which balances stability and plasticity by applying targeted masks based on modality-specific sensitivity. Experiments on VQA v2 and GQA under continual VQA settings show that AIM achieves state-of-the-art performance in both Average Performance (AP) and Average Forgetting (AF), while better preserving generalization to novel skill-concept compositions.

AI.tldr: Asymmetric Information Masking (AIM) mitigates catastrophic forgetting in continual VQA with Vision-Language Models by applying modality-specific masks to balance stability and plasticity, achieving state-of-the-art average performance and forgetting metrics on VQA v2 and GQA, with better generalization to novel compositions.
AI.motivation: VLMs are structurally asymmetric (large language decoder vs smaller visual encoders). Global regularization biases learning toward the decoder, causing forgetting in visual components and degraded compositional reasoning during continual learning.
AI.method: Introduce AIM, which applies targeted masks based on modality sensitivity to constrain updates in critical visual projection layers while allowing language components to adapt, balancing stability and plasticity in continual VQA.
AI.result: On VQA v2 and GQA under continual VQA settings, AIM attains state-of-the-art Average Performance (AP) and Average Forgetting (AF) and preserves generalization to novel skill-concept compositions.
AI.conclusion: Masking guided by modality sensitivity effectively mitigates forgetting in asymmetric VLMs, improving continual VQA performance and robustness of compositional reasoning.

title: Personalized and Context-Aware Transformer Models for Predicting Post-Intervention Physiological Responses from Wearable Sensor Data
summary: Consumer wearables enable continuous measurement of physiological data related to stress and recovery, but turning these streams into actionable, personalized stress-management recommendations remains a challenge. In practice, users often do not know how a given intervention, defined as an activity intended to reduce stress, will affect heart rate (HR), heart rate variability (HRV), or inter-beat intervals (BBI) over the next 15 to 120 minutes. We present a framework that predicts post-intervention trajectories and the direction of change for these physiological indicators across time windows. Our methodology combines a Transformer model for multi-horizon trajectories of percent change relative to a pre-intervention baseline, direction-of-change calls (positive, negative, or neutral) at each horizon, and an empirical study using wearable sensor data overlaid with user-tagged events and interventions. This proof of concept shows that personalized post-intervention prediction is feasible. We encourage future integration into stress-management tools for personalized intervention recommendations tailored to each person's day following further validation in larger studies and, where applicable, appropriate regulatory review.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based framework to forecast post-intervention trajectories of wearable-derived physiological signals (HR, HRV, BBI) to guide personalized stress-management interventions; evidence from a proof-of-concept study.
AI.motivation: Users need actionable, personalized predictions of how interventions affect physiological stress markers over minutes to hours to tailor recommendations.
AI.method: Multi-horizon trajectory forecasting using a Transformer for percent-change relative to baseline, along with direction-of-change calls at each horizon; empirical data from wearables with user-tagged events.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept demonstrates feasibility of personalized post-intervention predictions.
AI.conclusion: Framework shows promise for integration into stress-management tools pending validation in larger studies and regulatory review.

title: Wasserstein Formulation of Reinforcement Learning. An Optimal Transport Perspective on Policy Optimization
summary: We present a geometric framework for Reinforcement Learning (RL) that views policies as maps into the Wasserstein space of action probabilities. First, we define a Riemannian structure induced by stationary distributions, proving its existence in a general context. We then define the tangent space of policies and characterize the geodesics, specifically addressing the measurability of vector fields mapped from the state space to the tangent space of probability measures over the action space. Next, we formulate a general RL optimization problem and construct a gradient flow using Otto's calculus. We compute the gradient and the Hessian of the energy, providing a formal second-order analysis. Finally, we illustrate the method with numerical examples for low-dimensional problems, computing the gradient directly from our theoretical formalism. For high-dimensional problems, we parameterize the policy using a neural network and optimize it based on an ergodic approximation of the cost.

AI.tldr: A geometric RL framework that treats policies as points in Wasserstein space, endowing the policy manifold with a Riemannian metric induced by stationary distributions, and develops gradient/Hessian calculus via Otto's calculus for energy functionals, with both low-dimensional numerical demonstrations and neural-network-based high-dimensional extensions using ergodic cost approximations.
AI.motivation: To provide a principled geometric and second-order (Riemannian) perspective on policy optimization, enabling gradient and Hessian analysis in policy space through optimal transport geometry, and to bridge theory with scalable approximations.
AI.method: Define a Riemannian structure on the policy space using stationary distributions; characterize the tangent space of policies and geodesics with attention to measurability of vector fields mapping states to probability measures over actions; formulate a general RL objective and derive a gradient flow via Otto’s calculus; compute the gradient and Hessian of the energy functional; validate with numerical experiments in low dimension and extend to high dimension by parameterizing policies with neural networks and using an ergodic approximation of the cost.
AI.result: Derived explicit gradient and Hessian expressions for the energy in the Wasserstein/Riemannian framework; characterized geodesics and measurability conditions; demonstrated tractability on low-dimensional problems by computing gradients from the theory; proposed a scalable neural-network-based approach for high-dimensional problems using ergodic cost approximations.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a rigorous geometric lens for RL policy optimization, enabling gradient-based updates grounded in optimal transport geometry; it shows practical feasibility for low-dimensional problems and a viable path to scalability in high dimensions via neural-network parametrization with ergodic cost approximations, albeit with computational considerations inherent to OT-inspired methods.

title: Integrating Object Detection, LiDAR-Enhanced Depth Estimation, and Segmentation Models for Railway Environments
summary: Obstacle detection in railway environments is crucial for ensuring safety. However, very few studies address the problem using a complete, modular, and flexible system that can both detect objects in the scene and estimate their distance from the vehicle. Most works focus solely on detection, others attempt to identify the track, and only a few estimate obstacle distances. Additionally, evaluating these systems is challenging due to the lack of ground truth data. In this paper, we propose a modular and flexible framework that identifies the rail track, detects potential obstacles, and estimates their distance by integrating three neural networks for object detection, track segmentation, and monocular depth estimation with LiDAR point clouds. To enable a reliable and quantitative evaluation, the proposed framework is assessed using a synthetic dataset (SynDRA), which provides accurate ground truth annotations, allowing for direct performance comparison with existing methods. The proposed system achieves a mean absolute error (MAE) as low as 0.63 meters by integrating monocular depth maps with LiDAR, enabling not only accurate distance estimates but also spatial perception of the scene.

AI.tldr: A modular framework combines object detection, track segmentation, and monocular depth estimation with LiDAR to detect obstacles and estimate distance in railway scenes, evaluated on SynDRA with MAE 0.63 m.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of a complete, modular system that jointly detects obstacles and estimates their distance in railway environments; previous work tends to focus on detection or track identification, and evaluation is hindered by scarce ground-truth data.
AI.method: Integrates three neural networks for object detection, track segmentation, and monocular depth estimation, and fuses monocular depth maps with LiDAR point clouds. Evaluation is conducted on the SynDRA synthetic dataset providing ground-truth annotations for robust quantitative benchmarking.
AI.result: Achieves mean absolute error (MAE) as low as 0.63 meters for distance estimation, demonstrating that fusing monocular depth with LiDAR improves spatial perception and distance accuracy.
AI.conclusion: A flexible, modular pipeline enables reliable obstacle detection and distance estimation in railway settings, with quantitative evaluation on a synthetic dataset enabling direct comparison to existing methods.

title: Disentangle-then-Refine: LLM-Guided Decoupling and Structure-Aware Refinement for Graph Contrastive Learning
summary: Conventional Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) on Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) relies on blind stochastic augmentations, inadvertently entangling task-relevant signals with noise. We propose SDM-SCR, a robust framework anchored in Approximate Orthogonal Decomposition. First, the Semantic Decoupling Module (SDM) leverages the instruction-following capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to actively parse raw attributes into asymmetric, task-oriented signal and noise views. This shifts the paradigm from random perturbation to semantic-aware disentanglement. Subsequently, Semantic Consistency Regularization (SCR) exploits the spectral observation that semantic signals are topologically smooth while residual noise is high-frequency. SCR functions as a selective spectral filter, enforcing consistency only on the signal subspace to eliminate LLM hallucinations without over-smoothing. This ``Disentangle-then-Refine'' mechanism ensures rigorous signal purification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SDM-SCR achieves SOTA performance in accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Introduces SDM-SCR, a robust GCL framework for text-attributed graphs that replaces random augmentations with LLM-guided semantic disentanglement (signal vs noise) and a spectral regularizer that enforces consistency on the signal subspace, yielding state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Conventional Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) on Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) relies on blind stochastic augmentations that conflate task-relevant signals with noise; a semantic-aware approach is needed to disentangle signals from noise.
AI.method: SDM uses an instruction-following Large Language Model (LLM) to actively parse raw attributes into asymmetric, task-oriented signal and noise views. SCR then operates as a spectral regularizer by enforcing consistency only on the signal subspace, leveraging the observation that semantic signals are smooth on the graph while residual noise is high-frequency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments report state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency on TAGs, demonstrating robust signal purification and improved performance over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Disentangle-then-Refine: a two-stage framework that first extracts semantically meaningful signals via LLM-guided SDM and then refines them with SCR to suppress noise without over-smoothing, achieving robust GCL on text-attributed graphs.

title: Constraint-based Pre-training: From Structured Constraints to Scalable Model Initialization
summary: The pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm has become the dominant approach for model adaptation. However, conventional pre-training typically yields models at a fixed scale, whereas practical deployment often requires models of varying sizes, exposing its limitations when target model scales differ from those used during pre-training. To address this, we propose an innovative constraint-based pre-training paradigm that imposes structured constraints during pre-training to disentangle size-agnostic knowledge into reusable weight templates, while assigning size-specific adaptation to lightweight weight scalers, thereby reformulating variable-sized model initialization as a multi-task adaptation problem. Within this paradigm, we further introduce WeiT, which employs Kronecker-based constraints to regularize the pre-training process. Specifically, model parameters are represented as compositions of weight templates via concatenation and weighted aggregation, with adaptive connections governed by lightweight weight scalers whose parameters are learned from limited data. This design enables flexible and efficient construction of model weights across diverse downstream scales. Extensive experiments demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of WeiT, achieving state-of-the-art performance in initializing models with varying depths and widths across a broad range of perception and embodied learning tasks, including Image Classification, Image Generation, and Embodied Control. Moreover, its effectiveness generalizes to both Transformer-based and Convolution-based architectures, consistently enabling faster convergence and improved performance even under full training.

AI.tldr: WeiT introduces a constraint-based pre-training framework that decouples size-agnostic knowledge into reusable weight templates and assigns size-specific adaptation to lightweight weight scalers, enabling flexible initialization across variable model depths and widths for Transformer and CNN architectures.
AI.motivation: Standard pre-training yields fixed-scale models, which hinders deployment on target scales differing from pre-training. A mechanism to generate models at arbitrary sizes from shared templates is highly desirable for efficiency and adaptability.
AI.method: Model parameters are formed as compositions of weight templates via concatenation and weighted aggregation, with adaptive connections controlled by lightweight weight scalers derived from limited data. Kronecker-based constraints regularize pre-training to promote reusable templates. The approach frames initialization across varying scales as a multi-task adaptation problem and is compatible with both Transformer-based and Convolution-based architectures across perception and embodied learning tasks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show WeiT achieves state-of-the-art initialization for models with varying depths and widths across Image Classification, Image Generation, and Embodied Control. It generalizes to both Transformer and CNN architectures and accelerates convergence and improves performance, even when trained end-to-end.
AI.conclusion: Constraint-based pre-training with size-agnostic weight templates and scale-specific adapters enables flexible, efficient multi-scale model initialization across diverse architectures and tasks, with broad applicability and data-efficient adaptation.

title: One-shot Compositional 3D Head Avatars with Deformable Hair
summary: We propose a compositional method for constructing a complete 3D head avatar from a single image. Prior one-shot holistic approaches frequently fail to produce realistic hair dynamics during animation, largely due to inadequate decoupling of hair from the facial region, resulting in entangled geometry and unnatural deformations. Our method explicitly decouples hair from the face, modeling these components using distinct deformation paradigms while integrating them into a unified rendering pipeline. Furthermore, by leveraging image-to-3D lifting techniques, we preserve fine-grained textures from the input image to the greatest extent possible, effectively mitigating the common issue of high-frequency information loss in generalized models. Specifically, given a frontal portrait image, we first perform hair removal to obtain a bald image. Both the original image and the bald image are then lifted to dense, detail-rich 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) representations. For the bald 3DGS, we rig it to a FLAME mesh via non-rigid registration with a prior model, enabling natural deformation that follows the mesh triangles during animation. For the hair component, we employ semantic label supervision combined with a boundary-aware reassignment strategy to extract a clean and isolated set of hair Gaussians. To control hair deformation, we introduce a cage structure that supports Position-Based Dynamics (PBD) simulation, allowing realistic and physically plausible transformations of the hair Gaussian primitives under head motion, gravity, and inertial effects. Striking qualitative results, including dynamic animations under diverse head motions, gravity effects, and expressions, showcase substantially more realistic hair behavior alongside faithfully preserved facial details, outperforming state-of-the-art one-shot methods in perceptual realism.

AI.tldr: Decoupled hair and face in a single-image 3D head avatar using 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and a hair cage with Position-Based Dynamics (PBD); bald head is lifted to 3DGS and rigged to FLAME, while hair Gaussians are semantically supervised and deformed physically, yielding realistic hair dynamics and preserved facial textures.
AI.motivation: Address prevalent failures of one-shot holistic methods: entangled hair–face geometry causing unrealistic hair dynamics and loss of high-frequency details during texture lifting. Provide a modular pipeline that maintains facial fidelity while enabling plausible hair animation.
AI.method: 1) Input frontal image; compute a bald version by hair removal. 2) Lift both images to dense 3D Gaussian Splatting representations. 3) Rig bald 3DGS to a FLAME mesh via non-rigid registration for natural animation. 4) Extract hair Gaussians using semantic label supervision and boundary-aware reassignment to obtain a clean hair set. 5) Use a cage structure and Position-Based Dynamics to drive hair deformation under head motion, gravity, and inertia. 6) Integrate into a unified rendering pipeline.
AI.result: Qualitative results show striking realistic hair dynamics during diverse head motions, gravity effects, and expressions, with faithful preservation of facial details and improved perceptual realism over state-of-the-art one-shot methods.
AI.conclusion: Explicit decoupling of hair from the face with distinct deformation paradigms and a PBD-driven hair model, combined with high-fidelity texture lifting, yields more realistic dynamic head avatars than prior one-shot approaches.

title: CoTEvol: Self-Evolving Chain-of-Thoughts for Data Synthesis in Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong mathematical reasoning when trained on high-quality Chain-of-Thought (CoT) that articulates intermediate steps, yet costly CoT curation hinders further progress. While existing remedies such as distillation from stronger LLMs and self-synthesis based on test-time search alleviate this issue, they often suffer from diminishing returns or high computing overhead.In this work, we propose CoTEvol, a genetic evolutionary framework that casts CoT generation as a population-based search over reasoning trajectories.Candidate trajectories are iteratively evolved through reflective global crossover at the trajectory level and local mutation guided by uncertainty at the step level, enabling holistic recombination and fine-grained refinement. Lightweight, task-aware fitness functions are designed to guide the evolutionary process toward accurate and diverse reasoning. Empirically, CoTEvol improves correct-CoT synthesis success by over 30% and enhances structural diversity, with markedly improved efficiency. LLMs trained on these evolutionary CoT data achieve an average gain of 6.6% across eight math benchmarks, outperforming previous distillation and self-synthesis approaches. These results underscore the promise of evolutionary CoT synthesis as a scalable and effective method for mathematical reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces CoTEvol, a genetic evolutionary framework for generating chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning trajectories to improve mathematical reasoning in LLMs; uses trajectory-level crossover and step-level mutation; achieves improved CoT synthesis and downstream math accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reduce the cost and inefficiency of high-quality CoT data while improving reasoning quality and diversity by treating CoT generation as an evolutionary search over trajectories.
AI.method: Maintain a population of CoT trajectories; apply global crossover across trajectories to recombine reasoning patterns and local mutation guided by uncertainty at individual steps; use lightweight, task-aware fitness functions to optimize for accuracy and diversity; iteratively evolve trajectories and use them to train LLMs.
AI.result: CoTEvol yields >30% improvement in correct-CoT synthesis success and increased structural diversity; it also improves efficiency. LLMs trained on evolutionary CoT data gain an average of 6.6% across eight math benchmarks, outperforming distillation and self-synthesis approaches.
AI.conclusion: Evolutionary CoT synthesis is a scalable and effective approach for mathematical reasoning tasks, offering a compelling alternative to costly CoT data curation and existing synthesis methods.

title: From Boundaries to Semantics: Prompt-Guided Multi-Task Learning for Petrographic Thin-section Segmentation
summary: Grain-edge segmentation (GES) and lithology semantic segmentation (LSS) are two pivotal tasks for quantifying rock fabric and composition. However, these two tasks are often treated separately, and the segmentation quality is implausible albeit expensive, time-consuming, and expert-annotated datasets have been used. Recently, foundation models, especially the Segment Anything Model (SAM), have demonstrated impressive robustness for boundary alignment. However, directly adapting SAM to joint GES and LSS is nontrivial due to 1) severe domain gap induced by extinction-dependent color variations and ultra-fine grain boundaries, and 2) lacking novel modules for joint learning on multi-angle petrographic image stacks. In this paper, we propose Petro-SAM, a novel two-stage, multi-task framework that can achieve high-quality joint GES and LSS on petrographic images. Specifically, based on SAM, we introduce a Merge Block to integrate seven polarized views, effectively solving the extinction issue. Moreover, we introduce multi-scale feature fusion and color-entropy priors to refine the detection.

AI.tldr: A two-stage, multi-task Petro-SAM framework extending SAM to jointly perform grain-edge segmentation (GES) and lithology semantic segmentation (LSS) on petrographic images, addressing extinction-induced color gaps by fusing seven polarized views with a Merge Block, and enhancing results via multi-scale feature fusion and color-entropy priors.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, joint GES and LSS on petrographic images using foundation models like SAM, overcoming domain gaps from extinction-dependent color variations and the lack of multi-angle joint-learning modules.
AI.method: A two-stage framework built on SAM. Introduces a Merge Block to integrate seven polarized views to mitigate extinction effects; uses multi-scale feature fusion and color-entropy priors to refine boundary alignment and segmentation; targets joint learning for GES and LSS on multi-angle petrographic stacks.
AI.result: No quantitative results provided in the abstract; claims indicate improved boundary alignment and refined detection for joint GES and LSS via the proposed Merge Block and priors.
AI.conclusion: Petro-SAM offers a viable approach to joint GES and LSS on petrographic images, addressing domain gaps from extinction and leveraging multi-angle fusion and priors to enhance segmentation quality; sets the stage for quantitative evaluation on petrographic datasets.

title: MirrorBench: Evaluating Self-centric Intelligence in MLLMs by Introducing a Mirror
summary: Recent progress in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has demonstrated remarkable advances in perception and reasoning, suggesting their potential for embodied intelligence. While recent studies have evaluated embodied MLLMs in interactive settings, current benchmarks mainly target capabilities to perceive, understand, and interact with external objects, lacking a systematic evaluation of self-centric intelligence. To address this, we introduce MirrorBench, a simulation-based benchmark inspired by the classical Mirror Self-Recognition (MSR) test in psychology. MirrorBench extends this paradigm to embodied MLLMs through a tiered framework of progressively challenging tasks, assessing agents from basic visual perception to high-level self-representation. Experiments on leading MLLMs show that even at the lowest level, their performance remains substantially inferior to human performance, revealing fundamental limitations in self-referential understanding. Our study bridges psychological paradigms and embodied intelligence, offering a principled framework for evaluating the emergence of general intelligence in large models. Project page: https://fflahm.github.io/mirror-bench-page/.

AI.tldr: MirrorBench is a psychology-inspired, simulation-based benchmark for embodied MLLMs to assess self-centric intelligence via Mirror Self-Recognition tasks; results show AI gaps even at basic levels, highlighting limits in self-awareness and providing a framework to study emergent general intelligence in large models.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for embodied MLLMs emphasize perception and interaction with external objects but neglect self-referential or self-representation capabilities. MirrorBench addresses this gap by evaluating self-centric intelligence through MSR-inspired tasks.
AI.method: A tiered, simulation-based benchmark (MirrorBench) that adapts the Mirror Self-Recognition paradigm to embodied MLLMs, with progressively challenging tasks spanning from basic visual perception to high-level self-representation; evaluated on leading MLLMs with quantitative metrics.
AI.result: Experiments show that leading MLLMs perform substantially worse than humans even at the lowest tier, indicating fundamental limitations in self-referential understanding.
AI.conclusion: The framework bridges psychological paradigms and embodied intelligence, offering a principled approach to evaluating the emergence of general intelligence in large models and guiding future research into self-centric AI capabilities.

title: NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction: Methods and Results
summary: This paper presents an overview of the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction. The goal of the challenge participants was to develop automatic saliency map prediction methods for the provided video sequences. The novel dataset of 2,000 diverse videos with an open license was prepared for this challenge. The fixations and corresponding saliency maps were collected using crowdsourced mouse tracking and contain viewing data from over 5,000 assessors. Evaluation was performed on a subset of 800 test videos using generally accepted quality metrics. The challenge attracted over 20 teams making submissions, and 7 teams passed the final phase with code review. All data used in this challenge is made publicly available - https://github.com/msu-video-group/NTIRE26_Saliency_Prediction.

AI.tldr: NTIRE 2026 Video Saliency Prediction Challenge: a 2,000-video open dataset with crowdsourced fixation data from 5k+ assessors; 800-video test subset; 20+ submissions; 7 teams passed final code review; data publicly available.
AI.motivation: Advance automatic video saliency prediction by providing a large, diverse, openly licensed benchmark and standardized evaluation to enable cross-method comparison.
AI.method: Dataset creation: 2,000 diverse videos with open license. Fixations and saliency maps collected via crowdsourced mouse-tracking from >5,000 assessors. Evaluation conducted on 800-video test subset using standard saliency metrics. Competition organized with 20+ submissions, 7 teams passed final phase after code review.
AI.result: Dataset released publicly; 7 teams demonstrated compliant methods; broader community access enables benchmarking and method development.
AI.conclusion: Public dataset and benchmark will accelerate progress in video saliency prediction by enabling reproducible evaluation and cross-method comparisons.

title: CogEvolution: A Human-like Generative Educational Agent to Simulate Student's Cognitive Evolution
summary: Generative Agents, owing to their precise modeling and simulation capabilities of human behavior, have become a pivotal tool in the field of Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIEd) for uncovering complex cognitive processes of learners. However, existing educational agents predominantly rely on static personas to simulate student learning behaviors, neglecting the decisive role of deep cognitive capabilities in learning outcomes during practice interactions. Furthermore, they struggle to characterize the dynamic fluidity of knowledge internalization, transfer, and cognitive state transitions. To overcome this bottleneck, this paper proposes a human-like educational agent capable of simulating student cognitive evolution: CogEvolution. Specifically, we first construct a cognitive depth perceptron based on the Interactive, Constructive, Active, Passive (ICAP) taxonomy from cognitive psychology, achieving precise quantification of learner cognitive engagement. Subsequently, we propose a memory retrieval method based on Item Response Theory (IRT) to simulate the connection and assimilation of new and prior knowledge. Finally, we design a dynamic cognitive update mechanism based on evolutionary algorithms to simulate the real-time integration of student learning behaviors and cognitive evolution processes. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that CogEvolution not only significantly outperforms baseline models in behavioral fidelity and learning curve fitting but also uniquely reproduces plausible and robust cognitive evolutionary paths consistent with educational psychology expectations, providing a novel paradigm for constructing highly interpretable educational agents.

AI.tldr: CogEvolution is a human-like educational agent that dynamically models student cognitive evolution using an ICAP-based cognitive depth measure, IRT-based memory retrieval, and an evolutionary-algorithm update, achieving higher behavioral fidelity and interpretable cognitive trajectories than baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing educational agents rely on static personas and cannot capture dynamic cognitive state transitions and knowledge internalization during practice. The work aims to simulate genuine cognitive evolution to improve interpretability and realism in AIEd agents.
AI.method: Three components: (1) a cognitive-depth perceptron based on the ICAP taxonomy to quantify learner engagement; (2) an memory retrieval model grounded in Item Response Theory to link new and prior knowledge; (3) a dynamic cognitive update mechanism driven by evolutionary algorithms to simulate real-time integration of learning behaviors and cognitive evolution.
AI.result: CogEvolution significantly outperforms baseline models in behavioral fidelity and learning-curve fitting and uniquely reproduces plausible, robust cognitive evolution paths consistent with educational psychology expectations, offering a novel, interpretable educational agent paradigm.
AI.conclusion: The work presents CogEvolution as a novel framework for highly interpretable educational agents that can simulate dynamic cognitive evolution, with potential to advance AIEd by enabling realistic, psychology-consistent agent behaviors.

title: Curvature-Aligned Probing for Local Loss-Landscape Stabilization
summary: Local loss-landscape stabilization under sample growth is typically measured either pointwise or through isotropic averaging in the full parameter space. Despite practical value, both choices probe directions that contribute little to the dominant local deformation of strongly anisotropic neural landscapes. We recast stabilization as an observational problem and introduce a unified family of criteria parameterized by an aggregation order and a probing distribution; within this family we propose a curvature-aligned criterion $Δ_2^{(D)}$ that probes the loss increment field in the top-$D$ eigenspace of the empirical Hessian near a trained solution. Solely from a local quadratic model, we prove that $Δ_2^{(D)}$ preserves the $O(k^{-2})$ mean-squared rate of the full-space criterion while replacing ambient-dimension curvature dependence with dependence on the subspace dimension $D$; a corollary gives a closed-form spectral expression and a proposition identifies the top-$D$ eigenspace as extremal within the eigenspace-aligned family. We also derive scalable estimators based on Hessian-vector products, subspace Monte Carlo, and a closed-form Gaussian-moment proxy. On a decoder-only transformer, a curvature-aligned probe occupying a tiny fraction of parameter space already reproduces the full-space mean-squared signal to within numerical noise throughout the validated local regime, and the closed-form estimator is orders of magnitude faster than direct Monte Carlo after subspace construction.

AI.tldr: A curvature-aligned local loss stabilization criterion Δ_2^{(D)} uses the top-D Hessian eigenspace to measure loss increments, preserving the full-space mean-squared rate with subspace dimension D; it enables scalable estimators and demonstrates near full-space signal reproduction on a decoder-only transformer with a tiny subspace.
AI.motivation: Current stabilization measures either sample pointwise or average over all parameters, which misaligns with the anisotropic local curvature of neural landscapes. A subspace- and curvature-aware probe can better capture the dominant local deformation.
AI.method: Introduce a unified family of aggregation criteria parameterized by a probing distribution and aggregation order; define Δ_2^{(D)} that probes the loss increment in the top-D empirical Hessian eigenspace near a trained solution; prove under a local quadratic model that the mean-squared rate remains O(k^{-2}) while replacing ambient curvature with subspace curvature; derive a closed-form spectral expression; prove the top-D eigenspace is extremal within the family; develop scalable estimators via Hessian-vector products, subspace Monte Carlo, and a Gaussian-moment proxy.
AI.result: Theoretically, Δ_2^{(D)} preserves the same O(k^{-2}) MSE rate as the full-space criterion but with dependence on D instead of ambient dimension; the top-D eigenspace is shown to be extremal in the family; practical estimators enable scalable computation; empirically on a decoder-only transformer, a curvature-aligned probe in a tiny subspace matches the full-space mean-squared signal within numerical noise; the closed-form estimator outperforms direct Monte Carlo after subspace construction.
AI.conclusion: Curvature-aligned, subspace-focused loss stabilization is effective and scalable, capturing the dominant local curvature with reduced dimensionality; it provides both theoretical guarantees and practical estimators, validated on a transformer model.

title: Sequence Search: Automated Sequence Design using Neural Architecture Search
summary: Developing an MR sequence is challenging and remains largely constrained by human intuition. Recently, AI-driven approaches have been proposed; however, most require an initial sequence for parameter optimization or extensive training datasets, limiting their general applicability. In this study, we propose "Sequence Search," an automated sequence design framework based on neural architecture search. The method takes tissue properties, imaging parameters, and design objectives as inputs and generates pulse sequences satisfying the design objectives, without requiring prior knowledge of conventional sequence structures. Sequence Search iteratively generates candidate sequences through neural architecture search and optimizes them via a differentiable Bloch simulator and objective-specific loss functions using gradient-based learning. The framework successfully replicated conventional spin-echo, T2-weighted spin-echo, and inversion recovery sequences. Less intuitive solutions were also discovered, such as three-RF spin-echo-like sequences with reduced RF energy and refocusing phases deviating from the conventional Hahn-echo. This work establishes a generalizable framework for automated MR sequence design, highlighting the potential to explore configurations beyond conventional designs based on human intuition.

AI.tldr: An automated MR sequence design framework that uses neural architecture search (Sequence Search) to generate pulse sequences from tissue properties, imaging parameters, and design objectives without relying on conventional templates. It reproduces standard sequences and uncovers novel, energy-efficient configurations through a differentiable Bloch simulator and gradient-based optimization.
AI.motivation: Human intuition constrains MR sequence design; existing AI methods often require an initial template or large training data, limiting generality. A generalizable, template-free design framework could explore configurations beyond conventional, intuition-driven designs.
AI.method: Sequence Search framework that takes tissue properties, imaging parameters, and design objectives as input. Neural architecture search iteratively generates candidate sequences and optimizes them via a differentiable Bloch simulator using gradient-based learning with objective-specific losses, without assuming predefined sequence structures.
AI.result: Successfully replicated conventional spin-echo, T2-weighted spin-echo, and inversion recovery sequences. Also discovered less intuitive solutions, such as three-RF spin-echo-like sequences with reduced RF energy and refocusing phases deviating from Hahn-echo.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a generalizable, automated MR sequence design framework capable of exploring configurations beyond conventional designs guided by human intuition, with potential to broaden design space.

title: Does RL Expand the Capability Boundary of LLM Agents? A PASS@(k,T) Analysis
summary: Does reinforcement learning genuinely expand what LLM agents can do, or merely make them more reliable? For static reasoning, recent work answers the second: base and RL pass@k curves converge at large k. We ask whether this holds for agentic tool use, where T rounds of interaction enable compositional strategies that re-sampling cannot recover. We introduce PASS@(k,T), a two-dimensional metric that jointly varies sampling budget k and interaction depth T, separating capability expansion from efficiency improvement. Our main finding is that, contrary to the static-reasoning result, tool-use RL genuinely enlarges the capability boundary: the RL agent's pass-curve pulls above the base model's and the gap widens at large k rather than converging. The expansion is specific to compositional, sequential information gathering; on simpler tasks RL behaves as prior work predicts. Under matched training data, supervised fine-tuning regresses the boundary on the same compositional tasks, isolating self-directed exploration as the causal factor. Mechanism analysis shows RL reweights the base strategy distribution toward the subset whose downstream reasoning more often yields a correct answer, with the improvement concentrated on how the agent integrates retrieved information. These results reconcile optimistic and pessimistic readings of RL for LLMs: both are correct, on different task types.

AI.tldr: RL with tool-use expands compositional, sequential reasoning beyond base models under a two-dimensional budget (sampling k and interaction T).
AI.motivation: Resolve whether RL's gains are due to reliability or genuine capability growth in agentic LLMs; quantify with PASS@(k,T) to separate efficiency vs expansion.
AI.method: Define PASS@(k,T) metric; compare base model vs RL agent across tasks varying k and T; analyze training data effects via matched data; perform mechanism analysis by inspecting strategy distribution and information integration; test on compositional versus simpler tasks.
AI.result: RL tool-use raises the capability boundary: pass-curve for RL lies above base and gap grows with k/T; effect is specific to compositional sequential information gathering; supervised fine-tuning reduces boundary; RL improves via self-directed exploration and reweighting strategy distribution toward better downstream reasoning, especially in information integration.
AI.conclusion: RL can both enlarge capabilities and improve reliability, depending on task type; for compositional tasks, RL yields genuine expansion; implications for evaluating RL in LLMs and for task design and training data.

title: Zero-Shot Retail Theft Detection via Orchestrated Vision Models: A Model-Agnostic, Cost-Effective Alternative to Trained Single-Model Systems
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures, Code to be released at https://github.com/xHaileab/Paza-AI
summary: Retail theft costs the global economy over \$100 billion annually, yet existing AI-based detection systems require expensive custom model training on proprietary datasets and charge \$200-500/month per store. We present Paza, a zero-shot retail theft detection framework that achieves practical concealment detection without training any model. Our approach orchestrates multiple existing models in a layered pipeline - cheap object detection and pose estimation running continuously, with an expensive vision-language model (VLM) invoked only when behavioral pre-filters trigger. A multi-signal suspicion pre-filter (requiring dwell time plus at least one behavioral signal) reduces VLM invocations by 240x compared to per-frame analysis, bounding calls to <=10/minute and enabling a single GPU to serve 10-20 stores. The architecture is model-agnostic: the VLM component accepts any OpenAI-compatible endpoint, enabling operators to swap between models such as Gemma 4, Qwen3.5-Omni, GPT-4o, or future releases without code changes - ensuring the system improves as the VLM landscape evolves. We evaluate the VLM component on the DCSASS synthesized shoplifting dataset (169 clips, controlled environment), achieving 89.5% precision and 92.8% specificity at 59.3% recall zero-shot - where the recall gap is attributable to sparse frame sampling in offline evaluation rather than VLM reasoning failures, as precision and specificity are the operationally critical metrics determining false alarm rates. We present a detailed cost model showing viability at \$50-100/month per store (3-10x cheaper than commercial alternatives), and introduce a privacy-preserving design that obfuscates faces in the detection pipeline. The source code is available at https://github.com/xHaileab/Paza-AI.

AI.tldr: Paza is a zero-shot, multi-model pipeline for retail theft detection that layers cheap detectors with an on-demand VLM; it cuts VLM calls drastically, scales per-GPU to multiple stores, reduces per-store cost, preserves privacy, and is open-source.
AI.motivation: Retail theft imposes >$100B annual cost; existing AI detectors require costly custom training on proprietary data and subscription fees. A zero-shot, scalable, cost-effective solution is highly desirable.
AI.method: A layered pipeline combining continuous cheap detectors (object detection, pose estimation) with a trigger-based expensive vision-language model (VLM). A multi-signal suspicion pre-filter (dwell time + behavioral signals) triggers VLM usage; VLM is OpenAI-compatible and interchangeable. The system is model-agnostic and designed to serve 10–20 stores per GPU.
AI.result: Evaluated on the DCSASS synthetic shoplifting dataset (169 clips). Zero-shot VLM achieved 89.5% precision, 92.8% specificity, 59.3% recall. The pre-filter reduced VLM invocations by ~240x, bounding calls ≤10 per minute. Cost model suggests $50–$100/month per store (3–10x cheaper than commercial options); privacy-preserving face obfuscation is implemented; source code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Paza demonstrates that a zero-shot, layered approach can deliver practical, scalable retail theft detection with low false alarms and favorable cost, while remaining adaptable to evolving VLM APIs and preserving privacy.

title: A Comparative Study of CNN Optimization Methods for Edge AI: Exploring the Role of Early Exits
summary: Deploying deep neural networks on edge devices requires balancing accuracy, latency, and resource constraints under realistic execution conditions. To fit models within these constraints, two broad strategies have emerged: static compression techniques such as pruning and quantization, which permanently reduce model size, and dynamic approaches such as early-exit mechanisms, which adapt computational cost at runtime. While both families are widely studied in isolation, they are rarely compared under identical conditions on physical hardware. This paper presents a unified deployment-oriented comparison of static compression and dynamic early-exit mechanisms, evaluated on real edge devices using ONNX based inference pipelines. Our results show that static and dynamic techniques offer fundamentally different trade-offs for edge deployment. While pruning and quantization deliver consistent memory footprint reduction, early-exit mechanisms enable input-adaptive computation savings that static methods cannot match. Their combination proves highly effective, simultaneously reducing inference latency and memory usage with minimal accuracy loss, expanding what is achievable at the edge.

AI.tldr: A unified, hardware-grounded comparison shows static pruning/quantization and dynamic early-exit offer distinct, complementary trade-offs for edge deployment, with combined use yielding the best latency/memory reductions and minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Edge devices must balance accuracy, latency, and resources under real execution conditions. Existing work studies static compression or dynamic early-exits in isolation; there is a need for fair, hardware-grounded comparison to guide deployment choices.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on real edge hardware using ONNX-based inference pipelines. The study separately measures static compression (pruning/quantization), dynamic early-exit mechanisms, and their combination under identical conditions, focusing on latency, memory usage, and accuracy.
AI.result: Static compression reliably reduces memory usage with stable footprints. Dynamic early-exits enable input-adaptive computation savings not achievable by static methods alone. A combined approach significantly reduces both inference latency and memory usage with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: Static and dynamic strategies offer fundamentally different deployment trade-offs. Their combination is highly effective for edge deployments, expanding feasible performance by jointly reducing latency and memory while maintaining accuracy.

title: SOLIS: Physics-Informed Learning of Interpretable Neural Surrogates for Nonlinear Systems
comment: in the International Joint Conference on Neural Networks, 2026
summary: Nonlinear system identification must balance physical interpretability with model flexibility. Classical methods yield structured, control-relevant models but rely on rigid parametric forms that often miss complex nonlinearities, whereas Neural ODEs are expressive yet largely black-box. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) sit between these extremes, but inverse PINNs typically assume a known governing equation with fixed coefficients, leading to identifiability failures when the true dynamics are unknown or state-dependent. We propose \textbf{SOLIS}, which models unknown dynamics via a \emph{state-conditioned second-order surrogate model} and recasts identification as learning a Quasi-Linear Parameter-Varying (Quasi-LPV) representation, recovering interpretable natural frequency, damping, and gain without presupposing a global equation. SOLIS decouples trajectory reconstruction from parameter estimation and stabilizes training with a cyclic curriculum and \textbf{Local Physics Hints} windowed ridge-regression anchors that mitigate optimization collapse. Experiments on benchmarks show accurate parameter-manifold recovery and coherent physical rollouts from sparse data, including regimes where standard inverse methods fail.

AI.tldr: SOLIS proposes a state-conditioned second-order surrogate to identify unknown nonlinear dynamics by learning a quasi-linear parameter-varying (Quasi-LPV) representation, decoupling trajectory estimation from parameter learning, and stabilizing training with a cyclic curriculum and Local Physics Hints. It yields interpretable natural frequency, damping, and gain and coherent rollouts from sparse data, even when inverse PINNs fail.
AI.motivation: To address identifiability and interpretability gaps in nonlinear system identification. Inverse PINNs assume a known governing equation and fixed coefficients, while classical models may be rigid parametric forms; there is a need for interpretable, data-efficient identification when dynamics are unknown or state-dependent.
AI.method: SOLIS introduces a state-conditioned second-order surrogate model and casts identification as learning a quasi-linear parameter-varying representation. Key components include decoupling trajectory reconstruction from parameter estimation, a cyclic curriculum to stabilize training, and Local Physics Hints: a windowed ridge-regression anchor to prevent optimization collapse.
AI.result: Benchmark experiments show accurate recovery of parameter manifolds (natural frequency, damping, gain) and coherent physical rollouts from sparse data, including regimes where standard inverse methods fail.
AI.conclusion: SOLIS enables interpretable, physics-informed model identification without presupposing a global equation, delivering robust, state-dependent dynamics through a Quasi-LPV framework and improved identifiability and data efficiency.

title: Efficient Search of Implantable Adaptive Cells for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Purpose: Adaptive skip modules can improve medical image segmentation, but searching for them is computationally costly. Implantable Adaptive Cells (IACs) are compact NAS modules inserted into U-Net skip connections, reducing the search space compared with full-network NAS. However, the original IAC framework still requires a 200-epoch differentiable search for each backbone and dataset. Methods: We analyzed the temporal behavior of operations and edges within IAC cells during differentiable search on public medical image segmentation benchmarks. We found that operations selected in the final discrete cell typically emerge among the strongest candidates early in training, and their architecture parameters stabilize well before the final epoch. Based on this, we propose a Jensen--Shannon-divergence-based stability criterion that tracks per-edge operation-importance distributions and progressively prunes low-importance operations during search. The accelerated framework is called IAC-LTH. Results: Across four public benchmarks (ACDC, BraTS, KiTS, AMOS), several 2-D U-Net backbones, and a 2-D nnU-Net pipeline, IAC-LTH discovers IAC cells whose patient-level segmentation performance matches and sometimes slightly exceeds that of cells found by the original full-length search, while reducing wall-clock NAS cost by 3.7x to 16x across datasets and backbones. These results are consistent across architectures, benchmarks, and both non-augmented and augmented training settings, while preserving the gains of IAC-equipped U-Nets over strong attention-based and dense-skip baselines. Conclusion: Competitive IAC architectures can be identified from early-stabilizing operations without running the full search, making adaptive skip-module design more practical for medical image segmentation under realistic computational constraints.

AI.tldr: IAC-LTH accelerates neural architecture search for implantable adaptive cells in U-Net skip connections for medical image segmentation by early-stabilization-based pruning, achieving 3.7x–16x NAS speedups with performance comparable to full-length search.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost of NAS for adaptive skip modules in medical image segmentation while preserving performance gains of IAC-equipped U-Nets.
AI.method: Empirically analyze per-edge operation-importance distributions during differentiable search; introduce Jensen–Shannon divergence-based stability criterion to prune low-importance operations early; implement in IAC-LTH framework; evaluate across public benchmarks and 2D backbones/nnU-Net pipeline.
AI.result: IAC-LTH finds IAC cells whose segmentation performance matches or slightly exceeds cells from full-length search, with NAS wall-clock cost reduced by 3.7x to 16x across datasets/backbones; results robust across architectures, benchmarks, training settings; preserves gains over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Competitive IAC architectures can be identified from early-stabilizing operations without full search, enabling practical adaptive skip-module design under realistic compute.

title: Diffusion Crossover: Defining Evolutionary Recombination in Diffusion Models via Noise Sequence Interpolation
summary: Interactive Evolutionary Computation (IEC) provides a powerful framework for optimizing subjective criteria such as human preferences and aesthetics, yet it suffers from a fundamental limitation: in high-dimensional generative representations, defining crossover in a semantically consistent manner is difficult, often leading to a mutation-dominated search. In this work, we explicitly define crossover in diffusion models. We propose Diffusion crossover, which formulates evolutionary recombination as step-wise interpolation of noise sequences in the reverse process of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs). By applying spherical linear interpolation (Slerp) to the noise sequences associated with selected parent images, the proposed method generates offspring that inherit characteristics from both parents while preserving the geometric structure of the diffusion process. Furthermore, controlling the time-step range of interpolation enables a principled trade-off between diversity (exploration) and convergence (exploitation). Experimental results using PCA analysis and perceptual similarity metrics (LPIPS) demonstrate that Diffusion crossover produces perceptually smooth and semantically consistent transitions between parent images. Qualitative interactive evolution experiments further confirm that the proposed method effectively supports human-in-the-loop image exploration. These findings suggest a new perspective: diffusion models are not only powerful generators, but also structured evolutionary search spaces in which recombination can be explicitly defined and controlled.

AI.tldr: Diffusion crossover defines explicit crossover in diffusion models by interpolating noise sequences via Slerp in the DDPM reverse process, enabling semantically meaningful offspring and a controllable diversity-exploitation trade-off; validated with PCA and LPIPS and interactive human-in-the-loop experiments.
AI.motivation: Overcome the difficulty of semantically meaningful crossover in high-dimensional generative representations within interactive evolutionary computation, where mutation-dominated search degrades performance.
AI.method: Formulate crossover as step-wise interpolation of diffusion noise sequences; apply spherical linear interpolation (Slerp) on parent-associated noise sequences during the reverse diffusion process; control interpolation time-step range to balance diversity and convergence; evaluate with PCA and LPIPS metrics and qualitative human-in-the-loop experiments.
AI.result: Offspring inherit characteristics from both parents with perceptually smooth, semantically coherent transitions; diffusion crossover maintains the diffusion process geometry; time-step range enables a principled exploration-exploitation trade-off; PCA and LPIPS metrics corroborate perceptual smoothness, and interactive experiments confirm efficacy in human-in-the-loop image exploration.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models function as structured evolutionary search spaces where recombination can be explicitly defined and controlled, offering a principled method for semantic crossover in high-dimensional generative models.

title: xFODE+: Explainable Type-2 Fuzzy Additive ODEs for Uncertainty Quantification
comment: in IEEE International Conference on Fuzzy Systems, 2026
summary: Recent advances in Deep Learning (DL) have boosted data-driven System Identification (SysID), but reliable use requires Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) alongside accurate predictions. Although UQ-capable models such as Fuzzy ODE (FODE) can produce Prediction Intervals (PIs), they offer limited interpretability. We introduce Explainable Type-2 Fuzzy Additive ODEs for UQ (xFODE+), an interpretable SysID model which produces PIs alongside point predictions while retaining physically meaningful incremental states. xFODE+ implements each fuzzy additive model with Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic Systems (IT2-FLSs) and constraints membership functions to the activation of two neighboring rules, limiting overlap and keeping inference locally transparent.
