AI.result: Per-step training time matches or improves FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity >60%; on downstream tasks, matches softmax at short context and yields substantial gains in long-context settings.
AI.conclusion: AdaSplash-2 advances practical sparse α-entmax attention by enabling fast forward/backward computation and better long-context performance, showing that input-dependent sparsity can be efficiently exploited.

title: How to Correctly Make Mistakes: A Framework for Constructing and Benchmarking Mistake Aware Egocentric Procedural Videos
summary: Reliable procedural monitoring in video requires exposure to naturally occurring human errors and the recoveries that follow. In egocentric recordings, mistakes are often partially occluded by hands and revealed through subtle object state changes, while existing procedural datasets provide limited and inconsistent mistake and correction traces. We present PIE-V (Psychologically Inspired Error injection for Videos), a framework for constructing and benchmarking mistake-aware egocentric procedural videos by augmenting clean keystep procedures with controlled, human-plausible deviations. PIE-V combines a psychology-informed error planner conditioned on procedure phase and semantic step load, a correction planner that models recovery behavior, an LLM writer that performs cascade-consistent rewrites, and an LLM judge that validates procedural coherence and repairs failures. For video segment edits, PIE-V synthesizes replacement clips with text-guided video generation and stitches them into the episode to preserve visual plausibility. Applied to 17 tasks and 50 Ego-Exo4D scenarios, PIE-V injects 102 mistakes and generates 27 recovery corrections. For benchmarking, we introduce a unified taxonomy and a human rubric with nine metrics that cover step-level and procedure-level quality, including plausibility, procedure logic with annotator confidence, state change coherence, and grounding between text and video. Using this protocol, we audit several existing resources and compare PIE-V against a freeform LLM generation baseline under the same criteria. Together, the framework and rubric support post-completion verification for egocentric procedural mistake detection and correction.

AI.tldr: PIE-V is a framework to synthesize and benchmark mistake-aware egocentric procedural videos, injecting controlled, human-plausible errors and recoveries, with an LLM-based writers/judges, psychology-informed planners, and video generation; it provides a unified taxonomy and rubric, and benchmarks 17 tasks/50 scenarios with 102 mistakes and 27 corrections.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in procedurally annotated egocentric data capturing natural mistakes and recoveries; existing datasets have limited or inconsistent traces; need for standardized evaluation of mistake injection and correction.
AI.method: PIE-V comprises a psychology-informed error planner conditioned on procedure phase and semantic step load, a correction planner for recovery, an LLM writer for cascade-consistent rewrites, an LLM judge for coherence and repairs, and video synthesis to replace segments with text-guided generation; evaluated on 17 tasks and 50 Ego-Exo4D scenarios; outputs 102 injected mistakes and 27 corrections; provides taxonomy and nine-metric human rubric; compares to freeform LLM baseline.
AI.result: Generated 102 mistakes and 27 recovery corrections across 17 tasks/50 scenarios; introduced unified taxonomy and nine-metric rubric; audited existing resources and benchmarked PIE-V against a freeform LLM baseline; shows ability to generate plausible, coherent procedural deviations.
AI.conclusion: PIE-V and its rubric enable post-completion verification for egocentric procedural mistake detection and correction, providing a standardized framework for constructing and evaluating mistake-aware videos and advancing benchmarking in this domain.

title: COEVO: Co-Evolutionary Framework for Joint Functional Correctness and PPA Optimization in LLM-Based RTL Generation
summary: LLM-based RTL code generation methods increasingly target both functional correctness and PPA quality, yet existing approaches universally decouple the two objectives, optimizing PPA only after correctness is fully achieved. Whether through sequential multi-agent pipelines, evolutionary search with binary correctness gates, or hierarchical reward dependencies, partially correct but architecturally promising candidates are systematically discarded. Moreover, existing methods reduce the multi-objective PPA space to a single scalar fitness, obscuring the trade-offs among area, delay, and power. To address these limitations, we propose COEVO, a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization within a single evolutionary loop. COEVO formulates correctness as a continuous co-optimization dimension alongside area, delay, and power, enabled by an enhanced testbench that provides fine-grained scoring and detailed diagnostic feedback. An adaptive correctness gate with annealing allows PPA-promising but partially correct candidates to guide the search toward jointly optimal solutions. To preserve the full PPA trade-off structure, COEVO employs four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting, replacing scalar fitness without manual weight tuning. Evaluated on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0, COEVO achieves 97.5\% and 94.5\% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, while attaining the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.

AI.tldr: COEVO is a co-evolutionary framework that jointly optimizes correctness and PPA (area, delay, power) in LLM-guided RTL code generation, using a continuous correctness dimension, a four-dimensional Pareto sorting, and annealed gating to leverage partially correct candidates, achieving strong Pass@k and PPA performance across multiple LLM backbones.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based RTL code generation methods decouple correctness and PPA, rely on sequential architectures, binary gates, or scalar fitness, and discard partially correct but architecturally promising candidates, obscuring trade-offs among area, delay, and power.
AI.method: COEVO embeds correctness as a continuous co-optimization objective alongside area, delay, and power within a single evolutionary loop. It uses an enhanced testbench for fine-grained scoring and diagnostics, an adaptive correctness gate with annealing to allow PPA-promising candidates to guide search, and a four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting to preserve the full PPA trade-off without manual weighting.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0 shows COEVO achieving 97.5% and 94.5% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, outperforming all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, and attaining the best PPA on 43 of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.
AI.conclusion: COEVO demonstrates that unifying correctness and PPA in a single evolutionary loop with four-dimensional Pareto sorting and an annealed correctness gate delivers superior RTL synthesis results, preserving the full PPA trade-offs and improving search efficiency for LLM-assisted RTL generation.

title: One-shot learning for the complex dynamical behaviors of weakly nonlinear forced oscillators
summary: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics remains a central challenge in engineering. This study proposes a one-shot learning method to identify global frequency-response curves from a single excitation time history by learning governing equations. We introduce MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) to infer the governing equations of non-autonomous and multi-frequency systems. The methodology leverages the Generalized Harmonic Balance (GHB) method to decompose complex forced responses into a set of slow-varying evolution equations. We validated the capabilities of MEv-SINDy on two critical Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). These applications include a nonlinear beam resonator and a MEMS micromirror. Our results show that the model trained on a single point accurately predicts softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across a wide range of excitation levels. This approach significantly reduces the data acquisition burden for the characterization and design of nonlinear microsystems.

AI.tldr: One-shot learning (MEv-SINDy) with Generalized Harmonic Balance to infer nonlinear governing equations from a single excitation history, predicting global frequency-response curves for non-autonomous multi-frequency MEMS.; validated on a nonlinear beam resonator and MEMS micromirror; reduces data requirements.
AI.motivation: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics is challenging; reducing data needs for characterizing and designing nonlinear microsystems in engineering.
AI.method: MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) identifies governing equations of non-autonomous, multi-frequency systems. Uses Generalized Harmonic Balance to decompose forced responses into slow-varying evolution equations and learns equations from a single time history.
AI.result: Model trained on a single excitation point accurately predicts softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across a wide range of excitation levels; validated on two MEMS devices (nonlinear beam resonator and MEMS micromirror).
AI.conclusion: MEv-SINDy enables extrapolative prediction with substantially reduced data requirements for characterization and design of nonlinear microsystems.

title: KVNN: Learnable Multi-Kernel Volterra Neural Networks
summary: Higher-order learning is fundamentally rooted in exploiting compositional features. It clearly hinges on enriching the representation by more elaborate interactions of the data which, in turn, tends to increase the model complexity of conventional large-scale deep learning models. In this paper, a kernelized Volterra Neural Network (kVNN) is proposed. The key to the achieved efficiency lies in using a learnable multi-kernel representation, where different interaction orders are modeled by distinct polynomial-kernel components with compact, learnable centers, yielding an order-adaptive parameterization. Features are learned by the composition of layers, each of which consists of parallel branches of different polynomial orders, enabling kVNN filters to directly replace standard convolutional kernels within existing architectures. The theoretical results are substantiated by experiments on two representative tasks: video action recognition and image denoising. The results demonstrate favorable performance-efficiency trade-offs: kVNN consistently yields reduced model (parameters) and computational (GFLOPs) complexity with competitive and often improved performance. These results are maintained even when trained from scratch without large-scale pretraining. In summary, we substantiate that structured kernelized higher-order layers offer a practical path to balancing expressivity and computational cost in modern deep networks.

AI.tldr: Introduces kernelized Volterra Neural Network (kVNN) with a learnable multi-kernel, order-adaptive design that models higher-order interactions via parallel polynomial-order branches; can replace standard convolutions and improves efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency of employing higher-order representations in deep networks by enabling expressive higher-order interactions with significantly reduced parameter count and compute via structured kernelized layers.
AI.method: Proposes kVNN layers consisting of parallel branches for different polynomial orders, each with a distinct polynomial-kernel component and learnable centers; these components form an order-adaptive parameterization and can replace standard convolutions in existing CNN architectures; evaluated on video action recognition and image denoising, including training from scratch.
AI.result: kVNN achieves favorable trade-offs, decreasing parameters and GFLOPs while delivering competitive or improved accuracy; performance remains strong without large-scale pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Structured kernelized higher-order layers offer a practical approach to balance expressivity and computational cost in modern deep networks and can serve as drop-in, order-adaptive alternatives to conventional convolutional layers.

title: Towards Faster Language Model Inference Using Mixture-of-Experts Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching retains the generation quality of diffusion models while enabling substantially faster inference, making it a compelling paradigm for generative modeling. However, when applied to language modeling, it exhibits fundamental limitations in representing complex latent distributions with irregular geometries, such as anisotropy and multimodality. To address these challenges, we propose a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework, which captures complex global transport geometries in latent space by decomposing them into locally specialized vector fields. Building on MoE-FM, we develop a non-autoregressive (NAR) language modeling approach, named YAN, instantiated with both Transformer and Mamba architectures. Across multiple downstream tasks, YAN achieves generation quality on par with both autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based NAR language models, while requiring as few as three sampling steps. This yields a $40\times$ speedup over AR baselines and up to a $10^3\times$ speedup over diffusion language models, demonstrating substantial efficiency advantages for language modeling.

AI.tldr: MoE-FM enables expressive latent transport for language modeling by decomposing complex geometries with mixture-of-experts flow matching, enabling a fast non-autoregressive LM (YAN) that matches AR and diffusion-based NAR quality with only ~3 sampling steps and large speedups.
AI.motivation: Flow matching preserves diffusion-model quality but struggles with irregular latent distributions in language modeling (anisotropy, multimodality). There is a need for more expressive latent transport and faster inference to make FM viable for language tasks.
AI.method: Introduce a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework to capture complex global transport geometries via locally specialized vector fields. Build a non-autoregressive LM, YAN, using both Transformer and Mamba architectures, leveraging MoE-FM for latent transport. Evaluate on multiple downstream tasks against autoregressive and diffusion-based NAR baselines.
AI.result: YAN achieves generation quality on par with autoregressive and diffusion-based NAR models, using as few as three sampling steps. Provides ~40x speedup over AR baselines and up to ~1000x speedup over diffusion LMs, demonstrating substantial efficiency gains.
AI.conclusion: MoE-FM effectively represents complex latent geometries in language modeling, enabling fast, high-quality non-autoregressive generation. YAN offers a practical, scalable alternative to AR and diffusion-based LMs, with strong efficiency gains; further work should explore broader task regimes and potential stability/training considerations.

title: RL-STPA: Adapting System-Theoretic Hazard Analysis for Safety-Critical Reinforcement Learning
summary: As reinforcement learning (RL) deployments expand into safety-critical domains, existing evaluation methods fail to systematically identify hazards arising from the black-box nature of neural network enabled policies and distributional shift between training and deployment. This paper introduces Reinforcement Learning System-Theoretic Process Analysis (RL-STPA), a framework that adapts conventional STPA's systematic hazard analysis to address RL's unique challenges through three key contributions: hierarchical subtask decomposition using both temporal phase analysis and domain expertise to capture emergent behaviors, coverage-guided perturbation testing that explores the sensitivity of state-action spaces, and iterative checkpoints that feed identified hazards back into training through reward shaping and curriculum design. We demonstrate RL-STPA in the safety-critical test case of autonomous drone navigation and landing, revealing potential loss scenarios that can be missed by standard RL evaluations. The proposed framework provides practitioners with a toolkit for systematic hazard analysis, quantitative metrics for safety coverage assessment, and actionable guidelines for establishing operational safety bounds. While RL-STPA cannot provide formal guarantees for arbitrary neural policies, it offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive verification methods remain intractable.

AI.tldr: RL-STPA adapts STPA hazard analysis to reinforcement learning, addressing black-box policy dangers and distributional shift via hierarchical subtask decomposition, coverage-guided perturbation testing, and iterative reward-based training tweaks; demonstrated on autonomous drone navigation to reveal hazards not caught by standard RL evaluation; offers practical safety metrics and guidelines but no formal guarantees.
AI.motivation: As RL deployments enter safety-critical domains, conventional evaluation misses hazards from neural policy opacity and distributional shift. RL-STPA provides a structured hazard analysis framework tailored to RL to systematically identify and mitigate such hazards.
AI.method: 1) Hierarchical subtask decomposition using temporal phase analysis and domain expertise to capture emergent behaviors. 2) Coverage-guided perturbation testing to explore sensitivity of state-action spaces. 3) Iterative checkpoints that feed hazards back into training via reward shaping and curriculum design.
AI.result: Applied to autonomous drone navigation and landing, RL-STPA reveals loss scenarios that standard RL evaluations miss. It yields quantitative safety-coverage metrics and actionable guidelines for safety bounds, serving as a practical hazard-analysis toolkit.
AI.conclusion: RL-STPA is a practical, scalable methodology for evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive formal verification is intractable, though it does not provide formal guarantees for arbitrary neural policies.

title: Class Unlearning via Depth-Aware Removal of Forget-Specific Directions
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 Workshop on Machine Unlearning for Vision (MUV)
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model without the cost of retraining from scratch. In class unlearning, however, reducing accuracy on forget classes does not necessarily imply true forgetting: forgotten information can remain encoded in internal representations, and apparent forgetting may arise from classifier-head suppression rather than representational removal. We show that existing class-unlearning methods often exhibit weak or negative selectivity, preserve forget-class structure in deep representations, or rely heavily on final-layer bias shifts. We then introduce DAMP (Depth-Aware Modulation by Projection), a one-shot, closed-form weight-surgery method that removes forget-specific directions from a pretrained network without gradient-based optimization. At each stage, DAMP computes class prototypes in the input space of the next learnable operator, extracts forget directions as residuals relative to retain-class prototypes, and applies a projection-based update to reduce downstream sensitivity to those directions. To preserve utility, DAMP uses a parameter-free depth-aware scaling rule derived from probe separability, applying smaller edits in early layers and larger edits in deeper layers. The method naturally extends to multi-class forgetting through low-rank subspace removal. Across MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet, and across convolutional and transformer architectures, DAMP more closely resembles the retraining gold standard than some of the prior methods, improving selective forgetting while better preserving retain-class performance and reducing residual forget-class structure in deep layers.

AI.tldr: DAMP is a one-shot, closed-form weight-surgery method that removes forget-specific directions from a pretrained network by projecting updates per layer, using class prototypes and residual forget directions, with depth-aware scaling to preserve utility and enable multi-class forgetting.
AI.motivation: Current class-unlearning approaches often fail to erase forgetful information from internal representations, instead exploiting classifier-head biases or preserving memory in deep layers. A robust, efficient forgetting mechanism should remove forget directions from representations while preserving retain-class accuracy, and should generalize across architectures and tasks.
AI.method: At each stage, compute class prototypes in the next layer's input space; extract forget directions as residuals relative to retain-class prototypes; apply a projection-based, gradient-free update to reduce downstream sensitivity to forget directions. Use a depth-aware scaling rule derived from probe separability (smaller edits in early layers, larger in deeper layers). The method is one-shot and parameter-free and extends to multi-class forgetting via low-rank subspace removal.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on MNIST, CIFAR-10/100, and Tiny ImageNet across CNNs and transformers shows DAMP yielding forgetting closer to the retraining gold standard than some prior methods, with better retention of retain-class performance and reduced residual forget-class structure in deep layers.
AI.conclusion: DAMP provides a scalable, architecture-agnostic, parameter-free approach to class forgetting that achieves selective forgetting while preserving utility, approaching retraining performance and enabling robust multi-class forgetting through low-rank subspace removal.

title: Autogenesis: A Self-Evolving Agent Protocol
summary: Recent advances in LLM based agent systems have shown promise in tackling complex, long horizon tasks. However, existing agent protocols (e.g., A2A and MCP) under specify cross entity lifecycle and context management, version tracking, and evolution safe update interfaces, which encourages monolithic compositions and brittle glue code. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis Protocol (AGP)}}, a self evolution protocol that decouples what evolves from how evolution occurs. Its Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol registered resources\footnote{Unless otherwise specified, resources refer to instances of the five RSPL entity types: \emph{prompt}, \emph{agent}, \emph{tool}, \emph{environment}, \emph{memory} with agent \emph{outputs}.} with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces. Its Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) specifies a closed loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback. Building on \textbf{\textsc{AGP}}, we present \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis System (AGS)}}, a self-evolving multi-agent system that dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution. We evaluate \textbf{\textsc{AGS}} on multiple challenging benchmarks that require long horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources. The results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, supporting the effectiveness of agent resource management and closed loop self evolution.

AI.tldr: AGP decouples what evolves from how evolution occurs by introducing a Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) that versions prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol-registered resources, and a Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) for auditable proposals, assessments, and rollbacks. Built atop this, AGS is a self-evolving multi-agent system that dynamically instantiates and refines these resources; evaluated on long-horizon, cross-resource benchmarks with consistent improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness and monolithic glue-code in existing LLM-based agent protocols by providing lifecycle-aware, versioned, and auditable management of cross-entity resources and safe evolution interfaces.
AI.method: Model resources (prompt, agent, tool, environment, memory) as versioned protocol-registered entities with explicit state and interfaces under RSPL; implement SEPL as a closed-loop operator for proposing, evaluating, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback; deploy AGS to dynamically instantiate, retrieve, and refine resources during execution; evaluate on benchmarks demanding long-horizon planning and diverse tool/resource usage.
AI.result: AGS achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines on long-horizon benchmarks, validating the benefits of resource-centric management and closed-loop self-evolution for multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: The AGP/AGS framework offers a robust, evolvable foundation for agent systems by separating evolution mechanics from execution, enabling safer updates, traceability, and better cross-resource coordination; future work may address scalability and broader applicability.

title: Optimal last-iterate convergence in matrix games with bandit feedback using the log-barrier
summary: We study the problem of learning minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games. Fiegel et al. (2025) recently showed that achieving last-iterate convergence in this setting is harder when the players are uncoupled, by proving a lower bound on the exploitability gap of Omega(t^{-1/4}). Some online mirror descent algorithms were proposed in the literature for this problem, but none have truly attained this rate yet. We show that the use of a log-barrier regularization, along with a dual-focused analysis, allows this O-tilde(t^{-1/4}) convergence with high-probability. We additionally extend our idea to the setting of extensive-form games, proving a bound with the same rate.

AI.tldr: Introduces log-barrier online mirror descent with dual-focused analysis to achieve Õ(t^{-1/4}) last-iterate convergence for uncoupled zero-sum matrix games, matching a known lower bound; extends to extensive-form games.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of last-iterate convergence in minimax policies for zero-sum games, where recent lower bounds show exploitability gaps scale as Ω(t^{-1/4}) for uncoupled players.
AI.method: Apply log-barrier regularization within online mirror descent and develop a dual-focused analytical framework to bound exploitability, yielding high-probability Õ(t^{-1/4}) rates; extend the approach to extensive-form games.
AI.result: Proves Õ(t^{-1/4}) convergence with high probability for matrix games; shows the method extends to extensive-form games with the same rate.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that log-barrier OMD with a dual-centric analysis attains near-optimal last-iterate convergence in uncoupled zero-sum settings and generalizes to EF games, aligning with the lower-bound rate and guiding future algorithm design.

title: OmniLight: One Model to Rule All Lighting Conditions
comment: CVPRW 2026; NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal & Ambient Lighting Normalization Challenges (1st Perceptual Rank for White Lighting, 2nd Fidelity Rank & 4th Perceptual Rank for Color Lighting)
summary: Adverse lighting conditions, such as cast shadows and irregular illumination, pose significant challenges to computer vision systems by degrading visibility and color fidelity. Consequently, effective shadow removal and ALN are critical for restoring underlying image content, improving perceptual quality, and facilitating robust performance in downstream tasks. However, while achieving state-of-the-art results on specific benchmarks is a primary goal in image restoration challenges, real-world applications often demand robust models capable of handling diverse domains. To address this, we present a comprehensive study on lighting-related image restoration by exploring two contrasting strategies. We leverage a robust framework for ALN, DINOLight, as a specialized baseline to exploit the characteristics of each individual dataset, and extend it to OmniLight, a generalized alternative incorporating our proposed Wavelet Domain Mixture-of-Experts (WD-MoE) that is trained across all provided datasets. Through a comparative analysis of these two methods, we discuss the impact of data distribution on the performance of specialized and unified architectures in lighting-related image restoration. Notably, both approaches secured top-tier rankings across all three lighting-related tracks in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge, demonstrating their outstanding perceptual quality and generalization capabilities. Our codes are available at https://github.com/OBAKSA/Lighting-Restoration.

AI.tldr: A study comparing specialized (DINOLight) vs unified (OmniLight with Wavelet Domain MoE) frameworks for lighting-related image restoration across diverse datasets; both achieve top NTIRE 2026 results, highlighting cross-domain robustness and the impact of data distribution on model performance; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of robust lighting-related image restoration across diverse real-world domains, where shadow removal and illumination normalization must generalize beyond single-domain benchmarks.
AI.method: Two strategies are explored: (1) DINOLight, a specialized baseline tailored to each dataset, exploiting dataset-specific characteristics; (2) OmniLight, a generalized approach trained across all datasets, incorporating the proposed Wavelet Domain Mixture-of-Experts (WD-MoE) to capture multi-domain illumination patterns. A comparative analysis assesses the influence of data distribution on specialization vs generalization.
AI.result: Both methods achieved top-tier rankings across all three lighting-related tracks in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge, indicating strong perceptual quality and cross-domain generalization. The authors provide code at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: Specialized and generalized architectures each have merits; data distribution significantly affects cross-domain performance. The WD-MoE within OmniLight offers a robust, cross-dataset solution for lighting restoration, with demonstrated state-of-the-art results and practical availability of code for replication.

title: From Reactive to Proactive: Assessing the Proactivity of Voice Agents via ProVoice-Bench
summary: Recent advancements in LLM agents are gradually shifting from reactive, text-based paradigms toward proactive, multimodal interaction. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on reactive responses, overlooking the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProVoice-Bench, the first evaluation framework specifically designed for proactive voice agents, featuring four novel tasks. By leveraging a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline, we curate 1,182 high-quality samples for rigorous testing. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs reveals a significant performance gap, particularly regarding over-triggering and reasoning capabilities. These findings highlight the limitations of current models and offer a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents.

AI.tldr: First benchmark for proactive voice agents; includes four tasks and 1,182 samples, revealing gaps in over-triggering and reasoning for current multimodal LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks emphasize reactive, text-based interactions and fail to capture proactive intervention, monitoring, and multimodal context understanding required for natural proactive voice agents.
AI.method: ProVoice-Bench uses a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline to curate 1,182 high-quality samples across four novel tasks designed for proactive settings, and evaluates state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs on these tasks.
AI.result: Evaluation shows a notable performance gap for proactive capabilities, especially in over-triggering and reasoning, indicating limitations of existing models in proactive, context-aware interaction.
AI.conclusion: The framework highlights the need for models that better handle proactive intervention and context awareness and provides a roadmap for developing more natural proactive voice agents.

title: Stability and Generalization in Looped Transformers
summary: Looped transformers promise test-time compute scaling by spending more iterations on harder problems, but it remains unclear which architectural choices let them extrapolate to harder problems at test time rather than memorize training-specific solutions. We introduce a fixed-point based framework for analyzing looped architectures along three axes of stability -- reachability, input-dependence, and geometry -- and use it to characterize when fixed-point iteration yields meaningful predictions. Theoretically, we prove that looped networks without recall have countable fixed points and cannot achieve strong input-dependence at any spectral regime, while recall combined with outer normalization reliably produces a regime in which fixed points are simultaneously reachable, locally smooth in the input, and supported by stable backpropagation. Empirically, we train single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums and find that downstream performance tracks the framework's predictions across tasks and architectural configurations. We additionally introduce internal recall, a novel recall placement variant, and show that it becomes competitive with -- and on sudoku, substantially better than -- standard recall placement once outer normalization is applied.

AI.tldr: A fixed-point framework analyzes looped transformers along three stability axes—reachability, input-dependence, and geometry—showing that recall with outer normalization yields stable, input-sensitive fixed points and better test-time extrapolation; internal recall is a competitive variant. Empirically, single-layer looped transformers align with the framework across chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums.
AI.motivation: To understand when looped architectures extrapolate to harder problems at test time rather than memorizing training data, by establishing principled criteria that connect architectural choices to extrapolative performance.
AI.method: Develop a fixed-point based framework across three stability axes (reachability, input-dependence, geometry); prove theoretical results for looped networks with and without recall, and with outer normalization; empirically evaluate single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums; propose internal recall and study recall placement under outer normalization.
AI.result: Theoretically, networks without recall have countable fixed points and lack strong input-dependence across all spectral regimes; recall combined with outer normalization yields a regime where fixed points are reachable, locally input-smooth, and backpropagatable with stability. Empirically, downstream performance tracks the framework’s predictions across tasks and configurations; internal recall becomes competitive with standard recall and improves Sudoku performance notably under outer normalization.
AI.conclusion: The framework clarifies design choices enabling extrapolation in looped transformers; recall and outer normalization are key to attaining stable, input-sensitive fixed points, and the introduced internal recall variant shows practical gains, guiding architectural decisions for looped Transformer setups.

title: Where are the Humans? A Scoping Review of Fairness in Multi-agent AI Systems
comment: In proceedings of European Conference on Information Systems (ECIS) 2026
summary: Rapid advances in Generative AI are giving rise to increasingly sophisticated Multi-Agent AI (MAAI) systems. While AI fairness has been extensively studied in traditional predictive scenarios, its examination in MAAI remains nascent and fragmented. This scoping review critically synthesizes existing research on fairness in MAAI systems. Through a qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies, we identify five archetypal approaches. Our findings reveal that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks the complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. We argue that fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI, rather than appended as a post-hoc consideration. Meaningful evaluation requires explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and a precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. This review provides a foundation for advancing fairness research in MAAI systems by highlighting critical gaps, exposing prevailing limitations, and suggesting pathways.

AI.tldr: A qualitative scoping review of fairness in multi-agent AI (MAAI) across 23 studies identifies five archetypal fairness approaches. It finds that current work often treats fairness superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and neglects dynamics from agent autonomy and system-level interactions. The authors argue for embedding fairness structurally throughout the MAAI development lifecycle, requiring explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and clear articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries, and they outline gaps and pathways for future work.
AI.motivation: Fairness in MAAI is understudied compared with single-agent predictive settings. As MAAs become more capable, there is a need to synthesize existing research to establish normative foundations, define objectives, and guide lifecycle-wide integration of fairness.
AI.method: Qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies within a scoping review to identify recurring approaches and gaps, culminating in five archetypal fairness strategies in MAAI.
AI.result: Identification of five archetypal fairness approaches in MAAI. Across the literature, fairness is often addressed superficially, with weak normative grounding and insufficient attention to agent autonomy and system-level interactions. The study emphasizes that meaningful fairness evaluation requires structural embedding, explicit human oversight, clear normative objectives, and precise beneficiaries. The review also highlights critical gaps and limitations and proposes directions for future research.
AI.conclusion: Fairness in MAAI should be structurally integrated throughout the development lifecycle rather than added post hoc. Success hinges on normative clarity, explicit articulation of objectives and beneficiaries, and ongoing human oversight. The review lays a foundation for advancing rigorous, lifecycle-spanning fairness research in MAAI by outlining gaps and actionable pathways.

title: How Embeddings Shape Graph Neural Networks: Classical vs Quantum-Oriented Node Representations
summary: Node embeddings act as the information interface for graph neural networks, yet their empirical impact is often reported under mismatched backbones, splits, and training budgets. This paper provides a controlled benchmark of embedding choices for graph classification, comparing classical baselines with quantum-oriented node representations under a unified pipeline. We evaluate two classical baselines alongside quantum-oriented alternatives, including a circuit-defined variational embedding and quantum-inspired embeddings computed via graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions. All variants are trained and tested with the same backbone, stratified splits, identical optimization and early stopping, and consistent metrics. Experiments on five different TU datasets and on QM9 converted to classification via target binning show clear dataset dependence: quantum-oriented embeddings yield the most consistent gains on structure-driven benchmarks, while social graphs with limited node attributes remain well served by classical baselines. The study highlights practical trade-offs between inductive bias, trainability, and stability under a fixed training budget, and offers a reproducible reference point for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning.

AI.tldr: A controlled benchmark comparing classical and quantum-oriented node embeddings for graph classification under a unified pipeline, showing dataset-dependent gains and offering a reproducible reference for embedding choice under fixed budgets.
AI.motivation: Prior work lacks fair, apples-to-apples comparisons due to mismatched backbones, data splits, and training budgets; a controlled benchmark isolates the effect of embedding choices on graph classification performance.
AI.method: Evaluate two classical baselines and multiple quantum-oriented embeddings (including a circuit-defined variational embedding and quantum-inspired embeddings via graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions) within a single, consistent pipeline: identical backbone, stratified splits, identical optimization and early stopping, fixed budget; test on five TU datasets and QM9 (binned for classification).
AI.result: Quantum-oriented embeddings provide the most consistent gains on structure-driven benchmarks; social graphs with limited node attributes remain well served by classical baselines; dataset-dependent performance; trade-offs among inductive bias, trainability, and stability under fixed training budgets.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a reproducible reference point for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning and clarifies practical trade-offs between embedding choices, data structure, and training constraints.

title: Boundary-Centric Active Learning for Temporal Action Segmentation
summary: Temporal action segmentation (TAS) demands dense temporal supervision, yet most of the annotation cost in untrimmed videos is spent identifying and refining action transitions, where segmentation errors concentrate and small temporal shifts disproportionately degrade segmental metrics. We introduce B-ACT, a clip-budgeted active learning framework that explicitly allocates supervision to these high-leverage boundary regions. B-ACT operates in a hierarchical two-stage loop: (i) it ranks and queries unlabeled videos using predictive uncertainty, and (ii) within each selected video, it detects candidate transitions from the current model predictions and selects the top-$K$ boundaries via a novel boundary score that fuses neighborhood uncertainty, class ambiguity, and temporal predictive dynamics. Importantly, our annotation protocol requests labels for only the boundary frames while still training on boundary-centered clips to exploit temporal context through the model's receptive field. Extensive experiments on GTEA, 50Salads, and Breakfast demonstrate that boundary-centric supervision delivers strong label efficiency and consistently surpasses representative TAS active learning baselines and prior state of the art under sparse budgets, with the largest gains on datasets where boundary placement dominates edit and overlap-based F1 scores.

AI.tldr: B-ACT introduces boundary-focused active learning for temporal action segmentation, prioritizing high-leverage boundary regions to improve labeling efficiency under sparse budgets.
AI.motivation: TAS requires dense supervision, but annotation cost concentrates at action transitions where segmentation errors are most impactful. Targeting boundary regions offers high leverage to improve metrics with limited labels.
AI.method: A two-stage loop: (i) rank and query unlabeled videos by predictive uncertainty; (ii) within a selected video, detect candidate transitions from current model predictions and select the top-K boundaries via a boundary score that combines neighborhood uncertainty, class ambiguity, and temporal predictive dynamics. Annotations label only boundary frames, but training uses boundary-centered clips to exploit the model’s receptive field.
AI.result: Experiments on GTEA, 50Salads, and Breakfast show boundary-centric supervision yields strong label efficiency and consistently surpasses representative TAS active learning baselines and prior state-of-the-art under sparse budgets, with the largest gains on datasets where boundary placement dominates edit/overlap-based F1 scores.
AI.conclusion: Boundary-focused active learning is effective for TAS, enabling improved performance with fewer labels, particularly when boundary placement drives evaluation metrics.

title: OpenMobile: Building Open Mobile Agents with Task and Trajectory Synthesis
summary: Mobile agents powered by vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in automating mobile tasks, with recent leading models achieving a marked performance leap, e.g., nearly 70% success on AndroidWorld. However, these systems keep their training data closed and remain opaque about their task and trajectory synthesis recipes. We present OpenMobile, an open-source framework that synthesizes high-quality task instructions and agent trajectories, with two key components: (1) The first is a scalable task synthesis pipeline that constructs a global environment memory from exploration, then leverages it to generate diverse and grounded instructions. and (2) a policy-switching strategy for trajectory rollout. By alternating between learner and expert models, it captures essential error-recovery data often missing in standard imitation learning. Agents trained on our data achieve competitive results across three dynamic mobile agent benchmarks: notably, our fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL reach 51.7% and 64.7% on AndroidWorld, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Furthermore, we conduct transparent analyses on the overlap between our synthetic instructions and benchmark test sets, and verify that performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting. We release data and code at https://njucckevin.github.io/openmobile/ to bridge the data gap and facilitate broader mobile agent research.

AI.tldr: OpenMobile presents an open-source pipeline for generating task instructions and trajectories for mobile agents, using a scalable environment-memory task synthesis and a policy-switching trajectory rollout, achieving competitive AndroidWorld results and releasing data/code to enable broader research.
AI.motivation: To address the opacity and closed-data nature of current vision-language mobile agents, by providing reproducible, diverse, and grounded synthetic data to enable broader research and avoid benchmark overfitting.
AI.method: 1) Scalable task synthesis: build a global environment memory from exploration and generate diverse, grounded instructions. 2) Policy-switching trajectory rollout: alternate between learner and expert models to capture error-recovery data often missing in standard imitation learning.
AI.result: Agents trained on the synthetic data achieve competitive performance on dynamic mobile agent benchmarks, with Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL attaining 51.7% and 64.7% on AndroidWorld, surpassing existing open-data approaches; analyses show gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than overfitting. Data and code are released.
AI.conclusion: OpenMobile lowers the data barrier and provides transparent, reusable resources to bridge the data gap, facilitating broader research in mobile agents.

title: Benchmarking Optimizers for MLPs in Tabular Deep Learning
summary: MLP is a heavily used backbone in modern deep learning (DL) architectures for supervised learning on tabular data, and AdamW is the go-to optimizer used to train tabular DL models. Unlike architecture design, however, the choice of optimizer for tabular DL has not been examined systematically, despite new optimizers showing promise in other domains. To fill this gap, we benchmark \Noptimizers optimizers on \Ndatasets tabular datasets for training MLP-based models in the standard supervised learning setting under a shared experiment protocol. Our main finding is that the Muon optimizer consistently outperforms AdamW, and thus should be considered a strong and practical choice for practitioners and researchers, if the associated training efficiency overhead is affordable. Additionally, we find exponential moving average of model weights to be a simple yet effective technique that improves AdamW on vanilla MLPs, though its effect is less consistent across model variants.

AI.tldr: Muon optimizer generally beats AdamW for MLP-based tabular DL; EMA helps AdamW but with inconsistent gains.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of systematic evaluation of optimizers for tabular DL and MLP backbones; benchmark various optimizers on tabular datasets under a shared protocol to determine practical optimizer choices.
AI.method: Benchmark N optimizers on Ndatasets tabular datasets training MLP-based models under a standardized supervised-learning protocol; compare performance and training efficiency; also assess exponential moving average (EMA) of weights on AdamW.
AI.result: Muon consistently outperforms AdamW in the benchmarks; EMA improves AdamW on vanilla MLPs but results vary across model variants; training efficiency overhead associated with Muon is a consideration.
AI.conclusion: Muon is a strong and practical optimizer choice for tabular MLPs when training efficiency is acceptable; EMA is a simple, effective augmentation for AdamW but with mixed consistency.

title: VisPCO: Visual Token Pruning Configuration Optimization via Budget-Aware Pareto-Frontier Learning for Vision-Language Models
summary: Visual token pruning methods effectively mitigate the quadratic computational growth caused by processing high-resolution images and video frames in vision-language models (VLMs). However, existing approaches rely on predefined pruning configurations without determining whether they achieve computation-performance optimality. In this work, we introduce , a novel framework that formulates visual token pruning as a Pareto configuration optimization problem to automatically identify optimal configurations. Our approach employs continuous relaxation and straight-through estimators to enable gradient-based search, solved via the Augmented Lagrangian method. Extensive experiments across 8 visual benchmarks demonstrate that effectively approximates the empirical Pareto frontier obtained through grid search and generalizes well across various pruning methods and VLM architectures. Furthermore, through learnable kernel functions, we investigate layer-wise pruning patterns and reveal that multi-step progressive pruning captures VLMs' hierarchical compression structure, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to single-layer approaches.

AI.tldr: A framework automatically discovers Pareto-optimal visual token pruning configurations for vision-language models, achieving near-grid-search Pareto fronts and enabling multi-step progressive pruning to exploit hierarchical compression.
AI.motivation: Mitigate quadratic compute growth in VLMs due to high-resolution inputs and frames; existing pruning uses fixed configurations and risks suboptimal accuracy-efficiency trade-offs; need data-driven, computation-performance optimal pruning configurations.
AI.method: Formulate visual token pruning as a Pareto configuration optimization problem. Use continuous relaxation and straight-through estimators for gradient-based search, solved with the Augmented Lagrangian method. Introduce learnable kernel functions to study layer-wise pruning patterns, enabling multi-step progressive pruning across layers.
AI.result: The approach closely approximates the empirical Pareto frontier obtained from grid search and generalizes across pruning methods and VLM architectures on 8 visual benchmarks. It reveals that multi-step progressive pruning captures hierarchical compression and yields better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs than single-layer pruning.
AI.conclusion: Automatic, principled discovery of Pareto-optimal pruning configurations; progressive, multi-layer pruning reveals the hierarchical structure of VLMs and improves efficiency-accuracy trade-offs across architectures and pruning methods.

title: HyperSpace: A Generalized Framework for Spatial Encoding in Hyperdimensional Representations
summary: Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a well-defined algebraic framework for compositional representations in hyperdimensional spaces. We introduce HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators for encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, and regression. Using HyperSpace, we analyze and benchmark two representative VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR). Although FHRR provides lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, HyperSpaces modularity reveals that similarity and cleanup dominate runtime in spatial domains. As a result, HRR and FHRR exhibit comparable end-to-end performance. Differences in memory footprint introduce additional deployment trade-offs where HRR requires approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors. By enabling modular, system-level evaluation, HyperSpace reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that are not apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone.

AI.tldr: HyperSpace provides a modular, system-level analysis of vector symbolic architectures (VSAs) and shows that, for HRR and FHRR backends, end-to-end performance is similar despite FHRR's lower per-op complexity; HRR uses about half the memory, highlighting memory vs. CPU trade-offs revealed only at the system level.
AI.motivation: To enable modular decomposition of VSA systems into operators and to evaluate practical trade-offs between backends through system-level benchmarking, bridging theory and practice.
AI.method: Introduce HyperSpace, a framework that decomposes VSA operations into encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, and regression. Benchmark two backends (HRR and FHRR) in spatial domains, comparing runtime, memory footprint, and end-to-end performance under modular pipelines.
AI.result: FHRR has lower theoretical complexity per operation, but similarity and cleanup become the dominant costs in spatial domains; end-to-end performance of HRR and FHRR is comparable. HRR uses about half the memory of FHRR, introducing a deployment trade-off where memory dominates for FHRR.
AI.conclusion: System-level, modular evaluation via HyperSpace exposes practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that are not evident from operator-level theory, underscoring the importance of considering bottlenecks and memory alongside theoretical operation costs.

title: Unsupervised Skeleton-Based Action Segmentation via Hierarchical Spatiotemporal Vector Quantization
summary: We propose a novel hierarchical spatiotemporal vector quantization framework for unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation. We first introduce a hierarchical approach, which includes two consecutive levels of vector quantization. Specifically, the lower level associates skeletons with fine-grained subactions, while the higher level further aggregates subactions into action-level representations. Our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical baseline, while primarily exploiting spatial cues by reconstructing input skeletons. Next, we extend our approach by leveraging both spatial and temporal information, yielding a hierarchical spatiotemporal vector quantization scheme. In particular, our hierarchical spatiotemporal approach performs multi-level clustering, while simultaneously recovering input skeletons and their corresponding timestamps. Lastly, extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks, including HuGaDB, LARa, and BABEL, demonstrate that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art performance and reduces segment length bias in unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hierarchical spatiotemporal vector quantization framework for unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation, with a two-level (subaction and action) quantization, extended to spatiotemporal clustering that also recovers timestamps; achieves state-of-the-art on HuGaDB, LARa, BABEL and reduces segment length bias.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation by capturing hierarchical structure and leveraging both spatial and temporal cues, while mitigating segment-length bias.
AI.method: Introduce a two-level vector quantization architecture: (1) lower level maps skeletons to fine-grained subactions; (2) higher level aggregates subactions into action-level representations. Extend to a hierarchical spatiotemporal scheme with multi-level clustering that reconstructs input skeletons and their timestamps.
AI.result: Outperforms the non-hierarchical baseline; achieves state-of-the-art on HuGaDB, LARa, and BABEL; reduces segment length bias.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical spatiotemporal vector quantization is effective for unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation, improving accuracy and reducing bias, with demonstrated value of multi-level clustering and reconstruction.

title: SRMU: Relevance-Gated Updates for Streaming Hyperdimensional Memories
summary: Sequential associative memories (SAMs) are difficult to build and maintain in real-world streaming environments, where observations arrive incrementally over time, have imbalanced sampling, and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a biologically-inspired framework for building SAMs. Entities and attributes are encoded as quasi-orthogonal hyperdimensional vectors and processed with well defined algebraic operations. Despite this rich framework, most VSA systems rely on simple additive updates, where repeated observations reinforce existing information even when no new information is introduced. In non-stationary environments, this leads to the persistence of stale information after the underlying system changes. In this work, we introduce the Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a domain- and cleanup-agnostic update rule for VSA-based SAMs. The SRMU combines temporal decay with a relevance gating mechanism. Unlike prior approaches that solely rely on cleanup, the SRMU regulates memory formation by filtering redundant, conflicting, and stale information before storage. We evaluate the SRMU on streaming state-tracking tasks that isolate non-uniform sampling and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Our results show that the SRMU increases memory similarity by $12.6\%$ and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by $53.5\%$. This shows that the SRMU produces more stable memory growth and stronger alignment with the ground-truth state.

AI.tldr: A relevance-guided update rule (SRMU) for VSA-based sequential associative memories improves robustness to non-stationary streaming data by filtering redundant/stale information before storage, yielding higher memory similarity and smaller cumulative memory magnitude.
AI.motivation: SAMs in streaming environments suffer from stale info due to simple additive updates; there is a need for a memory update rule that filters information in non-stationary, imbalanced streams.
AI.method: Introduce Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU) with temporal decay and relevance gating; domain- and cleanup-agnostic; filters before storage to regulate memory formation; evaluated on streaming state-tracking tasks with non-uniform sampling and non-stationary dynamics.
AI.result: SRMU increases memory similarity by 12.6% and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by 53.5%, indicating more stable memory growth and better alignment with ground-truth.
AI.conclusion: SRMU provides a robust, generalizable update mechanism for VSA-based SAMs in non-stationary streams, reducing stale information and improving alignment with true state without relying solely on memory cleanup.

title: StreamCacheVGGT: Streaming Visual Geometry Transformers with Robust Scoring and Hybrid Cache Compression
summary: Reconstructing dense 3D geometry from continuous video streams requires stable inference under a constant memory budget. Existing $O(1)$ frameworks primarily rely on a ``pure eviction'' paradigm, which suffers from significant information destruction due to binary token deletion and evaluation noise from localized, single-layer scoring. To address these bottlenecks, we propose StreamCacheVGGT, a training-free framework that reimagines cache management through two synergistic modules: Cross-Layer Consistency-Enhanced Scoring (CLCES) and Hybrid Cache Compression (HCC). CLCES mitigates activation noise by tracking token importance trajectories across the Transformer hierarchy, employing order-statistical analysis to identify sustained geometric salience. Leveraging these robust scores, HCC transcends simple eviction by introducing a three-tier triage strategy that merges moderately important tokens into retained anchors via nearest-neighbor assignment on the key-vector manifold. This approach preserves essential geometric context that would otherwise be lost. Extensive evaluations on five benchmarks (7-Scenes, NRGBD, ETH3D, Bonn, and KITTI) demonstrate that StreamCacheVGGT sets a new state-of-the-art, delivering superior reconstruction accuracy and long-term stability while strictly adhering to constant-cost constraints.

AI.tldr: StreamCacheVGGT introduces a training-free cache-management framework for dense 3D reconstruction under a fixed memory budget, combining Cross-Layer Consistency-Enhanced Scoring (CLCES) and Hybrid Cache Compression (HCC) to outperform prior pure-eviction approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing O(1) methods for streaming 3D reconstruction rely on binary token eviction, which causes information loss and evaluation noise. A stable, memory-limited inference pipeline requires better retention of geometric context across the Transformer hierarchy.
AI.method: CLCES tracks token importance trajectories across Transformer layers and uses order-statistics to identify tokens with sustained geometric salience. HCC uses these scores in a three-tier triage to merge moderately important tokens into retained anchors via nearest-neighbor assignment on the key-vector manifold, enabling more informative eviction than simple deletion. The framework is training-free.
AI.result: On five benchmarks (7-Scenes, NRGBD, ETH3D, Bonn, KITTI), StreamCacheVGGT achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction accuracy and long-term stability while maintaining constant-cost memory usage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CLCES + HCC pipeline effectively mitigates activation noise, preserves essential geometric context, and enables a rigorous, memory-constant dense 3D reconstruction in streaming video, outperforming existing pure-eviction approaches.

title: An Axiomatic Benchmark for Evaluation of Scientific Novelty Metrics
summary: The rigorous evaluation of the novelty of a scientific paper is, even for human scientists, a challenging task. With the increasing interest in AI scientists and AI involvement in scientific idea generation and paper writing, it also becomes increasingly important that this task be automatable and reliable, lest both human attention and compute tokens be wasted on ideas that have already been explored. Due to the challenge of quantifying ground-truth novelty, however, existing novelty metrics for scientific papers generally validate their results against noisy, confounded signals such as citation counts or peer review scores. These proxies can conflate novelty with impact, quality, or reviewer preference, which in turn makes it harder to assess how well a given metric actually evaluates novelty. We therefore propose an axiomatic benchmark for scientific novelty metrics. We first define a set of axioms that a well-behaved novelty metric should satisfy, grounded in human scientific norms and practice, then evaluate existing metrics across ten tasks spanning three domains of AI research. Our results reveal that no existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently, and that metrics fail on systematically different axioms, reflecting their underlying architectures. Additionally, we show that combining metrics of complementary architectures leads to consistent improvements on the benchmark, with per-axiom weighting achieving 90.1% versus 71.5% for the best individual metric, suggesting that developing architecturally diverse metrics is a promising direction for future work. We release the benchmark code as supplementary material to encourage the development of more robust scientific literature novelty metrics.

AI.tldr: An axiomatic benchmark for scientific novelty metrics in AI is proposed; no existing metric satisfies all axioms; combining architecturally diverse metrics improves performance; benchmark code released.
AI.motivation: Quantifying novelty in scientific papers is hard and current proxies (citations, peer judgments) conflate novelty with impact or quality. An axiomatic, automatable benchmark is needed to evaluate and guide novelty metrics.
AI.method: Define a set of axioms rooted in scientific norms, evaluate ten tasks across three AI domains using existing metrics, analyze per-axiom failures, and test combinations of metrics with per-axiom weighting.
AI.result: No single metric satisfies all axioms; metrics fail on different axioms due to their underlying architectures. Combining diverse metrics with per-axiom weights yields 90.1% performance vs 71.5% for the best single metric.
AI.conclusion: Architecturally diverse novelty metrics are a promising direction; releasing the benchmark code should spur development of more robust and reliable novelty assessments in scientific literature.

title: TokenGS: Decoupling 3D Gaussian Prediction from Pixels with Learnable Tokens
summary: In this work, we revisit several key design choices of modern Transformer-based approaches for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) prediction. We argue that the common practice of regressing Gaussian means as depths along camera rays is suboptimal, and instead propose to directly regress 3D mean coordinates using only a self-supervised rendering loss. This formulation allows us to move from the standard encoder-only design to an encoder-decoder architecture with learnable Gaussian tokens, thereby unbinding the number of predicted primitives from input image resolution and number of views. Our resulting method, TokenGS, demonstrates improved robustness to pose noise and multiview inconsistencies, while naturally supporting efficient test-time optimization in token space without degrading learned priors. TokenGS achieves state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction performance on both static and dynamic scenes, producing more regularized geometry and more balanced 3DGS distribution, while seamlessly recovering emergent scene attributes such as static-dynamic decomposition and scene flow.

AI.tldr: TokenGS replaces depth regression with 3D mean regression in a Transformer-based 3D Gaussian Splatting framework, enabling an encoder-decoder with learnable Gaussian tokens. This decouples primitives from image resolution, improves robustness to pose and view inconsistencies, and achieves state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction for static and dynamic scenes with token-space test-time optimization and emergent scene attributes.
AI.motivation: Regressing Gaussian means as depths along camera rays can be suboptimal for 3D Gaussian Splatting. A direct 3D mean regression with a self-supervised rendering loss can enable decoupling from input resolution and views, improve robustness, and allow a more flexible architecture.
AI.method: Replace depth regression with direct regression of 3D mean coordinates using a self-supervised rendering loss. Transition from an encoder-only design to an encoder-decoder with learnable Gaussian tokens, enabling decoupling of the number of primitives from image resolution and number of views.
AI.result: TokenGS achieves improved robustness to pose noise and multiview inconsistencies, supports efficient test-time optimization in token space without harming learned priors, and sets state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction for both static and dynamic scenes with more regularized geometry and balanced 3DGS distribution; it also enables emergent attributes such as static-dynamic decomposition and scene flow.
AI.conclusion: The approach advances 3D Gaussian Splatting by shifting the regression target to 3D mean coordinates and adopting an encoder-decoder with Gaussian tokens, yielding robustness, efficiency, and superior reconstruction quality, along with interpretable emergent scene attributes.

title: IG-Search: Step-Level Information Gain Rewards for Search-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has emerged as an effective paradigm for training large language models to perform search-augmented reasoning. However, existing approaches rely on trajectory-level rewards that cannot distinguish precise search queries from vague or redundant ones within a rollout group, and collapse to a near-zero gradient signal whenever every sampled trajectory fails. In this paper, we propose IG-Search, a reinforcement learning framework that introduces a step-level reward based on Information Gain (IG). For each search step, IG measures how much the retrieved documents improve the model's confidence in the gold answer relative to a counterfactual baseline of random documents, thereby reflecting the effectiveness of the underlying search query. This signal is fed back to the corresponding search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO, enabling fine-grained, step-level credit assignment within a rollout. Unlike prior step-level methods that require either externally annotated intermediate supervision or shared environment states across trajectories, IG-Search derives its signals from the policy's own generation probabilities, requiring no intermediate annotations beyond standard question-answer pairs. Experiments on seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks demonstrate that IG-Search achieves an average EM of 0.430 with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming the strongest trajectory-level baseline (MR-Search) by 1.6 points and the step-level method GiGPO by 0.9 points on average across benchmarks, with particularly pronounced gains on multi-hop reasoning tasks. Despite introducing a dense step-level signal, IG-Search adds only ~6.4% to per-step training wall-clock time over the trajectory-level baseline and leaves inference latency unchanged, while still providing a meaningful gradient signal even when every sampled trajectory answers incorrectly.

AI.tldr: IG-Search introduces step-level rewards based on Information Gain for search queries in reinforcement learning, enabling fine-grained credit assignment and improved QA performance with modest time overhead.
AI.motivation: Address credit-assignment and gradient-signal sparsity in trajectory-level RL for search-augmented LMs by differentiating effective vs. ineffective search queries and avoiding reliance on external annotations.
AI.method: Compute per-step Information Gain as the gain in the model's confidence in the gold answer when retrieving documents, relative to a counterfactual baseline of random documents. Propagate this dense signal to corresponding search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO, enabling step-level credit assignment using only standard QA pairs.
AI.result: Average exact match (EM) 0.430 on Qwen2.5-3B across seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks, outperforming MR-Search by 1.6 EM points and GiGPO by 0.9 EM points. Gains are especially pronounced on multi-hop tasks. Training time increases ~6.4% per-step over trajectory-level baseline; inference latency remains unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Dense step-level Information Gain rewards provide meaningful gradients even when all trajectories fail, enabling more precise credit assignment and improved QA performance with modest computational overhead.

title: SegWithU: Uncertainty as Perturbation Energy for Single-Forward-Pass Risk-Aware Medical Image Segmentation
summary:
Reliable uncertainty estimation is critical for medical image segmentation, where automated contours feed downstream quantification and clinical decision support. Many strong uncertainty methods require repeated inference, while efficient single-forward-pass alternatives often provide weaker failure ranking or rely on restrictive feature-space assumptions. We present $\textbf{SegWithU}$, a post-hoc framework that augments a frozen pretrained segmentation backbone with a lightweight uncertainty head. SegWithU taps intermediate backbone features and models uncertainty as perturbation energy in a compact probe space using rank-1 posterior probes. It produces two voxel-wise uncertainty maps: a calibration-oriented map for probability tempering and a ranking-oriented map for error detection and selective prediction. Across ACDC, BraTS2024, and LiTS, SegWithU is the strongest and most consistent single-forward-pass baseline, achieving AUROC/AURC of $0.9838/2.4885$, $0.9946/0.2660$, and $0.9925/0.8193$, respectively, while preserving segmentation quality. These results suggest that perturbation-based uncertainty modeling is an effective and practical route to reliability-aware medical segmentation.
  Source code is available at https://github.com/ProjectNeura/SegWithU.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc, single-forward-pass uncertainty estimation for medical image segmentation. SegWithU augments a frozen pretrained backbone with a lightweight uncertainty head that uses rank-1 posterior probes in a compact feature space to produce two voxel-wise uncertainty maps (calibration and ranking). It achieves state-of-the-art single-pass AUROC/AURC on ACDC, BraTS2024, and LiTS while preserving segmentation quality; code available.
AI.motivation: Reliable uncertainty estimation is crucial for medical image segmentation due to downstream quantification and clinical decision support. Many strong methods require repeated inference or rely on strong feature-space assumptions, which can be costly or fragile. A fast, robust single-pass method is desirable for practical deployment.
AI.method: SegWithU freezes a pretrained segmentation backbone and adds a lightweight uncertainty head. It taps intermediate backbone features and models uncertainty as perturbation energy in a compact probe space using rank-1 posterior probes. It yields two voxel-wise maps: a calibration-oriented map for probability tempering and a ranking-oriented map for error detection and selective prediction.
AI.result: On ACDC, BraTS2024, and LiTS, SegWithU is the strongest and most consistent single-forward-pass baseline, achieving AUROC/AURC of 0.9838/2.4885, 0.9946/0.2660, and 0.9925/0.8193 respectively, while preserving segmentation quality.
AI.conclusion: Perturbation-based uncertainty modeling in a lightweight post-hoc head is an effective and practical approach for reliability-aware medical segmentation, offering strong discriminatory capability with a single forward pass.

title: Agent-Aided Design for Dynamic CAD Models
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, to be published in CAIS'26
summary: In the past year, researchers have started to create agentic systems that can design real-world CAD-style objects in a training-free setting, a new variety of system that we call Agent-Aided Design. Generally speaking, these systems place an agent in a feedback loop in which it can write code, compile that code to an assembly of CAD model(s), visualize the model, and then iteratively refine its code based on visual and other feedback. Despite rapid progress, a key problem remains: none of these systems can build complex 3D assemblies with moving parts. For example, no existing system can build a piston, a pendulum, or even a pair of scissors. In order for Agent-Aided Design to make a real impact in industrial manufacturing, we need a system that is capable of generating such 3D assemblies. In this paper we present a prototype of AADvark, an agentic system designed for this task. Unlike previous state-of-the-art systems, AADvark captures the dynamic part interactions with one or more degrees-of-freedom. This design decision allows AADvark to reason directly about assemblies with moving parts and can thereby achieve cross-cutting goals, including but not limited to mechanical movements. Unfortunately, current LLMs are imperfect spatial reasoners, a problem that AADvark addresses by incorporating external constraint solver tools with a specialized visual feedback mechanism. We demonstrate that, by modifying the agent's tools (FreeCAD and the assembly solver), we are able to create a strong verification signal which enables our system to build 3D assemblies with movable parts.

AI.tldr: AADvark introduces a prototype agentic system that designs 3D assemblies with moving parts by modeling dynamics with degrees of freedom and using external constraint solvers and visual feedback to overcome LLM-based spatial reasoning limits.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of existing agent-aided design systems that cannot produce complex movable-part assemblies, hindering industrial applicability; improve verification and reliability via external solvers and feedback.
AI.method: Develop AADvark with dynamic DOFs to capture movements; integrate external tools (FreeCAD, assembly solver) and a specialized visual feedback loop; enable iterative code generation and refinement in a training-free setting; modify tools to provide a strong verification signal.
AI.result: Empirical demonstration that tool modifications create a strong verification signal enabling the construction of 3D assemblies with movable parts (pistons, pendulums, scissors), showing cross-cutting goals like mechanical movements.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic DOF-based agent-aided design can extend to movable-part assemblies; external constraint solvers and visual feedback are essential to mitigate imperfect spatial reasoning in LLMs, paving the way for industrially relevant CAD-like intelligent design systems.

title: Why Do Vision Language Models Struggle To Recognize Human Emotions?
summary: Understanding emotions is a fundamental ability for intelligent systems to be able to interact with humans. Vision-language models (VLMs) have made tremendous progress in the last few years for many visual tasks, potentially offering a promising solution for understanding emotions. However, it is surprising that even the most sophisticated contemporary VLMs struggle to recognize human emotions or to outperform even specialized vision-only classifiers. In this paper we ask the question "Why do VLMs struggle to recognize human emotions?", and observe that the inherently continuous and dynamic task of facial expression recognition (DFER) exposes two critical VLM vulnerabilities. First, emotion datasets are naturally long-tailed, and the web-scale data used to pre-train VLMs exacerbates this head-class bias, causing them to systematically collapse rare, under-represented emotions into common categories. We propose alternative sampling strategies that prevent favoring common concepts. Second, temporal information is critical for understanding emotions.
