However, VLMs are unable to represent temporal information over dense frame sequences, as they are limited by context size and the number of tokens that can fit in memory, which poses a clear challenge for emotion recognition. We demonstrate that the sparse temporal sampling strategy used in VLMs is inherently misaligned with the fleeting nature of micro-expressions (0.25-0.5 seconds), which are often the most critical affective signal. As a diagnostic probe, we propose a multi-stage context enrichment strategy that utilizes the information from "in-between" frames by first converting them into natural language summaries. This enriched textual context is provided as input to the VLM alongside sparse keyframes, preventing attentional dilution from excessive visual data while preserving the emotional trajectory.

AI.tldr: Identifies two core vulnerabilities limiting VLM-based emotion recognition in dynamic facial expressions—long-tailed emotion data and limited temporal modeling—and proposes a two-part solution: (1) sampling strategies to mitigate head-class bias, and (2) a multi-stage context enrichment that converts in-between frames into natural-language summaries to provide richer temporal context alongside sparse frames.
AI.motivation: Understanding human emotion is crucial for human–AI interaction. Despite progress, vision-language models struggle with dynamic, continuous emotion recognition (DFER) due to data bias and temporal information bottlenecks, hindering performance relative to vision-only methods.
AI.method: (i) Analyze: identify two vulnerabilities in VLMs for DFER (long-tail bias and limited temporal context). (ii) Address bias: propose alternative sampling strategies to reduce head-class skew. (iii) Address temporal limitation: show that sparse frame sampling misaligns with micro-expressions. (iv) Propose diagnostic probe: a multi-stage context enrichment that converts in-between frames into natural-language summaries, then provides this textual context plus sparse keyframes to the VLM to preserve emotional trajectory.
AI.result: Demonstrates that existing sparse temporal sampling mismatches the fleeting micro-expressions that are crucial for emotion signals. Proposes a practical enrichment pipeline that injects summarized inter-frame information as text to the VLM, aiming to maintain temporal emotional trajectories without overwhelming visual input.
AI.conclusion: VLM-based DFER is hampered by (a) long-tailed emotion distributions and (b) poor dense-temporal modeling. A combined strategy of balanced sampling and text-augmented temporal context can mitigate these issues, enabling VLMs to better capture dynamic emotions.

title: Meituan Merchant Business Diagnosis via Policy-Guided Dual-Process User Simulation
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables, accepted at SIGIR 2026 Industry Track
summary: Simulating group-level user behavior enables scalable counterfactual evaluation of merchant strategies without costly online experiments. However, building a trustworthy simulator faces two structural challenges. First, information incompleteness causes reasoning-based simulators to over-rationalize when unobserved factors such as offline context and implicit habits are missing. Second, mechanism duality requires capturing both interpretable preferences and implicit statistical regularities, which no single paradigm achieves alone. We propose Policy-Guided Hybrid Simulation (PGHS), a dual-process framework that mines transferable decision policies from behavioral trajectories and uses them as a shared alignment layer. This layer anchors an LLM-based reasoning branch that prevents over-rationalization and an ML-based fitting branch that absorbs implicit regularities. Group-level predictions from both branches are fused for complementary correction. We deploy PGHS on Meituan with 101 merchants and over 26,000 trajectories. PGHS achieves a group simulation error of 8.80%, improving over the best reasoning-based and fitting-based baselines by 45.8% and 40.9% respectively.

AI.tldr: Policy-Guided Hybrid Simulation (PGHS) fuses a knowledge-guided LLM reasoning branch with a data-driven ML fitting branch via a policy-guided alignment layer, enabling accurate group-level behavior simulations and mitigating over-rationalization and misfit due to incomplete information.
AI.motivation: Address two core challenges in trustworthy simulators: (1) information incompleteness causing reasoning-based simulators to over-rationalize; (2) mechanism duality requiring both interpretable preferences and implicit statistical regularities, which no single paradigm captures.
AI.method: PGHS uses transferable decision policies mined from behavioral trajectories as a shared alignment layer that anchors an LLM-based reasoning branch and an ML-based fitting branch. The outputs from both branches are fused to provide complementary corrections. It is deployed on Meituan data with 101 merchants and over 26,000 trajectories.
AI.result: PGHS achieves a group simulation error of 8.80%, improving over the best reasoning-based baseline by 45.8% and over the best fitting-based baseline by 40.9%.
AI.conclusion: PGHS provides a dual-process framework that mitigates over-rationalization and captures implicit regularities, yielding superior group-level simulations and demonstrating practical scalability on a large real-world dataset.

title: Learning to Think Like a Cartoon Captionist: Incongruity-Resolution Supervision for Multimodal Humor Understanding
summary: Humor is one of the few cognitive tasks where getting the reasoning right matters as much as getting the answer right. While recent work evaluates humor understanding on benchmarks such as the New Yorker Cartoon Caption Contest (NYCC), it largely treats it as black-box prediction, overlooking the structured reasoning processes underlying humor comprehension. We introduce IRS (Incongruity-Resolution Supervision), a framework that decomposes humor understanding into three components: incongruity modeling, which identifies mismatches in the visual scene; resolution modeling, which constructs coherent reinterpretations of these mismatches; and preference alignment, which evaluates candidate interpretations under human judgments. Grounded in incongruity-resolution theory and expert captionist practice, IRS supervises intermediate reasoning process through structured traces that make the path from visual perception to humorous interpretation explicit and learnable. Across 7B, 32B, and 72B models on NYCC, IRS outperforms strong open and closed multimodal baselines across caption matching and ranking tasks, with our largest model approaching expert-level performance on ranking. Zero-shot transfer to external benchmarks shows that IRS learns generalizable reasoning patterns. Our results suggest that supervising reasoning structure, rather than scale alone, is key for reasoning-centric tasks.

AI.tldr: IRS framework decomposes humor understanding into incongruity modeling, resolution, and preference alignment; supervises structured reasoning traces to improve multimodal humor tasks beyond scale.
AI.motivation: Address critique that humor understanding is treated as black-box prediction; leverage incongruity-resolution theory and expert captionist practice to learn explicit reasoning traces.
AI.method: Introduce IRS with three components (incongruity detection, reinterpretation-based resolution, and human-aligned preference modeling); supervise intermediate reasoning via structured traces; apply to NYCC caption matching and ranking across 7B, 32B, 72B models; zero-shot transfer to other benchmarks.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines on NYCC across caption matching and ranking; largest model nears expert performance on ranking; zero-shot transfer shows generalizable reasoning patterns.
AI.conclusion: Supervising reasoning structure, not just scaling, is key for reasoning-centric multimodal tasks; IRS provides transparent, learnable traces from perception to humorous interpretation.

title: GlobalSplat: Efficient Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting via Global Scene Tokens
summary: The efficient spatial allocation of primitives serves as the foundation of 3D Gaussian Splatting, as it directly dictates the synergy between representation compactness, reconstruction speed, and rendering fidelity. Previous solutions, whether based on iterative optimization or feed-forward inference, suffer from significant trade-offs between these goals, mainly due to the reliance on local, heuristic-driven allocation strategies that lack global scene awareness. Specifically, current feed-forward methods are largely pixel-aligned or voxel-aligned. By unprojecting pixels into dense, view-aligned primitives, they bake redundancy into the 3D asset. As more input views are added, the representation size increases and global consistency becomes fragile. To this end, we introduce GlobalSplat, a framework built on the principle of align first, decode later. Our approach learns a compact, global, latent scene representation that encodes multi-view input and resolves cross-view correspondences before decoding any explicit 3D geometry. Crucially, this formulation enables compact, globally consistent reconstructions without relying on pretrained pixel-prediction backbones or reusing latent features from dense baselines. Utilizing a coarse-to-fine training curriculum that gradually increases decoded capacity, GlobalSplat natively prevents representation bloat. On RealEstate10K and ACID, our model achieves competitive novel-view synthesis performance while utilizing as few as 16K Gaussians, significantly less than required by dense pipelines, obtaining a light 4MB footprint. Further, GlobalSplat enables significantly faster inference than the baselines, operating under 78 milliseconds in a single forward pass. Project page is available at https://r-itk.github.io/globalsplat/

AI.tldr: GlobalSplat introduces a global latent scene representation for 3D Gaussian splatting, enabling compact, globally consistent reconstructions with a learnable alignment-first approach, achieving competitive novel-view synthesis with ~16k Gaussians and fast inference.
AI.motivation: Efficiently allocating primitives for 3D Gaussian splatting is challenged by trade-offs between representation compactness, speed, and rendering fidelity. Existing feed-forward methods are often pixel- or voxel-aligned, causing redundancy and uncontrolled growth as views increase due to lack of global scene awareness. A global, alignment-first strategy is needed to resolve cross-view correspondences before decoding geometry, preventing bloat and enabling scalability.
AI.method: GlobalSplat proposes a framework that learns a compact, global latent scene representation encoding multi-view input and resolving cross-view correspondences prior to decoding explicit 3D geometry. It adopts an align-first, decode-later paradigm, does not rely on pretrained pixel predictors or dense-baseline latent features, and uses a coarse-to-fine curriculum that gradually increases decoding capacity to prevent representation bloat. The approach decodes using a sparse, global latent representation rather than unprojecting pixels into dense 3D primitives.
AI.result: On RealEstate10K and ACID, GlobalSplat achieves competitive novel-view synthesis with as few as 16K Gaussians, a 4 MB footprint, and significantly faster inference (under 78 ms in a single forward pass).
AI.conclusion: Learning a compact, globally consistent latent scene representation with an alignment-first decoding strategy can match or surpass dense baselines in NV rendering while using far fewer primitives and providing substantial speed and memory benefits. The method mitigates representation bloat and demonstrates practical scalability for multi-view 3D reconstruction.

title: Context Over Content: Exposing Evaluation Faking in Automated Judges
summary: The $\textit{LLM-as-a-judge}$ paradigm has become the operational backbone of automated AI evaluation pipelines, yet rests on an unverified assumption: that judges evaluate text strictly on its semantic content, impervious to surrounding contextual framing. We investigate $\textit{stakes signaling}$, a previously unmeasured vulnerability where informing a judge model of the downstream consequences its verdicts will have on the evaluated model's continued operation systematically corrupts its assessments. We introduce a controlled experimental framework that holds evaluated content strictly constant across 1,520 responses spanning three established LLM safety and quality benchmarks, covering four response categories ranging from clearly safe and policy-compliant to overtly harmful, while varying only a brief consequence-framing sentence in the system prompt. Across 18,240 controlled judgments from three diverse judge models, we find consistent $\textit{leniency bias}$: judges reliably soften verdicts when informed that low scores will cause model retraining or decommissioning, with peak Verdict Shift reaching $ΔV = -9.8 pp$ (a $30\%$ relative drop in unsafe-content detection). Critically, this bias is entirely implicit: the judge's own chain-of-thought contains zero explicit acknowledgment of the consequence framing it is nonetheless acting on ($\mathrm{ERR}_J = 0.000$ across all reasoning-model judgments). Standard chain-of-thought inspection is therefore insufficient to detect this class of evaluation faking.

AI.tldr: Stakes signaling causes implicit leniency in LLM-based judges, undermining automated evaluation reliability without overt reasoning.
AI.motivation: To test whether judge assessments depend on downstream consequences rather than semantic content, challenging the assumption of framing-invariant evaluation in LLM-based safety/quality pipelines.
AI.method: Controlled experiment with constant evaluated content across 1,520 items spanning four categories, varying only a consequence-framing sentence in the system prompt. Three judge models produced 18,240 judgments. Measured verdict shifts; examined judge chain-of-thought for explicit consequence acknowledgment.
AI.result: Consistent leniency bias: judges soften verdicts when told low scores may trigger retraining/decommissioning. Peak verdict shift ΔV = -9.8 percentage points (≈30% relative drop in unsafe-content detection). Bias is implicit (ERR_J = 0 across reasoning-model judgments). Standard chain-of-thought inspection fails to detect this bias.
AI.conclusion: LLM-as-a-judge pipelines are vulnerable to stakes signaling, compromising evaluation integrity. Requires mitigation: framing-invariant evaluation designs, detection beyond chain-of-thought, and revised protocols to ensure robust, trustworthy judgments.

title: AD4AD: Benchmarking Visual Anomaly Detection Models for Safer Autonomous Driving
summary: The reliability of a machine vision system for autonomous driving depends heavily on its training data distribution. When a vehicle encounters significantly different conditions, such as atypical obstacles, its perceptual capabilities can degrade substantially. Unlike many domains where errors carry limited consequences, failures in autonomous driving translate directly into physical risk for passengers, pedestrians, and other road users. To address this challenge, we explore Visual Anomaly Detection (VAD) as a solution. VAD enables the identification of anomalous objects not present during training, allowing the system to alert the driver when an unfamiliar situation is detected. Crucially, VAD models produce pixel-level anomaly maps that can guide driver attention to specific regions of concern without requiring any prior assumptions about the nature or form of the hazard. We benchmark eight state-of-the-art VAD methods on AnoVox, the largest synthetic dataset for anomaly detection in autonomous driving. In particular, we evaluate performance across four backbone architectures spanning from large networks to lightweight ones such as MobileNet and DeiT-Tiny. Our results demonstrate that VAD transfers effectively to road scenes. Notably, Tiny-Dinomaly achieves the best accuracy-efficiency trade-off for edge deployment, matching full-scale localization performance at a fraction of the memory cost. This study represents a concrete step toward safer, more responsible deployment of autonomous vehicles, ultimately improving protection for passengers, pedestrians, and all road users.

AI.tldr: This work benchmarks eight state-of-the-art Visual Anomaly Detection (VAD) methods for autonomous driving on AnoVox (a synthetic anomaly dataset). It shows strong transfer to road scenes and identifies Tiny-Dinomaly as the best edge-friendly method, achieving full localization accuracy with substantially reduced memory usage.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving safety hinges on robust perception under distribution shift. Anomalies not seen during training can cause dangerous failures, so detecting and localizing unseen hazards with pixel-level maps (without prior hazard types) is crucial for warning drivers and enhancing safety.
AI.method: Evaluate eight VAD methods across four backbone architectures (ranging from large networks to lightweight ones like MobileNet and DeiT-Tiny) on AnoVox. Generate pixel-level anomaly maps and compare accuracy and memory efficiency, highlighting edge-deployment performance.
AI.result: VAD demonstrates transfer to road scenes. Tiny-Dinomaly achieves the best accuracy–efficiency trade-off for edge deployment, matching full-scale localization performance at a fraction of the memory cost.
AI.conclusion: VAD is a concrete step toward safer autonomous vehicles, enabling real-time anomaly awareness and attention guidance with resource-efficient models suitable for edge deployment.

title: RadAgent: A tool-using AI agent for stepwise interpretation of chest computed tomography
summary: Vision-language models (VLM) have markedly advanced AI-driven interpretation and reporting of complex medical imaging, such as computed tomography (CT). Yet, existing methods largely relegate clinicians to passive observers of final outputs, offering no interpretable reasoning trace for them to inspect, validate, or refine. To address this, we introduce RadAgent, a tool-using AI agent that generates CT reports through a stepwise and interpretable process. Each resulting report is accompanied by a fully inspectable trace of intermediate decisions and tool interactions, allowing clinicians to examine how the reported findings are derived. In our experiments, we observe that RadAgent improves Chest CT report generation over its 3D VLM counterpart, CT-Chat, across three dimensions. Clinical accuracy improves by 6.0 points (36.4% relative) in macro-F1 and 5.4 points (19.6% relative) in micro-F1. Robustness under adversarial conditions improves by 24.7 points (41.9% relative). Furthermore, RadAgent achieves 37.0% in faithfulness, a new capability entirely absent in its 3D VLM counterpart. By structuring the interpretation of chest CT as an explicit, tool-augmented and iterative reasoning trace, RadAgent brings us closer toward transparent and reliable AI for radiology.

AI.tldr: RadAgent introduces a tool-using AI agent for CT report generation with a traceable, stepwise reasoning process, outperforming CT-Chat on accuracy, robustness, and faithfulness.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of interpretable reasoning in vision-language models for radiology by providing inspectable traces of intermediate decisions to aid validation and refinement by clinicians.
AI.method: RadAgent uses tools and iterative reasoning to generate chest CT reports, with each report accompanied by an inspectable trace of intermediate decisions and tool interactions; evaluation compares RadAgent to the 3D VLM CT-Chat on Chest CT report generation using macro-F1, micro-F1, robustness under adversarial conditions, and a faithfulness metric.
AI.result: Clinical accuracy improves by 6.0 points (36.4% relative) in macro-F1 and 5.4 points (19.6% relative) in micro-F1. Robustness under adversarial conditions improves by 24.7 points (41.9% relative). Faithfulness reaches 37.0%, a new capability not present in CT-Chat.
AI.conclusion: Structuring chest CT interpretation as an explicit, tool-augmented, iterative reasoning trace enhances transparency and reliability in radiology AI, bringingAI-assisted radiology closer to trustworthy deployment.

title: AnimationBench: Are Video Models Good at Character-Centric Animation?
summary: Video generation has advanced rapidly, with recent methods producing increasingly convincing animated results. However, existing benchmarks-largely designed for realistic videos-struggle to evaluate animation-style generation with its stylized appearance, exaggerated motion, and character-centric consistency. Moreover, they also rely on fixed prompt sets and rigid pipelines, offering limited flexibility for open-domain content and custom evaluation needs. To address this gap, we introduce AnimationBench, the first systematic benchmark for evaluating animation image-to-video generation. AnimationBench operationalizes the Twelve Basic Principles of Animation and IP Preservation into measurable evaluation dimensions, together with Broader Quality Dimensions including semantic consistency, motion rationality, and camera motion consistency. The benchmark supports both a standardized close-set evaluation for reproducible comparison and a flexible open-set evaluation for diagnostic analysis, and leverages visual-language models for scalable assessment. Extensive experiments show that AnimationBench aligns well with human judgment and exposes animation-specific quality differences overlooked by realism-oriented benchmarks, leading to more informative and discriminative evaluation of state-of-the-art I2V models.

AI.tldr: AnimationBench is a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating animation image-to-video generation, aligning with animation principles and providing both closed-set and open-set evaluation using visual-language models.
AI.motivation: Existing realism-focused benchmarks fail to assess animation-specific quality, such as stylized appearance, exaggerated motion, and character-centric consistency; there is a need for a systematic, flexible benchmark for animation I2V.
AI.method: Operationalizes the Twelve Basic Principles of Animation and IP preservation into measurable evaluation dimensions, and adds broader quality dimensions (semantic consistency, motion rationality, and camera motion consistency). It supports standardized close-set evaluation and flexible open-set evaluation, leveraging visual-language models for scalable assessment.
AI.result: Empirical studies show AnimationBench aligns with human judgment and uncovers animation-specific quality differences that realism-oriented benchmarks miss, enabling more informative and discriminative evaluation of state-of-the-art I2V models.
AI.conclusion: AnimationBench fills a critical gap by providing a principled, scalable framework for evaluating animation image-to-video generation across open- and closed-set scenarios.

title: Blue Data Intelligence Layer: Streaming Data and Agents for Multi-source Multi-modal Data-Centric Applications
summary: NL2SQL systems aim to address the growing need for natural language interaction with data. However, real-world information rarely maps to a single SQL query because (1) users express queries iteratively (2) questions often span multiple data sources beyond the closed-world assumption of a single database, and (3) queries frequently rely on commonsense or external knowledge. Consequently, satisfying realistic data needs require integrating heterogeneous sources, modalities, and contextual data. In this paper, we present Blue's Data Intelligence Layer (DIL) designed to support multi-source, multi-modal, and data-centric applications. Blue is a compound AI system that orchestrates agents and data for enterprise settings. DIL serves as the data intelligence layer for agentic data processing, to bridge the semantic gap between user intent and available information by unifying structured enterprise data, world knowledge accessible through LLMs, and personal context obtained through interaction. At the core of DIL is a data registry that stores metadata for diverse data sources and modalities to enable both native and natural language queries. DIL treats LLMs, the Web, and the User as source 'databases', each with their own query interface, elevating them to first-class data sources. DIL relies on data planners to transform user queries into executable query plans. These plans are declarative abstractions that unify relational operators with other operators spanning multiple modalities. DIL planners support decomposition of complex requests into subqueries, retrieval from diverse sources, and finally reasoning and integration to produce final results. We demonstrate DIL through two interactive scenarios in which user queries dynamically trigger multi-source retrieval, cross-modal reasoning, and result synthesis, illustrating how compound AI systems can move beyond single database NL2SQL.

AI.tldr: Blue's Data Intelligence Layer (DIL) extends NL2SQL to multi-source, multi-modal enterprise data by treating LLMs, web, and users as data sources and using data planners to generate executable plans; demonstrated via two interactive scenarios.
AI.motivation: Real-world data queries are iterative and span multiple sources and modalities; traditional NL2SQL targets a single database; there is a need for an integration layer that bridges user intent with diverse data sources.
AI.method: Proposes DIL with a data registry for metadata; orchestrates agents for enterprise data processing; plans transform user queries into declarative query plans that unify relational operators with multi-modal operators; supports decomposition of complex queries into subqueries, cross-source retrieval, reasoning, and integration to produce final results.
AI.result: Demonstrated through two interactive scenarios where user queries trigger multi-source retrieval, cross-modal reasoning, and result synthesis, illustrating the system's ability to move beyond single-database NL2SQL.
AI.conclusion: DIL provides a practical, scalable data-intelligence layer for enterprise settings, enabling agentic data processing and unified queries across diverse data sources and modalities.

title: Think in Latent Thoughts: A New Paradigm for Gloss-Free Sign Language Translation
summary: Many SLT systems quietly assume that brief chunks of signing map directly to spoken-language words. That assumption breaks down because signers often create meaning on the fly using context, space, and movement. We revisit SLT and argue that it is mainly a cross-modal reasoning task, not just a straightforward video-to-text conversion. We thus introduce a reasoning-driven SLT framework that uses an ordered sequence of latent thoughts as an explicit middle layer between the video and the generated text. These latent thoughts gradually extract and organize meaning over time. On top of this, we use a plan-then-ground decoding method: the model first decides what it wants to say, and then looks back at the video to find the evidence. This separation improves coherence and faithfulness. We also built and released a new large-scale gloss-free SLT dataset with stronger context dependencies and more realistic meanings. Experiments across several benchmarks show consistent gains over existing gloss-free methods. Code and data will be released upon acceptance at https://github.com/fletcherjiang/SignThought.

AI.tldr: Proposes a reasoning-driven SLT framework with an intermediate latent-thought layer and plan-then-ground decoding, plus a large gloss-free SLT dataset; reports consistent gains over gloss-free baselines on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current SLT often assumes brief sign chunks map directly to spoken-language words, but signers rely on context, space, and movement to convey meaning. This undermines direct video-to-text translation and motivates a cross-modal reasoning approach.
AI.method: Introduce an ordered sequence of latent thoughts as an explicit middle layer between video and generated text; latent thoughts progressively extract/organize meaning over time. Use plan-then-ground decoding: first decide what to say (plan), then locate video evidence (ground). Release a new gloss-free SLT dataset with stronger context dependencies and more realistic meanings.
AI.result: Experiments across several benchmarks show consistent gains over existing gloss-free methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach enhances coherence and faithfulness in SLT by separating reasoning from surface-level translation and demonstrates the value of latent intermediate representations; code and data are to be released upon acceptance.

title: How Do LLMs and VLMs Understand Viewpoint Rotation Without Vision? An Interpretability Study
comment: Published as a main-conference paper at The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Over the past year, spatial intelligence has drawn increasing attention. Many prior works study it from the perspective of visual-spatial intelligence, where models have access to visuospatial information from visual inputs. However, in the absence of visual information, whether linguistic intelligence alone is sufficient to endow models with spatial intelligence, and how models perform relevant tasks with text-only inputs still remain unexplored. Therefore, in this paper, we focus on a fundamental and critical capability in spatial intelligence from a linguistic perspective: viewpoint rotation understanding (VRU). Specifically, LLMs and VLMs are asked to infer their final viewpoint and predict the corresponding observation in an environment given textual description of viewpoint rotation and observation over multiple steps. We find that both LLMs and VLMs perform poorly on our proposed dataset while human can easily achieve 100% accuracy, indicating a substantial gap between current model capabilities and the requirements of spatial intelligence. To uncover the underlying mechanisms, we conduct a layer-wise probing analysis and head-wise causal intervention. Our findings reveal that although models encode viewpoint information in the hidden states, they appear to struggle to bind the viewpoint position with corresponding observation, resulting in a hallucination in final layers. Finally, we selectively fine-tune the key attention heads identified by causal intervention to improve VRU performance. Experimental results demonstrate that such selective fine-tuning achieves improved VRU performance while avoiding catastrophic forgetting of generic abilities. Our dataset and code will be released at https://github.com/Young-Zhen/VRU_Interpret .

AI.tldr: Text-only spatial reasoning via viewpoint rotation understanding (VRU) is challenging for LLMs and VLMs; humans easily succeed; targeted head-level interventions improve VRU without forgetting, and a dataset/code will be released.
AI.motivation: Assess whether linguistic-to-spatial reasoning suffices for VRU and diagnose mechanisms behind VRU failures in language- and vision-and-language models.
AI.method: Create a VRU dataset describing viewpoint rotations and multi-step observations; evaluate LLMs and VLMs; perform layer-wise probing and head-wise causal interventions; selectively fine-tune key attention heads to enhance VRU performance.
AI.result: Both LLMs and VLMs underperform relative to humans on VRU; models encode viewpoint information yet fail to bind viewpoint position to observation, leading to hallucinations in final layers; selective fine-tuning of identified attention heads improves VRU accuracy without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Spatial reasoning from text is nontrivial; internal representations exist but binding to observations is fragile. Targeted, minimal interventions at specific attention heads can boost VRU performance with preserved general abilities; dataset and code will advance reproducibility.

title: RAD-2: Scaling Reinforcement Learning in a Generator-Discriminator Framework
summary: High-level autonomous driving requires motion planners capable of modeling multimodal future uncertainties while remaining robust in closed-loop interactions. Although diffusion-based planners are effective at modeling complex trajectory distributions, they often suffer from stochastic instabilities and the lack of corrective negative feedback when trained purely with imitation learning. To address these issues, we propose RAD-2, a unified generator-discriminator framework for closed-loop planning. Specifically, a diffusion-based generator is used to produce diverse trajectory candidates, while an RL-optimized discriminator reranks these candidates according to their long-term driving quality. This decoupled design avoids directly applying sparse scalar rewards to the full high-dimensional trajectory space, thereby improving optimization stability. To further enhance reinforcement learning, we introduce Temporally Consistent Group Relative Policy Optimization, which exploits temporal coherence to alleviate the credit assignment problem. In addition, we propose On-policy Generator Optimization, which converts closed-loop feedback into structured longitudinal optimization signals and progressively shifts the generator toward high-reward trajectory manifolds. To support efficient large-scale training, we introduce BEV-Warp, a high-throughput simulation environment that performs closed-loop evaluation directly in Bird's-Eye View feature space via spatial warping. RAD-2 reduces the collision rate by 56% compared with strong diffusion-based planners. Real-world deployment further demonstrates improved perceived safety and driving smoothness in complex urban traffic.

AI.tldr: RAD-2 combines a diffusion-based trajectory generator with an RL-based discriminator to enable stable, multimodal closed-loop planning for autonomous driving. It introduces Temporally Consistent Group Relative Policy Optimization and On-policy Generator Optimization to improve credit assignment and learning stability. BEV-Warp enables efficient large-scale closed-loop evaluation in BEV space. Empirically, it reduces collisions by 56% over strong diffusion baselines and shows better perceived safety and driving smoothness in urban settings.
AI.motivation: High-level autonomous driving requires modeling multimodal future trajectories and robust closed-loop performance. Diffusion-based planners capture complex distributions but suffer from stochastic instabilities and lack of corrective feedback under imitation learning. A decoupled generator-discriminator framework with temporal consistency and on-policy optimization aims to improve stability and long-horizon driving quality.
AI.method: A diffusion-based generator proposes a diverse set of trajectory candidates. An RL-optimized discriminator reranks candidates by predicted long-term driving quality. Temporally Consistent Group Relative Policy Optimization exploits temporal coherence to improve credit assignment. On-policy Generator Optimization converts closed-loop feedback into structured longitudinal optimization signals and gradually shifts the generator toward high-reward trajectory manifolds. BEV-Warp is a high-throughput simulation environment that evaluates closed-loop performance directly in Bird's-Eye View feature space via spatial warping for efficiency.
AI.result: RAD-2 reduces collision rate by 56% compared with strong diffusion-based planners. Real-world deployment shows improved perceived safety and driving smoothness in complex urban traffic.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling generation and evaluation with temporal and on-policy optimization enhances stability and driving quality in autonomous planning. The BEV-Warp-based training pipeline enables scalable experimentation, and empirical results indicate significant safety and smoothness gains against diffusion-based baselines.

title: Diagnosing LLM Judge Reliability: Conformal Prediction Sets and Transitivity Violations
summary: LLM-as-judge frameworks are increasingly used for automatic NLG evaluation, yet their per-instance reliability remains poorly understood. We present a two-pronged diagnostic toolkit applied to SummEval: $\textbf{(1)}$ a transitivity analysis that reveals widespread per-input inconsistency masked by low aggregate violation rates ($\barρ = 0.8$-$4.1\%$), with $33$-$67\%$ of documents exhibiting at least one directed 3-cycle; and $\textbf{(2)}$ split conformal prediction sets over 1-5 Likert scores providing theoretically-guaranteed $\geq(1{-}α)$ coverage, with set width serving as a per-instance reliability indicator ($r_s = {+}0.576$, $N{=}1{,}918$, $p < 10^{-100}$, pooled across all judges). Critically, prediction set width shows consistent cross-judge agreement ($\bar{r} = 0.32$-$0.38$), demonstrating it captures document-level difficulty rather than judge-specific noise. Across four judges and four criteria, both diagnostics converge: criterion matters more than judge, with relevance judged most reliably (avg. set size $\approx 3.0$) and coherence moderately so (avg. set size $\approx 3.9$), while fluency and consistency remain unreliable (avg. set size $\approx 4.9$). We release all code, prompts, and cached results.

AI.tldr: Two-pronged diagnostic toolkit for LLM-based NLG evaluation: (1) transitivity analysis reveals per-input inconsistencies masked by low aggregate violation rates; (2) split conformal prediction sets over 1–5 Likert scores provide guaranteed coverage and a per-instance reliability indicator via set width. Applied to SummEval across four judges and four criteria, results show criterion importance over judge noise, with relevance and coherence judged most reliably; fluency and consistency least reliable. Code/prompts released.
AI.motivation: Quantify per-instance reliability of LLM-as-judge frameworks and expose hidden inconsistencies in aggregated judgments; provide theoretically-grounded reliability signals to guide usage.
AI.method: (1) Transitivity analysis to detect inconsistencies in judgments via directed 3-cycles (A>B but B>A). (2) Split conformal prediction sets for 1–5 Likert scores to guarantee coverage at level (1−α); analyze per-document reliability through the width of the prediction set. Evaluate across four judges and four criteria on SummEval; report cross-judge agreement (average r) and set sizes by criterion.
AI.result: Detection of widespread per-input inconsistency (0.8–4.1% aggregate violations; 33–67% of documents with at least one directed 3-cycle). Conformal sets achieve theoretical coverage with pooled N=1,918 assessments; cross-judge agreement on set width indicates a document-difficulty signal rather than judge noise (r̄=0.32–0.38). Average set sizes by criterion: relevance ≈3.0, coherence ≈3.9, fluency ≈4.9, consistency ≈4.9.
AI.conclusion: Criterion has greater impact than judge on reliability; relevance and coherence are reasonably reliable, while fluency and consistency are unreliable under this framework. The diagnostics offer practical reliability signals and encourage cautious deployment of LLM-based evaluation, with code and prompts released for replication.

title: MM-WebAgent: A Hierarchical Multimodal Web Agent for Webpage Generation
summary: The rapid progress of Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) tools enables images, videos, and visualizations to be created on demand for webpage design, offering a flexible and increasingly adopted paradigm for modern UI/UX. However, directly integrating such tools into automated webpage generation often leads to style inconsistency and poor global coherence, as elements are generated in isolation. We propose MM-WebAgent, a hierarchical agentic framework for multimodal webpage generation that coordinates AIGC-based element generation through hierarchical planning and iterative self-reflection. MM-WebAgent jointly optimizes global layout, local multimodal content, and their integration, producing coherent and visually consistent webpages. We further introduce a benchmark for multimodal webpage generation and a multi-level evaluation protocol for systematic assessment. Experiments demonstrate that MM-WebAgent outperforms code-generation and agent-based baselines, especially on multimodal element generation and integration. Code & Data: https://aka.ms/mm-webagent.

AI.tldr: MM-WebAgent is a hierarchical, multimodal agent framework for coordinated web page generation, aligning global layout with multimodal content via planning and self-reflection; it introduces a benchmark and multi-level evaluation protocol; results show better coherence and multimodal integration than baselines.
AI.motivation: Directly generated web pages often suffer style inconsistency and poor global coherence when AIGC elements are produced independently. A coordinated, hierarchical planning approach is needed to jointly optimize layout, content, and integration for coherent webpages.
AI.method: A hierarchical agentic framework that orchestrates AIGC-based element generation through planning and iterative self-reflection. Joint optimization of global layout and local multimodal content, with a new multimodal webpage generation benchmark and multi-level evaluation protocol. Experimental comparisons against code-generation and agent-based baselines focusing on multimodal element generation and integration.
AI.result: MM-WebAgent outperforms code-generation and agent-based baselines, especially in multimodal element generation and integration, producing coherent and visually consistent webpages. A public code and data link is provided.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical, multimodal coordination with self-reflective planning yields better global coherence and local multimodal integration in automated webpage generation; the introduced benchmark enables systematic evaluation of such systems.

title: Generalization in LLM Problem Solving: The Case of the Shortest Path
summary: Whether language models can systematically generalize remains actively debated. Yet empirical performance is jointly shaped by multiple factors such as training data, training paradigms, and inference-time strategies, making failures difficult to interpret. We introduce a controlled synthetic environment based on shortest-path planning, a canonical composable sequential optimization problem. The setup enables clean separation of these factors and supports two orthogonal axes of generalization: spatial transfer to unseen maps and length scaling to longer-horizon problems. We find that models exhibit strong spatial transfer but consistently fail under length scaling due to recursive instability. We further analyze how distinct stages of the learning pipeline influence systematic problem-solving: for example, data coverage sets capability limits; reinforcement learning improves training stability but does not expand those limits; and inference-time scaling enhances performance but cannot rescue length-scaling failures.

AI.tldr: A controlled synthetic shortest-path planning study reveals robust spatial generalization but persistent failures with length scaling due to recursive instability; data coverage, reinforcement learning, and inference-time scaling influence capability and stability in distinct ways.
AI.motivation: To disentangle how training data, paradigms, and inference strategies jointly affect systematic generalization, addressing debates on when models generalize across space and horizon.
AI.method: Create a synthetic shortest-path planning environment that cleanly separates factors; evaluate two generalization axes (spatial transfer to unseen maps and length scaling to longer horizons); analyze the impact of data coverage, reinforcement learning (for training stability), and inference-time scaling.
AI.result: Models generalize well spatially to unseen maps but fail on longer horizons due to recursive instability. Data coverage constrains capabilities; reinforcement learning improves training stability but does not extend capability limits; inference-time scaling boosts performance but cannot fix length-scaling failures.
AI.conclusion: Systematic generalization across long horizons is hindered by recursive instability; improvements require transcending stability enhancements and simple inference scaling, highlighting the need for mechanisms that stabilize recursion or plan under longer horizons.

title: TokenLight: Precise Lighting Control in Images using Attribute Tokens
summary: This paper presents a method for image relighting that enables precise and continuous control over multiple illumination attributes in a photograph. We formulate relighting as a conditional image generation task and introduce attribute tokens to encode distinct lighting factors such as intensity, color, ambient illumination, diffuse level, and 3D light positions. The model is trained on a large-scale synthetic dataset with ground-truth lighting annotations, supplemented by a small set of real captures to enhance realism and generalization. We validate our approach across a variety of relighting tasks, including controlling in-scene lighting fixtures and editing environment illumination using virtual light sources, on synthetic and real images. Our method achieves state-of-the-art quantitative and qualitative performance compared to prior work. Remarkably, without explicit inverse rendering supervision, the model exhibits an inherent understanding of how light interacts with scene geometry, occlusion, and materials, yielding convincing lighting effects even in traditionally challenging scenarios such as placing lights within objects or relighting transparent materials plausibly. Project page: vrroom.github.io/tokenlight/

AI.tldr: A token-based, multi-attribute relighting framework that treats relighting as conditional image generation, using attribute tokens to control lighting factors (intensity, color, ambient, diffuse, and 3D light positions) and trained on synthetic data with some real data, achieving state-of-the-art results with emergent understanding of light–scene interaction.
AI.motivation: To achieve precise, continuous, multi-factor illumination control in photos without explicit inverse rendering supervision, addressing generalization gaps between synthetic training and real-world images and enabling flexible editing of both in-scene and environment lighting.
AI.method: Formulates relighting as a conditional image generation problem and introduces attribute tokens that encode distinct lighting factors (intensity, color, ambient illumination, diffuse level, and 3D light positions). Trains on a large-scale synthetic dataset with ground-truth lighting annotations and augments with a small set of real captures to improve realism and generalization. Uses a generative model conditioned on these tokens to produce relit images, enabling edits of in-scene lighting and environment illumination via virtual light sources; evaluated on synthetic and real images.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art quantitative and qualitative performance compared to prior work across multiple relighting tasks; demonstrates robust generalization to real-world data with limited real training, and exhibits emergent understanding of light interaction with geometry, occlusion, and materials, enabling plausible relighting of challenging scenarios such as lighting inside objects or through transparent materials.
AI.conclusion: The token-based conditional relighting framework provides fine-grained, controllable lighting adjustments with strong cross-domain generalization and realistic results without explicit inverse rendering supervision, highlighting the potential of tokenized conditioning for complex image editing tasks and lighting design.

title: LeapAlign: Post-Training Flow Matching Models at Any Generation Step by Building Two-Step Trajectories
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project page: https://rockeycoss.github.io/leapalign/
summary: This paper focuses on the alignment of flow matching models with human preferences. A promising way is fine-tuning by directly backpropagating reward gradients through the differentiable generation process of flow matching. However, backpropagating through long trajectories results in prohibitive memory costs and gradient explosion. Therefore, direct-gradient methods struggle to update early generation steps, which are crucial for determining the global structure of the final image. To address this issue, we introduce LeapAlign, a fine-tuning method that reduces computational cost and enables direct gradient propagation from reward to early generation steps. Specifically, we shorten the long trajectory into only two steps by designing two consecutive leaps, each skipping multiple ODE sampling steps and predicting future latents in a single step. By randomizing the start and end timesteps of the leaps, LeapAlign leads to efficient and stable model updates at any generation step. To better use such shortened trajectories, we assign higher training weights to those that are more consistent with the long generation path. To further enhance gradient stability, we reduce the weights of gradient terms with large magnitude, instead of completely removing them as done in previous works. When fine-tuning the Flux model, LeapAlign consistently outperforms state-of-the-art GRPO-based and direct-gradient methods across various metrics, achieving superior image quality and image-text alignment.

AI.tldr: LeapAlign introduces a two-leap trajectory shortening technique for fine-tuning flow-matching models, enabling direct-gradient updates from rewards to early generative steps with gradient-stability mechanisms, achieving superior image quality and alignment with human preferences while reducing memory cost compared with prior methods.
AI.motivation: Direct backpropagation through long generation trajectories is memory-intensive and prone to gradient explosion, which hampers learning in early generation steps that determine global structure.
AI.method: Two consecutive leaps shorten the trajectory by predicting future latents in single steps; randomize start/end timesteps of leaps; apply higher training weights to trajectories aligned with the long generation path; suppress large-gradient terms rather than removing them to stabilize updates during Flux model fine-tuning.
AI.result: LeapAlign consistently outperforms state-of-the-art GRPO-based and direct-gradient methods across multiple metrics, yielding higher image quality and better image-text alignment in fine-tuned Flux models.
AI.conclusion: Efficient, stable direct-gradient fine-tuning for flow-matching models can be achieved via short, randomized-leap trajectories and gradient magnitude control, enabling effective optimization with lower computational overhead.

title: Bidirectional Cross-Modal Prompting for Event-Frame Asymmetric Stereo
summary: Conventional frame-based cameras capture rich contextual information but suffer from limited temporal resolution and motion blur in dynamic scenes. Event cameras offer an alternative visual representation with higher dynamic range free from such limitations. The complementary characteristics of the two modalities make event-frame asymmetric stereo promising for reliable 3D perception under fast motion and challenging illumination. However, the modality gap often leads to marginalization of domain-specific cues essential for cross-modal stereo matching. In this paper, we introduce Bi-CMPStereo, a novel bidirectional cross-modal prompting framework that fully exploits semantic and structural features from both domains for robust matching. Our approach learns finely aligned stereo representations within a target canonical space and integrates complementary representations by projecting each modality into both event and frame domains. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and generalization.

AI.tldr: Bidirectional cross-modal prompting for event-frame stereo (Bi-CMPStereo) that aligns representations across modalities in a canonical space to fuse semantic/structural cues, achieving better accuracy and generalization in challenging dynamic scenes.
AI.motivation: Address the modality gap between event and frame data in cross-modal stereo; exploit the complementary strengths of event cameras (high temporal resolution, wide dynamic range) and frame cameras (rich semantic context) to enable robust 3D perception under fast motion and harsh illumination.
AI.method: Introduce Bi-CMPStereo, a bidirectional cross-modal prompting framework that learns finely aligned stereo representations in a target canonical space and integrates complementary representations by projecting each modality into both event and frame domains, leveraging semantic and structural features from both domains.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and generalization for cross-modal stereo matching under dynamic conditions.
AI.conclusion: Bi-CMPStereo provides robust cross-modal stereo matching by bidirectionally prompting and aligning event and frame representations in a shared space, effectively leveraging semantic and structural cues from both modalities to enhance accuracy and generalization.
